topics.title: Desirable Properties and Testing of Materials: Introduction to Highway Pavements - Flexible and Rigid Pavements - Component Parts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET746 - HIGHWAY MATERIALS AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Desirable Properties and Testing of Materials: Introduction to Highway Pavements - Flexible and Rigid Pavements - Component Parts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Highway Pavement — Formal Definition

A **highway pavement** is a durable, engineered surface structure constructed over the natural subgrade soil, designed to distribute the applied vehicular wheel loads over a sufficiently large area of the underlying soil mass such that the induced stresses remain within the safe bearing capacity of the subgrade, while simultaneously providing a smooth, skid-resistant, comfortable, and durable riding surface for traffic.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A pavement is fundamentally a **load-distributing medium**, NOT a structural slab. This is the single most important conceptual difference between a flexible pavement and a rigid pavement.

## 1.2 Classification of Highway Pavements

Highway pavements are broadly classified into two major categories based on their **structural response to wheel loads**:

| Classification | Load Transfer Mechanism | Governing Property | Typical Material |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Flexible Pavement** | Grain-to-grain particle contact (aggregate interlock) | Elastic Modulus of subgrade + layer thickness | Bituminous (asphalt) layers over granular base |
| **Rigid Pavement** | Slab action (beam/slab bending) | Flexural (tensile) strength of the slab | Portland Cement Concrete (PCC) slab |

A third category, **Semi-rigid / Composite pavement**, exists where a bituminous layer is laid over a cement-treated or lean concrete base, exhibiting a hybrid response.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Cardboard on Mud" Analogy (Flexible Pavement):**
> Imagine placing a piece of **thick corrugated cardboard** on a tray of soft wet mud. When you press your finger on it, the cardboard bends, the load spreads outward through the fibres, and the mud below is mildly compressed. The cardboard "flexes" with the load. The total thickness of the layers must be high because the mud cannot take much stress. **This is exactly how a flexible pavement works** — the bituminous layer bends, the load spreads laterally through the aggregate skeleton, and the stress reaching the subgrade is a function of total pavement thickness.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Concrete Slab on Sand" Analogy (Rigid Pavement):**
> Now imagine placing a **flat concrete paver tile** on a bed of compacted sand. Press your finger on it. The tile barely bends — instead, it acts like a beam cantilevered over the sand. The load is distributed over a large plate area (the entire tile footprint), so the sand below experiences very low pressure. **This is how a rigid pavement works** — the concrete slab bridges over weak spots in the subgrade, and the load is carried primarily by the slab's flexural rigidity, not by the underlying layers.

## 1.4 Fundamental Performance Requirements of a Pavement

A pavement, irrespective of type, must satisfy the following **desirable functional and structural requirements**:

1. **Sufficient Structural Strength** — to resist deformation and cracking under repeated traffic loading.
2. **Adequate Riding Quality (Smoothness)** — measured by International Roughness Index (IRI) in **m/km**.
3. **Skid Resistance** — surface friction coefficient typically maintained at **≥ 0.35 to 0.40** for safe braking.
4. **Drainage Efficiency** — cross-fall of **2% to 2.5%** for bituminous surfaces and **1.5% to 2%** for concrete surfaces.
5. **Durability** — resistance to weathering, abrasion, and fatigue over a design life of **15 to 30 years**.
6. **Economy** — minimum life-cycle cost considering initial construction, maintenance, and rehabilitation.

## 1.5 KTU 2024 Module 1 — Scope Definition

As per the official KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **PECET746**, Module 1 establishes the foundational vocabulary that every subsequent module (soil, aggregate, bitumen, mix design, pavement design) will build upon. Students must master the component terminology *before* attempting numerical problems on layer thickness or stress distribution.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Flexible Pavement — Theoretical Framework

A **flexible pavement** is a multi-layered system in which each layer receives load from the layer above, spreads it over a progressively larger area, and transfers the reduced stress to the layer below. The total structure deflects (flexes) under wheel load, and the deflection is maximum at the surface and decreases with depth.

### 2.1.1 The Layered System Principle

The flexible pavement operates on the **layered elastic half-space principle** developed by Burmister (1943, 1945). The fundamental assumptions are:

- Each layer is **homogeneous, isotropic, and linearly elastic**.
- Each layer has a defined **Elastic Modulus (E)** and **Poisson's ratio (μ)**.
- The subgrade is treated as a **semi-infinite elastic half-space**.
- Layers are **infinite in horizontal extent** but finite in thickness.
- Full friction (no slip) is assumed at layer interfaces (typically).

### 2.1.2 Load Distribution Concept

The vertical compressive stress at the interface of a flexible pavement layer is governed by the **telescoping stress distribution** concept:

$$\sigma_z = \frac{P}{A_e}$$

where:
- $\sigma_z$ = vertical stress at depth $z$ (in **kPa** or **MPa**)
- $P$ = applied wheel load (in **kN** or **N**)
- $A_e$ = effective contact area at depth $z$ (in **m²** or **mm²**)

The effective contact area **grows with depth** because the load spreads through the layer interfaces, typically assumed at a stress distribution angle of **1 vertical : 2 horizontal (1V:2H)** for granular layers and **1V:1H to 1V:0.5H** for bituminous layers.

### 2.1.3 Vertical Stress at Subgrade Level — Burmister's Concept

A simplified but highly useful relationship for stress at the subgrade surface:

$$\sigma_{z,\text{sub}} = \frac{P}{(a + h)^2}$$

where:
- $P$ = wheel load (kN)
- $a$ = radius of circular contact area (mm)
- $h$ = total flexible pavement thickness above subgrade (mm)
- $\sigma_{z,\text{sub}}$ = vertical stress on subgrade (MPa)

## 2.2 Rigid Pavement — Theoretical Framework

A **rigid pavement** is a pavement structure in which the cement concrete slab provides the dominant load-carrying capacity. The slab behaves as a **Westergaard plate (1926)** resting on a dense liquid subgrade (Winkler foundation) or an elastic solid subgrade.

### 2.2.1 Westergaard's Theory — Key Assumptions

- The concrete slab is **homogeneous, isotropic, and elastic**.
- The slab has **uniform thickness (h)**.
- The subgrade reaction is **vertical only** (no shear transfer).
- The subgrade is characterized by the **Modulus of Subgrade Reaction (k)** in **MPa/m** or **pci (lb/in³)**.
- The slab is in **full contact** with the subgrade.

### 2.2.2 Westergaard's Critical Stress Equations

For a **corner loading** condition (most critical for rigid pavement design):

$$\sigma_c = \frac{3P}{h^2} \left[ 1 - \left( \frac{a\sqrt{2}}{l} \right)^{0.6} \right]$$

For an **edge loading** condition (interior edge, midway between transverse joints):

$$\sigma_e = \frac{0.803 P}{h^2} \left[ 4 \log_{10}\left(\frac{l}{a}\right) + 0.666 \frac{a}{l} - 0.034 \right]$$

For an **interior loading** condition (load applied far from edges):

$$\sigma_i = \frac{0.316 P}{h^2} \left[ 4 \log_{10}\left(\frac{l}{b}\right) + 1.069 \right]$$

where:
- $P$ = wheel load (kg or N)
- $h$ = slab thickness (cm or m)
- $a$ = radius of equivalent circular contact area (cm or m)
- $b$ = equivalent radius for interior loading, $b = \sqrt{1.6 a^2 + h^2} - 0.675 h$
- $l$ = **radius of relative stiffness** of the slab-subgrade system

### 2.2.3 Radius of Relative Stiffness (l)

This is the most important parameter in Westergaard's analysis:

$$l = \left[ \frac{E h^3}{12 k (1 - \mu^2)} \right]^{1/4}$$

where:
- $E$ = Elastic Modulus of concrete (MPa)
- $h$ = slab thickness (m)
- $k$ = Modulus of subgrade reaction (MPa/m)
- $\mu$ = Poisson's ratio of concrete (typically **0.15**)

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Application | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sigma_z = P / A_e$ | Vertical stress distribution in flexible pavement | kPa |
| 2 | $A_e = \pi (a + z \tan\alpha)^2$ | Effective contact area at depth $z$, where $\alpha$ is load spread angle | m² |
| 3 | $l = \left[ \frac{E h^3}{12 k (1 - \mu^2)} \right]^{1/4}$ | Westergaard radius of relative stiffness | m |
| 4 | $\sigma_c = \frac{3P}{h^2} \left[ 1 - \left( \frac{a\sqrt{2}}{l} \right)^{0.6} \right]$ | Critical corner stress in rigid pavement | N/mm² |
| 5 | $\sigma_e = \frac{0.803 P}{h^2} \left[ 4 \log_{10}\left(\frac{l}{a}\right) + 0.666 \frac{a}{l} - 0.034 \right]$ | Critical edge stress in rigid pavement | N/mm² |
| 6 | $\sigma_i = \frac{0.316 P}{h^2} \left[ 4 \log_{10}\left(\frac{l}{b}\right) + 1.069 \right]$ | Critical interior stress in rigid pavement | N/mm² |
| 7 | $b = \sqrt{1.6 a^2 + h^2} - 0.675 h$ | Equivalent radius for interior loading | m |
| 8 | $\text{IRI} \leq 2.5 \text{ m/km}$ | International Roughness Index for new bituminous surface | m/km |
| 9 | $E_{\text{bitumen}} \approx 1000 \text{ to } 5000 \text{ MPa}$ | Typical elastic modulus of bituminous mix | MPa |
| 10 | $E_{\text{PCC}} \approx 28000 \text{ to } 35000 \text{ MPa}$ | Typical elastic modulus of cement concrete | MPa |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory Trick for KTU Exam:** "**CEI** = Corner, Edge, Interior" — these are the three Westergaard loading conditions, in order of decreasing criticality. Corner is *most* critical, Interior is *least* critical.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Flexible pavements** are preferred for **highways, urban arterials, and rural roads** with moderate to heavy traffic but are unsuitable for locations with standing water or fuel spillage.
- **Rigid pavements** are preferred for **airfield runways, taxiways, container terminals, bus bays, toll plazas, and urban intersections** due to their ability to bridge weak subgrade spots and resist fuel/oil attack.
- The selection between the two is a function of **traffic loading (ESAL/Equivalent Single Axle Load), subgrade strength (CBR), available materials, initial budget, and life-cycle cost**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Component Parts of a Flexible Pavement — Exhaustive Layer Analysis

A flexible pavement, viewed as a cross-section from top to bottom, consists of **four principal layers** stacked above the natural subgrade. Each layer has a distinct material composition, function, and thickness range.

### 3.1.1 Layer 1 — Surface Course (Wearing Course + Binder Course)

**Material:** Dense Bituminous Macadam (DBM), Bituminous Concrete (BC), Asphaltic Concrete (AC), Stone Matrix Asphalt (SMA), or Mastic Asphalt (MA).

**Functions:**
- Provides a smooth, skid-resistant, dust-free, and water-tight riding surface.
- Resists the **direct abrasive wear** from pneumatic tyres and **environmental oxidation** of the underlying bitumen.
- Transmits wheel load to the underlying binder course.

**Typical Thickness:** **25 mm to 50 mm** for BC (wearing course) + **50 mm to 100 mm** for DBM (binder course).

### 3.1.2 Layer 2 — Base Course

**Material:** Wet Mix Macadam (WMM), Water Bound Macadam (WBM), Crusher Run Macadam (CRM), or Cement Treated Base (CTB).

**Functions:**
- The **principal load-distributing layer** of the flexible pavement.
- Carries the bulk of the traffic-induced compressive and shear stresses.
- Provides **structural rigidity** to the pavement system.
- Must possess high **California Bearing Ratio (CBR ≥ 80% to 100%)** and resistance to deformation.

**Typical Thickness:** **100 mm to 300 mm** (laid in compacted layers of ≤ 100 mm each).

### 3.1.3 Layer 3 — Sub-base Course

**Material:** Granular Sub-base (GSB) of crushed stone, moorum, gravel, or stabilized soil.

**Functions:**
- Acts as a **secondary load-distributing layer** between the base and the subgrade.
- Provides **drainage** of infiltrated water, preventing pore-pressure buildup.
- Protects the subgrade from frost action in cold climates.
- Acts as a **construction platform** for the placement of base course materials.
- Typical CBR requirement: **≥ 20% to 30%**.

**Typical Thickness:** **100 mm to 200 mm**.

### 3.1.4 Layer 4 — Subgrade (Natural Soil Foundation)

**Material:** The natural in-situ soil, compacted to ≥ 97% of Modified Proctor Maximum Dry Density (MPMDD).

**Functions:**
- The **foundation** that ultimately carries all pavement loads.
- Must be evaluated for **CBR, plasticity index (PI), swelling potential, and moisture susceptibility**.
- For low-CBR subgrades (< 5%), a **stabilized subgrade** with lime/cement/fly-ash is used.

**Minimum CBR requirement:** **≥ 5% to 8%** for low-volume roads; **≥ 8% to 10%** for expressways.

### 3.1.5 Total Flexible Pavement Thickness

$$\text{Total Thickness} = h_{\text{surface}} + h_{\text{base}} + h_{\text{sub-base}}$$

For a typical **NH/SH (National/State Highway)** flexible pavement: **400 mm to 750 mm** total.

## 3.2 Component Parts of a Rigid Pavement — Exhaustive Layer Analysis

A rigid pavement cross-section, from top to bottom, consists of the following layers:

### 3.2.1 Layer 1 — Cement Concrete Slab (Surface Course)

**Material:** M40 to M50 grade Plain Cement Concrete (PCC), typically with dowel bars and tie bars as per IRC:15.

**Functions:**
- The **primary structural element** that distributes wheel loads via slab action.
- Provides the riding surface directly.
- Resists flexural (bending) tensile stresses generated under wheel loads.

**Typical Thickness:** **150 mm to 350 mm** depending on traffic and design life.

### 3.2.2 Layer 2 — Base Course (under the slab)

**Material:** Dry Lean Concrete (DLC), Cement Treated Base (CTB), or WMM.

**Functions:**
- Provides a **uniform, stable, and erosion-resistant** platform for the slab.
- Prevents **pumping** of subgrade fines through joints.
- Adds to the **effective structural capacity** of the pavement.

**Typical Thickness:** **100 mm to 150 mm** (DLC) or **150 mm to 200 mm** (WMM).

### 3.2.3 Layer 3 — Sub-base Course

**Material:** Granular Sub-base (GSB) or stabilized soil.

**Functions:** Identical to flexible pavement sub-base (drainage + construction platform).

**Typical Thickness:** **100 mm to 200 mm**.

### 3.2.4 Layer 4 — Subgrade

**Material:** Compacted natural soil or stabilized subgrade.

**Functions:** As in flexible pavement.

### 3.2.5 Total Rigid Pavement Thickness

$$\text{Total Thickness} = h_{\text{PCC slab}} + h_{\text{base}} + h_{\text{sub-base}}$$

For a typical **highway rigid pavement**: **350 mm to 600 mm** total.

## 3.3 Derivation — Deriving Westergaard's Radius of Relative Stiffness

This is a KTU-favoured derivation. We start from the classical plate theory and the Winkler foundation.

### Step 1: Governing Differential Equation of a Plate on Elastic Foundation

For a thin elastic plate of flexural rigidity $D$ resting on a Winkler foundation of modulus $k$ (force per unit area per unit deflection), the governing equation for deflection $w(x,y)$ under load is:

$$D \nabla^4 w + k w = q(x,y)$$

where:
- $D = \frac{E h^3}{12(1 - \mu^2)}$ is the **flexural rigidity** of the plate
- $q$ = applied pressure
- $\nabla^4 = \frac{\partial^4}{\partial x^4} + 2 \frac{\partial^4}{\partial x^2 \partial y^2} + \frac{\partial^4}{\partial y^4}$ is the biharmonic operator

### Step 2: Dimensional Analysis — Forming the Characteristic Length

The homogeneous part of the equation is:

$$D \nabla^4 w + k w = 0$$

Dividing by $D$:

$$\nabla^4 w + \frac{k}{D} w = 0$$

For dimensional consistency, $\nabla^4$ has units of $\text{length}^{-4}$. Therefore, the term $\frac{k}{D}$ must also have units of $\text{length}^{-4}$. Define a characteristic length $\lambda$ such that:

$$\lambda^4 = \frac{D}{k} = \frac{E h^3}{12 k (1 - \mu^2)}$$

This characteristic length has units of length (e.g., m or mm).

### Step 3: Define the Radius of Relative Stiffness $l$

The **radius of relative stiffness** is the fourth root of this characteristic length, conventionally expressed as:

$$\boxed{l = \left[ \frac{E h^3}{12 k (1 - \mu^2)} \right]^{1/4}}$$

### Step 4: Physical Interpretation

- A **larger $l$** indicates that the slab is **relatively stiff** compared to the subgrade, meaning the load is distributed over a larger plate area.
- A **smaller $l$** indicates a **relatively soft slab** on a stiff subgrade, leading to concentrated deflection patterns.
- For typical highway rigid pavements, $l$ ranges from **700 mm to 1200 mm** (0.7 m to 1.2 m).

### Step 5: Worked Numerical Example

> **Problem:** A rigid pavement slab has $E = 30000$ MPa, $h = 200$ mm = 0.2 m, $k = 50$ MPa/m, $\mu = 0.15$. Calculate $l$.

**Solution:**

$$l = \left[ \frac{30000 \times (0.2)^3}{12 \times 50 \times (1 - 0.15^2)} \right]^{1/4}$$

Compute numerator:

$$30000 \times 0.008 = 240 \text{ MPa·m}^3$$

Compute denominator:

$$12 \times 50 \times (1 - 0.0225) = 600 \times 0.9775 = 586.5$$

Compute ratio:

$$\frac{240}{586.5} = 0.4092 \text{ m}^4$$

Take fourth root:

$$l = (0.4092)^{0.25} \approx 0.799 \text{ m} \approx 800 \text{ mm}$$

## 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix — Flexible vs Rigid

| Parameter | Flexible Pavement | Rigid Pavement |
|---|---|---|
| Governing structural property | Elastic Modulus (E) of each layer | Flexural strength ($\sigma_{cf}$) of concrete |
| Design philosophy | Layered elastic theory (Burmister) | Plate theory (Westergaard) |
| Stress distribution | Distributed over wide area via layer thickness | Distributed via slab bending action |
| Load transfer at joints | Not applicable (continuous) | Through dowel bars, tie bars, aggregate interlock |
| Failure mode | Rutting, fatigue cracking, pot-holes | Cracking (longitudinal, transverse, corner breaks), pumping |
| Design life | 15 years (overlay design period) | 30 years (no overlay) |
| Construction time | Faster | Slower (curing required) |
| Initial cost | Lower (1.0 to 1.2x base) | Higher (1.5 to 2.5x base) |
| Life-cycle cost | Higher maintenance | Lower maintenance |
| Temperature sensitivity | Moderate (bitumen softens in heat) | High (curling stresses, warping) |
| Skid resistance | High (with proper surface texture) | Moderate (requires tining or grooving) |

## 3.5 Desirable Properties of Highway Materials — Material-Wise Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus specifically asks for the **desirable properties** of the constituent materials. This matrix is a high-yield revision tool.

### 3.5.1 Desirable Properties of Aggregates

| Property | Test Standard (IS) | Typical Desired Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Strength | Aggregate Crushing Value (ACV) — IS:2386 Part IV | **≤ 30%** for surface, **≤ 45%** for base | Resistance to crushing under load |
| Toughness | Aggregate Impact Value (AIV) — IS:2386 Part IV | **≤ 30%** for wearing course | Resistance to impact (shock loading) |
| Hardness | Los Angeles Abrasion Value (LAAV) — IS:2386 Part IV | **≤ 30%** for BC, **≤ 40%** for base | Resistance to surface wear by abrasion |
| Durability | Soundness Test (Na₂SO₄ or MgSO₄) — IS:2386 Part V | Loss **≤ 12%** (Na₂SO₄), **≤ 18%** (MgSO₄) | Resistance to weathering |
| Shape | Flakiness Index (FI) & Elongation Index (EI) — IS:2386 Part I | FI + EI **≤ 30%** | Better packing, higher stability |
| Specific Gravity | Pycnometer — IS:2386 Part III | **≥ 2.6 to 2.7** | Quality indicator |
| Water Absorption | IS:2386 Part III | **≤ 2%** | Porosity / durability indicator |
| Stripping | Coating & Stripping Test — IS:6241 | Retained coating **≥ 95%** | Bitumen-aggregate adhesion |
| Polishing | Polished Stone Value (PSV) — BS:812 | **≥ 60** for high-speed roads | Skid resistance |

### 3.5.2 Desirable Properties of Bitumen

| Property | Test Standard (IS) | Typical Desired Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Consistency | Penetration Test — IS:1203 | **30/40, 60/70, 80/100** grade | Grade classification |
| Viscosity (high temp) | Absolute Viscosity / Kinematic Viscosity — IS:1206 | Specified by grade | Workability, pumping |
| Softening point | Ring & Ball Test — IS:1205 | **35°C to 70°C** | Temperature susceptibility |
| Temperature susceptibility | Penetration Index (PI) | **-1 to +1** (typically -0.5 to +0.5) | Rheological stability |
| Ductility | Ductility Test — IS:1208 | **≥ 75 cm** (for paving grade) | Flexibility & crack resistance |
| Flash point | Cleveland Open Cup — IS:1209 | **≥ 220°C** | Fire safety during heating |
| Adhesion | Stripping test | ≥ 95% coating | Aggregate-binder bond |
| Durability | Thin Film Oven Test (TFOT) — IS:9382 | Retained penetration ≥ 55% | Aging resistance |
| Solubility | Solubility in Trichloroethylene — IS:1216 | **≥ 99%** | Purity (no impurities) |

### 3.5.3 Desirable Properties of Soil (Subgrade)

| Property | Test Standard (IS) | Typical Desired Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Strength | California Bearing Ratio (CBR) — IS:2720 Part 16 | **≥ 5% to 10%** | Load-bearing capacity |
| Compaction | Modified Proctor — IS:2720 Part 8 | ≥ 97% MPMDD | Density control |
| Plasticity | Atterberg Limits (LL, PL, PI) — IS:2720 Part 5 | PI **≤ 6** for subgrade | Expansivity control |
| Swelling | Free Swell Index — IS:2720 Part 40 | **≤ 50%** | Volume change |
| Moisture | Optimum Moisture Content (OMC) | Field compaction at OMC ± 2% | Compaction efficiency |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Cross-Section of a Typical Flexible Pavement

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA["SURFACE COURSE: Bituminous Concrete BC 40mm + Dense Bituminous Macadam DBM 60mm"] --> nodeB
    nodeB["BASE COURSE: Wet Mix Macadam WMM 150mm to 250mm"] --> nodeC
    nodeC["SUB-BASE COURSE: Granular Sub-base GSB 150mm to 200mm"] --> nodeD
    nodeD["SUBGRADE: Compacted Natural Soil CBR greater than 8 percent"]

    nodeE["Functions: Riding surface, Skid resistance, Waterproofing"] -.-> nodeA
    nodeF["Functions: Main load distribution, High CBR greater than 80"] -.-> nodeB
    nodeG["Functions: Secondary load distribution, Drainage, Frost protection"] -.-> nodeC
    nodeH["Functions: Foundation, All loads ultimately reach here"] -.-> nodeD

    nodeI["Typical Materials: Bitumen, Aggregates, DBM BC SMA"] -.-> nodeA
    nodeJ["Typical Materials: Crushed stone, WMM, WBM, CTB"] -.-> nodeB
    nodeK["Typical Materials: Gravel, Moorum, Crushed rock, Stabilized soil"] -.-> nodeC
    nodeL["Typical Materials: Natural soil, Lime stabilized soil, Cement stabilized soil"] -.-> nodeD
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Cross-Section of a Typical Rigid Pavement

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeM["PCC SLAB: M40 to M50 Concrete 200mm to 300mm with dowel and tie bars"] --> nodeN
    nodeN["BASE COURSE: Dry Lean Concrete DLC 100mm to 150mm"] --> nodeO
    nodeO["SUB-BASE COURSE: Granular Sub-base GSB 100mm to 200mm"] --> nodeP
    nodeP["SUBGRADE: Compacted Natural Soil CBR greater than 8 percent"]

    nodeQ["Functions: Primary load carrying via slab action, Provides riding surface"] -.-> nodeM
    nodeR["Functions: Uniform support, Prevents pumping of fines"] -.-> nodeN
    nodeS["Functions: Drainage, Construction platform"] -.-> nodeO
    nodeT["Functions: Foundation, Must have uniform k value"] -.-> nodeP

    nodeU["Joint types: Expansion, Contraction, Longitudinal, Construction"] -.-> nodeM
    nodeV["Reinforcement: Dowel bars for load transfer, Tie bars for lane tie"] -.-> nodeM
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Functional Architecture of Pavement Load Distribution

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    LoadA["WHEEL LOAD P in kN"] --> RespA{"Flexible Response"}
    LoadA --> RespB{"Rigid Response"}

    RespA --> L1A["Surface Course bends, spreads stress to base"]
    L1A --> L2A["Base course compresses, spreads further to subbase"]
    L2A --> L3A["Subbase distributes to subgrade over large area"]
    L3A --> L4A["Subgrade accepts low uniform stress"]

    RespB --> L1B["Concrete slab acts as plate, distributes over large footprint"]
    L1B --> L2B["Subbase receives low uniform pressure"]
    L2B --> L3B["Subgrade experiences minimal stress"]

    StyleNode["KEY INSIGHT: Flexible has total thickness 400 to 750mm, Rigid has total thickness 350 to 600mm. Rigid is thinner but carries higher unit stresses in the slab itself."]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Material Testing Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    matTest["Highway Material Testing"] --> aggTest["Aggregate Tests"]
    matTest --> bitTest["Bitumen Tests"]
    matTest --> soilTest["Soil Tests"]
    matTest --> mixTest["Mix Tests"]

    aggTest --> aggA["Strength ACV AIV LAAV"]
    aggTest --> aggB["Shape FI EI"]
    aggTest --> aggC["Durability Soundness Stripping"]
    aggTest --> aggD["Specific Gravity Water Absorption"]

    bitTest --> bitA["Consistency Penetration"]
    bitTest --> bitB["Viscosity Viscosity tests"]
    bitTest --> bitC["Temperature Softening Point Ductility"]
    bitTest --> bitD["Durability TFOT Aging"]

    soilTest --> soilA["Strength CBR"]
    soilTest --> soilB["Compaction Proctor Test"]
    soilTest --> soilC["Plasticity Atterberg Limits"]

    mixTest --> mixA["Marshall Stability Test for Bituminous Mix"]
    mixTest --> mixB["Compressive Strength Test for Concrete"]
```
