# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 (Adapted)]**
**Q: Differentiate between flexible and rigid pavements with respect to load distribution mechanism and mode of failure. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Flexible Pavement | Rigid Pavement |
|---|---|---|
| **Load Distribution** | Grain-to-grain contact through layer thickness; stress reduces progressively with depth (Burmister's theory) | Slab action; load distributed over a large plate area due to high flexural rigidity (Westergaard's theory) |
| **Mode of Failure** | Rutting, fatigue cracking (alligator pattern), pot-holes, shoving | Structural cracking (transverse, longitudinal, corner breaks), pumping, faulting at joints |
| **Critical Stress** | Vertical compressive strain at subgrade level | Flexural tensile stress at the bottom of the concrete slab |

*(Examiner's Note: A clear two-column comparison with one example each is sufficient for full 3 marks.)*

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - June 2024 (Adapted)]**
**Q: List the component parts of a flexible pavement in descending order from the surface, stating the function of each layer. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Surface Course (Bituminous Layer)** — provides smooth, skid-resistant, water-proof riding surface; resists abrasive wear.
2. **Base Course (WMM/WBM)** — principal load-distributing layer; carries bulk of traffic stresses.
3. **Sub-base Course (Granular)** — secondary load distribution; provides drainage and construction platform.
4. **Subgrade (Natural Soil)** — foundation; receives and dissipates all loads.

*(Examiner's Note: Order matters. Wrong order = zero. Function of at least one layer is essential.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper, Module 1 Internal Choice A]**

**Q: (a)** Explain in detail the desirable properties of road aggregates. State the relevant IS tests and acceptance criteria for a wearing course aggregate. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A flexible pavement has a granular base of 250 mm and a sub-base of 200 mm. The wheel load is 40 kN transmitted through a circular contact area of radius 150 mm. Assuming a load spread angle of 1V:2H through the granular layers, calculate the vertical stress reaching the top of the subgrade. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Desirable Properties of Road Aggregates:**

1. **Strength** — resistance to crushing under wheel load. Tested by **Aggregate Crushing Value (ACV) test** as per **IS:2386 Part IV**. Acceptance: **ACV ≤ 30%** for wearing course.

2. **Toughness** — resistance to impact/shock loading. Tested by **Aggregate Impact Value (AIV) test** as per **IS:2386 Part IV**. Acceptance: **AIV ≤ 30%** for wearing course.

3. **Hardness (Abrasion Resistance)** — resistance to surface wear. Tested by **Los Angeles Abrasion Test** as per **IS:2386 Part IV**. Acceptance: **LAAV ≤ 30%** for wearing course.

4. **Durability (Weathering Resistance)** — tested by **Soundness Test** using Na₂SO₄ or MgSO₄ as per **IS:2386 Part V**. Acceptance: Loss **≤ 12%** (Na₂SO₄) or **≤ 18%** (MgSO₄).

5. **Particle Shape** — cubical particles preferred. Tested by **Flakiness Index (FI) and Elongation Index (EI)** as per **IS:2386 Part I**. Acceptance: **FI + EI ≤ 30%**.

6. **Specific Gravity** — quality indicator. Tested by **Pycnometer method** as per **IS:2386 Part III**. Acceptance: **≥ 2.6 to 2.7**.

7. **Water Absorption** — porosity indicator. Tested as per **IS:2386 Part III**. Acceptance: **≤ 2%**.

8. **Adhesion with Bitumen** — tested by **Coating and Stripping Test** as per **IS:6241**. Acceptance: Retained coating **≥ 95%**.

9. **Polishing Resistance (Skid Resistance)** — tested by **Polished Stone Value (PSV)** as per **BS:812 Part 114**. Acceptance: **PSV ≥ 60** for high-speed roads.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given:**
- Wheel load $P = 40$ kN
- Contact radius $a = 150$ mm
- Base thickness $h_b = 250$ mm
- Sub-base thickness $h_{sb} = 200$ mm
- Load spread angle: 1V : 2H (i.e., $\tan\alpha = 1/2 = 0.5$)

**Step 1: Effective radius at the bottom of the base course**

$$a_1 = a + h_b \tan\alpha = 150 + 250 \times 0.5 = 150 + 125 = 275 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2: Effective radius at the top of the subgrade (bottom of sub-base)**

$$a_2 = a_1 + h_{sb} \tan\alpha = 275 + 200 \times 0.5 = 275 + 100 = 375 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3: Effective contact area at the subgrade level**

$$A_e = \pi (a_2)^2 = \pi \times (375)^2 = \pi \times 140625 = 441,786 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 4: Vertical stress on subgrade**

$$\sigma_z = \frac{P}{A_e} = \frac{40 \times 10^3 \text{ N}}{441,786 \text{ mm}^2} = 0.0905 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_z \approx 0.091 \text{ MPa} = 91 \text{ kPa}}$$

*(Examiner's Note: Students often forget to convert kN to N. Also, the 1V:2H ratio means horizontal spread is 2 units per 1 unit vertical, so the radius increment is $h \times (1/2)$, not $h \times 2$.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper, Module 1 Internal Choice B]**

**Q: (a)** With neat sketches, describe the component parts of a rigid pavement. Explain the functions of each component. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A cement concrete slab of a rigid pavement has the following properties: Elastic Modulus $E = 30000$ MPa, thickness $h = 220$ mm, Poisson's ratio $\mu = 0.15$, and the subgrade has a Modulus of Subgrade Reaction $k = 60$ MPa/m. Calculate the **Radius of Relative Stiffness** $l$. If the wheel load is $P = 50$ kN and the equivalent contact radius is $a = 150$ mm, calculate the **Westergaard corner stress** $\sigma_c$. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Component Parts of a Rigid Pavement (top to bottom):**

1. **Cement Concrete Slab (Surface Course)**
   - Material: M40 to M50 grade PCC
   - Functions: (i) Primary structural element carrying wheel loads via slab action; (ii) provides the riding surface directly; (iii) distributes loads over a large footprint through flexural rigidity.

2. **Base Course (DLC / CTB / WMM)**
   - Functions: (i) Provides uniform, stable support; (ii) prevents pumping of fines; (iii) resists erosion at the slab-subbase interface.

3. **Sub-base Course (Granular)**
   - Functions: (i) Construction platform; (ii) capillary cut-off; (iii) secondary drainage; (iv) frost protection.

4. **Subgrade (Compacted Soil)**
   - Functions: (i) Foundation for the entire pavement; (ii) must possess uniform $k$-value to prevent differential support.

**Additional Components:**
- **Joints** — Expansion, Contraction, Longitudinal, Construction (per IRC:15).
- **Reinforcement** — Dowel bars (load transfer across transverse joints), Tie bars (lane-to-lane tie across longitudinal joints).
- **Shoulders** — paved or unpaved, providing lateral support to the outer slab edge.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given:**
- $E = 30000$ MPa
- $h = 220$ mm = 0.22 m
- $\mu = 0.15$
- $k = 60$ MPa/m
- $P = 50$ kN = 50000 N
- $a = 150$ mm = 0.15 m

**Step 1: Calculate Radius of Relative Stiffness**

$$l = \left[ \frac{E h^3}{12 k (1 - \mu^2)} \right]^{1/4}$$

Numerator:

$$E h^3 = 30000 \times (0.22)^3 = 30000 \times 0.010648 = 319.44 \text{ MPa·m}^3$$

Denominator:

$$12 k (1 - \mu^2) = 12 \times 60 \times (1 - 0.0225) = 720 \times 0.9775 = 703.8 \text{ MPa·m}$$

Ratio:

$$\frac{E h^3}{12 k (1 - \mu^2)} = \frac{319.44}{703.8} = 0.4539 \text{ m}^4$$

Fourth root:

$$l = (0.4539)^{0.25} = 0.8205 \text{ m} \approx 820.5 \text{ mm}$$

$$\boxed{l \approx 0.821 \text{ m} = 821 \text{ mm}}$$

**Step 2: Calculate Westergaard Corner Stress**

$$\sigma_c = \frac{3P}{h^2} \left[ 1 - \left( \frac{a\sqrt{2}}{l} \right)^{0.6} \right]$$

Compute the bracketed term:

$$\frac{a\sqrt{2}}{l} = \frac{150 \times 1.4142}{820.5} = \frac{212.13}{820.5} = 0.2586$$

$$(0.2586)^{0.6} = e^{0.6 \times \ln(0.2586)} = e^{0.6 \times (-1.3526)} = e^{-0.8116} = 0.4442$$

Bracketed term:

$$1 - 0.4442 = 0.5558$$

Compute $\frac{3P}{h^2}$ (using consistent units: $P$ in N, $h$ in mm):

$$\frac{3P}{h^2} = \frac{3 \times 50000}{(220)^2} = \frac{150000}{48400} = 3.099 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 3.099 \text{ MPa}$$

Final corner stress:

$$\sigma_c = 3.099 \times 0.5558 = 1.722 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_c \approx 1.72 \text{ MPa}}$$

*(Examiner's Note: Consistency of units is critical here. If you mix mm and m, you will get nonsensical answers by a factor of 1000. Always use either all-mm or all-m, but maintain $\sigma$ in MPa = N/mm².)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Module 1 Questions:**
> 1. **Wrong order of pavement layers** — listing subgrade on top costs full marks. The correct order is always: Surface → Base → Sub-base → Subgrade.
> 2. **Mixing units** in Westergaard problems — using $h$ in mm and $k$ in MPa/m without conversion. Remember $k$ in MPa/m means "MPa per metre of deflection", so $h$ must be in metres when used in the $l$ formula.
> 3. **Confusing ACV with AIV** — both are strength/impact tests, but ACV is static crushing and AIV is dynamic impact. Different acceptance values.
> 4. **Forgetting to convert kN to N** in stress calculations (factor of 1000 error).
> 5. **Confusing load spread angles** — 1V:2H means 1 vertical to 2 horizontal (mild spread for granular); 1V:1H means 45° spread (typical for cement-treated base).
> 6. **Not defining all variables** in a formula — KTU deducts 1 mark if units are omitted from the final answer.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **HIGH-YIELD LAST-MINUTE REVISION — Module 1: Highway Pavement Fundamentals**

- ✅ A **flexible pavement** bends with the load; the load is carried by **layered aggregate interlock**; designed by **layered elastic theory (Burmister)**.
- ✅ A **rigid pavement** acts as a slab; load is carried by **flexural rigidity of the concrete**; designed by **plate theory (Westergaard)**.
- ✅ The **four layers** of any pavement (top to bottom): **Surface Course → Base Course → Sub-base Course → Subgrade**.
- ✅ **Flexible pavement** total thickness: **400–750 mm**; **Rigid pavement** total thickness: **350–600 mm** (the slab is thicker individually but the overall structure can be thinner because the slab is so stiff).
- ✅ **Westergaard's three critical stress locations**: Corner (most critical) > Edge > Interior (least critical).
- ✅ **Radius of Relative Stiffness $l$** is the **fourth root** of $\frac{E h^3}{12 k (1-\mu^2)}$; typical value 700–1200 mm.
- ✅ **Aggregate acceptance values (wearing course)**: ACV ≤ 30%, AIV ≤ 30%, LAAV ≤ 30%, FI+EI ≤ 30%, Soundness loss ≤ 12% (Na₂SO₄).
- ✅ **Bitumen acceptance (60/70 grade)**: Penetration 60–70 mm, Softening Point ~50°C, Ductility ≥ 75 cm, Flash Point ≥ 220°C.
- ✅ **Subgrade acceptance**: CBR ≥ 5% (low-volume) to ≥ 8% (highway), PI ≤ 6, compacted to ≥ 97% MPMDD at OMC.
- ✅ **Rigid pavement joints**: Expansion, Contraction, Longitudinal, Construction (per **IRC:15**).
- ✅ **Load transfer devices in rigid pavement**: Dowel bars (transverse joints) + Tie bars (longitudinal joints).
- ✅ **Load spread angle in flexible pavement granular layers**: typically **1V:2H** (1:2).
- ✅ **Typical cross-fall**: 2%–2.5% for bituminous, 1.5%–2% for concrete.
- ✅ **IRC codes to remember**: IRC:15 (Concrete Roads), IRC:37 (Flexible Pavement Design), IRC:58 (Rigid Pavement Design), IS:2386 (Aggregate Testing), IS:1201–1220 (Bitumen Testing).
- ✅ **Master the three Westergaard equations** — Corner, Edge, Interior — and remember the **order of magnitude** of $l$ (~0.8 m) for typical highway slabs.
- ✅ **Memory aid**: "**Pavement = a sandwich**" — the subgrade is the bread bottom, and the layers above are fillings, each with a distinct job.

---

**End of Module 1 Notes — Desirable Properties, Testing of Materials, Introduction to Highway Pavements — Flexible and Rigid Pavements — Component Parts**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Functions and Significance of Layers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET746 - HIGHWAY MATERIALS AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Functions and Significance of Layers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **pavement** in highway engineering is a multi-layered structure constructed over the natural soil (subgrade) to provide a smooth, durable, and safe riding surface for vehicular traffic. The "Functions and Significance of Layers" refers to the systematic role played by each individual stratum of the pavement system in transferring and distributing traffic-induced wheel loads from the top surface down to the natural earth, while simultaneously resisting environmental degradation.

According to **IRC:37-2018 (Tentative Guidelines for the Design of Flexible Pavements)** and **IRC:58-2015 (Guidelines for the Design of Plain Jointed Rigid Pavements for Highways and Expressways)**, a pavement is engineered as a **layered elastic/viscoelastic system** where each layer has distinct material properties (Elastic Modulus, Poisson's Ratio, and Thickness) tailored to perform a specific structural or functional duty.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The pavement is NOT a single solid block. It is a **composite structure** of carefully selected materials arranged in layers, where each layer has a specific job, a specific stress regime, and specific material specifications.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Cake & Cushion" Intuition

Imagine you place a heavy dining table on a soft, freshly-ploughed field. The table legs will sink and tilt because the soft soil cannot bear the concentrated load. To fix this, you place a **soft foam mat** first, then a **thick rubber pad**, then a **hard wooden board**, and finally a **polished marble top** on the table.

- The **marble top** gives a smooth finish (just like the **Wearing Course**).
- The **wooden board** distributes the table's weight over a larger area (like the **Base Course**).
- The **rubber pad** absorbs shocks from items placed on the table (like the **Sub-base Course**).
- The **foam mat** prevents the assembly from punching into the field (like the **Subgrade/Subgrade Preparation**).

In a road, the heavy "table" is the **moving truck wheel load**, and the foam-rubber-board-marble stack is the **pavement structure**. Each layer has a unique job, and if you remove any one of them, the road fails prematurely.

## 1.3 Why Do We Need Layers? (The Governing Principle)

A single layer of, say, **Asphalt Concrete (50 cm thick)** would be enormously expensive. The principle of layered construction is based on the fact that:

1. **Vertical Compressive Stress decreases with depth** as the load spreads over a wider area (load dispersion cone).
2. Hence, **deeper layers experience lower stresses** and can therefore be built from **cheaper, weaker materials**.
3. The topmost layer (in contact with the tyre) must withstand the **maximum stress, abrasion, and weather** — so it must be the **strongest and most expensive**.
4. This **graded material approach** makes pavement construction **economically viable** without compromising performance.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric:** A standard truck wheel applies a contact pressure of approximately **0.56 MPa to 0.80 MPa (80–115 psi)** over a circular contact area of radius ~110–150 mm. This pressure is what the topmost layer must withstand.

## 1.4 Typical Cross-Section of a Flexible Pavement (Top to Bottom)

| Layer No. | Layer Name | Typical Thickness | Material (Typical) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Surface / Wearing Course** | 20–40 mm | Bituminous Concrete (BC) / Mastic Asphalt |
| 2 | **Binder Course** | 50–100 mm | Dense Bituminous Macadam (DBM) / Bituminous Macadam (BM) |
| 3 | **Base Course** | 100–300 mm | Wet Mix Macadam (WMM) / Granular Sub-base (GSB) / Crushed Stone |
| 4 | **Sub-base Course** | 100–300 mm | Granular Sub-base (GSB) / Laterite / Moorum / Stabilized soil |
| 5 | **Subgrade** | Semi-infinite | Compacted Natural Soil (CBR ≥ 8% for rural, ≥ 10% for NH/SH) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Function-by-Function Theoretical Breakdown

The pavement layers are not just stacked; each one serves multiple overlapping functions. Below is the complete engineering logic for each layer.

### 2.1.1 The Subgrade (The Foundation Soil)

The **subgrade** is the top 300–500 mm of the compacted natural soil upon which the entire pavement rests. It is the most fundamental layer because **every other layer transfers its load ultimately to the subgrade**.

**Functions of the Subgrade:**
- **Load Bearing:** Acts as the terminal receiver of all distributed traffic loads. Stress reaching the subgrade must not exceed its bearing capacity.
- **Uniform Support:** Provides a uniform, stable platform so that the upper layers do not experience differential settlement.
- **Drainage Conduit:** When designed with a camber, it helps in removing infiltrated water laterally to the side drains.
- **Frost & Swelling Resistance:** Properly selected and stabilized subgrade resists volumetric changes due to moisture and temperature.

**Significance:** A weak subgrade causes **rutting, undulations, fatigue cracking, and settlement** in the entire pavement structure. That is why the **California Bearing Ratio (CBR)** of the subgrade is the single most important input parameter in flexible pavement design (per IRC:37).

### 2.1.2 The Sub-base Course

The **sub-base course** is the layer immediately above the subgrade. It is the **first engineered layer** above the natural soil.

**Functions of the Sub-base Course:**
- **Secondary Load Distribution:** Further spreads the load from the base course over an even larger area of subgrade.
- **Drainage Layer:** In modern designs, the sub-base is often a **granular, free-draining material** that intercepts infiltrated water and prevents it from saturating the subgrade (this is critical — water is the #1 enemy of pavements).
- **Frost Protection:** In cold regions (Kashmir, Himachal), the sub-base provides a non-frost-susceptible layer between the frost-susceptible subgrade and the road surface.
- **Construction Platform:** Provides a stable working platform for the placement and compaction of the base course layers.
- **Economy:** Acts as a transition between the cheap subgrade and the more expensive base course, allowing the use of moderately good (but not premium) material.

**Significance:** A missing or weak sub-base is the leading cause of **pumping failure** in flexible pavements under heavy traffic.

### 2.1.3 The Base Course

The **base course** is the principal structural layer of the pavement. It lies just below the bituminous layer and above the sub-base. It carries the **bulk of the traffic-induced stresses**.

**Functions of the Base Course:**
- **Primary Load Distribution:** Receives concentrated load from the thin bituminous layer and spreads it to a much larger area, reducing stress on the sub-base and subgrade.
- **Structural Strength:** Provides the major portion of the **Structural Number (SN)** in AASHTO design. It resists vertical compressive stresses and shear stresses.
- **Resists Deformation:** High-quality granular or bound base resists rutting and shoving under repeated traffic loads.
- **Resists Fatigue:** Provides stiffness to prevent bottom-up fatigue cracking of the bituminous layer.

**Significance:** The base course is often the **thickest layer** in the pavement system because it has to absorb the maximum structural stress before it dissipates further down.

### 2.1.4 The Binder Course (Intermediate Layer)

**Functions of the Binder Course:**
- **Thickens the Bituminous Layer:** Provides additional thickness to the total bituminous (asphalt) layer so that the tensile strain at the bottom of the bituminous layer (which governs fatigue cracking) remains within limits.
- **Stress Reduction:** Reduces the vertical compressive stress transmitted to the granular base.
- **Smooths Out Base Irregularities:** Compensates for minor surface irregularities in the base course.
- **Impermeability:** Provides a dense, relatively impermeable barrier that prevents water from penetrating into the base and sub-base.

### 2.1.5 The Surface / Wearing Course (Top Layer)

**Functions of the Wearing Course:**
- **Smooth Riding Surface:** Provides a skid-resistant, smooth, and quiet riding surface for traffic safety and comfort.
- **Waterproofing:** Seals the pavement from surface water infiltration — this is its most critical role.
- **Resists Abrasion & Disintegration:** Must withstand the direct wear and tear of pneumatic tyres, including shear stresses during acceleration, braking, and cornering.
- **Resists Environmental Degradation:** Withstands oxidation of bitumen by UV rays, temperature variations, and weathering.
- **Transfers Vertical Load:** Transmits the wheel load to the binder/base course with minimal deformation.

**Significance:** This is the only layer **directly visible and felt by road users**. Its quality dictates **serviceability** and **ride quality** (per IRC and AASHTO, governed by **International Roughness Index — IRI**).

## 2.2 The Load Distribution Principle — Boussinesq's Theory

The fundamental principle that justifies layering is the **stress dispersion cone** described by Boussinesq (1885). A point load `P` applied on a homogeneous semi-infinite elastic medium produces a vertical stress at depth `z` on the axis of loading as:

$$\sigma_z = \frac{3P}{2\pi z^2} \cos^5 \theta$$

where `θ` is the angle made by the line from the point of application to the depth point with respect to the vertical. As `z` increases, `σ_z` decreases rapidly (inversely proportional to `z²` for a point load, and to `z^1.5` for a circular loaded area using Westergaard's solution).

This is exactly why **deeper layers can be made of weaker and cheaper material**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Below is the consolidated KTU-ready formula sheet. Use `\vert` instead of `|` to avoid table breakage.

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Application / Significance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Boussinesq vertical stress (point load) | $\sigma_z = \dfrac{3P}{2\pi z^2} \cos^5\theta$ | Stress on subgrade below a wheel load |
| 2 | Pressure on subgrade (approx.) | $p_z = \dfrac{p_0 \cdot a^2}{(a+z)^2}$ (for circular load radius `a`) | Used to verify subgrade bearing capacity |
| 3 | Structural Number (AASHTO) | $SN = a_1 D_1 + a_2 D_2 m_2 + a_3 D_3 m_3$ | Quantifies total pavement strength; sum of layer coefficients × thickness |
| 4 | AASHTO layer coefficient (asphalt) | $a_1 \approx 0.40$ to $0.45$ per inch | Empirical strength contribution |
| 5 | Effective Modulus of Subgrade (k) | $k = \dfrac{M_R}{19.4}$ (where $M_R$ is resilient modulus in psi) | Used in rigid pavement design |
| 6 | Contact Pressure (Standard Axle) | $p \approx 0.56$ to $0.80$ MPa | Tyre–pavement contact stress |
| 7 | Standard Axle Load (India) | $8.2$ tonnes $\equiv 80$ kN | Basis of all IRC design traffic calculations |
| 8 | ESAL Conversion (4th Power Law) | $ESAL = \left( \dfrac{W}{80} \right)^4$ | AASHTO Load Equivalency Factor |
| 9 | Subgrade CBR Design Equation (IRC:37) | $T = f(MSV, CBR)$ (read from catalogue) | Determines total pavement thickness |
| 10 | Vertical Stress Reduction Factor | $n = \dfrac{\sigma_z}{p_0}$ | For 2-layer system, depends on $z/a$ and $E_1/E_2$ |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The layered concept is used in:
- **Flexible Pavement Design (IRC:37 / AASHTO 1993)** — Layered elastic theory.
- **Rigid Pavement Design (IRC:58 / Westergaard)** — Subgrade as Winkler foundation (k-value).
- **Airport Pavement Design (FAA, ICAO)** — Layered elastic / CBR method.
- **Railway Track Design** — Ballast, sub-ballast, formation (the same layered philosophy).
- **Dam Embankments, Retaining Walls** — Filter and drainage layers follow the same logic.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Stress Distribution Through a Two-Layer System (Burmister's Theory)

In 1943, **D.M. Burmister** extended Boussinesq's single-layer theory to a **two-layer elastic system**, which is the foundation of all modern layered pavement design.

### 3.1.1 Problem Statement

A **uniformly distributed circular load** of pressure `p₀` and radius `a` is applied on a two-layer system.
- **Layer 1 (Top):** Thickness `h`, Elastic Modulus `E₁`, Poisson's ratio `μ₁`.
- **Layer 2 (Bottom, semi-infinite):** Modulus `E₂`, Poisson's ratio `μ₂`.

We want to find the **vertical stress `σ_z` at the interface** (i.e., at depth `h`, just above the subgrade) along the axis of loading.

### 3.1.2 Assumptions

1. Each layer is **homogeneous, isotropic, and linearly elastic**.
2. Layers are **infinite in horizontal extent** (no edge effects).
3. **Full friction** exists at the interface between layers (no slip).
4. The bottom layer extends to **infinity** (semi-infinite half-space).
5. Loads are **static** (we use a quasi-static approximation for slow-moving wheel loads).

### 3.1.3 Derivation Steps

**Step 1 — Set up the stress function.**

Burmister proposed an Airy-type stress function `φ` for the two-layer system. For the **upper layer (1)**, he used:

$$\phi_1 = \int_0^\infty J_0(mr) \left[ A e^{-mz} + B e^{mz} + C z e^{-mz} \right] dm$$

For the **lower layer (2)**, since it is semi-infinite, only the decaying exponential term is valid:

$$\phi_2 = \int_0^\infty J_0(mr) \, D e^{-mz} \, dm$$

where:
- `J₀(mr)` is the **Bessel function of the first kind, order zero**.
- `m` is the integration variable (a dummy parameter with units of 1/length).
- `A, B, C, D` are constants to be determined from **boundary conditions**.

**Step 2 — Apply the boundary conditions.**

- **BC 1:** At `z = 0` (top surface), the vertical stress equals the applied load:
  $$\sigma_z \big\vert_{z=0} = -p_0 \text{ for } 0 \le r \le a$$
- **BC 2:** At `z = 0`, the shear stress is zero:
  $$\tau_{rz} \big\vert_{z=0} = 0$$
- **BC 3:** At `z = h` (interface), stresses and displacements are **continuous**:
  $$\sigma_z^{(1)} = \sigma_z^{(2)}, \quad \tau_{rz}^{(1)} = \tau_{rz}^{(2)}, \quad u^{(1)} = u^{(2)}, \quad w^{(1)} = w^{(2)}$$
- **BC 4:** As `z → ∞`, all stresses and displacements → 0 in the lower layer.

**Step 3 — Solve for constants.**

Substituting the stress function into the elasticity equations of equilibrium, compatibility, and the boundary conditions above yields the **four constants** `A, B, C, D` in terms of the applied load `p₀`, the radius `a`, the thickness `h`, and the **modular ratio** `E₁/E₂`.

**Step 4 — Compute the vertical stress at the interface.**

After lengthy but rigorous algebra (omitted here only for brevity, but fully shown in Burmister's 1943 paper), the final expression for the vertical stress at the interface (`z = h`) along the axis (`r = 0`) is:

$$\sigma_z \big\vert_{z=h, r=0} = \frac{p_0}{1 + \left( \dfrac{2h}{a} \right) \cdot \tan^{-1}\left( \dfrac{h}{a} \right) \cdot \left( \dfrac{E_1}{E_2} \right)}$$

Wait — let me correct this. The actual Burmister expression (for the special case `μ₁ = μ₂ = 0.5`) is more complex and is usually presented as **charts** rather than a closed-form equation. A widely-used approximate closed form is:

$$\sigma_z \approx \frac{p_0}{1 + 2 \cdot \left(\dfrac{h}{a}\right) \cdot \left( \dfrac{E_2}{E_1} \right)}$$

This equation clearly shows the engineering logic:

> **As `h/a` increases (thicker top layer) OR `E₂/E₁` decreases (weaker subgrade), the stress on the subgrade reduces.**

**Step 5 — Practical conclusion.**

$$\boxed{\text{Stronger top layer} + \text{Thicker top layer} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Lower stress on subgrade} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Longer pavement life}}$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Structural Number (AASHTO) for Multi-Layer System

The **Structural Number (SN)** is an empirical index of the total structural strength of a flexible pavement.

**Step 1:** The AASHTO design equation (simplified) for a given reliability is:

$$\log_{10}(W_{18}) = Z_R \cdot S_0 + 9.36 \cdot \log_{10}(SN+1) - 0.20 + \frac{\log_{10}\left[ \dfrac{\Delta PSI}{4.2 - 1.5} \right]}{0.40 + \dfrac{1094}{(SN+1)^{5.19}}}$$

**Step 2:** The required `SN` is decomposed into layer contributions:

$$SN = a_1 D_1 + a_2 D_2 m_2 + a_3 D_3 m_3$$

where:
- $a_i$ = **layer coefficient** (material strength per unit thickness).
- $D_i$ = **layer thickness** (in inches).
- $m_i$ = **drainage coefficient** (0.7 to 1.3 depending on drainage quality).

**Step 3 — Interpretation.** Each layer's `aᵢ × Dᵢ` represents its structural contribution. The sum of all layer contributions must equal the required `SN`.

This formalizes the layered design philosophy: **any acceptable combination of layers summing to the required SN will work**.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Stress Magnitudes Through the Layers (Typical Highway)

Assuming a standard axle load of 80 kN on dual wheels, contact pressure $p_0 = 0.56$ MPa, contact radius $a \approx 110$ mm:

| Depth from Surface | Layer | Approx. Vertical Stress | % of Applied Stress |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 0 mm | Tyre contact patch | 0.56 MPa | 100% |
| 50 mm | Mid-wearing course | 0.50 MPa | ~89% |
| 100 mm | Bottom of binder course | 0.40 MPa | ~71% |
| 200 mm | Middle of base course | 0.20 MPa | ~36% |
| 400 mm | Top of sub-base | 0.09 MPa | ~16% |
| 600 mm | Top of subgrade | 0.04 MPa | ~7% |

**Inference from the matrix:** The subgrade sees only **~7% of the original wheel pressure** thanks to the load-spreading action of the upper layers. This justifies the use of **weak, cheap material** in the subgrade.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow of the Layered Pavement System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Vehicle Wheel Load - 80 kN Axle] --> B[Pavement Structure - Layered System]
    B --> C1[Layer 1: Wearing Course - Asphalt Concrete]
    C1 --> C2[Layer 2: Binder Course - DBM]
    C2 --> C3[Layer 3: Base Course - WMM or Granular]
    C3 --> C4[Layer 4: Sub-base Course - Granular]
    C4 --> C5[Layer 5: Subgrade - Compacted Natural Soil]
    C5 --> D[Earthworks Foundation]

    E1[Function: Smooth ride, skid resistance, waterproofing] -.-> C1
    E2[Function: Load distribution, fatigue resistance] -.-> C2
    E3[Function: Primary load spreading, structural strength] -.-> C3
    E4[Function: Drainage, secondary load spread, frost protection] -.-> C4
    E5[Function: Uniform support, terminal load bearing] -.-> C5
```

## 4.2 Cross-Section Schematic of Pavement Layering

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SURF["SURFACE LAYERS"]
        L1["Wearing Course (20-40 mm) Bituminous Concrete"]
        L2["Binder Course (50-100 mm) Dense Bituminous Macadam"]
    end
    subgraph BASE["BASE LAYERS"]
        L3["Base Course (100-300 mm) Wet Mix Macadam"]
        L4["Sub-base Course (100-300 mm) Granular Sub-base"]
    end
    subgraph FOUND["FOUNDATION"]
        L5["Subgrade (Compacted Natural Soil)"]
    end
    L1 --> L2 --> L3 --> L4 --> L5
```

## 4.3 Functional Flowchart — Load Path Through Layers

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Point Load P on Surface] --> Q1{Stress Dispersion Cone}
    Q1 --> R1[Top Layer Receives Maximum Stress, Tyre Abrasion, Weather Attack]
    R1 --> S1[Top Layer Spreads Load to Larger Area]
    S1 --> T1[Base Layer Receives Reduced Stress, Bears Bulk of Structural Load]
    T1 --> U1[Base Layer Spreads Load Further]
    U1 --> V1[Sub-base Receives Even Lower Stress, Provides Drainage]
    V1 --> W1[Subgrade Receives Minimum Stress Within Bearing Capacity]
    W1 --> X[Stable Long-term Performance Achieved]
```

## 4.4 Comparison Diagram — Flexible vs. Rigid Pavement Layering

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FLEX["FLEXIBLE PAVEMENT (IRC:37)"]
        F1[Wearing Course - BC] --> F2[Base Course - Granular]
        F2 --> F3[Sub-base - Granular]
        F3 --> F4[Subgrade - Soil]
    end
    subgraph RIG["RIGID PAVEMENT (IRC:58)"]
        R1[Concrete Slab - PQC] --> R2[DLC or Lean Concrete]
        R2 --> R3[Sub-base - Granular]
        R3 --> R4[Subgrade - Soil]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation from the comparison diagram:** In a rigid pavement, the **Concrete Slab itself is the principal load-distributing layer** because of its high flexural rigidity. Hence, the underlying granular layers can be thinner compared to a flexible pavement. This is the direct consequence of the **layered design philosophy** — layer thickness and material are matched to the stress regime.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Question]
**List and briefly explain the functions of the subgrade in a flexible pavement structure.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The subgrade is the top portion of the natural soil, compacted and prepared to support the pavement. Its functions are:

1. **Load-bearing Foundation (1 mark):** It acts as the terminal receiver of all distributed traffic loads. The vertical stress reaching the subgrade (after dissipation through upper layers) must remain within its safe bearing capacity, typically assessed by the **California Bearing Ratio (CBR)**.

2. **Uniform Support Platform (1 mark):** It provides a stable, uniform platform to prevent differential settlement that would otherwise manifest as cracks and undulations on the surface.

3. **Drainage and Frost Resistance (1 mark):** When properly cambered and stabilized, it allows lateral drainage of infiltrated water and resists volumetric changes due to frost action or moisture variation.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]
**Why is the wearing course the most expensive layer in a flexible pavement? Give two reasons.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

1. **Direct Stress Exposure (1.5 marks):** The wearing course directly receives the wheel contact pressure of 0.56–0.80 MPa, including dynamic impact loads, tyre shear stresses during acceleration/braking, and abrasive wear. It must be made of high-quality, **polymer-modified bitumen with hard stone aggregates** to withstand this.

2. **Environmental Attack and Functional Demands (1.5 marks):** It is the only layer exposed to **UV radiation, oxidation of bitumen, rainwater infiltration, and temperature extremes** (ranging from -10 °C to +60 °C in India). It must also provide **skid resistance, smoothness (low IRI), and waterproofing**, all of which require premium materials like **gap-graded or dense-graded bituminous concrete with high-quality bitumen (VG-30 or VG-40)**.

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — July 2024 Model Question — Module 1 Choice A]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the typical cross-section of a flexible pavement and list the functions of each layer. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the principle of stress distribution through a layered pavement system. How does this justify the use of cheaper materials in lower layers? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a) — 7 Marks

**Sketch (3 marks):**

A neat cross-sectional sketch should be drawn showing, from top to bottom:
- **Wearing Course** (20–40 mm)
- **Binder Course** (50–100 mm)
- **Base Course** (100–300 mm)
- **Sub-base Course** (100–300 mm)
- **Subgrade** (compacted natural soil, semi-infinite)
- Indicate **camber/cross-fall** for drainage.
- Mark the **load dispersion cone** (a trapezoidal shape showing stress spread).

**Functions (4 marks — distribute ~1 mark per layer):**

| Layer | Function |
| :--- | :--- |
| Wearing Course | Smooth, skid-resistant, waterproof riding surface |
| Binder Course | Provides additional bituminous thickness, stress reduction |
| Base Course | Primary load distribution, structural strength |
| Sub-base Course | Secondary load distribution, drainage, frost protection |
| Subgrade | Uniform foundation, terminal load bearing |

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b) — 7 Marks

**Principle of Stress Distribution (4 marks):**

When a wheel load `P` is applied on a circular contact area of radius `a` with contact pressure `p₀`, the stress at depth `z` on the axis of loading is given by the **Boussinesq equation** (extended to circular load):

$$\sigma_z = p_0 \left[ 1 - \dfrac{z^3}{(a^2 + z^2)^{1.5}} \right]$$

As `z` increases, this stress decreases. More importantly, the **area over which the stress acts increases** with depth, forming a **load dispersion cone**. This cone has a typical spread of 2 horizontal units per 1 vertical unit (i.e., the stress spreads roughly at 2V:1H in a flexible pavement).

**Justification for Cheaper Materials in Lower Layers (3 marks):**

Since the vertical stress on the subgrade is only **~5–10% of the original wheel pressure** (e.g., from 0.56 MPa to 0.04 MPa at 600 mm depth), the subgrade does not need premium high-strength material. Cheaper local materials (moorum, laterite, naturally available soil) can be used. Similarly, the sub-base experiences only moderate stress and is built from moderate-quality **granular sub-base (GSB)** rather than the more expensive **wet mix macadam (WMM)** used in the base course. This graded material selection is the **key economic principle** of layered pavement design.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam — July 2024 Model Question — Module 1 Choice B]**

**(a)** Differentiate between the functions of the base course and the sub-base course. Why is drainage considered the most critical function of the sub-base? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A pavement has a total bituminous layer thickness of 100 mm (combined wearing + binder). The base course is 200 mm of WMM, and the sub-base is 250 mm of GSB. Using the AASHTO layer coefficients $a_1 = 0.42$ (asphalt), $a_2 = 0.14$ (granular base), $a_3 = 0.11$ (granular sub-base), and drainage coefficients $m_2 = m_3 = 1.0$, calculate the **Structural Number (SN)** of the pavement. Comment on whether the SN is sufficient if the design requirement is **SN = 4.5**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a) — 7 Marks

**Differences between Base and Sub-base (4 marks):**

| Aspect | Base Course | Sub-base Course |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Position | Above sub-base, below binder | Above subgrade, below base |
| Stress Level | Bears high stress (primary load spread) | Bears moderate stress |
| Material | WMM, CRM, cement-bound granular | Granular sub-base, moorum, laterite |
| Quality | High-quality crushed aggregate | Moderate-quality granular |
| Function | Structural strength, primary load distribution | Secondary load distribution, drainage, frost protection |

**Why Drainage is the Most Critical Function of Sub-base (3 marks):**

1. Water is the **single largest cause of pavement failure** (causes potholes, rutting, pumping, stripping of bitumen).
2. The sub-base, being a **granular, free-draining layer**, intercepts water infiltrating from the surface (through minor cracks) before it can reach the subgrade.
3. A saturated subgrade loses its bearing capacity drastically (CBR of a saturated soil can drop from 8% to 2%), causing catastrophic failure.
4. Hence, modern IRC and AASHTO guidelines require a **permeable sub-base (k > 100 m/day)** as the primary defence against moisture damage.

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b) — 7 Marks

**Given:**
- Asphalt layer thickness: $D_1 = 100 \text{ mm} = 3.937$ inches
- Base course thickness: $D_2 = 200 \text{ mm} = 7.874$ inches
- Sub-base thickness: $D_3 = 250 \text{ mm} = 9.843$ inches
- $a_1 = 0.42, a_2 = 0.14, a_3 = 0.11$
- $m_2 = m_3 = 1.0$

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Convert thicknesses to inches (since AASHTO uses inches):
$$D_1 = 100/25.4 = 3.937 \text{ in}$$
$$D_2 = 200/25.4 = 7.874 \text{ in}$$
$$D_3 = 250/25.4 = 9.843 \text{ in}$$

**Step 2 (3 marks):** Apply the Structural Number formula:
$$SN = a_1 D_1 + a_2 D_2 m_2 + a_3 D_3 m_3$$

$$SN = (0.42 \times 3.937) + (0.14 \times 7.874 \times 1.0) + (0.11 \times 9.843 \times 1.0)$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Compute each term:
$$SN = 1.654 + 1.102 + 1.083$$

$$SN = 3.84$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion:**
- Required SN = **4.5**
- Provided SN = **3.84**
- Since $3.84 < 4.5$, the **pavement is UNDERSIGNED** by an SN deficit of **0.66**.
- The designer must either **(i)** increase the bituminous layer thickness by ~10 mm, **(ii)** upgrade the base course to a higher-quality material (e.g., cement-treated), or **(iii)** strengthen the subgrade by stabilization, to meet the SN requirement.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Mixing up "Subgrade" and "Sub-base":** Many students interchange these terms. Remember: **Subgrade = Natural Soil; Sub-base = Engineered Granular Layer above it.**
> 2. **Unit Mismatch in AASHTO SN Calculations:** AASHTO layer coefficients are calibrated for **inches**, not millimetres. Forgetting the conversion (÷ 25.4) is the single most common error, leading to a wildly incorrect SN.
> 3. **Forgetting the drainage coefficient `mᵢ`:** AASHTO penalizes layers with poor drainage. Don't omit `m₂` and `m₃` — they range from 0.7 (poor drainage) to 1.3 (excellent drainage).
> 4. **Confusing CBR and k-value:** CBR is for **flexible pavements**; k-value (modulus of subgrade reaction) is for **rigid pavements**. They are NOT interchangeable.

---

## 📌 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- ✅ **Pavement = Layered Composite System** designed for **graded load distribution**.
- ✅ **Five typical layers (top to bottom):** Wearing Course → Binder Course → Base Course → Sub-base → Subgrade.
- ✅ **Stress decreases with depth** — this is the **fundamental justification for layering**.
- ✅ **Boussinesq's stress equation** governs stress dispersion in a homogeneous half-space; **Burmister's two-layer theory** is the basis for modern flexible pavement design.
- ✅ **Subgrade** = foundation; assessed by **CBR** (per IRC:37) or **Resilient Modulus $M_R$** (per AASHTO).
- ✅ **Sub-base** = drainage + secondary load spread; **granular, free-draining**.
- ✅ **Base course** = principal structural layer; **WMM, DBM, CTB (Cement Treated Base)**.
- ✅ **Wearing course** = direct tyre contact; **high-quality bituminous concrete, skid-resistant, waterproof**.
- ✅ **AASHTO Structural Number (SN)** = $a_1 D_1 + a_2 D_2 m_2 + a_3 D_3 m_3$ — in **inches**, not mm.
- ✅ **Standard Axle in India = 80 kN**; **contact pressure ≈ 0.56 MPa**.
- ✅ **CBR rule of thumb:** Subgrade CBR ≈ 8% (rural) to 10% (NH/SH).
- ✅ **Subgrade stress at 600 mm depth is only ~5–10% of surface pressure** — confirms layered design philosophy.
- ✅ **Water is the #1 enemy of pavements** — always design for adequate drainage through the sub-base.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pavement Materials
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET746 - HIGHWAY MATERIALS AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Pavement Materials

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PECET746 – Highway Materials and Design)**, **Pavement Materials** are defined as the engineering substances, both natural and processed, that are systematically selected, proportioned, and laid in horizontal layers above the formation level to withstand the cumulative destructive effects of traffic loading and environmental agents. They constitute the structural backbone of flexible, rigid, and composite highway pavements.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mandate (Module 1):** Students must be able to classify pavement materials, identify their engineering properties, and understand the role of each material in the layered pavement system as a precursor to thickness design (covered in Modules 2 and 3).

### 1.1.1 Broad Classification of Pavement Materials

Pavement materials are classified into four primary families based on their structural function and mechanical behavior:

1. **Cohesive / Subgrade Materials (Natural Soils)** – The foundation layer.
2. **Granular / Unbound Materials** – Used in sub-base and base courses.
3. **Bituminous / Bound Flexible Materials** – Used in surface and binder courses.
4. **Cementitious / Rigid Materials** – Used in rigid pavement slabs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Pearl:** The KTU question bank repeatedly frames the question as, *"Differentiate between the major classes of pavement materials and state their typical engineering functions."* Memorize the four-tier hierarchy above.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are building a **layered cake** that must support the weight of a rolling bowling ball (a moving truck wheel) thousands of times a day, in rain, heat, and cold:

- The **bottom layer of the cake (subgrade soil)** is the kitchen counter itself – it must be strong enough not to collapse, but it can be the original earth, provided we test it.
- The **sponge layer (granular sub-base)** distributes the weight gently and prevents water from seeping up.
- The **dense biscuit layer (WBM/WMM base)** carries the heavy load and spreads it wider.
- The **chocolate ganache (bituminous binder + surface course)** is smooth, water-resistant, and absorbs the shock.
- A **concrete version of the same cake (rigid pavement)** is like a hard cheesecake – the top layer itself is so strong it bridges over soft spots below.

Each ingredient (material) has a precise engineering job. Mixing them up — say, putting soft cake on the bottom — would cause the cake to fail. This is exactly why highway engineers classify and test materials rigorously before they go into the road.

---

## 1.3 Key Engineering Performance Requirements

Every pavement material must satisfy a set of fundamental engineering criteria as codified by **IS, IRC, ASTM, and AASHTO** standards:

| Performance Criterion | Engineering Meaning | Typical Metric (Bolded Standard) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Strength** | Resistance to crushing under wheel load | Aggregate Impact Value (AIV) $<$ **30%** for wearing course |
| **Stiffness** | Resistance to deformation under repeated load | Modulus of Elasticity (E) in **MPa** |
| **Durability** | Resistance to weathering & abrasion | Los Angeles Abrasion Value (LAA) $<$ **30%–40%** |
| **Stability** | Resistance to rutting and shoving | Marshall Stability in **kN** (typically **> 9 kN** for BC) |
| **Skid Resistance** | Surface friction for vehicle safety | Polished Stone Value (PSV) $>$ **60** for expressways |
| **Workability** | Ease of placement and compaction | Voids in Mineral Aggregate (VMA) and **Air Voids (Va)** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Constant for Highway Engineering:** The standard design wheel load for highway design in India is a **Single Axle Load of 80 kN (8.2 tonnes)** as per IRC:37-2018 and IRC:58-2015. This drives all material selection.

---

## 1.4 Overview of the Four Material Families

### 1.4.1 Subgrade Soil (Cohesive Material)
- **Origin:** Natural earth at formation level.
- **Primary Property:** **California Bearing Ratio (CBR)**.
- **Typical Required CBR:** $\geq$ **8%** for flexible pavements; $\geq$ **8%** for rigid pavements with higher design values preferred.
- **Role:** Distributes load to the natural ground.

### 1.4.2 Granular Materials
- **Crushed Stone Aggregates:** Used in WBM, WMM, and as surface dressing chips.
- **Unbound Granular Sub-base (GSB):** Locally available crushed rock, moorum, or gravel.
- **Key Tests:** Gradation (sieve analysis), Aggregate Impact Value, Aggregate Crushing Value, Los Angeles Abrasion, Specific Gravity, Water Absorption.

### 1.4.3 Bituminous Binders
- **Penetration Grade Bitumen (VG-10, VG-30, VG-40):** Viscosity-graded per IS 73.
- **Modified Bitumen (CRMB, PMB):** For heavy traffic / high-temperature regions.
- **Cutback Bitumen & Emulsions:** For prime coat, tack coat, and cold mix.
- **Key Tests:** Penetration test, Softening point (Ring & Ball), Ductility, Viscosity, Flash & Fire point, Specific gravity.

### 1.4.4 Cementitious Materials
- **Plain Cement Concrete (PCC):** Used in rigid pavements (PQC).
- **Cement-Treated Base (CTB):** Lower cement content, used as a stabilized base.
- **Key Properties:** 28-day compressive strength (typically **30–40 MPa** for PQC), flexural strength (typically **4.5 MPa** minimum), durability against freeze-thaw.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Memory Trick – "S-B-A-C":** Subgrade $\rightarrow$ Base $\rightarrow$ Asphalt $\rightarrow$ Concrete. The four letters represent the four material tiers from bottom to top in descending order of stiffness.

---

## 1.5 Historical & Standards Context

| Standard Code | Issuing Body | Scope |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **IS 73** | Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS) | Paving bitumen specification |
| **IS 2386 (Parts I–VIII)** | BIS | Methods of test for aggregates |
| **IS 1201–1220** | BIS | Tests for bituminous materials |
| **IRC:37-2018** | Indian Roads Congress | Flexible pavement design (4th revision) |
| **IRC:58-2015** | Indian Roads Congress | Rigid pavement design (plain jointed concrete) |
| **AASHTO M 145** | American Assoc. of State Highway & Transport Officials | Soil classification for highway subgrade |
| **ASTM D6927** | ASTM International | Marshall Mix design standard |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **2024 KTU scheme** explicitly tests the student's ability to map any test/property to the relevant IS/IRC code. Always quote the code number along with the property in your final exam answer.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Aggregate: The Skeleton of the Pavement

Aggregates constitute **90–95% by mass** of a typical bituminous mix and **70–85%** of concrete. They act as a stress-distributing skeleton, with the binder merely gluing the particles together.

### 2.1.1 Mechanical Properties of Aggregate

Aggregate must resist the **three primary distresses** of a flexible pavement:

1. **Crushing** under vertical wheel load $\Rightarrow$ Aggregate Crushing Value (ACV).
2. **Impact** from sudden load applications $\Rightarrow$ Aggregate Impact Value (AIV).
3. **Abrasion** from tyre–surface friction $\Rightarrow$ Los Angeles Abrasion (LAA).

### 2.1.2 Physical Properties of Aggregate

| Property | Engineering Significance | IS Test Method |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Specific Gravity** | Volume–mass relationship, mix design calculations | IS 2386 Part III |
| **Water Absorption** | Porosity & weathering resistance | IS 2386 Part III |
| **Bulk Density & Voids** | Compaction behavior | IS 2386 Part III |
| **Particle Shape (Flakiness & Elongation Index)** | Workability, strength, rutting resistance | IS 2386 Part I |
| **Angularity Number** | Internal friction | IS 2386 Part I |
| **Soundness (Na$_2$SO$_4$ / MgSO$_4$)** | Resistance to weathering | IS 2386 Part V |
| **Deleterious Materials** | Clay, silt, organic impurities | IS 2386 Part II |

---

## 2.2 Bituminous Binders: The Glue

Bitumen is a **viscoelastic, thermoplastic, adhesive, and water-resistant** material derived from petroleum refining. Its performance is highly temperature-dependent.

### 2.2.1 Rheological Classification

| Behavior Regime | Temperature | Engineering Consequence |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Elastic** | Low ( $<$ 0°C ) | Thermal cracking susceptibility |
| **Visco-elastic** | Moderate (10–40°C) | Normal pavement operation |
| **Viscous (Plastic flow)** | High ($>$ 50°C) | Rutting susceptibility |

### 2.2.2 Penetration Grading vs. Viscosity Grading

India switched from **penetration grading (80/100, 60/70, 30/40)** to **Viscosity Grading (VG-10, VG-20, VG-30, VG-40)** per **IS 73:2013** because viscosity is a more fundamental rheological property than penetration depth.

---

## 2.3 Subgrade Soil: The Foundation

The subgrade is classified per **IS 2720** and **AASHTO M 145**. The most critical design parameter is the **California Bearing Ratio (CBR)**.

### 2.3.1 The CBR Concept

CBR is the ratio of the load required to penetrate a standard plunger **2.5 mm (or 5 mm)** into a soaked soil sample, expressed as a percentage of the standard load required for the same penetration in a standard crushed-stone reference.

$$\text{CBR} = \frac{\text{Unit load on soil at 2.5 mm (or 5 mm) penetration}}{\text{Standard unit load at 2.5 mm (or 5 mm) penetration}} \times 100$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Loads (IS 2720 Part 16):**
> - At 2.5 mm penetration $\Rightarrow$ **1370 kg (13.44 kN)**
> - At 5.0 mm penetration $\Rightarrow$ **2055 kg (20.16 kN)**
> Usually, the 2.5 mm value is adopted; 5.0 mm is adopted only if it is greater than 2.5 mm.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This consolidated formula sheet covers all Module 1 numerical problems that can appear in the **ESE (End Semester Examination) Part B (14 marks)** questions.

| Sl. No. | Formula | Symbol Description | Units | Reference |
| :---: | :---: | :--- | :---: | :--- |
| 1 | $G_{sb} = \dfrac{M_{agg}}{V_{bulk} \cdot \rho_w}$ | Bulk Specific Gravity | dimensionless | IS 2386 Pt III |
| 2 | $G_{sa} = \dfrac{M_{agg}}{V_{impermeable} \cdot \rho_w}$ | Apparent Specific Gravity | dimensionless | IS 2386 Pt III |
| 3 | $G_{se} = \dfrac{100 - P_b}{\dfrac{100}{G_{mm}} - \dfrac{P_b}{G_b}}$ | Effective Specific Gravity | dimensionless | Mix Design |
| 4 | $AIV = \dfrac{W_2}{W_1} \times 100$ | Aggregate Impact Value | % | IS 2386 Pt IV |
| 5 | $ACV = \dfrac{W_f}{W_s} \times 100$ | Aggregate Crushing Value | % | IS 2386 Pt IV |
| 6 | $LAA = \dfrac{W_L}{W_O} \times 100$ | Los Angeles Abrasion | % | IS 2386 Pt IV |
| 7 | $CBR\ (\%) = \dfrac{P_{soil}}{P_{standard}} \times 100$ | California Bearing Ratio | % | IS 2720 Pt 16 |
| 8 | $D_{10} = $ size at 10% finer | Effective Size of Soil | mm | IS 2720 Pt 4 |
| 9 | $C_u = \dfrac{D_{60}}{D_{10}}$ | Coefficient of Uniformity | dimensionless | Soil Mechanics |
| 10 | $C_c = \dfrac{(D_{30})^2}{D_{10} \cdot D_{60}}$ | Coefficient of Curvature | dimensionless | Soil Mechanics |
| 11 | $\gamma_d = \dfrac{\gamma}{1 + w}$ | Dry Density from Bulk Density | kN/m$^3$ | IS 2720 Pt 14 |
| 12 | $S_{r} = \dfrac{w \cdot G_s}{e}$ | Degree of Saturation | dimensionless | Soil Mechanics |
| 13 | $V_a = \dfrac{G_{mm} - G_{mb}}{G_{mm}} \times 100$ | Air Voids in Compacted Mix | % | MS-2 (Asphalt Institute) |
| 14 | $VMA = 100 - \dfrac{G_{mb} \cdot P_s}{G_{sb}}$ | Voids in Mineral Aggregate | % | MS-2 |
| 15 | $VFA = \dfrac{VMA - V_a}{VMA} \times 100$ | Voids Filled with Asphalt | % | MS-2 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation Note:** In formula 4–6, $W_1$ = original oven-dry weight of aggregate; $W_2$ / $W_f$ / $W_L$ = weight of fines passing the 2.36 mm sieve after the respective test. The standard sieve for the fines fraction is **2.36 mm IS Sieve**.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The material classification and formula framework above is the foundation for:

- **Pavement Thickness Design** (covered in Modules 2 and 3): CBR $\rightarrow$ thickness curves (IRC:37).
- **Mix Design** (Marshall, Superpave): Specific gravity values, AIV, LAA feed into volumetric and strength criteria.
- **Quality Control at Site**: Every batch of aggregate and bitumen is tested against these parameters; rejection limits are strictly codified in **MoRTH Specifications for Road and Bridge Works (5th Revision)**.
- **Failure Investigation**: Premature pavement failure (e.g., rutting, cracking, raveling) is traced back to non-conforming material properties using the same formula set.

> [!TIP]
> **Cross-Disciplinary Utility:** The same Specific Gravity concept is used in Geotechnical Engineering (soil mechanics), Concrete Technology (mix design), and Transportation Engineering (bituminous mix design) — a true unifying concept.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All derivations below are aligned with the **KTU 2024 ESE** numerical pattern. Practice these with the exact step-by-step transcription style expected by the KTU Board Examiner.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Relationship Between Bulk, Apparent, and Effective Specific Gravity of Aggregate

### 3.1.1 Fundamental Volume Definitions

Let us define the three volume concepts for an aggregate particle:

- $V_{bulk}$ = Total volume of the aggregate particle (including impermeable and permeable voids) $\Rightarrow$ **Bulk Volume**
- $V_{imperm}$ = Volume of the solid aggregate excluding permeable voids $\Rightarrow$ **Impermeable Volume**
- $V_{permeable}$ = Volume of permeable (water-accessible) voids inside the particle

By definition:

$$
V_{bulk} = V_{imperm} + V_{permeable}
$$

### 3.1.2 Definition of the Three Specific Gravities

Let $M$ = oven-dry mass of aggregate; $\rho_w$ = density of water.

$$
G_{sb} = \frac{M}{V_{bulk} \cdot \rho_w} = \frac{M}{(V_{imperm} + V_{permeable}) \cdot \rho_w}
$$

$$
G_{sa} = \frac{M}{V_{imperm} \cdot \rho_w}
$$

### 3.1.3 Deriving the Bulk-Impermeable Relationship

Dividing $G_{sa}$ by $G_{sb}$:

$$
\frac{G_{sa}}{G_{sb}} = \frac{V_{imperm} + V_{permeable}}{V_{imperm}} = 1 + \frac{V_{permeable}}{V_{imperm}}
$$

Rearranging to obtain the permeable void volume in terms of specific gravities:

$$
\frac{V_{permeable}}{V_{imperm}} = \frac{G_{sa}}{G_{sb}} - 1 = \frac{G_{sa} - G_{sb}}{G_{sb}}
$$

### 3.1.4 Effective Specific Gravity $G_{se}$

The **effective specific gravity** excludes the permeable voids that get filled by bitumen during mix design. It is mathematically derived as a mass-weighted average between aggregate and bitumen, but in the volumetric form:

$$
G_{se} = \frac{M}{V_{imperm} \cdot \rho_w} \quad \text{(when permeable voids are filled with bitumen)}
$$

Wait — we must correct this. The **effective** specific gravity excludes the volume of permeable voids (because they are filled with binder in the mix, not water). Hence:

$$
G_{se} = \frac{M}{(V_{imperm}) \cdot \rho_w} = G_{sa}
$$

However, in a mix design, the **effective volume** is approximated empirically. The proper formula incorporating the binder content $P_b$ (as a percentage of total mix) and the binder specific gravity $G_b$ is:

$$
G_{se} = \frac{100 - P_b}{\dfrac{100}{G_{mm}} - \dfrac{P_b}{G_b}}
$$

where $G_{mm}$ is the maximum theoretical specific gravity of the mix (no air voids).

### 3.1.5 Final General Relationship

The three specific gravities follow the inequality:

$$
G_{sb} \ \le \ G_{se} \ \le \ G_{sa}
$$

> **Reasoning:** Bulk volume (largest) $\Rightarrow$ smallest $G_{sb}$. Impermeable volume (smallest) $\Rightarrow$ largest $G_{sa}$. Effective volume (in between, with bitumen-filled voids) $\Rightarrow$ $G_{se}$ is intermediate. This is a **standard 2-mark KTU conceptual question**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Aggregate Impact Value (AIV) — Worked Numerical

**Problem Statement:** An aggregate sample weighing **500 g** is subjected to the standard Aggregate Impact Test (IS 2386 Part IV). After 15 blows of a **14 kg hammer falling 380 mm**, the material passing the **2.36 mm IS Sieve** weighs **110 g**. Calculate the Aggregate Impact Value and comment on the suitability of the aggregate for use in the surface course of a heavily-trafficked national highway.

### Step 1: Identify the Given Data

- Original weight of aggregate, $W_1$ = **500 g**
- Weight of fines passing 2.36 mm after impact, $W_2$ = **110 g**
- Hammer mass = 14 kg
- Drop height = 380 mm (15 blows)
- Standard sieve = 2.36 mm

### Step 2: Apply the Standard AIV Formula

$$
AIV = \frac{W_2}{W_1} \times 100
$$

Substitute:

$$
AIV = \frac{110}{500} \times 100
$$

### Step 3: Compute the Numerical Result

$$
AIV = 0.22 \times 100 = 22.0\ \%
$$

### Step 4: Compare with IS/MoRTH Specifications

| Application | Maximum Permissible AIV |
| :--- | :---: |
| **Wearing course (surface) of heavily-trafficked road** | **$\leq$ 20%** (MoRTH) |
| **Bituminous macadam / base course** | $\leq$ 30% |
| **WBM base course** | $\leq$ 35% |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Alert:** Always express the percentage as a number **and** state the governing specification. Many students lose the "comment" sub-part mark by simply quoting the numerical result without comparing it to the standard.

### Step 5: Engineering Conclusion

Since the calculated **AIV = 22%** is **marginally higher than the 20% limit** for a wearing course on a national highway, the aggregate is **NOT suitable for direct use in the surface (wearing) course**. However, it can be used in:
- **Bituminous Macadam (base) course** (limit 30% $\Rightarrow$ **acceptable**).
- **WBM base course** (limit 35% $\Rightarrow$ **acceptable**).

A practical engineering recommendation would be to either **blend this aggregate with a stronger one** or **reject it for surface use** but utilize it in lower layers.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: CBR Determination from a Single-Point Load Test

**Problem Statement:** A soaked CBR test on a subgrade soil sample yields a plunger load of **480 kg** at **2.5 mm penetration** and **640 kg** at **5.0 mm penetration**. The standard loads at these penetrations are **1370 kg** and **2055 kg**, respectively. Compute the CBR of the subgrade.

### Step 1: Recall the CBR Formula

$$
CBR\ (\%) = \frac{\text{Test load}}{\text{Standard load}} \times 100
$$

### Step 2: Compute CBR at 2.5 mm Penetration

$$
CBR_{2.5} = \frac{480}{1370} \times 100
$$

$$
CBR_{2.5} = 0.3504 \times 100 = 35.04\ \%
$$

### Step 3: Compute CBR at 5.0 mm Penetration

$$
CBR_{5.0} = \frac{640}{2055} \times 100
$$

$$
CBR_{5.0} = 0.3114 \times 100 = 31.14\ \%
$$

### Step 4: Apply the IS Code Rule

Per **IS 2720 Part 16**, the CBR is the value at 2.5 mm penetration. The 5.0 mm value is adopted **only if it is greater** than the 2.5 mm value (which indicates the curve did not peak, suggesting a possible soft lower layer).

In our case:

$$
CBR_{2.5} = 35.04\% \ > \ CBR_{5.0} = 31.14\%
$$

Therefore, the **adopted CBR = 35.04%** (rounded to **35%** for design use).

### Step 5: Interpret for Design

> **Adopted Subgrade CBR = 35%** — This is an **excellent subgrade** (typical weak clayey subgrade has CBR 2–8%). Per IRC:37-2018, such a strong subgrade would allow significantly **reduced pavement thickness** and may qualify for a thin bituminous surfacing on a thin granular base.

> [!TIP]
> **Calculator Note:** KTU board examiners allow basic non-programmable calculators. Always show the substituted value before computing the final decimal to earn the substitution mark.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Common KTU Numerical Problem Patterns

| Pattern | Typical 7-Mark KTU Question | Approach |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Type 1: AIV / ACV / LAA Calculation** | Given weight of aggregate and weight of fines, compute the index and comment. | Use $AIV/ACV/LAA = (W_{fines} / W_{original}) \times 100$, compare with MoRTH spec. |
| **Type 2: CBR Computation** | Given loads at 2.5 and 5.0 mm, compute CBR. | Compute both, adopt higher rule. |
| **Type 3: Specific Gravity Relationship** | Given $G_{sb}$ and $G_{sa}$, find the percentage of permeable voids. | Use $V_{permeable} = (1/G_{sb} - 1/G_{sa}) \times 1000$ cm³/kg. |
| **Type 4: Sieve Analysis** | Plot the gradation curve and identify $D_{10}, D_{60}, C_u, C_c$. | Use semi-log graph; classify as well-graded or poorly-graded. |
| **Type 5: Bitumen Grade Selection** | Given climatic region and traffic, choose VG grade. | VG-10 cold regions, VG-30 hot/temperate, VG-40 heavy traffic. |
| **Type 6: Mix Design Volumetrics** | Given $G_{mb}, G_{mm}, P_b, P_s$, compute $V_a$, VMA, VFA. | Use the formula sheet above in sequence. |
