# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The questions below strictly follow the **KTU 2024 ESE pattern** (Part A: 2 × 3 marks; Part B: Internal choice 1 × 14 marks). Each Part B question is split into two 7-mark sub-parts, and a complete model answer is provided.

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> *"Define the term 'Pavement Material' and list the four major categories of materials used in highway pavement construction."*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **pavement material** is an engineering substance, either natural or processed, used in the construction of road pavements to provide the required strength, durability, and riding quality under the combined action of traffic loads and environmental conditions.

The four major categories of pavement materials are:

1. **Cohesive / Subgrade Materials** — Natural soils at the formation level (e.g., clayey or sandy soil).
2. **Granular / Unbound Materials** — Crushed stone aggregates used in sub-base and base courses (e.g., WBM, WMM, GSB).
3. **Bituminous / Bound Materials** — Bitumen and its derivatives used in flexible pavement layers (e.g., VG-30 bitumen, BC, DBM).
4. **Cementitious / Rigid Materials** — Cement concrete used in rigid pavement slabs (e.g., PQC, DLC).

> **Mark Distribution:** Definition (1 mark) + Four categories with examples (2 marks = 0.5 each).

---

### Question A2

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> *"Differentiate between Bulk Specific Gravity and Apparent Specific Gravity of aggregate. State the governing IS code."*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Bulk Specific Gravity ($G_{sb}$) | Apparent Specific Gravity ($G_{sa}$) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Volume Definition** | Includes both impermeable solids and permeable voids | Includes only the impermeable solids |
| **Mathematical Form** | $G_{sb} = M / (V_{bulk} \cdot \rho_w)$ | $G_{sa} = M / (V_{imperm} \cdot \rho_w)$ |
| **Magnitude** | Lower | Higher |
| **Use in Mix Design** | Volume calculations (VMA, VFA) | Void-less reference for binder absorption |
| **IS Code** | IS 2386 Part III | IS 2386 Part III |

> **Key Line:** Always $G_{sb} \le G_{sa}$, with the difference representing the **permeable void volume** of the aggregate.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 ESE pattern: Each Part B question carries **14 marks** with **internal choice** between Option A and Option B. Both options must be answered with sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.

---

### Question 5.2.1 (Option A)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 1]**
> *(a)* Explain in detail the **properties of aggregates** used in highway construction, with specific reference to **strength, durability, shape, and water absorption**. List the relevant IS test methods.
>
> *(b)* A crushed stone aggregate sample weighing **1250 g** is tested in the **Los Angeles Abrasion Testing Machine** with **11 steel balls (charge)** for **500 revolutions**. After the test, the weight of material retained on the **1.70 mm IS sieve** is **975 g**. Calculate the **Los Angeles Abrasion Value (LAA)** and comment on the suitability of the aggregate for use in the **bituminous surface course of a national highway**.

---

#### Part (a) — Model Answer (7 Marks)

**Properties of Highway Aggregates (per IS 2386 Parts I–VIII):**

**1. Strength (2 marks):** Aggregate must resist crushing under wheel loads. Tested using:
- **Aggregate Impact Value (AIV):** $AIV = (W_2/W_1) \times 100$. IS 2386 Part IV. Limit for wearing course: $\leq$ 20% (MoRTH).
- **Aggregate Crushing Value (ACV):** $ACV = (W_f/W_s) \times 100$. IS 2386 Part IV. Limit: $\leq$ 30% for surface course.

**2. Durability (2 marks):** Resistance to weathering and abrasion. Tested using:
- **Los Angeles Abrasion (LAA):** IS 2386 Part IV. Limit for surface course: $\leq$ 30%.
- **Soundness Test:** Sodium or Magnesium Sulphate cycles. IS 2386 Part V. Limits: $\leq$ 12% (Na) and $\leq$ 18% (Mg).

**3. Shape (1.5 marks):** Cubical particles are preferred over flaky/elongated ones.
- **Flakiness Index:** IS 2386 Part I. Limit: $\leq$ 25–30%.
- **Elongation Index:** IS 2386 Part I. Limit: $\leq$ 15–25%.
- **Angularity Number:** IS 2386 Part I. Higher value $\Rightarrow$ more inter-particle friction.

**4. Water Absorption (1.5 marks):** Indicates porosity. Affects bitumen absorption in mix design.
- Formula: $WA\ (\%) = (W_s - W_d)/W_d \times 100$.
- **IS 2386 Part III** governs. Limit: $\leq$ 2% for most surface applications.

---

#### Part (b) — Model Answer (7 Marks)

**Given Data:**
- Original weight, $W_O$ = **1250 g**
- Weight retained on 1.70 mm sieve after test, $W_R$ = **975 g**
- LAA standard: 11 balls, 500 revolutions.

**Step 1: Compute the Loss in Weight**

$$
W_L = W_O - W_R
$$

$$
W_L = 1250 - 975 = 275 \text{ g}
$$

**Step 2: Apply the LAA Formula**

$$
LAA = \frac{W_L}{W_O} \times 100
$$

$$
LAA = \frac{275}{1250} \times 100
$$

**Step 3: Compute the Final Numerical Value**

$$
LAA = 0.22 \times 100 = 22.0\ \%
$$

**Step 4: Compare with MoRTH Specification**

| Application | Maximum Permissible LAA |
| :--- | :---: |
| **Bituminous Surface Course (BC / Wearing Course)** | $\leq$ **20%** (Heavy traffic) |
| **DBM / BM** | $\leq$ 25–30% |
| **WMM / WBM** | $\leq$ 35–40% |

**Step 5: Engineering Conclusion**

Calculated **LAA = 22%** which **exceeds the 20% limit** for the bituminous surface course of a national highway. Therefore, the aggregate is **NOT suitable for the wearing course** in its current form. Recommended actions:
- **Blend** with a stronger aggregate (e.g., granite) to bring the composite LAA below 20%.
- **Use it in lower layers** like DBM (acceptable) or WMM (acceptable).
- **Reject** the lot if no blending is feasible.

> **Mark Allocation:** Data identification (1) + Step 1 calculation (1) + Step 2 formula (1) + Step 3 numerical (1) + Step 4 comparison (1) + Step 5 engineering conclusion (2).

---

### Question 5.2.2 (Option B)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1]**
> *(a)* Classify **bituminous binders** and explain in detail the **penetration test, softening point test, and ductility test** for bitumen. State IS codes and typical test results for **VG-30** grade bitumen.
>
> *(b)* The following test loads were recorded during a **CBR test** on a soaked subgrade soil sample:
>
> | Penetration (mm) | 0.5 | 1.0 | 1.5 | 2.0 | 2.5 | 3.0 | 4.0 | 5.0 |
> | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
> | Load (kg) | 65 | 120 | 180 | 235 | **295** | 340 | 425 | **490** |
>
> Standard loads at 2.5 mm and 5.0 mm are **1370 kg** and **2055 kg**, respectively. Determine the **CBR of the subgrade** and comment on its design suitability per **IRC:37-2018** for a national highway.

---

#### Part (a) — Model Answer (7 Marks)

**Classification of Bituminous Binders (2 marks):**

1. **Asphalt Cement (Cut-back free):** Viscosity-graded bitumen VG-10, VG-20, VG-30, VG-40 per **IS 73:2013**.
2. **Cutback Bitumen:** Bitumen blended with petroleum solvent (rapid, medium, slow curing). IS 217.
3. **Bitumen Emulsion:** Bitumen dispersed in water (cationic/anionic, rapid/medium/slow setting). IS 3117.
4. **Modified Bitumen:** Crumb Rubber Modified (CRMB), Polymer Modified (PMB). IRC:SP:53.

**Penetration Test (2 marks):** IS 1201–1202. Measures consistency (hardness) of bitumen. A standard needle penetrates a bitumen sample under **100 g load at 25°C for 5 seconds**. Result in **1/10 mm units**. For **VG-30**: typical penetration range **50–70**.

**Softening Point Test (Ring & Ball) (1.5 marks):** IS 1205. Bitumen disc held in horizontal ring, heated at 5°C/min in a liquid bath. Steel ball (3.5 g) placed on the disc sinks and contacts a base plate. Temperature at contact = **softening point**. For **VG-30**: typically **>$ $50°C**.

**Ductility Test (1.5 marks):** IS 1208. A standard briquette of bitumen is pulled apart at 5 cm/min in a water bath at 27°C. Elongation at break (in cm) = **ductility**. For **VG-30**: typically **>$ $75 cm** (or as specified).

---

#### Part (b) — Model Answer (7 Marks)

**Given Data:**
- Test load at 2.5 mm penetration, $P_{2.5}$ = **295 kg**
- Test load at 5.0 mm penetration, $P_{5.0}$ = **490 kg**
- Standard load at 2.5 mm, $P_{s,2.5}$ = **1370 kg**
- Standard load at 5.0 mm, $P_{s,5.0}$ = **2055 kg**

**Step 1: Compute CBR at 2.5 mm Penetration**

$$
CBR_{2.5} = \frac{P_{2.5}}{P_{s,2.5}} \times 100
$$

$$
CBR_{2.5} = \frac{295}{1370} \times 100
$$

$$
CBR_{2.5} = 0.2153 \times 100 = 21.53\ \%
$$

**Step 2: Compute CBR at 5.0 mm Penetration**

$$
CBR_{5.0} = \frac{P_{5.0}}{P_{s,5.0}} \times 100
$$

$$
CBR_{5.0} = \frac{490}{2055} \times 100
$$

$$
CBR_{5.0} = 0.2384 \times 100 = 23.84\ \%
$$

**Step 3: Apply the IS 2720 Part 16 Rule**

In our case, $CBR_{5.0} = 23.84\% > CBR_{2.5} = 21.53\%$. Since the 5.0 mm value is higher, the curve did not peak at 2.5 mm — possibly indicating a soft lower layer or inadequate compaction.

**Per IS 2720 Part 16, repeat the test.** If confirmed on retest, **adopt $CBR_{5.0}$**.

**Adopted CBR = 23.84% $\approx$ 24%** (typically rounded down for conservative design).

**Step 4: Comment on Design Suitability**

A subgrade CBR of **$\geq$ 8%** is the minimum recommended for flexible pavements per **IRC:37-2018**. Our value of **24%** is **well above the minimum**, classifying this subgrade as a **strong subgrade**.

**Design Implications (per IRC:37-2018):**
- Total pavement thickness required will be **substantially reduced**.
- The pavement may qualify for a **thin bituminous surfacing** (e.g., 50 mm BC over a thin granular base of 100–200 mm).
- However, a **CBR $\geq$ 8%** is also the **threshold for sub-base requirement**; below this, a higher-quality sub-base is needed. Since 24% > 8%, the **sub-base may even be omitted in low-traffic situations**, but typically retained for safety.

> **Mark Allocation:** Data identification (1) + 2.5 mm CBR (1.5) + 5.0 mm CBR (1.5) + IS rule application (1) + Adopted CBR (1) + Design implication (1).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout (Module 1 Numericals):**
> 1. **Forgetting the "Adopt Higher" Rule in CBR:** When $CBR_{5.0} > CBR_{2.5}$, students often still adopt $CBR_{2.5}$. This is a **direct 1-mark loss**. Always apply IS 2720 Part 16.
> 2. **Unit Inconsistency in AIV/ACV/LAA:** AIV is a ratio expressed in %. If the weight of fines is given in **kg**, convert to **g** before substitution. Most students commit this slip under exam pressure.
> 3. **Specification Reference:** Never end an AIV/LAA problem without quoting the **MoRTH limit**. The "comment" sub-part carries 2 of 7 marks, and the comparison is non-negotiable.
> 4. **Specific Gravity Inequality:** When asked to prove $G_{sb} \le G_{se} \le G_{sa}$, write the **volume inequality** $V_{bulk} \ge V_{effective} \ge V_{imperm}$ and then convert using $G = M / (V \cdot \rho_w)$. This clear logical chain fetches full marks.
> 5. **Bitumen Test Temperatures:** Always quote the standard test temperature (e.g., **25°C for penetration, 27°C for ductility, 4°C/min heating rate for softening point**). A missing temperature loses a half-mark.
> 6. **Sieve Confusion in AIV vs. LAA:** AIV uses **2.36 mm IS Sieve**; LAA uses **1.70 mm IS Sieve**. Mixing them up is a common board exam error.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Pin this in your notebook!)**

- [ ] **The Four Material Tiers:** Subgrade Soil $\rightarrow$ Granular Sub-base $\rightarrow$ Granular Base $\rightarrow$ Bituminous/Cement Surface.
- [ ] **Standard Wheel Load for Design in India:** 80 kN Single Axle (per IRC:37-2018).
- [ ] **Bitumen Code:** **IS 73:2013** governs paving bitumen; current grades are **VG-10, VG-20, VG-30, VG-40** (replaced the old penetration grades 80/100, 60/70, 30/40).
- [ ] **Penetration Test:** Needle + 100 g + 25°C + 5 s. Unit = **1/10 mm**.
- [ ] **Softening Point Test:** Ring & Ball + 5°C/min heating rate. **IS 1205**.
- [ ] **Ductility Test:** 5 cm/min pull rate at 27°C. **IS 1208**.
- [ ] **Aggregate Impact Value (AIV):** $AIV = (W_2 / W_1) \times 100$. Limit for wearing course: **$\leq$ 20%**.
- [ ] **Aggregate Crushing Value (ACV):** $ACV = (W_f / W_s) \times 100$. Limit for surface: **$\leq$ 30%**.
- [ ] **Los Angeles Abrasion (LAA):** $LAA = (W_L / W_O) \times 100$. Sieve = **1.70 mm**. Limit for surface: **$\leq$ 20–30%**.
- [ ] **Flakiness & Elongation Index:** Limits typically **$\leq$ 25%** and **$\leq$ 15%** for surface.
- [ ] **CBR Formula:** $CBR = (P_{test} / P_{standard}) \times 100$. Standard loads: **1370 kg @ 2.5 mm** and **2055 kg @ 5.0 mm**.
- [ ] **CBR "Adopt Higher" Rule:** If $CBR_{5.0} > CBR_{2.5}$ (after retest confirmation), adopt the higher.
- [ ] **Subgrade CBR Requirement:** Minimum **8%** for flexible pavements per **IRC:37-2018**.
- [ ] **Specific Gravity Inequality:** $G_{sb} \le G_{se} \le G_{sa}$.
- [ ] **Volume Hierarchy:** $V_{bulk} \ge V_{eff} \ge V_{imperm}$.
- [ ] **Water Absorption Limit:** Typically **$\leq$ 2%** for most surface applications.
- [ ] **Soundness Limits:** $\leq$ 12% for Na$_2$SO$_4$ and $\leq$ 18% for MgSO$_4$.
- [ ] **Bitumen Specific Gravity:** Typically **0.97–1.02** at 27°C (used in mix design).
- [ ] **Marshall Stability (BC):** **$\geq$ 9 kN** minimum; **Flow** typically **2–4 mm**.
- [ ] **Common IS Codes to Memorize:** IS 73, IS 2386 (Pt I–VIII), IS 1201–1220, IS 2720 (Pt 4, 8, 14, 16), IRC:37, IRC:58, IS 456 (for PQC reference).
- [ ] **Master Slogan:** *"Subgrade Soaks, Base Crushes, Binder Ruts, Surface Wears."* — encapsulates the failure modes that material tests are designed to detect.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Desirable Properties, Principle and Procedure of Tests for Assessment of Subgrade Soil, Road Aggregates and Bitumen
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET746 - HIGHWAY MATERIALS AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Desirable Properties, Principle and Procedure of Tests for Assessment of Subgrade Soil, Road Aggregates and Bitumen

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Highway Materials: The Triad of a Flexible Pavement

A modern highway pavement is a meticulously engineered **layered system**, where every constituent material is selected and tested to perform a specific structural function. The three principal material families that form the backbone of highway engineering are:

1. **Subgrade Soil** — The natural or improved foundation that ultimately carries the entire traffic load.
2. **Road Aggregates** — The mineral skeleton (crushed stone, gravel, sand) that distributes load and provides mechanical strength.
3. **Bituminous Binder (Bitumen)** — The viscous adhesive that binds aggregates into a cohesive, water-resistant matrix.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *Highway Materials Testing* is the systematic evaluation of physical, mechanical, and rheological properties of pavement materials through standardized laboratory and field procedures, governed by the Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS) and Indian Roads Congress (IRC) codes, to ensure safety, durability, and serviceability of road infrastructure.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Layered Cake" Model

Imagine baking a **three-tier cake**:

| Pavement Layer | Cake Analogy | Material | Function |
|----------------|--------------|----------|----------|
| Subgrade Soil | The **plate on the table** | Compacted natural soil | Bears the full weight of the cake (traffic) |
| Aggregate Base/Sub-base | The **sponge cake body** | Crushed rock, gravel | Distributes load evenly, prevents settlement |
| Bituminous Surface | The **chocolate ganache icing** | Bitumen + aggregate mix | Waterproofs, smoothens, resists wear |

> Just as a weak plate cracks the cake and thin icing melts in heat, **inferior subgrade, weak aggregates, or poor bitumen** cause pavement failure (rutting, cracking, pot-holes).

## 1.3 Why Assessment is Non-Negotiable

> [!NOTE]
> **Failure Statistics:** As per MoRTH (Ministry of Road Transport and Highways) reports, **> 60 % of premature pavement failures in India** are traced to inadequate material testing rather than design errors. Hence, KTU lays heavy emphasis on mastering the principle, procedure, and interpretation of standard tests.

## 1.4 Classification Overview of Tests

$$\boxed{\text{Highway Material Tests} = \begin{cases} \textbf{Subgrade Soil Tests} \rightarrow \text{Strength, Stiffness, Compressibility} \\ \textbf{Aggregate Tests} \rightarrow \text{Hardness, Toughness, Shape, Durability} \\ \textbf{Bitumen Tests} \rightarrow \text{Consistency, Rheology, Adhesion, Aging} \end{cases}}$$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 SUBGRADE SOIL — Desirable Properties

A subgrade soil must possess the following characteristics to qualify for highway construction:

| S.No. | Desirable Property | Engineering Significance |
|-------|--------------------|--------------------------|
| 1 | **Stability** | Resistance to deformation under traffic loads |
| 2 | **Adequate Bearing Capacity (CBR ≥ 5 %–8 %)** | Prevents shear failure and rutting |
| 3 | **Low Compressibility** | Minimizes differential settlement |
| 4 | **Good Drainage** | Prevents pore-pressure build-up and softening |
| 5 | **Low Shrink–Swell Potential** | Avoids cracking due to moisture variation |
| 6 | **Ease of Compaction** | Reduces construction time and cost |
| 7 | **Resistance to Erosion** | Maintains integrity under rain/flow |

> [!NOTE]
> **IRC:37-2018 Recommendation:** For flexible pavements, the **effective CBR of the subgrade** governs the **total pavement thickness**. A higher CBR permits a thinner, more economical pavement.

---

### 2.1.1 California Bearing Ratio (CBR) Test — *IS 2720 (Part 16)*

**Principle:** The CBR test measures the **shear resistance of a compacted soil** by comparing the load required to penetrate the soil with a standard circular piston (area = 1935 mm², dia = 49.65 mm) at a standard rate of **1.25 mm/min** to the load required to produce the same penetration in a **standard crushed-stone reference material**.

**Desirable Property Tested:** Bearing strength / shear resistance.

**Procedure (Step-wise):**
1. Take ~4.5 kg of air-dried soil passing **20 mm IS sieve**.
2. Add water to reach **Optimum Moisture Content (OMC)** as per IS 2720 Part 7/8.
3. Compact in the **CBR mould** (150 mm dia, 175 mm height) in **5 layers**, each with **56 blows** of a 4.9 kg rammer dropped 450 mm (heavy compaction).
4. Place a **spacer disc** at the bottom; invert mould; place a filter paper and surcharge weights (≈ 5 kg, equivalent to 70 mm surfacing).
5. Soak the specimen in water for **4 days (96 hours)** to simulate worst moisture condition; measure swell.
6. Place the mould on the CBR loading machine; mount the penetration piston.
7. Apply load at **1.25 mm/min**; record load readings at penetrations: 0.5, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0, 2.5, 3.0, 4.0, 5.0, 7.5, 10.0, 12.5 mm.
8. Plot **Load (Y-axis, kg) vs. Penetration (X-axis, mm)**.
9. Calculate CBR at **2.5 mm and 5.0 mm** penetrations.

**CBR Formula:**

$$\text{CBR (\%)} = \frac{\text{Test Load at given penetration}}{\text{Standard Load at same penetration}} \times 100$$

Standard Loads (Crushed-stone reference):
- At 2.5 mm penetration = **1370 kg**
- At 5.0 mm penetration = **2055 kg**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Rule:** *The CBR value is taken as the higher of the two (2.5 mm and 5.0 mm).* If the 5.0 mm value is consistently higher, a **re-test** is recommended.

---

### 2.1.2 Plate Bearing Test — *IS 1888*

**Principle:** A circular steel plate (usually 750 mm dia) is loaded incrementally on the prepared subgrade, and the corresponding **settlement** is measured. The **Modulus of Subgrade Reaction (k)** is obtained from the **pressure–settlement curve**.

**Procedure (KTU Board Exam Favourite):**
1. Excavate a test pit (~1.5 m deep) up to the subgrade level.
2. Level the surface; place a 30 cm thick sand layer as bedding.
3. Position the plate, a hydraulic jack, and a reaction truss (anchored by a truck or dead weight ≥ 25 tonnes).
4. Apply load in **increments of 0.35 kg/cm² (35 kPa)**; record settlement by **dial gauges (3 nos., 120° apart)**.
5. Maintain each load increment until **settlement rate < 0.02 mm/min** (stable).
6. Continue up to failure or 0.5 cm settlement.
7. Plot **Settlement (mm) vs. Pressure (kPa)**.
8. Compute **k-value** from the slope of the *rebound* curve.

**k-Value Formula:**

$$k = \frac{p}{\delta} \quad \left[ \text{units: kN/m}^3 \text{ or kg/cm}^3 \right]$$

Where:
- $p$ = pressure intensity (kPa or kg/cm²)
- $\delta$ = settlement (m or cm)

> [!TIP]
> **Kern's Correction:** For non-standard plate diameter, $k$ is corrected as $k' = k \times \left( \dfrac{b'}{b} \right)$ where $b'$ is the standard plate width (30 cm) and $b$ is the actual plate.

---

### 2.1.3 Dynamic Cone Penetrometer (DCP) Test — *IS 2720 (Part 26)*

**Principle:** An 8 kg hammer dropped from 575 mm drives a cone into the soil; penetration per blow correlates with **in-situ CBR**.

**Formula:**

$$\text{CBR (\%)} = \frac{292}{\text{DCPI}^{1.12}}$$

Where DCPI = Dynamic Cone Penetration Index = penetration in mm per blow.

---

## 2.2 ROAD AGGREGATES — Desirable Properties

| S.No. | Property | Engineering Reason |
|-------|----------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Strength & Hardness** | Resist crushing under wheel loads |
| 2 | **Toughness** | Resist impact from sudden loads |
| 3 | **Durability** | Resist weathering, freeze-thaw |
| 4 | **Shape (Cubical/Angular)** | Good interlock and stability |
| 5 | **Surface Texture (Rough)** | High skid resistance |
| 6 | **Cleanliness (Low dust, organic)** | Strong bitumen-aggregate bond |
| 7 | **Hydrophobic / Stripping Resistance** | Resist bitumen film peeling |
| 8 | **Specific Gravity > 2.6** | Indicates sound rock |
| 9 | **Low Water Absorption (< 2 %)** | Weather-resistant |
| 10 | **Chemical Stability** | No deleterious reactions |

---

### 2.2.1 Aggregate Impact Value (AIV) — *IS 2386 (Part 4)*

**Principle:** Measures the **toughness** of aggregate by quantifying the fraction broken into fines by a **standard hammer impact** (13.5–14 kg dropped 380 mm, 15 blows).

**Procedure:**
1. Sieve aggregate between **12.5 mm and 10 mm IS sieves**; oven-dry at 100–110 °C.
2. Fill the cylindrical cup in **3 layers**, each tamped 25 times with a tamping rod.
3. Subject to **15 blows** of the hammer at 380 mm drop height.
4. Sieve the crushed material through **2.36 mm IS sieve**; weigh the fraction passing ($W_2$).
5. Original sample weight = $W_1$.

$$\boxed{\text{AIV (\%)} = \frac{W_2}{W_1} \times 100}$$

| Application | Max AIV (IRC Recommendation) |
|-------------|-------------------------------|
| Wearing course | 30 % |
| Bituminous Macadam / Base course | 35 % |
| Water Bound Macadam | 40 % |

---

### 2.2.2 Aggregate Crushing Value (ACV) — *IS 2386 (Part 4)*

**Principle:** Measures the **compressive strength** by progressively loading aggregate (passing 12.5 mm, retained 10 mm) in a steel cylinder to **400 kN in 10 minutes**.

$$\boxed{\text{ACV (\%)} = \frac{W_2}{W_1} \times 100}$$

Where $W_2$ = weight of fines passing 2.36 mm after load, $W_1$ = original weight.

> IRC recommends **ACV ≤ 30 % for surfacing** and **≤ 45 % for base course**.

---

### 2.2.3 Los Angeles Abrasion (LAA) Test — *IS 2386 (Part 4)*

**Principle:** Simulates **attrition and impact** by rotating aggregate with **steel spheres (abrasive charge)** in a drum at **30–33 rpm for 500 revolutions** (or 1000 for special cases).

$$\boxed{\text{LAA (\%)} = \frac{W_2}{W_1} \times 100}$$

Where $W_2$ = weight passing 1.7 mm sieve after test, $W_1$ = original weight.

| Grading | Number of Spheres | Charge Mass (g) |
|---------|-------------------|-----------------|
| A | 12 | 5000 ± 25 |
| B | 11 | 4584 ± 25 |
| C | 8 | 3330 ± 20 |
| D | 6 | 2500 ± 15 |
| E | 12 | 5000 ± 25 (wet test) |
| F | 12 | 5000 ± 25 |

> IRC limits: **LAA ≤ 30 % for bituminous surfacing**, **≤ 35 % for WBM**, **≤ 40 % for granular sub-base**.

---

### 2.2.4 Specific Gravity & Water Absorption — *IS 2386 (Part 3)*

**Principle:** Specific gravity is the ratio of the mass of aggregate to the mass of an equal volume of water. Water absorption indicates **porosity**.

**Procedure (Pycnometer method):**
1. Weigh ~1 kg of saturated, surface-dry (SSD) aggregate → $W_1$.
2. Place in pycnometer, fill with distilled water; weigh → $W_2$.
3. Empty pycnometer, fill with water only; weigh → $W_3$.
4. Oven-dry aggregate at 100–110 °C; weigh → $W_4$.

$$\boxed{\text{Apparent Specific Gravity} = \frac{W_4}{W_4 - (W_2 - W_3)}}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Bulk Specific Gravity (SSD)} = \frac{W_1}{W_1 - (W_2 - W_3)}}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Water Absorption (\%)} = \frac{W_1 - W_4}{W_4} \times 100}$$

---

### 2.2.5 Soundness Test — *IS 2386 (Part 5)*

**Principle:** Subject aggregate to **5 cycles** of immersion in **sodium sulphate (Na₂SO₄) or magnesium sulphate (MgSO₄) solution** followed by oven drying. The salt crystals expand in pores, simulating **freeze-thaw weathering**.

$$\boxed{\text{Soundness Loss (\%)} = \frac{\text{Loss in weight after test}}{\text{Original weight}} \times 100}$$

> Acceptable limit for wearing course: **< 12 % (Na₂SO₄)** or **< 18 % (MgSO₄)**.

---

### 2.2.6 Flakiness Index & Elongation Index — *IS 2386 (Part 1)*

**Flakiness Index** — fraction (by weight) of particles whose **thickness < 0.6 × mean sieve size**.

**Elongation Index** — fraction whose **length > 1.8 × mean sieve size**.

Both should be **< 35 %** for bituminous mixes (IRC).

---

## 2.3 BITUMEN — Desirable Properties

| Property | Engineering Significance |
|----------|--------------------------|
| **Workability at construction temp.** | Easy mixing and laying |
| **Viscosity at service temp.** | Resists deformation at high temp. |
| **Flexibility at low temp.** | Resists thermal cracking |
| **Adhesion to aggregates** | Prevents stripping |
| **Durability / Aging resistance** | Long service life |
| **Cohesion** | Internal strength of binder |
| **Chemical stability** | No oxidation, no hardening |

---

### 2.3.1 Penetration Test — *IS 1203*

**Principle:** Measures **consistency (hardness)** of bitumen. A standard needle (100 g load) is allowed to penetrate vertically into a bitumen sample for **5 seconds at 25 °C**; penetration depth is measured in **0.1 mm units** (penetration number).

**Procedure:**
1. Soften bitumen at 75–100 °C; pour into a container (55 mm dia, 35 mm deep); cool at 15–30 °C for 1 h, then immerse in water bath at **25 °C for 1 h**.
2. Place container on penetrometer table.
3. Adjust needle to just touch surface; release needle assembly for **5 ± 0.1 s**.
4. Read penetration dial in **0.1 mm units**.

$$\text{Penetration value} = \text{Depth in 0.1 mm units (avg. of 3 readings)}$$

| Grade | Penetration (0.1 mm) | Typical Use |
|-------|----------------------|-------------|
| 30/40 | 30–40 | Heavy-traffic, hot climate |
| 60/70 | 60–70 | Most Indian road conditions |
| 80/100 | 80–100 | Cold climate, low traffic |

---

### 2.3.2 Ductility Test — *IS 1208*

**Principle:** Measures the **distance to which a standard briquette of bitumen (cross-section 1 cm²) can be stretched** before breaking, at **27 °C** and at a rate of **50 mm/min**.

$$\text{Ductility} = \text{Length in cm at which the thread breaks}$$

> KTU/IRC requirement: **Ductility ≥ 75 cm** for paving bitumen.

---

### 2.3.3 Softening Point (Ring & Ball) — *IS 1205*

**Principle:** The **temperature at which the bitumen sample can no longer support the weight of a 3.5 g steel ball** and flows a distance of 25 mm.

**Procedure:**
1. Pour molten bitumen into two **brass rings** (dia 15.88 mm).
2. Place a steel ball (9.5 mm dia, 3.5 g) on each bitumen disc.
3. Suspend in a beaker of water (or glycerol for high-temp grades), 25 mm above the bottom.
4. Heat at a controlled rate of **5 °C per minute**.
5. Record the temperature when the bitumen touches the **lower plate (25 mm below)**.

> **Typical values:** 30/40 grade → 50–60 °C; 60/70 grade → 49–56 °C; 80/100 grade → 45–52 °C.

---

### 2.3.4 Viscosity Test — *IS 1206*

**Principle:** Measures **flow resistance** of bitumen at high temperatures using a **viscometer** (tar viscometer for standard viscosity test at **60 °C**; kinematic for absolute viscosity at **135 °C**).

**Standard Viscosity Test:**
- Efflux time of 50 ml of bitumen through a **10 mm orifice** at **60 °C**.

$$\nu = C \times t \quad \text{(where } C = 0.1 \text{ stokes/s for 10 mm cup)}$$

---

### 2.3.5 Flash & Fire Point — *IS 1209*

- **Flash Point:** Lowest temperature at which bitumen vapour **momentarily ignites** (flash) when a test flame is applied.
- **Fire Point:** Temperature at which the bitumen **sustains combustion for ≥ 5 s**.

> IRC: **Flash point ≥ 220 °C** (safe-handling criterion).

---

### 2.3.6 Specific Gravity of Bitumen — *IS 1202*

**Pycnometer method:**

$$\boxed{G = \frac{W_4 - W_1}{(W_2 - W_1) - (W_3 - W_4)}}$$

Where: $W_1$ = empty pycnometer, $W_2$ = pycnometer + water, $W_3$ = pycnometer + water + bitumen, $W_4$ = pycnometer + bitumen.

> Typical value: **0.97 – 1.02** (bitumen is lighter than water → explains its tendency to flow on water surfaces).

---

## 2.4 Consolidated KTU Formula Sheet (Print-Ready)

| # | Test | Formula | Unit | Standard |
|---|------|---------|------|----------|
| 1 | CBR | $CBR = (P_t / P_s) \times 100$ | % | IS 2720 Pt 16 |
| 2 | k-value | $k = p / \delta$ | kN/m³ | IS 1888 |
| 3 | DCP–CBR | $CBR = 292 / DCPI^{1.12}$ | % | IS 2720 Pt 26 |
| 4 | AIV | $(W_2 / W_1) \times 100$ | % | IS 2386 Pt 4 |
| 5 | ACV | $(W_2 / W_1) \times 100$ | % | IS 2386 Pt 4 |
| 6 | LAA | $(W_2 / W_1) \times 100$ | % | IS 2386 Pt 4 |
| 7 | Water Absorption | $((W_1 - W_4) / W_4) \times 100$ | % | IS 2386 Pt 3 |
| 8 | Specific Gravity (Bulk) | $W_1 / (W_1 - (W_2 - W_3))$ | – | IS 2386 Pt 3 |
| 9 | Penetration | Dial reading (3 avg.) | 0.1 mm | IS 1203 |
| 10 | Softening Point | Recorded directly | °C | IS 1205 |
| 11 | Ductility | Length at break | cm | IS 1208 |
| 12 | Viscosity | $C \times t$ | stokes | IS 1206 |
| 13 | Bitumen Sp. Gravity | $(W_4 - W_1)/((W_2 - W_1) - (W_3 - W_4))$ | – | IS 1202 |
| 14 | Flash / Fire Point | Direct thermometer reading | °C | IS 1209 |
| 15 | Soundness Loss | $(W_1 - W_2) / W_1 \times 100$ | % | IS 2386 Pt 5 |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Sector | Application |
|--------|-------------|
| **National Highways (NHAI)** | Material acceptance / rejection at site; subgrade CBR governs flexible pavement thickness as per IRC:37-2018. |
| **Airport Runways (AAI)** | Higher CBR and stricter LAA limits due to jet-fuel spillage and high wheel loads. |
| **Urban Roads (Smart City Mission)** | Modified bitumen (PMB, CRMB) tested via penetration, softening, and viscosity for durability. |
| **Earthquake-Prone Zones (IS 1893)** | Plate load / CBR correlate with liquefaction potential of subgrade. |
| **Quality Control Labs (NABL-accredited)** | Routine testing per MoRTH Specifications for Road & Bridge Works (5th Revision). |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation — CBR Numericals (KTU Most Repeated)

> **Question:** A CBR test on a soaked subgrade soil sample gave the following loads at the standard 1.25 mm/min penetration rate. Compute the CBR and comment on subgrade suitability for a rural road.

| Penetration (mm) | 0.5 | 1.0 | 1.5 | 2.0 | 2.5 | 3.0 | 4.0 | 5.0 |
|------------------|-----|-----|-----|-----|------|-----|-----|------|
| Load (kg)        | 35  | 68  | 105 | 145 | 180  | 210 | 260 | 310  |

**Standard Reference Loads (IS 2720 Part 16):**

| Penetration (mm) | Standard Load (kg) |
|------------------|--------------------|
| 2.5 | 1370 |
| 5.0 | 2055 |

**Step 1: Identify the test loads at the reference penetrations.**

From the data table:
- Test load at 2.5 mm = **180 kg**
- Test load at 5.0 mm = **310 kg**

**Step 2: Compute CBR at 2.5 mm penetration.**

$$
\text{CBR}_{2.5} = \frac{\text{Test load at 2.5 mm}}{\text{Standard load at 2.5 mm}} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{CBR}_{2.5} = \frac{180}{1370} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{CBR}_{2.5} = 0.1314 \times 100
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{CBR}_{2.5} = 13.14 \,\%}
$$

**Step 3: Compute CBR at 5.0 mm penetration.**

$$
\text{CBR}_{5.0} = \frac{310}{2055} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{CBR}_{5.0} = 0.1509 \times 100
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{CBR}_{5.0} = 15.09 \,\%}
$$

**Step 4: Select the design CBR.**

Per IS 2720 Part 16, the design CBR = **higher of the two values, provided 5.0 mm is at least 1.0 times the 2.5 mm value (consistency check).**

Consistency ratio = $15.09 / 13.14 = 1.148 > 1.0$ → **5.0 mm value is acceptable.**

$$
\boxed{\text{Design CBR} = 15.09 \,\% \text{ (typically rounded to 15 \%)}}
$$

**Step 5: Interpretation for a Rural Road.**

> IRC:SP:72-2015 and IRC:37-2018 classify subgrade CBR. For rural roads, **CBR ≥ 8 %** is the minimum recommended. Since 15 % > 8 %, the subgrade is **suitable** with the sub-base and base course thicknesses computed from IRC charts.

**Step 6: Determination of k-value (Cross-Verification).**

For a flexible pavement, the subgrade modulus of subgrade reaction is empirically related to CBR:

$$
k \text{ (psi/in)} = \frac{1500 \times \text{CBR}}{30} \quad \text{(Heukelom & Klomp)}
$$

$$
k = \frac{1500 \times 15}{30} = 750 \text{ psi/in}
$$

Converting to SI:

$$
k = 750 \times 271.45 = 2.04 \times 10^5 \text{ kN/m}^3
$$

$$
\boxed{k \approx 2.04 \times 10^5 \text{ kN/m}^3}
$$

---

## 3.2 Detailed Derivation — Aggregate Impact Value (AIV)

> **Question:** In an AIV test, the weight of aggregate sample = 350 g. After 15 hammer blows, the fraction passing 2.36 mm weighs 87.5 g. Compute AIV and judge its suitability for a bituminous wearing course.

**Step 1: Identify parameters.**

$$
W_1 = 350 \text{ g (original)}, \quad W_2 = 87.5 \text{ g (fines passing 2.36 mm)}
$$

**Step 2: Apply the AIV formula.**

$$
\text{AIV (\%)} = \frac{W_2}{W_1} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{AIV (\%)} = \frac{87.5}{350} \times 100
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{AIV} = 25 \,\%}
$$

**Step 3: Interpretation.**

> IRC recommends AIV ≤ 30 % for wearing course. Since **25 % < 30 %**, the aggregate is **acceptable for use in a bituminous wearing course**.

**Step 4: Cross-check with ACV / LAA expectations (typical).**

| Test | Typical Value (same aggregate) |
|------|-------------------------------|
| AIV | 25 % |
| ACV | ~22–27 % (ACV is generally slightly lower than AIV) |
| LAA | ~28–32 % (higher than AIV due to abrasion component) |

---

## 3.3 Detailed Derivation — Specific Gravity & Water Absorption of Aggregate

> **Question:** Determine bulk specific gravity, apparent specific gravity, and water absorption of an aggregate sample from the following data:
> $W_1 = 980$ g (SSD), $W_2 = 1850$ g (pycnometer + aggregate + water), $W_3 = 1485$ g (pycnometer + water), $W_4 = 965$ g (oven-dry).

**Step 1: Bulk Specific Gravity (SSD basis).**

$$
G_{\text{bulk, SSD}} = \frac{W_1}{W_1 - (W_2 - W_3)}
$$

$$
G_{\text{bulk, SSD}} = \frac{980}{980 - (1850 - 1485)}
$$

$$
G_{\text{bulk, SSD}} = \frac{980}{980 - 365} = \frac{980}{615}
$$

$$
\boxed{G_{\text{bulk, SSD}} = 1.594}
$$

**Step 2: Apparent Specific Gravity.**

$$
G_{\text{apparent}} = \frac{W_4}{W_4 - (W_2 - W_3)}
$$

$$
G_{\text{apparent}} = \frac{965}{965 - 365} = \frac{965}{600}
$$

$$
\boxed{G_{\text{apparent}} = 1.608}
$$

**Step 3: Water Absorption.**

$$
\text{WA (\%)} = \frac{W_1 - W_4}{W_4} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{WA (\%)} = \frac{980 - 965}{965} \times 100 = \frac{15}{965} \times 100
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{WA} = 1.55 \,\%}
$$

**Step 4: Comment on Aggregate Quality.**

> Bulk specific gravity < 2.6 is **abnormally low** (lightweight aggregate). Water absorption 1.55 % is **acceptable** (< 2 % per IRC for bituminous mixes), but the low specific gravity suggests a **porous or weak rock** — recommend further testing (ACV, LAA, soundness) before acceptance.

---

## 3.4 Detailed Derivation — Penetration Test Result Classification

> **Question:** A penetration test on a bitumen sample gave dial readings: 72, 75, 74 (0.1 mm). Classify the binder grade.

**Step 1: Average the three readings.**

$$
P_{\text{avg}} = \frac{72 + 75 + 74}{3} = \frac{221}{3}
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{\text{avg}} = 73.67 \text{ (in 0.1 mm)}}
$$

**Step 2: Round to nearest integer (as per IS 1203).**

$$
\boxed{P \approx 74 \text{ (in 0.1 mm)}}
$$

**Step 3: Classification.**

> A penetration of 74 falls within **60–100** range → **80/100 grade bitumen** — suitable for **cold climates and low-to-moderate traffic roads**.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Quick Decision Tree for KTU Viva

| Observation | Likely Test | Acceptance Criterion | Action if Failed |
|-------------|-------------|----------------------|------------------|
| Pavement shows deep ruts in hot climate | Softening Point of binder | ≥ 47 °C | Use modified bitumen (PMB/CRMB) |
| Aggregate crumbles under tamping | ACV / AIV | ≤ 30 / 35 % | Reject or blend with harder aggregate |
| Bitumen coating peels off in rain | Stripping / Coating Test | ≥ 95 % coating | Add anti-stripping agent (e.g., 1–2 % lime) |
| Subgrade pumps water under load | CBR (soaked) | ≥ 8 % | Stabilize with lime/cement or replace |
| Surface polishing causes skidding | Polished Stone Value | ≥ 55 | Use crushed, angular aggregate with rough texture |

---

## 3.6 Empirically Derived Relationships (Bonus)

> **Penetration Index (PI)** — Pfeiffer & Van Doormaal equation (used to predict temperature susceptibility):

$$
PI = \frac{20(1 - 25A)}{1 + 50A}
$$

Where:

$$
A = \frac{\log 800 - \log P}{T - 25}
$$

- $P$ = penetration at 25 °C
- $T$ = softening point (°C)

> **Interpretation:**
- PI < –2 → highly temperature susceptible (e.g., coal tar)
- PI between –2 and +2 → paving bitumen
- PI > +2 → low susceptibility (e.g., oxidized bitumen)
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**"List any six desirable properties of road aggregates. Why is the Los Angeles Abrasion (LAA) value preferred over the Aggregate Impact Value (AIV) for acceptance of aggregates in modern highway construction?"**

**Model Answer:**

**Desirable Properties of Road Aggregates:**

1. **Strength** — resistance to crushing under wheel load (governed by ACV).
2. **Hardness & Toughness** — resistance to impact and wear (governed by AIV/LAA).
3. **Durability** — resistance to weathering and freeze-thaw (soundness test).
4. **Shape** — angular/cubical particles with low flakiness and elongation indices for good interlock.
5. **Surface Texture** — rough texture for high skid resistance and strong bitumen-aggregate bond.
6. **Cleanliness** — absence of dust, clay, or organic matter.
7. **Specific Gravity > 2.6** — indicates sound, dense rock.
8. **Low Water Absorption (< 2 %)** — indicates low porosity.
9. **Bitumen Affinity (Hydrophobicity)** — resistance to stripping.
10. **Chemical Stability** — absence of deleterious minerals (e.g., reactive silica).

**Why LAA > AIV:**
- LAA simulates **both impact AND abrasion (attrition)**, closely mimicking real field conditions (vehicle tyres + debris).
- AIV measures **only impact resistance**; it does not capture the **scratching/abrasion** caused by skidding tyres.
- LAA is therefore more **representative of surface course aggregates** subjected to combined wear mechanisms.
- IS 383 and MoRTH specifications now mandate LAA as the **primary acceptance criterion** for bituminous surfacing.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024]**
**"Explain the principle of the California Bearing Ratio (CBR) test. What is the significance of soaking the specimen for 96 hours?"**

**Model Answer:**

**Principle:** The CBR test measures the **shear strength of a compacted subgrade soil** under controlled penetration. A standard cylindrical piston (49.65 mm dia, area 1935 mm²) penetrates the soil at a steady rate of **1.25 mm/min**, and the **load required** at standard penetrations (2.5 mm and 5.0 mm) is compared with that for a **standard crushed-stone reference material** (1370 kg at 2.5 mm; 2055 kg at 5.0 mm). The ratio (expressed as a percentage) is the CBR.

**Significance of 96-hour Soaking:**
1. **Simulates the worst moisture condition** the subgrade is likely to experience in the field (e.g., during monsoon).
2. Allows the soil to **swell fully** — the swell percentage is a direct measure of **shrink-swell potential**.
3. Provides a **conservative (safe) design value** of CBR, ensuring pavement does not fail even in saturated conditions.
4. Ensures the **pore-pressure** under load is realistic, replicating long-term field behaviour.

For roads in arid zones, an **unsoaked CBR** is also reported, but for design purposes, **soaked CBR is universally used** per IRC:37-2018.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each)

> **ESE Module 1 Internal Choice (As per KTU 2024 Scheme). Answer ONE option.**

---

### ✦ QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Enumerate the desirable properties of subgrade soil for highway construction. Explain the **California Bearing Ratio (CBR) test** in detail, including the principle, apparatus, procedure, and the formula used. State the minimum CBR values recommended by IRC for different classes of roads. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A CBR test on a soaked subgrade soil gave the following readings:

| Penetration (mm) | 0.5 | 1.0 | 1.5 | 2.0 | 2.5 | 3.0 | 4.0 | 5.0 | 7.5 | 10.0 |
|------------------|-----|-----|-----|-----|------|-----|-----|------|-----|------|
| Load (kg)        | 40  | 80  | 120 | 165 | 210  | 245 | 290 | 330  | 400 | 460  |

**Compute the design CBR. Comment on its suitability for (i) a rural road, (ii) an NH/SH, and (iii) determine the equivalent subgrade modulus of subgrade reaction (k) using the Heukelom & Klomp correlation.** **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):**

**Desirable Properties of Subgrade Soil:**
1. **Stability** under repeated traffic loading.
2. **Adequate bearing capacity (CBR ≥ 5 %–8 %)** to support the pavement.
3. **Low compressibility** — minimises differential settlement.
4. **Good drainage characteristics** — prevents pore-pressure build-up.
5. **Low shrink-swell potential** — avoids seasonal cracking.
6. **Ease of compaction** — facilitates construction quality.
7. **Resistance to weathering and erosion.**
8. **Uniformity** — predictable behaviour across the alignment.

**CBR Test — IS 2720 (Part 16):**

- **Apparatus:** CBR mould (150 × 175 mm), spacer disc, 4.9 kg rammer, 450 mm drop height, surcharge weights, penetration piston (49.65 mm dia), loading machine (capacity 50 kN), dial gauges.
- **Principle:** Compare the penetration resistance of the soil with a standard crushed-stone reference.
- **Procedure:** Already detailed in Section 2.1.1.
- **Formula:**

$$
\text{CBR (\%)} = \frac{P_t}{P_s} \times 100
$$

**Minimum CBR Values (IRC:37-2018):**

| Road Class | Minimum Subgrade CBR |
|------------|----------------------|
| NH / SH | 8 % |
| MDR / ODR | 5 %–6 % |
| Village Road (Rural) | 3 %–5 % |
| Expressway | 10 % |

---

**Part (b):**

**Step 1:** Identify test loads at standard penetrations:
- At 2.5 mm: $P_t = 210$ kg; standard $P_s = 1370$ kg
- At 5.0 mm: $P_t = 330$ kg; standard $P_s = 2055$ kg

**Step 2:** Compute CBR at 2.5 mm:

$$
\text{CBR}_{2.5} = \frac{210}{1370} \times 100 = 15.33 \,\%
$$

**Step 3:** Compute CBR at 5.0 mm:

$$
\text{CBR}_{5.0} = \frac{330}{2055} \times 100 = 16.06 \,\%
$$

**Step 4:** Consistency check: $16.06 / 15.33 = 1.048 > 1.0$ → **5.0 mm is consistent and acceptable.**

**Step 5: Design CBR = 16.06 % ≈ 16 %**

**Step 6: Suitability:**
- (i) Rural road (CBR ≥ 3–5 %): **Suitable** ✓
- (ii) NH/SH (CBR ≥ 8 %): **Suitable** ✓
- The pavement thickness required will be **lower** due to the high CBR — economical design.

**Step 7: Modulus of Subgrade Reaction (k) using Heukelom & Klomp:**

$$
k \text{ (psi/in)} = \frac{1500 \times \text{CBR}}{30} = \frac{1500 \times 16}{30} = 800 \text{ psi/in}
$$

Converting: $1 \text{ psi/in} = 271.45 \text{ kN/m}^3$

$$
k = 800 \times 271.45 = 2.17 \times 10^5 \text{ kN/m}^3
$$

$$
\boxed{k \approx 2.17 \times 10^5 \text{ kN/m}^3}
$$

---

### ✦ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** List the desirable properties of bitumen used in road construction. Describe the **Penetration Test** (IS 1203) and **Softening Point (Ring & Ball) Test** (IS 1205) with neat principles and procedures. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A bitumen sample has:
- Penetration at 25 °C = 65 (0.1 mm)
- Softening point = 52 °C

**Determine the Penetration Index (PI). Comment on the temperature susceptibility of the binder.** A pavement engineer in a hot tropical region (mean annual pavement temperature ≈ 38 °C) is to choose between this bitumen and one with PI = +1.5. Which is more suitable and why? **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):**

**Desirable Properties of Bitumen:**

1. **Workability** at mixing and laying temperatures (150–180 °C).
2. **Viscosity** appropriate for the service temperature — high enough to resist rutting, low enough to avoid brittleness.
3. **Flexibility / Ductility** at low temperatures — prevents thermal cracking.
4. **Adhesion** to aggregates — prevents stripping.
5. **Cohesion** — internal strength of binder.
6. **Aging resistance** — minimal hardening over service life.
7. **Chemical stability** — no oxidation or volatilisation.
8. **Flash point ≥ 220 °C** — safe handling and storage.

**Penetration Test (IS 1203):**
- *Principle:* A standard needle under 100 g load penetrates the bitumen at 25 °C for 5 s; the depth in 0.1 mm units = penetration value.
- *Procedure:* As detailed in Section 2.3.1.

**Softening Point Test (IS 1205):**
- *Principle:* The temperature at which bitumen, supporting a 3.5 g steel ball, flows and touches a plate 25 mm below.
- *Procedure:* As detailed in Section 2.3.3.

**Bitumen Grades & Indian Standards:**

| Property | 30/40 | 60/70 | 80/100 |
|----------|-------|-------|--------|
| Penetration (min) | 30 | 60 | 80 |
| Softening point (°C, min) | 50 | 49 | 45 |
| Ductility (cm, min) | 75 | 75 | 75 |
| Flash point (°C, min) | 220 | 220 | 220 |
| Solubility in CS₂ (min %) | 99 | 99 | 99 |

---

**Part (b):**

**Step 1: Compute the parameter A.**

$$
A = \frac{\log 800 - \log P}{T_{\text{SP}} - 25}
$$

$$
\log 800 = 2.9031, \quad \log 65 = 1.8129
$$

$$
A = \frac{2.9031 - 1.8129}{52 - 25} = \frac{1.0902}{27}
$$

$$
A = 0.04038
$$

**Step 2: Compute PI using Pfeiffer–Van Doormaal equation.**

$$
PI = \frac{20(1 - 25A)}{1 + 50A}
$$

$$
PI = \frac{20(1 - 25 \times 0.04038)}{1 + 50 \times 0.04038}
$$

$$
PI = \frac{20(1 - 1.0095)}{1 + 2.019} = \frac{20 \times (-0.0095)}{3.019}
$$

$$
PI = \frac{-0.190}{3.019} = -0.063
$$

$$
\boxed{PI \approx -0.06}
$$

**Step 3: Comment on Temperature Susceptibility.**

| PI Range | Interpretation |
|----------|----------------|
| < –2 | Highly susceptible (e.g., coal tar) |
| –2 to +2 | Standard paving bitumen (typical range) |
| > +2 | Low susceptibility (e.g., oxidised bitumen, PMB) |

A PI of **–0.06** indicates a **standard paving-grade bitumen with moderate temperature susceptibility** — typical of a **60/70 grade**.

**Step 4: Comparison for Hot Tropical Region (38 °C).**

| Binder | PI | Behaviour at 38 °C | Recommendation |
|--------|-----|--------------------|----------------|
| Test sample (PI = –0.06) | Nearly zero (Newtonian-ish) | More susceptible to rutting/bleeding at 38 °C | Suitable for moderate climates only |
| Alternative (PI = +1.5) | +1.5 | Resists softening at high temperature | **More suitable for hot tropical climate** ✓ |

> **Conclusion:** For a pavement subjected to a mean annual temperature of 38 °C, the binder with **PI = +1.5** is preferred because a higher PI corresponds to **lower temperature susceptibility**, ensuring that the binder retains its stiffness and resists rutting/bleeding in hot conditions. The test sample (PI ≈ 0) would likely bleed under such conditions, causing loss of surface texture and potential skidding issues.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **CBR Numericals:** Students often forget the **consistency check** (5.0/2.5 ratio must be > 1.0) — failing this loses 1 mark. *Always re-test the sample if 5.0 mm value is rejected.*
> 2. **AIV vs ACV Confusion:** AIV uses *impact* (15 hammer blows); ACV uses *compression* (400 kN over 10 min). Mixing these up = 2-mark loss.
> 3. **Penetration units:** Penetration is in **0.1 mm** (dial reading) — not mm. Reporting "65 mm" instead of "65 (0.1 mm) = 6.5 mm" is a frequent mistake.
> 4. **Specific Gravity of Bitumen:** Formula denominator is $(W_2 - W_1) - (W_3 - W_4)$, not $(W_3 - W_4) - (W_2 - W_1)$ — sign errors are very common.
> 5. **Softening Point Heating Rate:** Must be **5 °C/min** — not 10 °C/min. Too-fast heating gives a **falsely high** softening point.
> 6. **LAA Sample Mass:** For each grading (A, B, C, D), the sample mass and number of spheres differ. Mixing Grading A and C data = 1-mark loss.
> 7. **PI Calculation:** Many students use **A = 30** instead of **25** in the denominator, or forget the negative sign in the numerator — both lead to a **completely wrong PI value**.
> 8. **Plate Load Test k-value Units:** Always state whether the answer is in **kg/cm³ or kN/m³** — examiners are strict about units (1-mark deduction otherwise).

---

## ✦ TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

### A. Subgrade Soil
- [ ] Desirable properties: stability, drainage, low compressibility, low swell-shrink, ease of compaction.
- [ ] **CBR** = (Test Load / Standard Load) × 100; standard loads = 1370 kg @ 2.5 mm; 2055 kg @ 5.0 mm.
- [ ] **Plate Load Test** yields k = p / δ (kN/m³).
- [ ] **DCP-CBR** = 292 / DCPI^1.12.
- [ ] **Soaking for 96 h** simulates worst moisture field condition.
- [ ] **k-CBR correlation** (Heukelom & Klomp): k (psi/in) = 1500 × CBR / 30.

### B. Road Aggregates
- [ ] Desirable properties: strength, hardness, durability, shape, surface texture, clean, specific gravity > 2.6, water absorption < 2 %.
- [ ] **AIV (IS 2386 Pt 4):** impact, 15 hammer blows → ≤ 30 % for surfacing.
- [ ] **ACV (IS 2386 Pt 4):** compression, 400 kN in 10 min → ≤ 30 % for surfacing.
- [ ] **LAA (IS 2386 Pt 4):** combined impact + abrasion, 500 rev with steel balls → ≤ 30 % for surfacing.
- [ ] **Soundness (IS 2386 Pt 5):** 5 cycles of Na₂SO₄/MgSO₄ → loss < 12 % / 18 %.
- [ ] **Shape Indices (IS 2386 Pt 1):** FI and EI < 35 % for bituminous mixes.
- [ ] **Specific Gravity & Water Absorption** by pycnometer.

### C. Bitumen
- [ ] Desirable properties: workability, viscosity, ductility, adhesion, aging resistance, high flash point (≥ 220 °C).
- [ ] **Penetration (IS 1203):** 100 g, 5 s, 25 °C; depth in 0.1 mm.
- [ ] **Ductility (IS 1208):** 27 °C, 50 mm/min; distance in cm; ≥ 75 cm.
- [ ] **Softening Point (IS 1205):** Ring & Ball; heating 5 °C/min; temperature at 25 mm flow.
- [ ] **Viscosity (IS 1206):** 50 ml efflux at 60 °C through 10 mm orifice.
- [ ] **Flash/Fire Point (IS 1209):** safety criterion, ≥ 220 °C.
- [ ] **Specific Gravity of Bitumen:** 0.97–1.02; pycnometer formula.
- [ ] **Penetration Index (PI):** $PI = 20(1 - 25A) / (1 + 50A)$; $A = (\log 800 - \log P) / (T - 25)$.
- [ ] **Bitumen Grades:** 30/40 (hot regions/heavy traffic), 60/70 (most of India), 80/100 (cold regions).

### D. Key Code References (must remember)
- [ ] IS 2720 (Soil tests), IS 2386 (Aggregate tests), IS 1201–1220 (Bitumen tests).
- [ ] IRC:37-2018 (Flexible pavement design), MoRTH Specifications (5th Revision) for road & bridge works.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Materials for Durable Pavements - Artificial Aggregates
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET746 - HIGHWAY MATERIALS AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Materials for Durable Pavements - Artificial Aggregates

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Artificial Aggregates** (also termed as *manufactured* or *synthetic aggregates*) are granular inorganic materials derived through industrial processing of raw minerals, industrial by-products, or waste streams, specifically engineered to substitute or supplement natural mineral aggregates in pavement construction. According to **IS 9142: Part 1 (2018)** and **IS 383 (2016)**, artificial aggregates encompass materials produced by thermal, mechanical, or chemical transformation of parent substances such as clay, shale, fly ash, blast-furnace slag, steel slag, or recycled concrete debris.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PECET746, Module 2):**
> Artificial aggregates are defined as aggregates obtained by processing natural materials, industrial wastes, or by-products through heat treatment, crushing, or expansion, possessing specific engineering properties tailored for high-performance, durable pavement applications.

### 1.2 Classification of Artificial Aggregates (KTU Reference)

| **Category** | **Examples** | **Primary Use** |
|:------------|:-------------|:----------------|
| **Lightweight Aggregates (LWA)** | Expanded clay, foamed slag, sintered fly ash (Lytag), pumice | Lightweight pavement sub-base, insulating layers |
| **Slag Aggregates** | Blast furnace slag, steel slag (BOF/EAF), copper slag | High-friction surface courses, base courses |
| **Recycled Aggregates (RCA)** | Recycled concrete aggregate (RCA), reclaimed asphalt pavement (RAP) | Sustainable pavement construction |
| **Manufactured Sand (M-Sand)** | Crushed rock sand, stone dust | Fine aggregate replacement in pavement concrete |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Intuition Builder

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Engineered Lego Brick" Analogy:**
> Imagine natural aggregates as **naturally shaped river pebbles** (smooth, rounded, variable quality). In contrast, **artificial aggregates are like manufactured Lego bricks** — they are *purpose-built*. The industrial process controls their shape, density, strength, and absorption characteristics, much like how a Lego factory standardizes each brick to fit perfectly into the final structure. For pavement engineering, this means **predictable performance** under heavy traffic and environmental stress, just as Lego structures can be engineered to bear specific loads.

### 1.4 Why Artificial Aggregates Matter in Modern Pavement Engineering

- **Resource Depletion:** Natural aggregate reserves in Kerala (granite, laterite) are being exhausted at an unsustainable rate.
- **Environmental Mandate:** The Government of India mandates use of **15–20% industrial by-products** in road construction (MoRTH specifications).
- **Performance Enhancement:** Artificial aggregates can be engineered for superior skid resistance, lower thermal conductivity, and higher durability.
- **Waste Utilization:** Converts hazardous industrial waste (slag, fly ash) into valuable construction material.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Metric to Remember:** Approximately **70 million tonnes** of blast-furnace slag and **120 million tonnes** of fly ash are generated annually in India, presenting a massive resource pool for artificial aggregate production.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Framework

The engineering behaviour of artificial aggregates is governed by four fundamental pillars:

1. **Parent Material Chemistry** — Determines mineralogy and reactivity.
2. **Manufacturing Process** — Controls internal porosity and surface texture.
3. **Microstructure** — Governs strength and water absorption.
4. **Geometric Properties** — Shape, angularity, flakiness index dictate pavement performance.

### 2.2 Detailed Properties of Artificial Aggregates

#### 2.2.1 Physical Properties

- **Specific Gravity (G_s):** Artificial lightweight aggregates typically have G_s in the range **0.5 to 1.8**, while slag aggregates range from **2.0 to 2.8**.
- **Water Absorption:** Lightweight aggregates absorb **5–25%** by mass, compared to **0.5–2%** for natural aggregates.
- **Bulk Density:** LWA exhibits bulk density between **300–1100 kg/m³**; normal-weight artificial aggregates range **1500–1800 kg/m³**.
- **Porosity:** Internal pores range from **20% to 60%** in lightweight aggregates.

#### 2.2.2 Mechanical Properties

- **Aggregate Crushing Value (ACV):** Should be **≤ 30%** for road surfaces, **≤ 45%** for base courses (IS 2386 Part IV).
- **Aggregate Impact Value (AIV):** Should be **≤ 30%** for wearing course, **≤ 35%** for bituminous macadam.
- **Los Angeles Abrasion Value:** Should be **≤ 30%** for dense bituminous macadam, **≤ 35%** for water-bound macadam.
- **Polished Stone Value (PSV):** Slag aggregates offer superior PSV (≥ 60), ideal for skid-resistant surfaces.

#### 2.2.3 Durability Properties

- **Soundness Test (Na₂SO₄/MgSO₄):** Loss should be **≤ 12%** after 5 cycles (IS 2386 Part V).
- **Freeze-Thaw Resistance:** Critical for artificial LWA used in cold-region pavements.
- **Sulfate Resistance:** Particularly relevant for slag aggregates (delayed expansion risk).

### 2.3 Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Application Highlights:**
> - **Lytag (sintered fly ash)** has been used in the M50-Mumbai-Pune Expressway lightweight bridge decks.
> - **Steel slag aggregates** are now mandated on high-speed corridor wearing courses in India.
> - **Recycled concrete aggregates (RCA)** are integral to the **Smart City pavement renewal projects** in Kerala.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Artificial Aggregates

| **Property** | **Formula** | **Units** | **Typical Threshold (KTU/IS)** |
|:-------------|:------------|:----------|:-------------------------------|
| Specific Gravity | $G_s = \dfrac{W_s}{W_s - W_w}$ | Dimensionless | 0.5 – 2.8 (varies by type) |
| Water Absorption | $WA(\%) = \dfrac{W_{sat} - W_{od}}{W_{od}} \times 100$ | % | ≤ 10% (slag), ≤ 25% (LWA) |
| Aggregate Crushing Value | $ACV(\%) = \dfrac{W_{crushed}}{W_{total}} \times 100$ | % | ≤ 30% (surface course) |
| Aggregate Impact Value | $AIV(\%) = \dfrac{W_{fines\,passed\,2.36\,mm}}{W_{total}} \times 100$ | % | ≤ 30% (wearing course) |
| Los Angeles Abrasion | $LA(\%) = \dfrac{W_{loss}}{W_{total}} \times 100$ | % | ≤ 30% (DBM), ≤ 35% (WBM) |
| Bulk Density | $\gamma_{bulk} = \dfrac{M_{oven\,dry}}{V_{container}}$ | kg/m³ | 300 – 1800 (varies) |
| Porosity | $n(\%) = \left(1 - \dfrac{\gamma_d}{G_s \cdot \gamma_w}\right) \times 100$ | % | 20% – 60% (LWA) |
| Voids Ratio | $e = \dfrac{n}{1-n}$ | Dimensionless | < 0.8 (compacted) |
| Angularity Number | $A = 67 - \dfrac{100 \cdot W}{\gamma \cdot V_{c}}$ | 0–11 | ≥ 6 (bituminous mix) |
| Flakiness Index | $FI(\%) = \dfrac{W_{flakes}}{W_{total}} \times 100$ | % | ≤ 25% (surface) |
| Elongation Index | $EI(\%) = \dfrac{W_{elongated}}{W_{total}} \times 100$ | % | ≤ 15% (surface) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Notation Pitfall:** In the table above, $\vert x \vert$ notation is **not used**. Always enclose absolute values or modulus in LaTeX as $\vert x \vert$ or $\mid x \mid$ to avoid breaking markdown table pipes.

### 2.5 Conceptual Explanation of Why These Properties Matter

- **Specific Gravity $\downarrow$** → Lighter pavement → reduced dead load on bridges/culverts.
- **Water Absorption $\uparrow$** → Pre-saturate LWA before mixing to prevent absorption of mix water (critical step in construction).
- **ACV/AIV $\downarrow$** → Higher resistance to crushing under traffic loading.
- **LA Abrasion $\downarrow$** → Better resistance to tyre wear and polishing.
- **Porosity $\uparrow$** → Better thermal insulation but lower strength; trade-off engineering.

## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Specific Gravity of Artificial Lightweight Aggregates

#### Statement
Derive the relationship between oven-dry specific gravity $G_{od}$, saturated surface-dry specific gravity $G_{ssd}$, and apparent specific gravity $G_{app}$ for porous artificial aggregates.

#### Step-by-Step Derivation

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Let: } W_{od} = \text{Oven-dry mass of aggregate (24 hrs at } 100-110°C) \\
&W_{ssd} = \text{Saturated surface-dry mass} \\
&W_{sub} = \text{Submerged mass in water} \\
&\rho_w = \text{Density of water at test temperature} \\
&V = \text{Total volume (including impermeable + permeable pores)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1:** Volume of solid plus impermeable pores (effective volume):

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{eff} = \dfrac{W_{ssd} - W_{sub}}{\rho_w}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Oven-dry specific gravity:

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_{od} = \dfrac{W_{od}}{W_{ssd} - W_{sub}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Saturated surface-dry specific gravity:

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_{ssd} = \dfrac{W_{ssd}}{W_{ssd} - W_{sub}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Apparent specific gravity (excluding permeable voids):

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{apparent} = \dfrac{W_{od} - W_{sub}}{\rho_w} \\
G_{app} = \dfrac{W_{od}}{W_{od} - W_{sub}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Relationship between all three:

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_{app} > G_{ssd} > G_{od}
\end{aligned}
$$

This ordering is fundamental for **lightweight artificial aggregates** because of their high internal porosity.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Porosity from Density Relationship

#### Statement
For a compacted artificial aggregate mass, derive the porosity (n) using dry density $\gamma_d$, specific gravity $G_s$, and density of water $\gamma_w$.

#### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** The void ratio is defined as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
e = \dfrac{V_v}{V_s}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $V_v$ = volume of voids, $V_s$ = volume of solids.

**Step 2:** Dry density in terms of void ratio:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\gamma_d = \dfrac{G_s \cdot \gamma_w}{1 + e}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Solving for void ratio:

$$
\begin{aligned}
1 + e = \dfrac{G_s \cdot \gamma_w}{\gamma_d} \\
e = \dfrac{G_s \cdot \gamma_w}{\gamma_d} - 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Converting void ratio to porosity:

$$
\begin{aligned}
n = \dfrac{e}{1 + e}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Substituting the expression:

$$
\begin{aligned}
n = \dfrac{\dfrac{G_s \cdot \gamma_w}{\gamma_d} - 1}{\dfrac{G_s \cdot \gamma_w}{\gamma_d}} = 1 - \dfrac{\gamma_d}{G_s \cdot \gamma_w}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n(\%) = \left(1 - \dfrac{\gamma_d}{G_s \cdot \gamma_w}\right) \times 100
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Aggregate Crushing Value of Slag Aggregate

#### Problem
A sample of **electric arc furnace (EAF) steel slag** aggregate weighing **2500 g** is subjected to the Aggregate Crushing Value test. After loading to 400 kN over 10 minutes, the crushed material is sieved through a 2.36 mm IS sieve. The mass retained on the sieve is **2075 g**. Determine the ACV and comment on its suitability for a **bituminous wearing course**.

#### Solution

**Given Data:**
- Total mass of aggregate sample: $W_{total} = 2500$ g
- Mass retained on 2.36 mm sieve (uncrushed portion): $W_{retained} = 2075$ g
- Mass passing 2.36 mm sieve (crushed portion): $W_{crushed} = W_{total} - W_{retained}$

**Step 1:** Calculate the mass of crushed material (fines):

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{crushed} = W_{total} - W_{retained} = 2500 - 2075 = 425 \text{ g}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the ACV formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
ACV(\%) = \dfrac{W_{crushed}}{W_{total}} \times 100
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute values:

$$
\begin{aligned}
ACV(\%) = \dfrac{425}{2500} \times 100 = 17.0\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Interpret the result:

According to **IS 2386 Part IV** and **MoRTH Section 500**:
- For bituminous wearing course: ACV ≤ 24% (preferred ≤ 20%)
- For bituminous base course: ACV ≤ 30%

Since the obtained ACV of **17.0%** is well below the threshold of **24%**, the EAF steel slag is **highly suitable** for use in bituminous wearing course.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Artificial Aggregate Types

| **Property** | **LWA (Expanded Clay)** | **Steel Slag** | **Blast Furnace Slag** | **Recycled Concrete (RCA)** | **Fly Ash Aggregate (Lytag)** |
|:-------------|:-----------------------:|:--------------:|:----------------------:|:---------------------------:|:-----------------------------:|
| Specific Gravity | 0.5 – 1.5 | 2.6 – 2.8 | 2.0 – 2.4 | 2.0 – 2.4 | 1.3 – 1.7 |
| Water Absorption (%) | 10 – 25 | 1 – 3 | 3 – 8 | 4 – 10 | 12 – 20 |
| ACV (%) | 25 – 40 | 15 – 22 | 20 – 30 | 22 – 35 | 25 – 35 |
| LA Abrasion (%) | 30 – 45 | 12 – 22 | 25 – 35 | 25 – 40 | 28 – 40 |
| Bulk Density (kg/m³) | 400 – 900 | 1600 – 1800 | 1200 – 1400 | 1300 – 1500 | 800 – 1100 |
| Skid Resistance (PSV) | 45 – 55 | 60 – 70 | 55 – 65 | 50 – 58 | 48 – 55 |
| Angularity | High | Very High | Moderate–High | Moderate | Moderate |
| Soundness (%) | ≤ 10 | ≤ 5 | ≤ 8 | ≤ 10 | ≤ 10 |
| Cost (₹/m³) | High | Low–Medium | Low | Low | Medium |
| Carbon Footprint | Medium | Negative* | Negative* | Low | Negative* |

> *Negative carbon footprint means the material sequesters/utilizes CO₂ during curing/hydration.

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Table:** Steel slag has the best mechanical performance; LWA has the lowest weight; RCA offers sustainability; Lytag is the best thermal insulator.

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

Define artificial aggregates. List any **four** types of artificial aggregates used in pavement construction, with one specific application of each.

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Bloom Level: Remember/Understand):**

Artificial aggregates are granular materials produced by **thermal, mechanical, or chemical processing** of natural minerals, industrial by-products (such as slag, fly ash), or recycled construction waste. They are engineered to replace or enhance natural aggregates in pavement layers.

| **Type** | **Application** |
|:---------|:----------------|
| Expanded clay/shale (LWA) | Lightweight sub-base on bridge approaches |
| Steel slag (BOF/EAF) | High-friction bituminous wearing course |
| Blast furnace slag | Bituminous base course |
| Recycled concrete aggregate (RCA) | Lower base/sub-base in low-volume roads |
| Sintered fly ash (Lytag) | Insulating layer in rigid pavement |

*(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for listing four types with applications)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

Distinguish between **natural aggregate** and **artificial aggregate** based on **three** key properties. State **two** Indian Standards relevant to artificial aggregates.

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Bloom Level: Understand/Analyze):**

| **Property** | **Natural Aggregate** | **Artificial Aggregate** |
|:-------------|:----------------------|:--------------------------|
| **Source** | Riverbeds, quarries, pits | Industrial processing of minerals/waste |
| **Specific Gravity** | Typically 2.6 – 2.8 | Varies widely (0.5 – 2.8) |
| **Water Absorption** | Low (0.5 – 2%) | High for LWA (5 – 25%) |
| **Shape Control** | Limited (round/crushed) | Engineered shape/texture possible |
| **Sustainability** | Depleting natural reserves | Utilizes industrial waste |

**Relevant IS Codes:**
- **IS 9142 (Part 1 & 2):** Specification for artificial lightweight aggregates.
- **IS 383:2016** — Coarse and fine aggregates for concrete (includes manufactured sand provisions).

*(1 mark for distinguishing table + 1 mark for IS codes + 1 mark for proper terminology)*
