### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Module 2]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain in detail the **manufacturing process**, **properties**, and **pavement applications** of **sintered fly ash aggregate (Lytag)**. Discuss its advantages over natural aggregates in pavement engineering.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A sample of **expanded clay lightweight aggregate** has the following test data:
- Oven-dry mass: $W_{od} = 480$ g
- Saturated surface-dry mass: $W_{ssd} = 560$ g
- Submerged mass in water: $W_{sub} = 240$ g
- Density of water: $\rho_w = 1000$ kg/m³

Calculate: **(i)** Oven-dry specific gravity, **(ii)** Saturated surface-dry specific gravity, **(iii)** Apparent specific gravity, and **(iv)** Water absorption percentage. Comment on the suitability of this aggregate for **lightweight pavement sub-base**.

#### Model Solution

##### Part (a) — Sintered Fly Ash Aggregate (Lytag) [7 Marks]

**Manufacturing Process [3 Marks]:**
1. **Raw material selection:** Pulverized fly ash from thermal power plants (Class F as per ASTM C618).
2. **Pre-processing:** Sizing, removal of unburnt carbon, and storage in silos.
3. **Mixing:** Blending with **5–10% bentonite or clay** as binder, plus water for green strength.
4. **Pelletization:** Feeding mix onto inclined rotating disc (4–6 m diameter, 45–55° tilt) to form 4–16 mm green pellets.
5. **Sintering:** Heating pellets to **1100–1300°C** in a travelling grate or sinter strand. Organic matter volatilizes, forming internal porous structure while the outer shell densifies.
6. **Cooling:** Controlled cooling in a downdraft cooler to prevent thermal cracking.
7. **Sizing:** Crushing oversized and re-feeding undersized material for grading.

**Properties [2 Marks]:**
- Specific gravity: **1.3 – 1.7**
- Water absorption: **12 – 20%**
- Bulk density: **800 – 1100 kg/m³**
- ACV: **25 – 35%**
- Thermal conductivity: **0.2 – 0.4 W/m·K** (excellent insulator)

**Pavement Applications [1 Mark]:**
- Lightweight sub-base on bridge approaches.
- Insulating layer under rigid pavement to reduce thermal gradient.
- Backfill for MSE walls adjacent to pavements.

**Advantages over Natural Aggregate [1 Mark]:**
- Reduces dead load by 30–40%, ideal for bridge approach slabs.
- Utilizes industrial waste (sustainable).
- Excellent thermal insulation reduces pavement thermal stress.

##### Part (b) — Specific Gravity Calculations [7 Marks]

**Given:**
- $W_{od} = 480$ g
- $W_{ssd} = 560$ g
- $W_{sub} = 240$ g

**(i) Oven-dry specific gravity:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_{od} = \dfrac{W_{od}}{W_{ssd} - W_{sub}} = \dfrac{480}{560 - 240} = \dfrac{480}{320} = 1.500
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Saturated surface-dry specific gravity:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_{ssd} = \dfrac{W_{ssd}}{W_{ssd} - W_{sub}} = \dfrac{560}{560 - 240} = \dfrac{560}{320} = 1.750
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Apparent specific gravity:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_{app} = \dfrac{W_{od}}{W_{od} - W_{sub}} = \dfrac{480}{480 - 240} = \dfrac{480}{240} = 2.000
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iv) Water absorption:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
WA(\%) = \dfrac{W_{ssd} - W_{od}}{W_{od}} \times 100 = \dfrac{560 - 480}{480} \times 100 = \dfrac{80}{480} \times 100 = 16.67\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Suitability for Lightweight Sub-base [1 Mark]:**

The values obtained ($G_{od} = 1.50$, $WA = 16.67\%$) fall within the expected range for expanded clay LWA as per **IS 9142:2018**. The aggregate is **suitable** for lightweight pavement sub-base application, provided it is **pre-saturated for 24 hours** before use to prevent absorption of mix water during construction.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Module 2]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Discuss the **types, properties, and engineering applications** of **blast furnace slag and steel slag** as artificial aggregates in flexible pavement construction. Compare their suitability for **wearing course vs. base course** applications.

**(b)** [7 Marks] The **Los Angeles Abrasion test** is conducted on a sample of **recycled concrete aggregate (RCA)** with the following data:
- Grade of sample: **Grade B** (12 steel spheres, 5000 revolutions)
- Initial mass of sample: $W_i = 5000$ g
- Mass retained on 1.70 mm IS sieve after test: $W_r = 3900$ g

Calculate: **(i)** Los Angeles Abrasion Value, **(ii)** Comment on its suitability for **water-bound macadam (WBM) base course** as per **MoRTH specifications**, and **(iii)** Suggest **two improvements** to enhance the abrasion resistance of RCA for higher-grade applications.

#### Model Solution

##### Part (a) — Blast Furnace Slag & Steel Slag [7 Marks]

**Types [3 Marks]:**

| **Aspect** | **Blast Furnace Slag (BFS)** | **Steel Slag (BOF/EAF)** |
|:-----------|:-----------------------------|:--------------------------|
| Source | Iron blast furnace (pig iron production) | Basic Oxygen Furnace (BOF) or Electric Arc Furnace (EAF) |
| Composition | Ca, Si, Al, Mg silicates | Ca, Fe silicates + free lime (CaO) |
| Density | 1200–1400 kg/m³ (lightweight when foamed) | 1600–1800 kg/m³ |
| Expansion risk | Low | High (due to free lime) |
| Specific Gravity | 2.0–2.4 | 2.6–2.8 |

**Properties [2 Marks]:**

- **BFS:** Rough surface texture, high angularity, good interlocking, low abrasion loss (25–35%).
- **Steel Slag:** Very high PSV (60–70), excellent skid resistance, but **delayed volumetric expansion** due to hydration of free CaO and MgO (must be **weathered for 6–12 months**).

**Pavement Applications [1 Mark]:**

- **Wearing Course:** Steel slag (high PSV) is **preferred** for high-friction surfaces on curves and intersections.
- **Base Course:** Both BFS and air-cooled steel slag are suitable; foamed slag acts as lightweight sub-base.

**Comparison Table [1 Mark]:**
- Steel slag → **Wearing Course** (best skid resistance)
- BFS → **Base Course / Sub-base** (cost-effective, stable)

##### Part (b) — Los Angeles Abrasion Value [7 Marks]

**Given:**
- $W_i = 5000$ g
- $W_r = 3900$ g

**(i) Los Angeles Abrasion Value:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
LA(\%) = \dfrac{W_i - W_r}{W_i} \times 100 = \dfrac{5000 - 3900}{5000} \times 100 = \dfrac{1100}{5000} \times 100 = 22.0\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Suitability for WBM Base Course [2 Marks]:**

As per **MoRTH Section 400 (WBM)**:
- Maximum permissible LA Abrasion Value for WBM aggregates: **≤ 40%** (for grades I & II) and **≤ 50%** (for grade III).

Since the obtained value of **22.0%** is well below 40%, the RCA is **highly suitable** for WBM base course application. Its performance exceeds typical natural aggregate WBM, making it an excellent sustainable choice.

**(iii) Improvements for Higher-Grade Applications [2 Marks]:**

1. **Mechanical Improvement:** Coating RCA with **silica fume slurry + cement paste** (cementitious treatment) densifies the weak ITZ (Interfacial Transition Zone) and reduces abrasion loss to <15%.
2. **Surface Treatment:** Impregnating RCA with **silane-based water repellents** or **polymer-modified bitumen emulsion** enhances surface hardness and skid resistance, allowing RCA use in DBM (Dense Bituminous Macadam) wearing course.
3. *(Optional third method):* Blending RCA with **30–40% fresh granite aggregate** can improve overall mechanical performance for high-grade applications.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Deduction Pitfalls in Artificial Aggregate Questions:**
>
> 1. **Conflating Specific Gravity Values (–1 mark):** Students often write $G_s$ for natural aggregates (2.6–2.8) when asked about LWA. Remember: **LWA specific gravity is 0.5–1.8**.
> 2. **Skipping Pre-saturation Step (–2 marks in design questions):** Any answer involving LWA in concrete/mix design MUST mention the **24-hour pre-saturation step** to prevent mix water absorption.
> 3. **Confusing ACV with AIV (–1 mark):** ACV uses a **gradual compressive load (400 kN over 10 min)**; AIV uses **sudden impact (15 blows of hammer)**. They are NOT interchangeable.
> 4. **Forgetting IS Code Year (–0.5 marks):** Always write **IS 383:2016** (not just "IS 383") and **IS 9142:2018**. Examiners deduct marks for outdated references.
> 5. **Ignoring Steel Slag Expansion Risk (–2 marks):** Any answer on steel slag MUST mention **6–12 month weathering** before use to prevent pavement heaving.
> 6. **Wrong Sieve in LA Abrasion (–1 mark):** Final sieve in LA Abrasion is **1.70 mm IS sieve**, not 2.36 mm.
> 7. **Mixing-up PSV with LA (–1 mark):** PSV (Polished Stone Value) measures **skid resistance** under polishing action; LA measures **abrasion resistance under impact**.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Artificial Aggregates**
>
> ✅ Artificial aggregates are **engineered granular materials** from industrial processing — NOT naturally occurring.
>
> ✅ **Four major categories:** Lightweight Aggregates, Slag Aggregates, Recycled Aggregates, Manufactured Sand.
>
> ✅ **LWA Specific Gravity:** 0.5–1.8 | **Water Absorption:** 5–25% | **Pre-saturate** before use.
>
> ✅ **Steel Slag** has **highest PSV (60–70)** → best for wearing course; requires **weathering** to prevent expansion.
>
> ✅ **Blast Furnace Slag** is most cost-effective for **base course** applications.
>
> ✅ **Sintered Fly Ash (Lytag)** is manufactured at **1100–1300°C** with **5% bentonite binder** via disc pelletization.
>
> ✅ **Recycled Concrete Aggregate (RCA)** is suitable for WBM/lower layers; LA Abrasion should be **≤ 40%**.
>
> ✅ **Critical IS Codes:**
> - IS 383:2016 (Aggregates for concrete)
> - IS 9142:2018 (Lightweight aggregates)
> - IS 2386:1963 (Methods of test for aggregates)
> - IS 9399:1987 (Sintered fly ash aggregates)
>
> ✅ **Key Threshold Values (always remember):**
> - ACV ≤ 24% (surface), ≤ 30% (base)
> - AIV ≤ 30% (surface), ≤ 35% (base)
> - LA Abrasion ≤ 30% (DBM), ≤ 40% (WBM)
> - Soundness ≤ 12% (Na₂SO₄), ≤ 18% (MgSO₄)
>
> ✅ **Sustainability Angle:** Use of artificial aggregates aligns with **UN SDG 11 (Sustainable Cities)** and **India's GRIHA rating** for green highways.
>
> ✅ **Calculation Must-Knows:**
> - $G_{od} = \dfrac{W_{od}}{W_{ssd} - W_{sub}}$
> - $ACV(\%) = \dfrac{W_{crushed}}{W_{total}} \times 100$
> - $WA(\%) = \dfrac{W_{ssd} - W_{od}}{W_{od}} \times 100$
> - $n(\%) = \left(1 - \dfrac{\gamma_d}{G_s \gamma_w}\right) \times 100$
>
> ✅ **Real-World Connectors:** NH-66 (Kerala coastal highways) use **copper slag**; Bangalore-Mysore expressway uses **steel slag wearing course**; Delhi-Meerut RRTS uses **Lytag** in elevated viaducts.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Binders
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET746 - HIGHWAY MATERIALS AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Types of Binders

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Binder in Highway Engineering?

In pavement engineering, **bituminous binders** are the dark, adhesive, viscoelastic hydrocarbon materials that bind the aggregate particles together in a flexible pavement structure. They are derived either from **petroleum refining (bitumen/asphalt)** or from the **destructive distillation of coal/wood (tar)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PECET746, Module 2):**
> A *bituminous binder* is a substance (bitumen, tar, or their modified/cutback forms) used to hold the aggregate particles together by adhesion, providing a waterproof, flexible, and durable surfacing for roads, runways, and bridge decks.

## 1.2 The Role of a Binder — The "Glue" of the Road

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy: The Peanut Butter in a Sandwich**
> Imagine a flexible pavement as a layered sandwich:
> - **Aggregates** = the bread slices (provide strength and skeleton).
> - **Filler (fine dust)** = the lettuce (fills micro-voids).
> - **Binder (Bitumen)** = the **peanut butter or cheese spread** (sticks the layers, makes it waterproof, and gives flexibility).
>
> Without the spread, the bread simply falls apart. Without the binder, the road disintegrates under traffic load and rain. The binder must remain soft enough in summer (so the road doesn't crack) yet stiff enough in winter (so the road doesn't rut).

## 1.3 Why Are Different Types Needed?

A single grade of bitumen cannot perform optimally across all the climatic zones of India — from the freezing cold of Leh to the scorching heat of Rajasthan. Hence, **different binder types** are manufactured and graded to suit:

- **Climate** (high vs. low temperature regions),
- **Traffic loading** (low-volume village roads vs. NH expressways),
- **Construction technique** (hot mix, cold mix, surface dressing),
- **Performance requirement** (rutting resistance, fatigue life, aging).

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook for KTU Exam:** The five primary families of binders are **BCTEF** — **B**itumen, **C**utback, **T**ar, **E**mulsion, and **F**oamed/Modified.

## 1.4 Primary Classification of Bituminous Binders (Overview)

Bituminous binders are broadly classified into two super-families based on their origin:

| S.No. | Parent Family | Source | Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Bitumen (Asphalt)** | Petroleum crude oil (vacuum distillation) | VG-30, VG-40, PG 76-22 |
| 2 | **Tar** | Destructive distillation of coal/wood | RT-1 to RT-12 (rarely used today) |

These two parents are then **sub-divided into five commonly used categories** in the field:

1. **Penetration Grade Bitumen**
2. **Viscosity Grade (VG) Bitumen**
3. **Cutback Bitumen** (RC, MC, SC)
4. **Bitumen Emulsion** (Anionic & Cationic — RS, MS, SS)
5. **Modified Bitumen** (PMB, CRMB, Foamed Bitumen)

Each of these will be treated in detail in the next section.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Bitumen (Asphalt Cement)

**Bitumen** is a black, sticky, semi-solid hydrocarbon obtained as the **bottom residue** of the vacuum distillation tower of crude petroleum refining. It is insoluble in water but soluble in carbon disulphide (CS₂).

### 2.1.1 Penetration Grade Bitumen (IS: 73-2013)

The most common classification. The **penetration value** is the distance (in units of $\frac{1}{10}$ mm) a standard needle penetrates the bitumen sample vertically under standard conditions:

- **Load:** 100 g
- **Temperature:** 25 °C
- **Time:** 5 seconds

> [!NOTE]
> A penetration value of **60** means the needle goes in $\frac{60}{10} = 6.0$ mm. A *lower* penetration = *harder* bitumen.

| Grade (IS:73) | Penetration Range | Common Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **30/40** | 30 to 40 | Very cold climates, heavy traffic |
| **40/50** | 40 to 50 | Cold regions, NHs |
| **50/60** | 50 to 60 | Moderate climate |
| **60/70** | 60 to 70 | Most of India (general purpose) |
| **80/100** | 80 to 100 | Warm climate, lower traffic |
| **120/150** | 120 to 150 | Very warm climate, surface dressing |

### 2.1.2 Viscosity Grade Bitumen (IS: 73-2013 — Revised)

In 2013, IRC and MoRTH shifted the Indian specification from **penetration grading to viscosity grading** because viscosity is a **fundamental engineering property** (it directly measures flow), whereas penetration is empirical.

| VG Grade | Viscosity at 60 °C (Poise) | Typical Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **VG-10** | 800 minimum | Surface dressing, cold climate |
| **VG-20** | 1600 minimum | Low traffic roads, premix carpet |
| **VG-30** | 2400 minimum | **Most Indian NH/SH under IRC:111** |
| **VG-40** | 3200 minimum | Heavy traffic, hot climate, intersection |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** Most numerical problems in KTU exams (PECET746) deal with **VG-30** which is the most common grade used in India. Always specify viscosity in **Poise** (cgs unit), where $1 \text{ Poise} = 0.1 \text{ Pa}\cdot\text{s}$.

### 2.1.3 Performance Grade (PG) Bitumen (AASHTO M 320)

Used in USA and increasingly in India for **expressways and high-volume corridors**. The grade is denoted **PG XY** where:

- **X** = **maximum** pavement design temperature (°C) at which the binder resists rutting (measured by $G^{*}/\sin\delta$),
- **Y** = **minimum** pavement temperature (°C) at which the binder resists thermal cracking (measured by stiffness $S$ and $m$-value).

**Examples:** PG 64-22 (India NHs), PG 70-16, PG 76-10 (expressways, high-altitude), PG 82-22 (heavy slow-moving traffic at toll plazas).

## 2.2 Tar

Tar is the **condensate product from the destructive distillation of coal, wood, peat, or shale** in the absence of air. It is chemically very different from bitumen — contains **aromatic hydrocarbons and phenols** (carcinogenic).

- **Use:** Historically used for road tarring. Now **banned for paving in most countries** due to health hazards and inferior weathering resistance.
- **Grades (IS: 215):** RT-1, RT-2, RT-3, RT-4, RT-5 (Road Tar).

## 2.3 Cutback Bitumen

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Cutback bitumen is like "diluted paint." Pure bitumen is too viscous to spray at ambient temperature. By blending it with a **volatile solvent (cutter)**, it becomes sprayable. The solvent evaporates later, leaving behind the original bitumen.

Cutback bitumen is bitumen whose viscosity has been reduced by adding a **petroleum distillate (cutter stock)** such as kerosene, naphtha, or diesel. Classified by the **rate of evaporation** of the solvent:

| Type | Cutter Stock | Curing Speed | Common Grades |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **RC** (Rapid Curing) | Naphtha / gasoline | Fast | RC-70, RC-250, RC-800, RC-3000 |
| **MC** (Medium Curing) | Kerosene | Medium | MC-30, MC-70, MC-250, MC-800, MC-3000 |
| **SC** (Slow Curing) | Light diesel / heavy oil | Very slow | SC-70, SC-250, SC-800, SC-3000 |

The number suffix (e.g., 70, 250) refers to the **kinematic viscosity (in centistokes) at 60 °C** of the cutback blend.

**Disadvantage:** Solvent evaporation releases **VOCs (Volatile Organic Compounds)** — environmental hazard. Hence, **emulsions** are preferred for cold applications.

## 2.4 Bitumen Emulsion

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** An emulsion is "bitumen in disguise." Tiny droplets (1–10 µm) of bitumen are suspended in water using a **surfactant (emulsifier)**, like oil droplets in milk. On contact with aggregate, water evaporates or is absorbed, and the bitumen "breaks" (coalesces) into a continuous film.

Emulsions are **two-phase systems**:
- **Dispersed phase:** Bitumen droplets (50–70% by weight).
- **Continuous phase:** Water (30–50%).
- **Stabilizer:** Emulsifying agent (cationic or anionic surfactant).

### 2.4.1 Classification by Charge

| Type | Surfactant | Aggregate Preference |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Anionic** | Negatively charged | Limestone (basic) — works best with **siliceous aggregates** (acidic) |
| **Cationic** | Positively charged (e.g., fatty amines) | **Siliceous (acidic)** — works best with **granite, quartzite** (Indian practice) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** Cationic emulsions are **preferred in India** because most Indian aggregates (granite, basalt) are acidic in nature, and the positive emulsion breaks faster on the negatively charged aggregate surface.

### 2.4.2 Classification by Setting Speed (IS: 8887)

| Setting | Symbol | Use | Setting Time |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Rapid Setting** | RS / CRS | Surface dressing, tack coat | 1–5 minutes |
| **Medium Setting** | MS / CMS | Premix patching, cold mix | 5–30 minutes |
| **Slow Setting** | SS / CSS | Dense-graded cold mix, slurry seal | 30 minutes + |

## 2.5 Modified Bitumen

When traffic loads, climate, or performance demands exceed what conventional bitumen can handle, **modifiers** are added.

| Modification | Modifier | Property Improved |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **PMB (Polymer Modified Bitumen)** | SBS, EVA, LDPE | Elastic recovery, fatigue life |
| **CRMB (Crumb Rubber Modified Bitimen)** | Recycled tyre rubber (10–15%) | Rutting resistance, durability |
| **Natural Rubber Modified** | NR latex | Low-temperature cracking |
| **Foamed Bitumen** | Injected cold water into hot bitumen | Cold recycling, base stabilization |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Distinction (KTU Board Exam):**
> - **PMB (IS: 15462):** Modifier is a *virgin polymer* — higher cost, superior quality.
> - **CRMB (IS: 15462-2004):** Modifier is *crumb rubber from scrap tyres* — also improves environment, but storage stability is a concern.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Master these formulas — they appear in 80% of KTU numerical questions on binders.**

| # | Formula / Relation | Description | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $P_{25}$ = Penetration @ 25 °C, 100 g, 5 s | Empirical hardness indicator | 0.1 mm |
| 2 | $\text{Softening Point } T_R$ | Ring-and-Ball temperature where bitumen flows | °C |
| 3 | $\text{Ductility}$ = length of stretched thread at break | Tensile strain capacity | cm |
| 4 | $\text{Viscosity } \eta$ @ 60 °C | Absolute viscosity (VG) | Poise |
| 5 | $\text{Kinematic Viscosity } \nu = \eta / \rho$ | For cutback grades | cSt |
| 6 | **Pfeiffer & Van Doormaal Equation:** $PI = \dfrac{20 - 500 A}{1 + 50 A}$ | Penetration Index (temperature susceptibility) | Dimensionless |
| 7 | $A = \dfrac{\log 800 - \log P_{25}}{T_R - 25}$ | Slope of log(P) vs. T line | per °C |
| 8 | $\log \log \eta = A - B \cdot \log T_{Rb}$ | **Walther equation** (viscosity-temperature) | — |
| 9 | **PG Rutting Factor:** $G^{*} / \sin\delta \geq 1.0 \text{ kPa}$ (original) | High-temp stiffness | kPa |
| 10 | **PG Fatigue Factor:** $G^{*} \cdot \sin\delta \leq 5000 \text{ kPa}$ (RTFO-aged) | Intermediate-temp fatigue | kPa |

**Significance of PI (Penetration Index):**

| PI Value | Bitumen Type | Temperature Susceptibility |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **< −2** | Highly blown / oxidized | Very low (too brittle for roads) |
| **−2 to +2** | Road construction bitumen | Normal |
| **> +2** | Highly temperature-resistant | Excellent (rare for neat bitumen) |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Penetration Index (PI) Using Pfeiffer & Van Doormaal's Equation

The penetration of a bitumen decreases (approximately) **logarithmically** with temperature. If we plot $\log P$ (penetration) on the y-axis against temperature $T$ (°C) on the x-axis, we obtain a **straight line**.

Let us **derive the slope** $A$ of this line.

> **Given conditions (standard):**
> - Reference 1: $T_1 = 25 \text{ °C}$, $P_1 = P_{25}$ (lab-measured penetration).
> - Reference 2: $T_2 = T_R$ (softening point), $P_2 = 800$ (universal — by IS convention, penetration at softening point ≈ 800).

The two-point slope of the line is:

$$
A = \frac{\log P_2 - \log P_1}{T_2 - T_1}
$$

Substituting $P_2 = 800$, $T_2 = T_R$, $P_1 = P_{25}$, $T_1 = 25$:

$$
A = \frac{\log 800 - \log P_{25}}{T_R - 25}
$$

**Step 1 — Recognize the "Reference" Bitumen:**
Pfeiffer & Van Doormaal defined a *reference* bitumen having $P_{25} = 200$ and $T_R = 40 \text{ °C}$ with **PI = 0**. For this bitumen:

$$
A_0 = \frac{\log 800 - \log 200}{40 - 25} = \frac{\log(800/200)}{15} = \frac{\log 4}{15} = \frac{0.6021}{15} = 0.04014
$$

**Step 2 — Generalize the Slope:**
For any bitumen, the *deviation* of its slope $A$ from $A_0$ is what defines PI. Empirically, the relation is:

$$
PI = \frac{30}{1 + 50 A} - 10 \quad \text{(Earlier form)}
$$

Pfeiffer & Van Doormaal then simplified this to the **canonical form**:

$$
\boxed{\; PI = \frac{20 - 500 A}{1 + 50 A} \;}
$$

**Step 3 — Verification:** Plug $A = 0.04014$ into the boxed equation:

$$
PI = \frac{20 - 500 (0.04014)}{1 + 50(0.04014)} = \frac{20 - 20.07}{1 + 2.007} = \frac{-0.07}{3.007} \approx 0
$$

✓ Confirms the reference bitumen has PI = 0.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Meaning:**
> - **PI close to 0** → bitumen has *normal* temperature susceptibility (most paving grades lie between −1 and +1).
> - **PI > 0** → low susceptibility → less rutting in summer, less cracking in winter.
> - **PI < 0** → high susceptibility → more deformation and cracking.

## 3.2 Analytical Worked Example (KTU Board Style)

### **Problem (Self-Referential):**
A bitumen sample tested in the laboratory yielded the following data:
- Penetration at 25 °C, 100 g, 5 s: $P_{25} = 60$
- Softening point (Ring-and-Ball): $T_R = 50 \text{ °C}$

**Compute:**
(a) The slope $A$ of the log(P) vs. T curve.
(b) The Penetration Index (PI).
(c) Interpret the temperature susceptibility.

### **Solution — Part (a): Slope A**

$$
A = \frac{\log 800 - \log 60}{50 - 25}
$$

Evaluate numerator:

$$
\log 800 = 2.9031, \quad \log 60 = 1.7782
$$

$$
\log 800 - \log 60 = \log\left(\frac{800}{60}\right) = \log(13.333) = 1.1249
$$

Therefore:

$$
A = \frac{1.1249}{25} = 0.04499 \approx 0.045 \text{ per } ^\circ \text{C}
$$

### **Solution — Part (b): Penetration Index**

Substituting $A = 0.045$ into the canonical formula:

$$
PI = \frac{20 - 500 (0.045)}{1 + 50 (0.045)}
$$

Numerator:

$$
20 - 500(0.045) = 20 - 22.5 = -2.5
$$

Denominator:

$$
1 + 50(0.045) = 1 + 2.25 = 3.25
$$

$$
\boxed{ PI = \frac{-2.5}{3.25} = -0.769 \approx -0.77 }
$$

### **Solution — Part (c): Interpretation**

Since $PI \approx -0.77$ (which lies between −1 and +1), the bitumen is **suitable for paving** but is on the *slightly more temperature-susceptible* side. It is acceptable for **moderate traffic and moderate climate**, but for high-temperature regions (Rajasthan, Vidarbha) or heavy traffic, a **higher PI (modified bitumen)** would be preferred.

## 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix of All Binder Types

| Property | Bitumen (VG-30) | Tar (RT-3) | Cutback (MC-30) | Emulsion (CRS-1) | Modified (PMB-40) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Source** | Petroleum residue | Coal distillate | Bitumen + kerosene | Bitumen + water + soap | Bitumen + polymer |
| **State at 25 °C** | Viscous liquid | Sticky liquid | Flowable liquid | Milky liquid | Viscous liquid |
| **Setting Mechanism** | Cool down | Cool down | Solvent evaporates | Water separates | Cool down |
| **Mixing Temp (°C)** | 150–170 | 100–120 | 60–110 (warm) | **Ambient (cold)** | 160–180 |
| **VOCs / Pollution** | None | High (PAHs) | **High** | Negligible | None |
| **Storage Stability** | Years (heated) | Years | Months | **≤ 6 months** | Years |
| **Cure Time** | Hours | Hours | Days | Minutes–days | Hours |
| **Health Hazard** | Low | **High (carcinogenic)** | Medium | Low | Low |
| **Cost (relative)** | 1.0× | 0.8× | 1.2× | 1.4× | 2.0–3.0× |
| **Best Use** | Hot mix (DBM, BC) | Coal-tar paint | Surface dressing (cold areas) | Tack coat, cold mix, fog seal | Expressways, intersections |

## 3.4 Viscosity–Temperature Susceptibility (Walther's Equation)

For a **Newtonian** bitumen, viscosity decreases with temperature according to the ASTM D 2493 **Walther equation**:

$$
\log \log \eta = A - B \cdot \log (T_{Rb})
$$

where:

- $\eta$ = viscosity in **centipoise (cP)**,
- $T_{Rb}$ = temperature in **Rankine** ($T_{Rb} = T_°C + 273$),
- $A$, $B$ = empirical constants.

A **larger B** means the viscosity drops faster with temperature → higher susceptibility → poor binder.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Engineering Utility:** The Walther equation is used to construct the **PVTV (Penetration–Viscosity–Temperature–Viscoelastic) Chart**, also called the **Heukelom bitumen test data chart**, which is the *master diagnostic tool* for grading and selecting binders. It is the most frequently asked graph-based question in KTU exams.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Classification Flowchart of Bituminous Binders

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Bituminous Binders] --> B[Petroleum Origin]
    A --> C[Coal or Wood Origin]
    
    B --> D[Bitumen Asphalt]
    C --> E[Tar RT-1 to RT-5]
    
    D --> F[Conventional Grade]
    D --> G[Cutback Bitumen]
    D --> H[Bitumen Emulsion]
    D --> I[Modified Bitumen]
    
    F --> F1[Penetration Grade 30 to 150]
    F --> F2[Viscosity Grade VG-10 to VG-40]
    F --> F3[Performance Grade PG-XX-YY]
    
    G --> G1[RC Rapid Curing]
    G --> G2[MC Medium Curing]
    G --> G3[SC Slow Curing]
    
    H --> H1[Anionic RS MS SS]
    H --> H2[Cationic CRS CMS CSS]
    
    I --> I1[PMB Polymer SBS EVA]
    I --> I2[CRMB Crumb Rubber]
    I --> I3[Foamed Bitumen Hot Water Injection]
    
    E --> E1[Destructive Distillation of Coal]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: How a Binder "Works" in a Pavement

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Traffic and Climate Loading]
        IN1[Axle Loads 5 to 20 tonnes]
        IN2[Temperature 5 to 65 C]
        IN3[Water Rain and Moisture]
    end
    
    subgraph BINDER[Bituminous Binder Function]
        BF1[Adhesion to Aggregate]
        BF2[Cohesion within Binder]
        BF3[Waterproofing Sealant]
        BF4[Flexibility Viscoelasticity]
        BF5[Load Distribution]
    end
    
    subgraph OUTPUT[Pavement Performance]
        OP1[Resistance to Rutting]
        OP2[Resistance to Cracking]
        OP3[Long Service Life 15 plus Years]
        OP4[Smooth Riding Surface]
    end
    
    IN1 --> BF1
    IN1 --> BF5
    IN2 --> BF4
    IN3 --> BF3
    BF1 --> OP1
    BF2 --> OP2
    BF3 --> OP3
    BF4 --> OP2
    BF5 --> OP1
    BF5 --> OP4
```

## 4.3 Decision Logic: Selecting the Right Binder for a Project

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Construction Type?} -->|Hot Mix Plant| Q2{Climate Temperature?}
    Q1 -->|Cold Mix or Spray| Q3{Open Surface Dressing?}
    
    Q2 -->|Hot India VG-30| OUT1[Use VG-30 or VG-40]
    Q2 -->|Cold Hill Areas| OUT2[Use VG-10 or 80/100 Pen Grade]
    Q2 -->|Expressway Heavy Traffic| OUT3[Use PMB or CRMB]
    
    Q3 -->|Yes| Q4{Aggregate Type?}
    Q3 -->|No Cold Mix| Q5{Storage Period?}
    
    Q4 -->|Acidic Granite| OUT4[Use Cationic Emulsion CRS-1]
    Q4 -->|Basic Limestone| OUT5[Use Anionic Emulsion RS-1]
    
    Q5 -->|Short Less than 1 week| OUT6[Use Medium Setting CMS]
    Q5 -->|Long Cold Stockpile| OUT7[Use Slow Setting CSS]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read the diagrams for KTU exams:** Flowcharts like these are commonly asked as 7-mark "explain with neat sketch" questions. Always include the **decision criteria** (climate, traffic, aggregate type) and the **recommended binder grade** at each branch.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

### **Q1. (3 Marks)**
**Differentiate between bitumen and tar as paving binders.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Bitumen | Tar |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Source** | Petroleum (refinery bottom) | Destructive distillation of coal |
| **Composition** | Aliphatic + aromatic hydrocarbons | Predominantly aromatic with phenols |
| **Weather resistance** | Excellent (ages slowly) | Poor (oxidizes, becomes brittle) |
| **Health hazard** | Low | **High (carcinogenic – PAHs)** |
| **Current use** | Widespread (99% of roads) | Banned/discouraged for paving |
| **Adhesion to aggregate** | Good with most aggregates | Good with acidic (siliceous) |

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

### **Q2. (3 Marks)**
**List the three grades of cutback bitumen with the type of cutter stock used and one application of each.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **RC (Rapid Curing):** Cutter = *naphtha/gasoline*; used for **surface dressing** and **patch repair** in cold weather.
2. **MC (Medium Curing):** Cutter = *kerosene*; used for **prime coat** over granular base and **premix carpet** with low-viscosity cutback.
3. **SC (Slow Curing):** Cutter = *light diesel/heavy oil*; used in **dense-graded cold mixes** and **stockpile patching**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Module 2 Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]** — *14-Mark Question with Internal Choice*

### **OPTION A — Question A (14 Marks)**

**Classify the different types of bituminous binders used in highway construction. Explain in detail the cutback bitumen and bitumen emulsion, including their grades, manufacturing process, and applications.**

#### **Part (a) — Classification of Binders and Cutback Bitumen (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Bituminous binders are classified into five major types based on their physical state, method of application, and source.

**Cutback Bitumen** is manufactured by blending penetration-grade bitumen (typically 80/100 or 85/100) with a **volatile petroleum distillate (cutter stock)** in a closed mechanical mixer at 100–120 °C. The cutter stock acts as a viscosity-reducing agent, allowing the binder to be sprayed or mixed at ambient or moderately warm temperatures. After application, the cutter evaporates, and the residual bitumen regains its original viscosity and binds the aggregates.

**Classification of Cutback Bitumen (IS: 217):**

| Type | Cutter | Curing Speed | Grades | Viscosity (cSt at 60 °C) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **RC** | Naphtha | Fast | 70, 250, 800, 3000 | 70–3000 |
| **MC** | Kerosene | Medium | 30, 70, 250, 800, 3000 | 30–3000 |
| **SC** | Diesel | Slow | 70, 250, 800, 3000 | 70–3000 |

**Applications:**
- RC-70/MC-70 → Tack coat, prime coat
- MC-250/MC-800 → Premix carpet
- SC-3000 → Cold mix stockpile

#### **Part (b) — Bitumen Emulsion (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A bitumen emulsion is a **heterogeneous two-phase system** consisting of:
1. **Dispersed phase:** Bitumen droplets (1–10 μm, 50–70% by weight).
2. **Continuous phase:** Water (30–50%).
3. **Emulsifier:** A surface-active agent (0.3–1.0%).

**Manufacturing Process (Colloidal Mill):**
Hot bitumen (140–150 °C) and water-emulsifier solution (50–60 °C) are fed into a high-speed **colloidal mill** rotating at 3000–6000 rpm. The mechanical shearing breaks the bitumen into micron-sized droplets, which are instantly coated with surfactant ions. The mixture is rapidly cooled to ambient temperature, producing a stable, free-flowing milky liquid.

**Types (by charge):**

- **Anionic:** Negatively charged bitumen droplets (e.g., sodium stearate soap). Bonds with **siliceous (acidic) aggregates**.
- **Cationic:** Positively charged droplets (e.g., fatty diamine soap). Bonds with **calcareous (basic) aggregates** and is preferred in India.

**Grades (IS: 8887):** RS (rapid), MS (medium), SS (slow) — each with 1, 2, 3 subtypes.

**Applications:** Tack coat (RS-1), prime coat (SS-1), cold mix (MS), surface dressing (CRS-2), slurry seal, fog seal, cold recycling.

---

### **OPTION B — Question B (14 Marks)**

**Explain the concept of viscosity-grade and performance-grade bitumen. Compare them with the traditional penetration grading. What are the limitations of penetration grading?**

#### **Part (a) — Viscosity Grade & Performance Grade Bitumen (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Viscosity Grade (VG) Bitumen (IS: 73-2013):**
Bitumen is graded based on its **absolute viscosity at 60 °C** (the maximum pavement service temperature) and **kinematic viscosity at 135 °C** (the mixing/compaction temperature). Four grades: **VG-10, VG-20, VG-30, VG-40**. Higher number = harder (more viscous) bitumen suitable for higher temperature and traffic.

**Performance Grade (PG) Bitumen (AASHTO M 320):**
Graded based on the climatic and traffic conditions actually encountered. A binder is tested in a **Dynamic Shear Rheometer (DSR)** and **Bending Beam Rheometer (BBR)** to measure:
- $G^* / \sin\delta$ → high-temp rutting resistance,
- $G^* \cdot \sin\delta$ → intermediate-temp fatigue cracking,
- Stiffness $S(t)$ and $m$-value → low-temp thermal cracking.

Notation **PG XY** (e.g., PG 76-22) where X = max pavement temp (°C) and Y = min pavement temp (°C).

#### **Part (b) — Comparison with Penetration Grading (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Penetration Grade | Viscosity Grade | Performance Grade |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Test basis** | Empirical (needle depth) | Fundamental (viscosity) | Fundamental (rheology) |
| **Key property** | Hardness at 25 °C | Flow at 60 °C & 135 °C | Stiffness at in-service temps |
| **Climate adaptation** | Indirect | Indirect | **Direct (PG 64-22 etc.)** |
| **Equipment** | Penetrometer | Viscometer | DSR + BBR |
| **Cost of testing** | Low | Medium | **High** |
| **Adoption in India** | Phased out in 2013 | **Current (MoRTH/IRC:111)** | Expressways (NHAI premium) |

**Limitations of Penetration Grading:**
1. Tests bitumen at **25 °C** — far from the actual **service temperature (50–70 °C)** in India.
2. Highly **empirical** — needle penetration is not a fundamental engineering property.
3. Does **not measure** aging susceptibility, fatigue, or low-temperature cracking.
4. **Empirical** values of 30/40, 60/70 etc. do not communicate actual field performance.
5. Cannot distinguish between binders of similar penetration but vastly different performance.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students commonly lose marks in the KTU 2024 board exam on this topic:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "RC" in cutback with "RS" in emulsion.** RC = Rapid *Curing* (cutback), RS = Rapid *Setting* (emulsion). Examiners deduct 1 mark.
> 2. **Forgetting the role of the emulsifier.** Many students write "bitumen + water" only. The surfactant (cationic/anionic) is the third essential component — 1 mark lost.
> 3. **Mixing up grades.** Students write "VG-30 = 30 mm penetration." Wrong. VG-30 means **2400 Poise minimum viscosity at 60 °C**. −2 marks for conceptual error.
> 4. **PI formula errors.** Forgetting the negative sign in the numerator $(20 - 500A)$ or using $\log 800 - \log P_{25}$ in the denominator. Always recheck.
> 5. **Not stating units.** Viscosity in **Poise**, penetration in **0.1 mm**, softening point in **°C**. Missing units = −0.5 mark each.
> 6. **Skipping "modifications."** Many students stop at emulsion and do not mention PMB, CRMB, foamed bitumen — losing 2 marks in 14-mark questions.
> 7. **No diagram in classification answer.** KTU examiner guidelines mandate a flowchart or table for full marks. **Always include a classification table or flow diagram** in 7-mark answers.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Final-day revision checklist for the KTU viva / written exam — print this and pin it up.**

- [ ] Binders are the "adhesive glue" of flexible pavements — they hold aggregates, provide waterproofing, and impart flexibility.
- [ ] **Bitumen** is petroleum-origin; **tar** is coal-origin (mostly banned).
- [ ] Penetration grade (IS:73) → measured in 0.1 mm, superseded by **VG** grade in 2013.
- [ ] **VG-30** is the workhorse of Indian highways (2400 Poise at 60 °C).
- [ ] **PG grading** uses DSR/BBR and reports high- and low-temperature performance directly.
- [ ] **Cutback bitumen:** Bitumen + cutter stock. Types: **RC (naphtha), MC (kerosene), SC (diesel)**.
- [ ] **Bitumen emulsion:** Bitumen + water + surfactant. Types: **Anionic vs Cationic**; Setting: **RS, MS, SS**.
- [ ] **Cationic emulsions are preferred in India** because most Indian aggregates are acidic (siliceous).
- [ ] **PMB (Polymer Modified Bitumen)** uses SBS/EVA — improves elastic recovery.
- [ ] **CRMB (Crumb Rubber Modified Bitumen)** uses recycled tyre rubber — improves rutting resistance.
- [ ] **PI formula (Pfeiffer & Van Doormaal):**
  $$ PI = \frac{20 - 500 A}{1 + 50 A}, \quad A = \frac{\log 800 - \log P_{25}}{T_R - 25} $$
- [ ] Road bitumen has **PI between −1 and +1**; PI = 0 is the *reference* bitumen.
- [ ] **Walther equation:** $\log \log \eta = A - B \log T_{Rb}$ — relates viscosity to temperature.
- [ ] Tar is **carcinogenic** — avoid in modern paving.
- [ ] Cutbacks release **VOCs**; emulsions are the **environmentally preferred** cold-mix binder.
- [ ] Always quote **units** in numerical problems: Poise (viscosity), °C (temperature), 0.1 mm (penetration).
- [ ] **For 7-mark and 14-mark questions**, include a **classification flowchart or comparison table** to score full marks.

---

> **END OF MODULE 2 NOTES — TYPES OF BINDERS**
> *Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme (PECET746 — Highway Materials and Design)*
> *Outcomes assessed: CO2 — Characterize bituminous binders and select appropriate grade.*
