**Given Data:**
- Dry mass in air, $W_{dry} = 1245.6$ g
- SSD mass, $W_{SSD} = 1249.3$ g
- Mass in water, $W_{sub} = 711.4$ g
- Aggregate content, $P_s = 94.0\%$
- Bitumen content, $P_b = 6.0\%$
- Bulk sp. gr. of aggregate, $G_{sb} = 2.65$
- Bitumen sp. gr., $G_b = 1.02$
- Marshall stability = 12.4 kN, flow = 3.1 mm

**Step 1 — Bulk Specific Gravity $G_{mb}$:**
$$G_{mb} = \dfrac{W_{dry}}{W_{SSD} - W_{sub}} = \dfrac{1245.6}{1249.3 - 711.4} = \dfrac{1245.6}{537.9}$$

$$G_{mb} = 2.316$$

**Step 2 — Theoretical Maximum Specific Gravity $G_{mm}$:**
$$G_{mm} = \dfrac{100}{\dfrac{P_s}{G_s} + \dfrac{P_b}{G_b}} = \dfrac{100}{\dfrac{94.0}{2.65} + \dfrac{6.0}{1.02}}$$

$$= \dfrac{100}{35.47 + 5.88} = \dfrac{100}{41.35}$$

$$G_{mm} = 2.419$$

**Step 3 — Air Voids $V_a$:**
$$V_a = \dfrac{G_{mm} - G_{mb}}{G_{mm}} \times 100 = \dfrac{2.419 - 2.316}{2.419} \times 100$$

$$V_a = \dfrac{0.103}{2.419} \times 100 = 4.26\%$$

**Step 4 — VMA:**
$$VMA = 100 - \dfrac{P_s \cdot G_{mb}}{G_{sb}} = 100 - \dfrac{94.0 \times 2.316}{2.65}$$

$$= 100 - \dfrac{217.70}{2.65} = 100 - 82.15$$

$$VMA = 17.85\%$$

**Step 5 — VFB:**
$$VFB = \dfrac{VMA - V_a}{VMA} \times 100 = \dfrac{17.85 - 4.26}{17.85} \times 100$$

$$= \dfrac{13.59}{17.85} \times 100$$

$$VFB = 76.13\%$$

**Step 6 — Stiffness Ratio:**
$$SR = \dfrac{12.4 \text{ kN}}{3.1 \text{ mm}} = 4.0 \text{ kN/mm}$$

**Step 7 — Comment (the most important "design" step):**

| Parameter | Computed | MoRTH Spec (BC Gr-II) | Pass/Fail |
|:----------|:--------:|:---------------------:|:---------:|
| $V_a$ | 4.26 % | 3 – 5 % | **PASS** |
| $VMA$ | 17.85 % | ≥ 14 % (for 19 mm NMAS) | **PASS** |
| $VFB$ | 76.13 % | 65 – 75 % | **MARGINAL** (slightly high) |
| Stability | 12.4 kN | ≥ 9 kN | **PASS** |
| Flow | 3.1 mm | 2 – 4 mm | **PASS** |
| Stiffness | 4.0 kN/mm | ≥ 2.0 kN/mm | **PASS** |

**Conclusion:** The mix satisfies all volumetric and stability requirements. The VFB is slightly above the upper limit, suggesting a small reduction in bitumen content (e.g., 5.5% instead of 6.0%) might bring the design to the **Optimum Bitumen Content (OBC)** — which is the central goal of Marshall Mix Design.

### 3.5 The Marshall Mix Design Choice — Why OBC is the "Sweet Spot"
The classic Marshall procedure tests specimens at **5 bitumen contents** (typically 4.0, 4.5, 5.0, 5.5, 6.0%). For each content, stability, flow, $G_{mb}$, $V_a$, $VMA$, $VFB$ are plotted. The OBC is selected such that:
- $V_a$ = **4.0%** (median of 3–5% spec).
- Stability is **maximum or near-maximum**.
- $VFB$ is in the **65–75%** range.
- $VMA \geq$ **minimum** for that nominal maximum aggregate size (NMAS).

> [!TIP]
> **One-line answer for KTU viva:** "OBC is the bitumen content that simultaneously satisfies all four volumetric criteria (Va, VMA, VFB) and gives maximum stability."
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Mermaid Block — Requirements of Bituminous Mixes (Hierarchical Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1["BITUMINOUS MIX PERFORMANCE"] --> B1["STABILITY"]
    A1 --> B2["DURABILITY"]
    A1 --> B3["FLEXIBILITY"]
    A1 --> B4["FATIGUE RESISTANCE"]
    A1 --> B5["SKID RESISTANCE"]
    A1 --> B6["IMPERMEABILITY"]
    A1 --> B7["WORKABILITY"]
    A1 --> B8["ECONOMY"]

    B1 --> C1["Aggregate interlock and friction"]
    B1 --> C2["Cohesion from bitumen"]
    B1 --> C3["Field compaction level"]

    B2 --> C4["Optimum bitumen content"]
    B2 --> C5["Air voids control 3 to 5 percent"]
    B2 --> C6["Bitumen film thickness 8 to 15 micrometre"]

    B3 --> C7["Penetration grade selection"]
    B3 --> C8["VG 10 for cold regions"]

    B4 --> C9["Higher binder volume"]
    B4 --> C10["Asphalt Institute equation Nf"]

    B5 --> C11["Polished Stone Value greater than 55"]
    B5 --> C12["Macro texture depth greater than 0.6 mm"]

    B6 --> C13["Dense graded aggregate"]
    B6 --> C14["Air voids below 5 percent"]

    B7 --> C15["Mix temperature control"]
    B7 --> C16["Aggregate shape rounded versus angular"]

    B8 --> C17["Minimum acceptable binder content"]
    B8 --> C18["Use of local aggregates"]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block — Volumetric Parameter Inter-Relationship

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    D1["Bulk Specific Gravity Gmb"] --> D3["Air Voids Va"]
    D2["Theoretical Maximum Specific Gravity Gmm"] --> D3
    D4["Aggregate percent Ps"] --> D5["VMA"]
    D6["Aggregate Bulk Sp Gr Gsb"] --> D5
    D1 --> D5
    D3 --> D7["VFB"]
    D5 --> D7
    D8["Stability kN"] --> D9["Stiffness Ratio kN per mm"]
    D10["Flow mm"] --> D9
    D3 --> D11["Spec Compliance Check 3 to 5 percent"]
    D5 --> D11
    D7 --> D11
    D9 --> D11
    D11 --> D12["Optimum Bitumen Content OBC"]
    D12 --> D13["Approved Mix for Site Use"]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block — Marshall Mix Design Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    F1["Select 5 binder contents"] --> F2["Prepare 3 specimens each 25 total"]
    F2 --> F3["Determine Gmb and Gmm"]
    F3 --> F4["Compute Va VMA VFB for all"]
    F4 --> F5["Plot graphs versus binder content"]
    F5 --> F6["Read peaks and targets"]
    F6 --> F7{"Va equals 4 percent?"}
    F7 -- YES --> F8["Read OBC from graph"]
    F7 -- NO --> F9["Adjust aggregate gradation"]
    F9 --> F1
    F8 --> F10{"All specs satisfied?"}
    F10 -- YES --> F11["Finalize OBC and report"]
    F10 -- NO --> F12["Reject and redesign"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*List any **four** essential requirements of a bituminous mix used in flexible pavement construction.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per correct point × 4, 1 mark for accuracy):**
The four essential requirements of a bituminous mix are:
1. **Stability** — Resistance to deformation (rutting, shoving) under traffic loads.
2. **Durability** — Resistance to weathering, oxidation of binder, and disintegration of aggregate.
3. **Impermeability** — Resistance to infiltration of water and air that would damage the underlying layers.
4. **Flexibility** — Ability to bend slightly under load and temperature changes without cracking.

*(Other acceptable answers: Skid Resistance, Fatigue Resistance, Workability, Economy.)*

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define **VMA** and **VFB**. State their typical specification ranges as per MoRTH.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **VMA (Voids in Mineral Aggregate)** is the sum of air voids and the volume of bitumen in a compacted mix, expressed as a percentage of the total mix volume. It represents the inter-granular void space available to accommodate the binder and air.
$$\text{VMA} = 100 - \dfrac{P_s \cdot G_{mb}}{G_{sb}}$$
- **VFB (Voids Filled with Bitumen)** is the percentage of the VMA that is filled with effective bitumen.
$$\text{VFB} = \dfrac{VMA - V_a}{VMA} \times 100$$
- **Specification range (MoRTH / IRC:111 for BC grade II, 19 mm NMAS):** VMA ≥ **14%**; VFB between **65% and 75%**.

---

### 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

#### ▶ Question A (14 Marks) — *Volumetric Calculations + Specification Check*
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, PECET746, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With the help of neat sketches, list and explain **any seven** requirements of a bituminous mix used in flexible pavements. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Marshall specimen gave the following results:
- $W_{dry} = 1230.0$ g, $W_{SSD} = 1234.0$ g, $W_{sub} = 700.0$ g
- $P_s = 93.5\%$, $P_b = 6.5\%$, $G_{sb} = 2.68$, $G_b = 1.03$
- Stability = 11.8 kN, Flow = 2.9 mm

Compute $G_{mb}$, $G_{mm}$, $V_a$, $VMA$, $VFB$ and the stiffness ratio. State whether the mix conforms to MoRTH spec for BC grade II. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Seven Requirements (7 marks — 1 mark per requirement):**

1. **Stability** — Ability to resist permanent deformation (rutting, corrugation, shoving) caused by traffic loading. Depends on aggregate interlock (friction) and binder cohesion.
2. **Durability** — Resistance to weathering, aging of bitumen, and stripping of bitumen from aggregate due to water action. Improved by adequate binder content and lower air voids.
3. **Flexibility** — Ability to deform under load and rebound without cracking. Higher penetration-grade binders provide more flexibility.
4. **Fatigue Resistance** — Resistance to cracking under repeated traffic loads. Higher binder content and adequate VMA improve fatigue life.
5. **Skid Resistance** — Surface texture and aggregate polish resistance to provide adequate friction for vehicles, especially in wet conditions. PSV ≥ 55 and texture depth ≥ 0.6 mm are typical.
6. **Impermeability** — Resistance to water and air ingress into the pavement structure. Achieved by dense gradation and field air voids of 3–5%.
7. **Workability** — Ease of mixing, transporting, placing, and compacting. Controlled by mix temperature, binder content, and aggregate angularity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sketch requirements:** A simple labelled cross-section of a flexible pavement with arrows indicating "wheel load → stability," "water → impermeability," "thermal expansion → flexibility" is sufficient for full marks.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks — 1 mark for each correct value + 1 mark for the final comment):**

**Step 1:** $G_{mb} = \dfrac{1230.0}{1234.0 - 700.0} = \dfrac{1230.0}{534.0} = \mathbf{2.303}$

**Step 2:** $G_{mm} = \dfrac{100}{\dfrac{93.5}{2.68} + \dfrac{6.5}{1.03}} = \dfrac{100}{34.89 + 6.31} = \dfrac{100}{41.20} = \mathbf{2.427}$

**Step 3:** $V_a = \dfrac{2.427 - 2.303}{2.427} \times 100 = \mathbf{4.53\%}$

**Step 4:** $VMA = 100 - \dfrac{93.5 \times 2.303}{2.68} = 100 - 80.36 = \mathbf{19.64\%}$

**Step 5:** $VFB = \dfrac{19.64 - 4.53}{19.64} \times 100 = \mathbf{76.93\%}$

**Step 6:** $\text{Stiffness Ratio} = \dfrac{11.8}{2.9} = \mathbf{4.07 \text{ kN/mm}}$

**Step 7 — Spec Compliance:**

| Property | Computed | MoRTH Spec (BC Gr-II) | Status |
|:---------|:--------:|:---------------------:|:------:|
| $V_a$ | 4.53 % | 3 – 5 % | ✅ PASS |
| $VMA$ | 19.64 % | ≥ 14 % | ✅ PASS |
| $VFB$ | 76.93 % | 65 – 75 % | ❌ FAIL (marginally high) |
| Stability | 11.8 kN | ≥ 9 kN | ✅ PASS |
| Flow | 2.9 mm | 2 – 4 mm | ✅ PASS |
| Stiffness | 4.07 kN/mm | ≥ 2.0 kN/mm | ✅ PASS |

**Conclusion:** The mix **does NOT fully conform** because VFB exceeds 75%. Recommended corrective action: **reduce the bitumen content slightly** (e.g., 5.7–5.9% by mass of mix) and re-test to obtain OBC.

---

#### ▶ Question B (14 Marks) — *Requirement Inter-relationships + Marshall Evaluation*
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, PECET746, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the **Marshall method of mix design** and the procedure for determining the **Optimum Bitumen Content (OBC)**. Briefly discuss the importance of VMA and VFB in the design. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A bituminous mix is to be designed for a heavily trafficked highway. The aggregates have $G_{sb} = 2.70$ and the binder has $G_b = 1.02$. If the mix contains **5.5% bitumen** by mass of total mix and the compacted specimen has $G_{mb} = 2.35$, calculate: (i) $G_{mm}$, (ii) $V_a$, (iii) $VMA$, (iv) $VFB$. What is the **stiffness ratio** if the Marshall stability and flow are 10.5 kN and 3.0 mm respectively? Comment on the suitability of the mix. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Marshall Method Explanation (7 marks):**
- **Step 1 (1 mark):** Select a specific aggregate gradation (e.g., 19 mm BC as per MoRTH Table 500-19). Determine $G_{sb}$ of the aggregate blend.
- **Step 2 (1 mark):** Choose 5 bitumen contents (typically 4.0, 4.5, 5.0, 5.5, 6.0% by mass of mix). For each, prepare 3 specimens = 15 total.
- **Step 3 (1 mark):** Heat aggregate and binder to mixing temperature (viscosity 170 ± 20 cSt). Mix, compact with 75 blows per face at 60 °C, and determine $G_{mb}$.
- **Step 4 (1 mark):** Measure stability (kN) and flow (mm) using the Marshall loading frame at 50 mm/min.
- **Step 5 (1 mark):** Compute $G_{mm}$, $V_a$, $VMA$, $VFB$ for each bitumen content. Plot stability, flow, $G_{mb}$, $V_a$, $VMA$, $VFB$ vs. bitumen content.
- **Step 6 (1 mark):** **OBC selection** — the bitumen content corresponding to:
   - $V_a = 4\%$ (mid of 3–5% spec),
   - Maximum stability,
   - VFB within 65–75%,
   - $VMA \geq$ minimum for that NMAS.
   - The **OBC is the average** of the bitumen contents satisfying all four conditions (if they coincide) or chosen so that all are simultaneously met.
- **Importance of VMA & VFB (1 mark):** **VMA** ensures there is enough room for the binder film (thicker film → more durable). **VFB** indicates whether the binder volume is sufficient — too low VFB means the mix is starved and prone to raveling; too high VFB means excess binder and risk of bleeding/ rutting.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $P_s = 94.5\%$, $P_b = 5.5\%$, $G_{sb} = 2.70$, $G_b = 1.02$, $G_{mb} = 2.35$, Stability = 10.5 kN, Flow = 3.0 mm.

**(i) Theoretical Maximum Specific Gravity:**
$$G_{mm} = \dfrac{100}{\dfrac{94.5}{2.70} + \dfrac{5.5}{1.02}} = \dfrac{100}{35.00 + 5.39} = \dfrac{100}{40.39} = \mathbf{2.476}$$

**(ii) Air Voids:**
$$V_a = \dfrac{2.476 - 2.35}{2.476} \times 100 = \mathbf{5.09\%}$$

**(iii) VMA:**
$$VMA = 100 - \dfrac{94.5 \times 2.35}{2.70} = 100 - 82.24 = \mathbf{17.76\%}$$

**(iv) VFB:**
$$VFB = \dfrac{17.76 - 5.09}{17.76} \times 100 = \mathbf{71.34\%}$$

**Stiffness Ratio:**
$$SR = \dfrac{10.5}{3.0} = \mathbf{3.5 \text{ kN/mm}}$$

**Comment on Suitability:**

| Property | Computed | Spec (BC Gr-II) | Status |
|:---------|:--------:|:----------------:|:------:|
| $V_a$ | 5.09 % | 3 – 5 % | ⚠ Marginally high |
| $VMA$ | 17.76 % | ≥ 14 % | ✅ PASS |
| $VFB$ | 71.34 % | 65 – 75 % | ✅ PASS |
| Stability | 10.5 kN | ≥ 9 kN | ✅ PASS |
| Flow | 3.0 mm | 2 – 4 mm | ✅ PASS |
| Stiffness | 3.5 kN/mm | ≥ 2.0 kN/mm | ✅ PASS |

**Conclusion:** The mix is **borderline acceptable**. The air voids (5.09%) are slightly above the upper 5% limit, indicating marginal under-compaction or a binder slightly below OBC. **Recommendation:** Increase field compaction effort or marginally increase binder content (e.g., 5.7%) to bring $V_a$ to the ideal 4% range.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Lose Marks Here:**
> 1. **Unit Mistakes:** Stability is in **kN** (not N or kN/m). Flow is in **mm**. Always write the unit.
> 2. **Formula Inversion:** Writing $V_a = (G_{mb}/G_{mm}) \times 100$ — this is **wrong**. The correct form is $(1 - G_{mb}/G_{mm}) \times 100$. Many students lose 2 marks on this.
> 3. **Spec Mismatch:** Quoting IRC:111 spec for BC grade II is **2.5–5%** for design Va and **3–6%** for field Va. Use the right spec for the right stage.
> 4. **Skipping Comments:** Always end numerical answers with a "**Suitable / Not Suitable / Marginal**" statement. KTU examiners allocate 1–2 marks specifically for the design conclusion.
> 5. **OBC Confusion:** OBC is determined from a **graph of all five properties**, not a single formula. Don't write "OBC = bitumen content with maximum stability" — that's only one of four criteria.

---

### 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!NOTE]
> **Read this checklist twice the night before the exam.**

- ✅ A bituminous mix must satisfy **8 requirements**: Stability, Durability, Flexibility, Fatigue Resistance, Skid Resistance, Impermeability, Workability, Economy.
- ✅ **Stability** ↑ with angular crushed aggregate and proper compaction. Mohr-Coulomb analogy: $\tau = c + \sigma \tan\phi$.
- ✅ **Durability** ↑ with optimum binder content and **film thickness 8–15 µm**.
- ✅ **Impermeability** requires **field Va between 3% and 5%**.
- ✅ **Skid Resistance** requires **PSV ≥ 55** and **texture depth ≥ 0.6 mm**.
- ✅ **VMA** is the **inter-granular void space** = air + bitumen volume. Formula: $VMA = 100 - (P_s \cdot G_{mb}/G_{sb})$.
- ✅ **VFB** is the **bitumen fraction of VMA** = $(VMA - V_a)/VMA \times 100$. Spec: **65–75%**.
- ✅ **Air Voids** = $(1 - G_{mb}/G_{mm}) \times 100$. Spec: **3–5%** design, **3–6%** field.
- ✅ **Marshall Stability** ≥ **9 kN** for heavy traffic; **Flow** in the range **2–4 mm**.
- ✅ **Stiffness Ratio** = Stability / Flow; typical ≥ 2.0 kN/mm.
- ✅ **OBC** is the bitumen content that simultaneously satisfies all 4 volumetric + 2 stability criteria.
- ✅ Permeability grows as $V_a^3$, so the 3–5% window is **strictly enforced** in the field.
- ✅ Fatigue life follows $N_f = k_1 \epsilon_t^{-k_2}$, with $k_2 \approx 4$. Stiffer mix → less fatigue life.
- ✅ The **three** main MoRTH spec values to memorize cold: **Va = 3–5%**, **VFB = 65–75%**, **Stability ≥ 9 kN** for BC.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Specifications for Bituminous Pavement Layers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET746 - HIGHWAY MATERIALS AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Specifications for Bituminous Pavement Layers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Bituminous Pavement Layer Specifications** refer to the standardized set of technical requirements—codified by the **Ministry of Road Transport and Highways (MoRTH)** and the **Indian Roads Congress (IRC)**—governing the composition, gradation, binder content, volumetric properties, mechanical performance (Marshall/Stability parameters), and construction tolerances of each bituminous layer in a flexible pavement structure.

These specifications ensure that every layer—**Bituminous Macadam (BM)**, **Dense Graded Bituminous Macadam (DBM)**, **Bituminous Concrete (BC)**, **Semi-Dense Bituminous Concrete (SDBC)**, **Surface Dressing**, and **Mastic Asphalt**—performs its designated structural and functional role over its design life of typically **15 years** for National Highways.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Focus (Dec 2023 Pattern):** The most frequently asked questions on this topic include:
> 1. Marshall Mix Design criteria and their numerical limits.
> 2. Aggregate gradation requirements for BC, DBM, SDBC.
> 3. Layer thickness and compaction specifications.
> 4. Comparison between BC, DBM, and SDBC.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine building a **layered cake** designed to support the weight of a moving truck without cracking:

- The **Base Course (DBM)** is the **thick, dense sponge layer** that absorbs the major structural load — it must be *strong and stiff*.
- The **Binder Course (BM/DBM)** acts as the **middle creamy filling** that distributes stresses evenly to the base.
- The **Wearing Course (BC/SDBC)** is the **smooth, glossy icing** on top — it must be *durable, skid-resistant, and waterproof*.
- The **Surface Dressing (Tack/Bitumen coat)** is the **thin sticky glaze** that bonds the icing to the cake.

Just as a baker must follow a **recipe** (gradation, proportions, temperature), a highway engineer must follow the **MoRTH/IRC specifications** to produce a pavement "cake" that will not deform, crack, or disintegrate under traffic and weather.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Reference Documents in KTU Curriculum:**
> - **MoRTH Specifications** (5th Revision) — for construction specifications
> - **IRC:111-2009** — Specifications for DBM
> - **IRC:29-1988** — Specifications for BC (revised)
> - **IS:73-2013** — Paving Bitumen specifications
> - **ASTM D6927 / ASTM D1559** — Marshall Test method

### 1.3 Physical Standards and Critical Metrics (Highlighted in Bold)

- **Nominal Maximum Aggregate Size (NMAS):** ranges from **9.5 mm to 37.5 mm** depending on layer.
- **Marshall Stability:** minimum **9.0 kN at 60 °C** for BC/DBM.
- **Design Traffic Range:** up to **150 MSA** (Million Standard Axles) for expressway specifications.
- **Layer Thickness Range:** **25–50 mm** for BC, **50–100 mm** for DBM, **80–250 mm** for BM.
- **Compaction Standard:** **96% of Maximum Theoretical Specific Gravity (G_mm)** for BC wearing course.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hierarchical Structure of Bituminous Pavement Layers

A standard flexible pavement consists of the following bituminous layers (top to bottom):

**Layer 1 – Wearing Course (Surface Layer)**
- **Material:** Bituminous Concrete (BC) or Mastic Asphalt
- **Function:** Resist abrasion, provide skid resistance, waterproofing
- **Thickness:** **25–40 mm** (BC) / **25–50 mm** (Mastic)

**Layer 2 – Binder Course**
- **Material:** Dense Graded Bituminous Macadam (DBM) or Semi-Dense Bituminous Concrete (SDBC)
- **Function:** Transfer load, resist rutting
- **Thickness:** **50–100 mm** (DBM) / **25–40 mm** (SDBC)

**Layer 3 – Base Course**
- **Material:** Bituminous Macadam (BM) or DBM
- **Function:** Primary load-bearing bituminous layer
- **Thickness:** **80–250 mm**

**Layer 4 – Tack Coat / Prime Coat**
- **Material:** Cationic Bitumen Emulsion (RS-1 / SS-1)
- **Function:** Bond successive bituminous layers
- **Rate:** **0.20–0.30 kg/m²** (tack coat)

### 2.2 Why Each Specification Exists — The Engineering Rationale

| Specification Parameter | Engineering Purpose |
|--------------------------|---------------------|
| **Aggregate Gradation Limits** | Ensure particle packing → controls **VMA, VFB, stability** |
| **Bitumen Content (%)** | Controls **flexibility, durability, and fatigue life** |
| **Air Voids (3–5%)** | Provides space for bitumen expansion at high temperature |
| **VMA (Min 12–15%)** | Ensures sufficient binder film thickness around aggregates |
| **VFB (65–75%)** | Indicates the degree of voids filled with bitumen |
| **Marshall Stability (≥ 9.0 kN)** | Resists plastic deformation under traffic loads |
| **Flow (2–4 mm)** | Indicates flexibility — too high = plastic; too low = brittle |
| **Retained Stability (≥ 75%)** | Measures resistance to moisture damage |
| **Filler/Bitumen Ratio (0.6–1.2)** | Prevents bitumen drainage (filler stiffens mastic) |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL: Avoid using the vertical pipe `|` symbol inside the table below — use `\vert` instead, as per protocol.**

| # | Formula / Property | Equation | Units | Standard Limit |
|---|--------------------|----------|-------|----------------|
| 1 | **Marshall Stability** | $S = F_{max}$ at 60 °C | kN | $\geq 9.0$ kN (BC/DBM) |
| 2 | **Flow Value** | $f = \Delta d$ at failure | mm | $2.0$ to $4.0$ mm |
| 3 | **Air Voids (V_a)** | $V_a = \dfrac{G_{mm} - G_{mb}}{G_{mm}} \times 100$ | % | $3.0$ to $5.0$ |
| 4 | **Voids in Mineral Aggregate (VMA)** | $VMA = 100 - \dfrac{G_{mb} \cdot P_s}{G_{sb}}$ | % | $\geq 13\%$ (12.5 mm NMAS) |
| 5 | **Voids Filled with Bitumen (VFB)** | $VFB = \dfrac{VMA - V_a}{VMA} \times 100$ | % | $65$ to $75$ |
| 6 | **Bulk Specific Gravity of Mix** | $G_{mb} = \dfrac{W_{dry}}{W_{SSD} - W_{sub}}$ | — | — |
| 7 | **Theoretical Max. Specific Gravity** | $G_{mm} = \dfrac{100}{\dfrac{P_s}{G_{sb}} + \dfrac{P_b}{G_b}}$ | — | — |
| 8 | **Compaction (Relative Density)** | $RC = \dfrac{G_{field}}{G_{lab}} \times 100$ | % | $\geq 97\%$ (BC) |
| 9 | **Tack Coat Application Rate** | $Q = 0.20 \text{ to } 0.30$ | kg/m² | — |
| 10 | **Bitumen Content (BC)** | $P_b$ | % by weight of mix | $5.0$ to $6.0\%$ |

Where:
- $G_{mm}$ = Theoretical Maximum Specific Gravity of loose mix
- $G_{mb}$ = Bulk Specific Gravity of compacted mix
- $G_{sb}$ = Bulk Specific Gravity of aggregate
- $G_b$ = Specific Gravity of bitumen
- $P_s$ = % Aggregate by weight of total mix
- $P_b$ = % Bitumen by weight of total mix

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

These specifications are not academic exercises — they are **legal and contractual documents** in India. Every National Highway, State Highway, and airport pavement in India is built as per MoRTH specifications. A deviation from Marshall Stability limits, for example, results in **rutting** under heavy truck axles, while excessive air voids cause **raveling and oxidation aging** of the binder.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Relationship between VMA, V_a, and VFB

The volumetric composition of a compacted bituminous mix consists of three phases:
1. **Aggregate volume** (including impermeable solids and permeable voids)
2. **Effective binder volume** (bitumen absorbed + effective binder)
3. **Air voids** (V_a)

**Step 1 — Definition of VMA**
VMA represents the total volume of inter-granular space between compacted aggregate particles, regardless of whether filled with air or bitumen.

$$
VMA = \text{Volume of air voids} + \text{Volume of effective binder}
$$

**Step 2 — Expressing in terms of Mix Specific Gravity**

Consider 1 unit mass of compacted mix. The volume occupied by aggregate is:

$$
V_{agg} = \frac{P_s}{G_{sb} \cdot \rho_w}
$$

The total volume of the compacted specimen is:

$$
V_{total} = \frac{1}{G_{mb} \cdot \rho_w}
$$

Therefore, by definition:

$$
VMA = \left(1 - \frac{P_s \cdot G_{mb}}{G_{sb}}\right) \times 100 \quad (\text{in \%})
$$

**Step 3 — Deriving V_a**
The theoretical maximum density $G_{mm}$ represents the mix with **zero air voids** (all inter-aggregate space filled with binder). The actual bulk density $G_{mb}$ is lower due to entrained air:

$$
V_a = \frac{G_{mm} - G_{mb}}{G_{mm}} \times 100
$$

**Step 4 — Deriving VFB (Voids Filled with Bitumen)**
VFB is the proportion of the VMA that is occupied by effective bitumen:

$$
\begin{aligned}
VFB &= \frac{\text{Volume of effective bitumen in VMA}}{\text{Total VMA}} \times 100 \\
VFB &= \frac{VMA - V_a}{VMA} \times 100
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Physical Interpretation**
- If $V_a$ is too high → mix is too dry, prone to raveling, oxidation, and cracking.
- If $V_a$ is too low → mix is plastic, prone to rutting and bleeding (bitumen flushing).
- If $VMA$ is too low → insufficient binder film thickness → premature cracking.
- If $VFB$ is too low → mix is open and porous; if too high → mix bleeds.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Specification Comparison Across Layers

| Specification Parameter | Bituminous Macadam (BM) | DBM | BC | SDBC | Mastic Asphalt |
|--------------------------|--------------------------|-----|-----|------|-----------------|
| **NMAS (mm)** | $26.5$ / $37.5$ | $19$ / $26.5$ / $37.5$ | $9.5$ / $13.2$ | $13.2$ | $13.2$ / $19$ |
| **Layer Thickness (mm)** | $80$ to $250$ | $50$ to $100$ | $25$ to $40$ | $25$ to $40$ | $25$ to $50$ |
| **Bitumen Grade** | VG-30 / VG-40 | VG-30 / VG-40 | VG-30 / VG-40 | VG-30 | Modified (CRMB/PMB) |
| **Bitumen Content (%)** | $3.5$ to $4.5$ | $4.0$ to $5.0$ | $5.0$ to $6.0$ | $4.5$ to $5.5$ | $7.0$ to $9.0$ |
| **Marshall Stability (kN)** | $\geq 5.0$ | $\geq 9.0$ | $\geq 9.0$ | $\geq 8.0$ | — |
| **Flow (mm)** | $2$ to $4$ | $2$ to $4$ | $2$ to $4$ | $2$ to $4$ | — |
| **Air Voids (%)** | $3$ to $6$ | $3$ to $5$ | $3$ to $5$ | $3$ to $5$ | $\leq 1.0$ |
| **VMA (%)** | $\geq 12$ | $\geq 13$ | $\geq 14$ | $\geq 14$ | $\geq 18$ |
| **VFB (%)** | $60$ to $75$ | $65$ to $75$ | $65$ to $75$ | $65$ to $75$ | $70$ to $85$ |
| **Compaction (% G_mm)** | $\geq 95$ | $\geq 96$ | $\geq 97$ | $\geq 96$ | $\geq 97$ |
| **Filler/Bitumen Ratio** | $\leq 1.0$ | $\leq 1.2$ | $0.6$ to $1.2$ | $0.6$ to $1.2$ | $2.0$ to $4.0$ |
| **Los Angeles Abrasion** | $\leq 35\%$ | $\leq 30\%$ | $\leq 25\%$ | $\leq 30\%$ | $\leq 25\%$ |
| **Aggregate Impact Value** | $\leq 30\%$ | $\leq 24\%$ | $\leq 18\%$ | $\leq 22\%$ | $\leq 18\%$ |

### 3.3 Worked Example: Air Voids Calculation

**Problem:** A compacted BC mix has $G_{mb} = 2.412$ and $G_{mm} = 2.498$. Compute the air void content.

**Solution:**

$$
V_a = \frac{G_{mm} - G_{mb}}{G_{mm}} \times 100
$$

Substituting:

$$
V_a = \frac{2.498 - 2.412}{2.498} \times 100
$$

$$
V_a = \frac{0.086}{2.498} \times 100
$$

$$
V_a = 3.44 \%
$$

This value lies within the specified range of $3\%$ to $5\%$ for BC. **Result: Mix is acceptable.**
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Bituminous Pavement Layer Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Layer0["Tractor Truck Axle Load 80 kN to 120 kN"]
    Layer1["WEARING COURSE - Bituminous Concrete BC 25 mm to 40 mm"]
    Layer2["TACK COAT - Cationic Emulsion RS1 0.20 kg per sqm to 0.30 kg per sqm"]
    Layer3["BINDER COURSE - DBM or SDBC 50 mm to 100 mm"]
    Layer4["TACK COAT - Cationic Emulsion 0.20 kg per sqm to 0.30 kg per sqm"]
    Layer5["BASE COURSE - BM or DBM 80 mm to 250 mm"]
    Layer6["PRIME COAT - Cutback Bitumen MC 30 0.60 kg per sqm to 0.90 kg per sqm"]
    Layer7["SUBBASE - Granular WMM 100 mm to 300 mm"]
    Layer8["SUBGRADE - Compacted Natural Soil CBR greater than 8 percent"]
    
    Layer0 --> Layer1
    Layer1 --> Layer2
    Layer2 --> Layer3
    Layer3 --> Layer4
    Layer4 --> Layer5
    Layer5 --> Layer6
    Layer6 --> Layer7
    Layer7 --> Layer8
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Marshall Mix Design Volumetric System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUTS["INPUTS REQUIRED"]
        AggProp["Aggregate Properties Gsb and Gradation"]
        BitProp["Bitumen Specific Gravity Gb and Grade"]
    end
    
    subgraph TESTDATA["MARSHALL TEST DATA"]
        WtDry["Dry Mass in air Wdry"]
        WtSSD["Saturated Surface Dry Mass Wssd"]
        WtSub["Submerged Mass Wsub"]
    end
    
    subgraph COMPUTE["COMPUTATIONAL STAGE"]
        Gmb["Compute Gmb Bulk Specific Gravity"]
        Vma["Compute VMA Voids in Mineral Aggregate"]
        Va["Compute Va Air Voids percentage"]
        Vfb["Compute VFB Voids Filled with Bitumen"]
        Stab["Compute Stability kN at 60 deg C"]
        Flow["Compute Flow mm"]
    end
    
    subgraph CHECK["SPECIFICATION VERIFICATION"]
        VaCheck["Va between 3 percent and 5 percent"]
        VmaCheck["VMA greater than 13 percent"]
        VfbCheck["VFB between 65 percent and 75 percent"]
        StabCheck["Stability greater than 9.0 kN"]
        FlowCheck["Flow between 2 mm and 4 mm"]
    end
    
    RESULT["ACCEPT MIX or REJECT MIX"]
    
    AggProp --> Gmb
    BitProp --> Gmb
    WtDry --> Gmb
    WtSSD --> Gmb
    WtSub --> Gmb
    
    Gmb --> Vma
    Gmb --> Va
    Vma --> Vfb
    Va --> Vfb
    
    Gmb --> StabCheck
    Gmb --> FlowCheck
    Va --> VaCheck
    Vma --> VmaCheck
    Vfb --> VfbCheck
    
    VaCheck --> RESULT
    VmaCheck --> RESULT
    VfbCheck --> RESULT
    StabCheck --> RESULT
    FlowCheck --> RESULT
```

### 4.3 Spec Selection Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["Start: Select Bituminous Layer Specification"]
    Q1{"Traffic Loading MSA?"}
    L1["LT greater than 150 MSA - Expressway Spec"]
    Q2{"Wearing Course Required?"}
    L2["BC 25 to 40 mm Grade 1"]
    L3["SDBC 25 to 40 mm Grade 2"]
    Q3{"Heavy Axle Loads?"}
    L4["DBM 50 to 100 mm with Modified Bitumen"]
    L5["Conventional DBM with VG-30 Bitumen"]
    
    Start --> Q1
    Q1 -->|Yes| L1
    Q1 -->|No| Q2
    Q2 -->|Yes| L2
    Q2 -->|No| L3
    L2 --> Q3
    L3 --> Q3
    Q3 -->|Yes| L4
    Q3 -->|No| L5
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Safeguard Applied:** All node IDs use alphanumeric prefixes (AggProp, BitProp, VaCheck, etc.) to avoid reserved keyword conflicts. Labels use plain uppercase text without bold/italic markdown formatting.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**List the Marshall Stability and Flow requirements for Bituminous Concrete (BC) wearing course as per MoRTH specifications.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
As per MoRTH 5th Revision specifications for BC wearing course:
1. **Marshall Stability:** minimum **9.0 kN** at **60 °C** test temperature, compacted with **75 blows per face** (heavy traffic).
2. **Flow value:** between **2.0 mm and 4.0 mm**.
3. **Air voids:** between **3% and 5%** of total mix volume.

The 75-blow compaction simulates heavy traffic densification, and the 60 °C test temperature corresponds to the highest pavement temperature experienced in tropical Indian conditions.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Distinguish between Bituminous Macadam (BM) and Dense Graded Bituminous Macadam (DBM) in three points.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| # | Bituminous Macadam (BM) | Dense Graded Bituminous Macadam (DBM) |
|---|--------------------------|----------------------------------------|
| 1 | Open-graded structure, **VFB 60–75%** | Dense-graded, **VFB 65–75%** with tighter gradation |
| 2 | Used as **base course** in light-to-medium traffic | Used as **base/binder course** in heavy-traffic NH/SH |
| 3 | NMAS up to **37.5 mm**, thickness **80–250 mm** | NMAS up to **37.5 mm**, thickness **50–100 mm** per layer |

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]*

**Q. (a)** Explain with a neat sketch the different bituminous pavement layers used in a flexible pavement as per MoRTH specifications. State the material, thickness range, and function of each layer. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A dense graded bituminous macadam mix has the following properties: $G_{mb} = 2.410$, $G_{mm} = 2.495$, $G_{sb} = 2.685$, bitumen content $P_b = 4.8\%$, and aggregate proportion $P_s = 95.2\%$. Determine the **Air Voids (V_a)**, **VMA**, and **VFB**. Comment on whether the mix satisfies MoRTH specifications for DBM. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

### Part (a) — Bituminous Pavement Layers (7 marks)

The flexible pavement structure (from top to bottom) consists of:

1. **Wearing Course (Bituminous Concrete BC)** — Thickness **25–40 mm**; NMAS **9.5–13.2 mm**; bitumen **5.0–6.0%**. Function: resists abrasion, provides skid resistance, waterproofs the pavement.

2. **Tack Coat** — Cationic bitumen emulsion at **0.20–0.30 kg/m²**. Function: provides bond between successive bituminous layers.

3. **Binder Course (DBM or SDBC)** — Thickness **50–100 mm** (DBM) or **25–40 mm** (SDBC); NMAS **19–26.5 mm**; bitumen **4.0–5.0%**. Function: distributes wheel load and reduces stress on base course.

4. **Base Course (BM or DBM)** — Thickness **80–250 mm** (BM); bitumen **3.5–4.5%**. Function: primary load-bearing bituminous layer.

5. **Prime Coat** — Cutback bitumen (MC-30) at **0.60–0.90 kg/m²**. Function: seals granular base and bonds it to bituminous layer.

6. **Sub-base (Granular WMM)** — Thickness **100–300 mm**. Function: additional load distribution, drainage.

7. **Subgrade** — Compacted natural soil (CBR ≥ 8%).

### Part (b) — Volumetric Calculations (7 marks)

**Given Data:**
- $G_{mb} = 2.410$
- $G_{mm} = 2.495$
- $G_{sb} = 2.685$
- $P_b = 4.8\%$
- $P_s = 95.2\%$

---

**Step 1 — Air Voids (V_a):**

$$
V_a = \frac{G_{mm} - G_{mb}}{G_{mm}} \times 100
$$

$$
V_a = \frac{2.495 - 2.410}{2.495} \times 100
$$

$$
V_a = \frac{0.085}{2.495} \times 100 = 3.41\%
$$

**V_a = 3.41%** ✓ (MoRTH spec: 3% to 5%) — **SATISFIED**

---

**Step 2 — Voids in Mineral Aggregate (VMA):**

$$
VMA = 100 - \frac{P_s \cdot G_{mb}}{G_{sb}}
$$

$$
VMA = 100 - \frac{95.2 \times 2.410}{2.685}
$$

$$
VMA = 100 - \frac{229.432}{2.685}
$$

$$
VMA = 100 - 85.45 = 14.55\%
$$

**VMA = 14.55%** ✓ (MoRTH spec for 19 mm NMAS: ≥ 13%) — **SATISFIED**

---

**Step 3 — Voids Filled with Bitumen (VFB):**

$$
VFB = \frac{VMA - V_a}{VMA} \times 100
$$

$$
VFB = \frac{14.55 - 3.41}{14.55} \times 100
$$

$$
VFB = \frac{11.14}{14.55} \times 100 = 76.56\%
$$

**VFB = 76.56%** ✗ (MoRTH spec: 65% to 75%) — **MARGINALLY EXCEEDS**

---

**Conclusion:**
The mix satisfies **Air Voids** and **VMA** requirements but **VFB slightly exceeds** the upper limit of 75%, indicating the mix is slightly rich in bitumen. To rectify, the bitumen content should be reduced by approximately **0.2–0.3%** and the mix re-verified.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q. (a)** What are the aggregate and bitumen specifications for a Bituminous Concrete (BC) wearing course as per MoRTH? List the quality tests required for aggregates and bitumen. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The following data are obtained from a Marshall Stability test on a BC mix:
- Stability = 11.5 kN
- Flow = 3.0 mm
- $G_{mb} = 2.420$, $G_{mm} = 2.500$, $G_{sb} = 2.670$
- $P_b = 5.2\%$, $P_s = 94.8\%$

Check whether the mix meets MoRTH specification limits for BC. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

### Part (a) — Aggregate & Bitumen Specifications for BC (7 marks)

**Aggregate Requirements:**
1. **Los Angeles Abrasion Value:** ≤ **25%** (IS:2386 Part-IV)
2. **Aggregate Impact Value (AIV):** ≤ **18%**
3. **Aggregate Crushing Value:** ≤ **24%**
4. **Flakiness Index:** ≤ **25%**
5. **Elongation Index:** ≤ **15%**
6. **Water Absorption:** ≤ **2%**
7. **Soundness (5 cycles Na₂SO₄):** ≤ **12%**
8. **Stripping Value (coated aggregate):** ≥ **95%**

**Bitumen Requirements:**
1. **Grade:** VG-30 or VG-40 (viscosity grade, IS:73-2013)
2. **Penetration at 25 °C:** **50–70** (for VG-30)
3. **Softening Point (R&B):** ≥ **47 °C**
4. **Flash Point (Cleveland Open Cup):** ≥ **220 °C**
5. **Ductility at 25 °C:** ≥ **75 cm**
6. **Specific Gravity:** ≥ **0.99**

**Quality Tests:**
- Los Angeles Abrasion Test, AIV, ACV
- Stripping Test, Soundness Test
- Penetration, Softening Point, Ductility, Viscosity, Flash & Fire Point

### Part (b) — Marshall Mix Verification (7 marks)

**Given:**
- Stability = 11.5 kN, Flow = 3.0 mm
- $G_{mb} = 2.420$, $G_{mm} = 2.500$, $G_{sb} = 2.670$
- $P_b = 5.2\%$, $P_s = 94.8\%$

**Step 1 — Air Voids:**

$$
V_a = \frac{2.500 - 2.420}{2.500} \times 100 = 3.20\%
$$

Spec: 3% to 5% → **SATISFIED** ✓

**Step 2 — VMA:**

$$
VMA = 100 - \frac{94.8 \times 2.420}{2.670} = 100 - 85.93 = 14.07\%
$$

Spec: ≥ 14% for 13.2 mm NMAS → **SATISFIED (marginally)** ✓

**Step 3 — VFB:**

$$
VFB = \frac{14.07 - 3.20}{14.07} \times 100 = 77.25\%
$$

Spec: 65% to 75% → **EXCEEDS upper limit by 2.25%** ✗

**Step 4 — Stability Check:** 11.5 kN ≥ 9.0 kN → **SATISFIED** ✓

**Step 5 — Flow Check:** 3.0 mm is within 2.0 to 4.0 mm → **SATISFIED** ✓

**Conclusion:** Stability and Flow are excellent, V_a and VMA are acceptable, but **VFB exceeds** the limit. Bitumen content should be reduced by ~0.3% to bring VFB within range.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake #1:** Students often confuse **VMA** with **V_a**. Remember: **VMA is for aggregate structure (with no air)**, **V_a is for air voids in mix**.
> 2. **Common Mistake #2:** Using $G_{mb}$ instead of $G_{sb}$ in VMA formula — this is a **3-mark deduction** in KTU valuation.
> 3. **Common Mistake #3:** Forgetting to comment on whether the result **passes/fails** the specification — marks are reserved for the engineering judgment step.
> 4. **Common Mistake #4:** Confusing **Marshall Stability (kN)** with **CBR (%)** — they are different tests for different materials.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Specifications for Bituminous Pavement Layers**

- **BC (Wearing Course):** NMAS 9.5–13.2 mm, Stability ≥ 9.0 kN, VFB 65–75%, thickness 25–40 mm.
- **DBM (Binder/Base Course):** NMAS 19–37.5 mm, Stability ≥ 9.0 kN, VMA ≥ 13%, thickness 50–100 mm.
- **BM (Base Course):** Open-graded, NMAS 26.5–37.5 mm, Stability ≥ 5.0 kN, thickness 80–250 mm.
- **SDBC (Intermediate Layer):** NMAS 13.2 mm, semi-dense, VFB 65–75%, thickness 25–40 mm.
- **Mastic Asphalt:** Dense, gap-graded, zero air voids, used for bridge decks, heavy-duty surfacing.
- **Surface Dressing:** Single or double coat of bitumen + chips; sealing and surface renewal.
- **Tack Coat Rate:** 0.20–0.30 kg/m² (Cationic Emulsion RS-1/SS-1).
- **Prime Coat Rate:** 0.60–0.90 kg/m² (Cutback MC-30).
- **Compaction Standard:** ≥ 97% of $G_{mm}$ for BC, ≥ 96% for DBM.
- **Marshal Stability Test:** Performed at **60 °C**, **75 blows/face** for heavy traffic.
- **VMA** depends on **NMAS** — larger stones require more binder film thickness.
- **VFB > 75%** = bleeding-prone mix (rich in bitumen).
- **VFB < 65%** = dry mix (prone to raveling and oxidation).
- **Modified Binders (CRMB/PMB)** are specified for expressways and heavy-traffic corridors.
- **Layer order is non-negotiable** — wearing course always on top, subgrade always at bottom.
- **MoRTH 5th Revision** is the controlling document for all bituminous specifications in India.
- **IRC:111-2009** governs DBM; **IS:73-2013** governs bitumen grade classification.
- **Most repeated KTU question:** "Compute V_a, VMA, VFB and comment on the mix."
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Grading of Aggregates
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET746 - HIGHWAY MATERIALS AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Grading of Aggregates

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Grading of Aggregates** is defined as the distribution of particle sizes within an aggregate sample, expressed as the percentage of material passing through (or retained on) a standard set of IS sieves arranged in descending order of aperture size. It is a fundamental index property that governs the mechanical performance, workability, and durability of bituminous and non-bituminous pavement layers.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Highway Materials and Design** syllabus, grading is treated as a *design-controlled property* — not an inherent material property. Engineers deliberately **blend (combine) two or more aggregate fractions** (e.g., coarse aggregate, fine aggregate, and filler) to achieve a *target gradation envelope* specified by regulatory codes such as **MoRTH (Ministry of Road Transport and Highways)**, **IRC (Indian Roads Congress)**, and **IS 383:2016**.

The grading is quantified by passing a representative aggregate sample through a **standard IS sieve stack** (e.g., 26.5 mm, 20 mm, 13.2 mm, 9.5 mm, 4.75 mm, 2.36 mm, 600 µm, 300 µm, 75 µm) and recording cumulative weight retained on each sieve.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy:** Imagine packing a suitcase for a long vacation. If you only bring large sweaters, the suitcase has many empty gaps and is floppy. If you only bring tiny socks, the suitcase becomes a tight, dense ball but uncomfortable to use. The *art of packing* is to mix large, medium, and small items so that small ones fill the gaps between large ones, maximizing density and stability.

Aggregate grading works identically. A **well-graded** aggregate is like a perfectly packed suitcase:
- **Large stones** form the skeleton (resist load).
- **Medium stones** fill medium gaps.
- **Sand** fills small gaps.
- **Filler (dust < 75 µm)** fills the tiniest voids and binds everything together with bitumen.

A **poorly-graded** (uniformly graded or gap-graded) aggregate leaves behind large empty voids, which leads to:
- Higher bitumen consumption (waste of money).
- Reduced density and strength.
- Higher permeability (water ingress → pavement failure).

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (KTU 2024 Highlight)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Maximum Size of Aggregate (MSA):** The smallest sieve opening through which **100%** of the aggregate particles pass. Standard values: **26.5 mm, 20 mm, 13.2 mm, 10 mm, 6 mm**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Nominal Maximum Size (NMS):** The smallest sieve opening through which a *major portion* (typically ≥ 90%) of the aggregate is retained. This is the size typically used in mix design and layer thickness specifications.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fineness Modulus (FM):** An empirical index number, dimensionless, that gives an *average* measure of aggregate fineness. Computed by summing cumulative percentage retained on sieves from **150 µm to 9.5 mm** and dividing by **100**.

$$FM = \frac{\sum \text{Cumulative \% Retained (9.5 mm to 150 µm)}}{100}$$

- Higher FM → **coarser** aggregate.
- Lower FM → **finer** aggregate.
- Typical FM for **fine aggregate** used in pavement works: **2.6 to 3.2**.
- Typical FM for **coarse aggregate**: **6.0 to 7.5**.

## 1.4 Types of Gradation — Quick Classification

| Gradation Type | Description | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Dense-Graded (Well-Graded)** | Continuous particle size distribution from coarse to fine | Dense Bituminous Macadam (DBM), Bituminous Concrete (BC), Wet Mix Macadam (WMM) |
| **Gap-Graded** | Missing one or more intermediate sizes | Stone Matrix Asphalt (SMA), some open-textured friction courses |
| **Open-Graded** | Mostly coarse aggregate, very little fines | Open-Graded Friction Course (OGFC), drainage layers |
| **Uniformly Graded (Poorly Graded)** | Particles of nearly one size | Sub-base, filter layers, railway ballast |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Why Gradation Matters

The mechanical behavior of a pavement layer is dictated by **inter-particle contact**, **void structure**, and **stress distribution**. Gradation controls all three:

1. **Load Distribution:** A well-graded aggregate transfers wheel load through a *stone-to-stone contact network*. Coarse aggregates carry the primary compressive stress, while fines distribute it more evenly.
2. **Density (Compaction):** Smaller particles physically occupy the voids between larger particles. According to the **maximum density theory** (Fuller-Thompson ideal grading curve), the densest packing occurs when the particle size distribution follows:

$$P = 100 \times \left(\frac{d}{D}\right)^{n}$$

where:
- $P$ = % passing sieve of aperture $d$
- $D$ = nominal maximum aggregate size
- $n$ = exponent (typically **0.45** for road aggregates, **0.5** for concrete)

3. **Bitumen Demand:** Higher voids → higher bitumen requirement → higher cost. Well-graded mixes minimize voids and thus minimize bitumen consumption.
4. **Resistance to Rutting & Fatigue:** Stone-to-stone contact (high in open-graded and SMA) resists rutting. Dense-graded mixes distribute stress uniformly, resisting fatigue cracking.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Sieve Analysis Procedure (KTU Lab Manual Standard)

1. Obtain a representative aggregate sample of **minimum 5 kg** (for 20 mm NMS).
2. Oven-dry the sample to **constant mass** at 110 ± 5 °C.
3. Weigh the dry sample to nearest **0.1 g** = $W_{total}$.
4. Stack sieves in **descending order**: 26.5 mm → 20 mm → 13.2 mm → 9.5 mm → 4.75 mm → 2.36 mm → 1.18 mm → 600 µm → 300 µm → 150 µm → 75 µm + pan.
5. Place the sample on the top sieve, cover, and shake on a **mechanical sieve shaker for 10 minutes**.
6. Weigh the material **retained on each sieve** to nearest 0.1 g.
7. Calculate % retained, cumulative % retained, and % passing for each sieve.
8. Cumulative weight retained should match $W_{total}$ within **± 1%** (loss tolerance).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Range | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | % Retained on sieve $i$ | $R_i = \frac{W_i}{W_{total}} \times 100$ | % | Individual sieve |
| 2 | Cumulative % Retained | $CR_i = \sum_{j=1}^{i} R_j$ | % | Up to sieve $i$ |
| 3 | Cumulative % Passing | $P_i = 100 - CR_i$ | % | Plot on gradation chart |
| 4 | Fineness Modulus | $FM = \frac{\sum CR_{9.5 \text{ mm to } 150 \text{ µm}}}{100}$ | Dimensionless | Index of fineness |
| 5 | Fuller-Thompson Maximum Density Curve | $P = 100 \left(\frac{d}{D}\right)^{0.45}$ | % | Target grading |
| 6 | 0.45 Power Maximum Density Line (MDL) | $\log(\% \text{ passing}) = n \log(d) + \log(100) - n \log(D)$ | log-log plot | Visual gradation check |
| 7 | Voids in Mineral Aggregate (VMA) | $VMA = 100 - \frac{G_{mb} \times P_s}{G_{sb}}$ | % | Mix design |
| 8 | Voids Filled with Bitumen (VFB) | $VFB = \frac{VMA - V_a}{VMA} \times 100$ | % | Mix design |
| 9 | Specific Gravity (Apparent, Bulk) | $G_s = \frac{W_{dry}}{(W_{SSD} - W_{submerged})}$ | Dimensionless | Aggregate property |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical rule for KTU board exam:** When using the 0.45 Power Gradation Chart, a mix is considered **well-graded** if the gradation curve **closely follows the 0.45 maximum density line**, generally staying within the upper and lower limits of the specified envelope without crossing the "Restricted Zone" (a forbidden band in the mid-sieve sizes).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Mix Design Selection:** Engineers select grading based on layer function — base, binder course, or wearing course.
- **Quality Control:** On-site gradation tests catch segregation in asphalt plants.
- **Performance Prediction:** Empirical performance equations (rutting, fatigue) are calibrated against gradation parameters.
- **Cost Optimization:** Right grading = minimum bitumen + maximum durability = lowest life-cycle cost.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Fuller-Thompson Maximum Density Curve

### Statement
For maximum density packing of aggregate particles, the percentage passing any sieve size $d$ is related to the nominal maximum size $D$ by a power law.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Empirical Hypothesis (Fuller & Thompson, 1907):**
Based on extensive experiments, Fuller and Thompson proposed that the cumulative percent passing a sieve of aperture $d$ is proportional to $(d/D)$ raised to an exponent:

$$P \propto \left(\frac{d}{D}\right)^{n}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Boundary Conditions:**
- At $d = D$ (the nominal maximum size), the percent passing should be **100%** because all particles pass the largest sieve (or by convention, at the NMS, 100% passes the control sieve).

Wait — let us be precise. The control point is $d = D$, and at this sieve, conventionally $P = 90$ to $100\%$ (i.e., 100% of the material is *smaller* than $D$ when we take $D$ as nominal maximum size, where 90–100% is retained *above* it). Using $P = 100$ at $d = D$:

$$100 = 100 \times \left(\frac{D}{D}\right)^{n} = 100 \times 1 = 100 \;\;\checkmark$$

- At $d = 0$ (theoretically the smallest particle), $P = 0$:

$$0 = 100 \times \left(\frac{0}{D}\right)^{n} = 0 \;\;\checkmark$$

**Step 3 — Generalize with the Exponent $n$:**

$$\boxed{P(d) = 100 \times \left(\frac{d}{D}\right)^{n}}$$

For maximum density in road construction, **$n = 0.45$** is empirically adopted (the *0.45 Power Law*). For hydraulic-cement concrete, **$n = 0.5$** is preferred.

**Step 4 — Linearization in Log-Log Coordinates (useful for the 0.45 Power Gradation Chart):**

Taking $\log_{10}$ on both sides:

$$\log P = n \log d + \log 100 - n \log D$$

$$\log P = n \log d + (2 - n \log D)$$

This is a straight line with **slope = $n = 0.45$** on a log-log plot of % passing vs. sieve size.

## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example — Sieve Analysis & FM Calculation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sample Data:** A 5,000 g dry aggregate sample is subjected to sieve analysis. The weights retained on each sieve are given below. Determine: (a) % passing on each sieve, (b) Fineness Modulus, (c) classify the gradation.

| Sieve Size (mm) | Weight Retained (g) | % Retained | Cumulative % Retained | Cumulative % Passing |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 26.5 | 0 | 0.00 | 0.00 | 100.00 |
| 19.0 | 250 | 5.00 | 5.00 | 95.00 |
| 13.2 | 750 | 15.00 | 20.00 | 80.00 |
| 9.5 | 800 | 16.00 | 36.00 | 64.00 |
| 4.75 | 1,100 | 22.00 | 58.00 | 42.00 |
| 2.36 | 800 | 16.00 | 74.00 | 26.00 |
| 1.18 | 500 | 10.00 | 84.00 | 16.00 |
| 0.600 | 300 | 6.00 | 90.00 | 10.00 |
| 0.300 | 250 | 5.00 | 95.00 | 5.00 |
| 0.150 | 150 | 3.00 | 98.00 | 2.00 |
| 0.075 | 75 | 1.50 | 99.50 | 0.50 |
| Pan | 25 | 0.50 | 100.00 | 0.00 |
| **Total** | **5,000** | **100.00** | — | — |

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Part (a) — % Passing:**
Already computed in the table above. For example, for 13.2 mm sieve:
$$P_{13.2} = 100 - CR_{13.2} = 100 - 20.00 = 80.00\%$$

**Part (b) — Fineness Modulus:**
Sum the cumulative % retained from **9.5 mm down to 150 µm** (the standard FM sieves — 0.075 mm is excluded from FM per IS 383):

$$CR_{9.5} + CR_{4.75} + CR_{2.36} + CR_{1.18} + CR_{0.600} + CR_{0.300} + CR_{0.150}$$
$$= 36.00 + 58.00 + 74.00 + 84.00 + 90.00 + 95.00 + 98.00$$
$$= 535.00$$

$$FM = \frac{535.00}{100} = \boxed{5.35}$$

Since this sample contains both coarse and fine fractions, $FM = 5.35$ suggests a **moderately coarse** combined aggregate.

**Part (c) — Classification:**
Plotting the % passing against sieve size on a 0.45 Power Gradation Chart, the curve should be compared with the 0.45 MDL (Maximum Density Line):

$$\text{At } d = 9.5 \text{ mm}: \;\; P_{MDL} = 100 \times \left(\frac{9.5}{19.0}\right)^{0.45} = 100 \times (0.5)^{0.45} \approx 73.5\%$$

The actual % passing at 9.5 mm is **64%**, which is *below* the MDL — indicating slightly **finer** material in the mid-size range. A typical **MoRTH Grade-2 Dense Bituminous Macadam (DBM)** envelope tolerates this.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Grading Specifications for Key Pavement Layers (MoRTH / IRC)

| Layer Type | IRC / MoRTH Spec | NMS (mm) | Key Control Sieves & Limits (% passing) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Granular Sub-Base (GSB)** | IRC: SP: 89-2018, MoRTH Clause 401 | 26.5 / 37.5 | Grading I, II, III (three options) |
| **Wet Mix Macadam (WMM)** | MoRTH Clause 406 | 26.5 | 26.5 mm: 100%; 4.75 mm: 30–55%; 75 µm: 0–10% |
| **Dense Bituminous Macadam (DBM)** | MoRTH Clause 505, IRC: 111-2009 | 26.5 | 26.5 mm: 100%; 13.2 mm: 56–88%; 4.75 mm: 38–54%; 75 µm: 2–8% |
| **Bituminous Concrete (BC)** | MoRTH Clause 507, IRC: 111-2009 | 13.2 | 13.2 mm: 100%; 9.5 mm: 79–100%; 4.75 mm: 60–78%; 2.36 mm: 40–58%; 75 µm: 4–10% |
| **Stone Matrix Asphalt (SMA)** | IRC: SP: 79-2008 | 13.2 / 16 | 13.2 mm: 100%; 9.5 mm: 75–95%; 4.75 mm: 30–50%; 75 µm: 8–13% (high filler) |
| **Open-Graded Friction Course (OGFC)** | MoRTH Clause 511 | 13.2 | 13.2 mm: 100%; 9.5 mm: 90–100%; 75 µm: 0–4% (very low fines) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Trend observation:** As we move from base → binder course → wearing course, the **NMS decreases** (26.5 → 13.2 mm) and the **% passing the mid-sieves increases** → the mix becomes **smoother, denser, and more impermeable** at the surface.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram — Aggregate Blending and Grading Decision Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Aggregate Available in Stockpile] --> B{Determine Use of Layer}
    B -->|Base Course| C[Select NMS 26.5 mm]
    B -->|Binder Course| D[Select NMS 26.5 mm DBM]
    B -->|Wearing Course| E[Select NMS 13.2 mm BC or 16 mm SMA]
    C --> F[Retrieve MoRTH Grading Envelope]
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G[Perform Sieve Analysis on Individual Fractions]
    G --> H[Ca20 mm, Ca10 mm, Sand, Filler]
    H --> I[Compute Individual Gradation Curves]
    I --> J{Apply Trial Blending Ratios}
    J --> K[Compute Combined Gradation]
    K --> L{Combined Gradation within Envelope?}
    L -->|No| J
    L -->|Yes| M[Verify against 0.45 Power MDL]
    M --> N{Crosses Restricted Zone?}
    N -->|Yes| O[Adjust Proportions]
    O --> J
    N -->|No| P[Final Proportions Locked]
    P --> Q[Proceed to Marshall Mix Design]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Conceptual Gradation Types

```mermaid
block-beta
    columns 4
    A["UNIFORMLY GRADED\nSingle dominant size\nHigh voids\nLow stability"]:1
    B["OPEN GRADED\nCoarse dominates\nVery few fines\nHigh permeability\nOGFC, drainage"]:1
    C["GAP GRADED\nMissing mid sizes\nStone to stone contact\nHigh rutting resistance\nSMA"]:1
    D["DENSE GRADED\nFull size spectrum\nLow voids\nHigh strength\nBC, DBM, WMM"]:1
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Chart — 0.45 Power Gradation Logic (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Log of Sieve Size d] --> B[Multiply by 0.45 slope]
    B --> C[Log of % Passing P]
    D[Anchor Point at NMS\nD = 20 mm, P = 100%] --> B
    E[Anchor Point at 0.075 mm\nP = 0 to 10%] --> B
    F[Mix Design Curve] --> G{Within Envelope?}
    G -->|Yes| H[Accept Mix]
    G -->|No| I[Reblend Aggregates]
```

## 4.4 Schematic Representation of Aggregate Packing

```mermaid
block-beta
    columns 1
    block:ROW1
        A["DENSE GRADED PACKING\nLarge stone forms skeleton\nMedium stone fills mid voids\nSand fills small voids\nFiller seals micro voids\n-> Minimum voids, max density"]
    end
    block:ROW2
        B["OPEN GRADED PACKING\nLarge stone forms skeleton\nDirect stone to stone contact\nVery few fines\n-> Connected void network for drainage"]
    end
    block:ROW3
        C["GAP GRADED PACKING\nCoarse stone dominates\nNo mid size particles\nFine filler only\n-> Skeleton stability plus mastic mortar"]
    end
```

## 4.5 0.45 Power Maximum Density Line — Conceptual Plot

```mermaid
xychart-beta
    title "0.45 Power Gradation Chart - Conceptual"
    x-axis "Sieve Size mm" [0.075, 0.3, 0.6, 1.18, 2.36, 4.75, 9.5, 13.2, 19, 26.5]
    y-axis "Cumulative Percent Passing %" 0 --> 100
    line [100, 95, 90, 84, 74, 58, 36, 20, 5, 0]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The line above represents the **0.45 Power Maximum Density Line** for $D = 26.5$ mm. Actual mix design curves are constrained to remain within **upper and lower envelopes** defined by MoRTH or IRC, while ideally *hugging* this maximum density line for optimum packing.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
**Define the term "Fineness Modulus" of aggregate. State its typical range for fine aggregate used in pavement works.**

**Model Answer:**
Fineness Modulus (FM) is an empirical, dimensionless index number that represents the mean size of particles in an aggregate sample. It is calculated as the sum of the cumulative percentages retained on standard IS sieves (from 9.5 mm down to 150 µm) divided by 100. For fine aggregate used in pavement works, the typical FM range is **2.6 to 3.2**. A higher FM indicates a coarser aggregate, while a lower FM indicates a finer one.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**Differentiate between "Maximum Size of Aggregate" (MSA) and "Nominal Maximum Size" (NMS) of aggregate.**

**Model Answer:**
The **Maximum Size of Aggregate (MSA)** is defined as the smallest sieve opening through which **100%** of the aggregate particles pass. It represents the absolute largest particle in the sample.
The **Nominal Maximum Size (NMS)** is the smallest sieve opening through which a **major portion** (typically ≥ 90%) of the aggregate is retained. NMS is the size commonly used in mix design specifications, layer thickness decisions, and Marshall/Superpave sample preparation. For example, in a DBM mix, MSA may be 26.5 mm while NMS is typically 19 mm or 20 mm.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the standard procedure for conducting **sieve analysis** of a coarse aggregate sample as per **IS 2386 (Part I) – 1963**. Mention the significance of the 0.075 mm sieve in this test. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 4,000 g sample of aggregate is sieved and the data are tabulated below. Compute the **fineness modulus** and the **% passing** on each sieve. Plot the gradation curve and comment on the suitability of the aggregate for **Wet Mix Macadam (WMM)** as per **MoRTH Clause 406**. **(7 marks)**

| Sieve (mm) | 26.5 | 19.0 | 9.5 | 4.75 | 2.36 | 0.600 | 0.075 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Wt. Retained (g) | 0 | 320 | 1,080 | 1,200 | 600 | 480 | 240 |

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

### Part (a) — Sieve Analysis Procedure (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Sample Preparation:**
A representative aggregate sample is obtained by **quartering** or by using a **sample splitter**. For aggregate of nominal maximum size 20 mm, the minimum sample weight is **5 kg** as per IS 2386 (Part I). The sample is **oven-dried** at 110 ± 5 °C until constant mass is achieved (typically 24 hours).

**Step 2 — Sieve Stack Assembly:**
A set of IS sieves is arranged in **descending order** of aperture from top to bottom. The standard stack for coarse aggregate testing is:

26.5 mm → 20 mm → 13.2 mm → 9.5 mm → 4.75 mm → 2.36 mm → 1.18 mm → 600 µm → 300 µm → 150 µm → 75 µm → Pan (receiver).

A **lid** is placed on top, and the stack is mounted on a **mechanical sieve shaker**.

**Step 3 — Shaking:**
The sample is placed on the top sieve, the lid is closed, and the assembly is shaken for a minimum of **10 minutes** (some standards require 5 minutes additional shaking if any sieve shows more than 1% loss).

**Step 4 — Weighing:**
The material retained on each sieve is carefully weighed to the nearest **0.1 g**. The total mass retained on all sieves plus the pan should equal the original sample mass within **± 1%** tolerance.

**Step 5 — Calculation:**
% Retained on each sieve = (Weight retained / Total weight) × 100. Cumulative % Retained is computed sequentially. Cumulative % Passing = 100 − Cumulative % Retained.

**Significance of the 0.075 mm sieve:**
The 75 µm sieve separates the **fines (dust/silt)** from the fine aggregate fraction. The **% passing 75 µm** is a critical control parameter in:
- Bituminous mixes (limits dust-to-bitumen ratio, affects VMA and stiffness).
- Granular sub-base (limits clay content to prevent loss of strength on wetting).
- It is **excluded** from the Fineness Modulus calculation per IS 383, but it is a key durability and mix-design parameter.

### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Tabulate the Computed Values:**

| Sieve (mm) | Wt. Retained (g) | % Retained | Cumulative % Retained | % Passing |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 26.5 | 0 | 0.00 | 0.00 | **100.00** |
| 19.0 | 320 | 8.00 | 8.00 | **92.00** |
| 9.5 | 1,080 | 27.00 | 35.00 | **65.00** |
| 4.75 | 1,200 | 30.00 | 65.00 | **35.00** |
| 2.36 | 600 | 15.00 | 80.00 | **20.00** |
| 0.600 | 480 | 12.00 | 92.00 | **8.00** |
| 0.075 | 240 | 6.00 | 98.00 | **2.00** |
| Pan (loss) | 80 | 2.00 | 100.00 | 0.00 |
| **Total** | **4,000** | **100.00** | — | — |

**Step 2 — Fineness Modulus Calculation:**

The FM uses sieves from **9.5 mm down to 150 µm**. Since 150 µm is not in the data, we include 0.075 mm result as the lower bound or stop at 0.600 mm + 0.075 mm. Strictly using **9.5, 4.75, 2.36, 0.600** (and the next standard sieve at 0.300 or 0.150 not in data):

Using available sieves 9.5, 4.75, 2.36, 0.600, 0.075 (the most common in pavement grading practice, we include 0.075 in cumulative for the lower end, though FM standardly stops at 150 µm):

$$\sum CR = 35.00 + 65.00 + 80.00 + 92.00 + 98.00 = 370.00$$

(Using 0.075 in place of 0.150 — close enough for exam)

$$FM = \frac{370.00}{100} = \boxed{3.70}$$

This FM lies within the typical range for **medium-fine combined aggregate**.

**Step 3 — Comparison with MoRTH WMM Clause 406 Specification:**

| Sieve (mm) | % Passing (Sample) | MoRTH WMM Spec | Compliance |
|---|---|---|---|
| 26.5 | 100 | 100 | ✓ |
| 19.0 | 92 | 95–100 (approx.) | Marginal — Slightly below |
| 4.75 | 35 | 30–55 | ✓ |
| 0.075 | 2.0 | 0–10 | ✓ |

**Step 4 — Comment:**
The gradation curve generally falls **within** the MoRTH WMM envelope, except marginally at the 19.0 mm sieve. The aggregate is **borderline suitable** for WMM. Slight reblending with a coarser fraction (e.g., increasing 20 mm aggregate proportion by 5–8%) would make it fully compliant. The sample also shows **adequate fines** (only 2% passing 75 µm), which is desirable to control dust generation but provides good workability.

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Internal Choice Alternative)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model]**
**(a)** Explain the **Fuller-Thompson maximum density curve** equation. With a labeled sketch, describe the **0.45 Power Gradation Chart** and its use in selecting aggregate blends for bituminous mixes. State the significance of the "Restricted Zone". **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **gradation requirements** specified by **MoRTH** for the following layers, with reference to control sieves: (i) **Granular Sub-Base (GSB)**, (ii) **Dense Bituminous Macadam (DBM)**, (iii) **Bituminous Concrete (BC)**, and (iv) **Open-Graded Friction Course (OGFC)**. Compare their NMS and dust content. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

### Part (a) — Fuller-Thompson & 0.45 Gradation Chart (7 marks)

**The Fuller-Thompson Maximum Density Curve:**
In 1907, **Fuller and Thompson** experimentally established that the densest packing of aggregate is achieved when the cumulative percent passing follows a power law with respect to particle size:

$$P = 100 \times \left(\frac{d}{D}\right)^{n}$$

where $P$ = % passing sieve of size $d$, $D$ = nominal maximum aggregate size, and $n$ = exponent. For road construction, $n = 0.45$ has been empirically standardized (originally proposed by the Federal Highway Administration). For concrete mixes, $n = 0.5$ is conventional.

**The 0.45 Power Gradation Chart:**
This chart has:
- **X-axis:** Log scale of sieve aperture (mm), with major sieves 0.075, 0.15, 0.30, 0.60, 1.18, 2.36, 4.75, 9.5, 12.5, 19, 25 mm.
- **Y-axis:** Log scale of cumulative % passing (typically 1 to 100).
- **0.45 MDL:** A straight line drawn from the origin of the smallest sieve (0.075 mm) through the 100% passing at the NMS.

**Use in Mix Design:**
A well-graded mix produces a gradation curve that **closely follows the 0.45 MDL**, ensuring maximum density, minimum voids, and optimum mechanical interlock. Engineers plot the combined gradation of blended aggregates and visually compare it to:
1. The 0.45 MDL (target ideal).
2. The MoRTH/IRC upper and lower limits (acceptable envelope).

**The "Restricted Zone":**
The **Restricted Zone** is a horizontal band on the 0.45 Power Chart, lying approximately between the **2.36 mm and 600 µm sieves** at moderate % passing values. The Highway Research Board (USA) studies (SHRP) established that gradation curves **passing through the restricted zone** tend to produce mixes with poor workability and excessive bitumen demand. While not strictly forbidden, **MoRTH specifications** generally advise that dense-graded mixes should **avoid** the restricted zone. Gap-graded mixes (e.g., SMA) intentionally bypass it.

### Part (b) — MoRTH Gradation Comparison (7 marks)

| Layer | NMS (mm) | 19/13.2 mm (% passing) | 4.75 mm (% passing) | 2.36 mm (% passing) | 75 µm (% passing) | Dust Content |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **GSB Grading II** | 26.5 / 37.5 | — | 25–55 | — | 0–10 | Moderate |
| **DBM Grade 2** | 26.5 | 56–88 (13.2) | 38–54 | 28–42 | 2–8 | Moderate |
| **BC Grade 2** | 13.2 | 79–100 (9.5) | 60–78 | 40–58 | 4–10 | Higher |
| **OGFC** | 13.2 | 90–100 (9.5) | 30–50 | 12–20 | 0–4 | Very low |

**Comparative Discussion:**
- **NMS decreases** progressively from the sub-base to the wearing course, producing **smoother surface texture**.
- **% passing 4.75 mm** is **lowest in OGFC** (open structure) and **highest in BC** (dense, smooth).
- **Dust content (75 µm)** is **critical** in bituminous mixes — too little causes drainage of bitumen into coarse aggregate voids (leading to **ravelling**); too much makes the mix stiff and crack-prone. The ideal dust-to-bitumen ratio is **0.6 to 1.2**.
- **OGFC** is intentionally gap/open graded with very low dust — it drains water off the surface to reduce hydroplaning but requires stiff, modified bitumen to prevent ravelling.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusion between % retained and % passing:** Many students interchange these. Always: **Cumulative % Passing = 100 − Cumulative % Retained**. The board examiner deducts 1 mark for this error.
> 2. **Forgetting the 0.075 mm sieve in FM:** FM is calculated from **9.5 mm to 150 µm** (i.e., 150 µm is the *last* sieve in FM). The 75 µm sieve is *excluded*. However, for bituminous mixes, 75 µm is separately critical.
> 3. **Mixing up Fuller (0.45) and Talbot (0.5) exponents:** The board gives 2 marks if you write the correct exponent. Fuller = **0.45 for road**; Talbot = **0.5 for concrete**.
> 4. **Not stating the standard reference:** Always cite **IS 2386 (Part I)**, **IS 383:2016**, **MoRTH 5th Revision**, and **IRC: 111-2009** wherever grading is discussed. Citing the correct code adds **1 mark** in the *apply* level of Bloom's taxonomy.
> 5. **Restricted Zone mistake:** It is a **forbidden** zone (or to be **avoided**), not a *target* zone. Students lose 1 mark for stating that the mix *should* pass through it.
> 6. **Skipping the gradation envelope comparison:** For numerical problems, merely computing FM is not enough. You **must** compare each sieve result with the corresponding MoRTH/IRC limit to receive full 7 marks in the numerical question.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY SUMMARY — Grading of Aggregates**

- **Grading** = particle size distribution expressed as % passing standard IS sieves.
- **MSA** = smallest sieve that passes **100%** of aggregate.
- **NMS** = smallest sieve retaining a **major portion** (≥ 90%).
- **Fineness Modulus (FM)** = Σ cumulative % retained (9.5 mm to 150 µm) / 100; dimensionless; typical fine-aggregate FM = **2.6–3.2**.
- **Fuller-Thompson Equation:** $P = 100(d/D)^{0.45}$ for maximum density road mixes; $n = 0.5$ for concrete.
- **0.45 Power Gradation Chart:** log-log plot with **0.45 slope** straight line; well-graded mixes hug this line.
- **Restricted Zone** lies between **2.36 mm and 600 µm** at moderate % passing; **avoid** for dense-graded mixes.
- **Types of gradation:** Uniformly graded, Open-graded, Gap-graded, **Dense (well) graded**.
- **Sieve stack (descending):** 26.5 → 20 → 13.2 → 9.5 → 4.75 → 2.36 → 1.18 → 0.600 → 0.300 → 0.150 → 0.075 mm → Pan.
- **Sieve analysis procedure per IS 2386 (Part I):** dry sample → sieve stack → mechanical shaking **10 min** → weigh retained → compute % passing.
- **WMM (MoRTH 406):** NMS 26.5 mm; 4.75 mm: 30–55%; 75 µm: 0–10%.
- **DBM (MoRTH 505):** NMS 26.5 mm; 13.2 mm: 56–88%; 4.75 mm: 38–54%; 75 µm: 2–8%.
- **BC (MoRTH 507):** NMS 13.2 mm; 9.5 mm: 79–100%; 4.75 mm: 60–78%; 75 µm: 4–10%.
- **SMA (IRC SP 79):** Gap-graded, 75 µm: 8–13% (high filler content).
- **OGFC (MoRTH 511):** NMS 13.2 mm; 75 µm: 0–4% (very low fines for drainage).
- **Dust-to-bitumen ratio:** ideal range **0.6 to 1.2** for dense-graded bituminous mixes.
- **NMS trend:** decreases from sub-base (26.5 mm) to wearing course (13.2 mm).
- **Engineering purpose of grading:** controls density, voids, workability, strength, durability, and bitumen demand.
- **Key standards to memorize:** IS 2386 (Part I) — test; IS 383:2016 — specification; MoRTH 5th Revision — pavement construction; IRC: 111-2009 — bituminous mix design.

topics.title: Design of Bituminous Mixes Using Marshall Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET746 - HIGHWAY MATERIALS AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Design of Bituminous Mixes Using Marshall Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Marshall Method of Bituminous Mix Design** is an **empirical, laboratory-based, trial-and-error procedure** standardized by the **Asphalt Institute (MS-2)** and adopted by the **Ministry of Road Transport and Highways (MoRTH) / IRC:111-2009** in India, used to determine the **Optimum Binder Content (OBC)** of a hot-mix asphalt (HMA) paving mixture. The procedure evaluates compacted cylindrical specimens (101.6 mm diameter × 63.5 mm height) under a diametral compressive load applied through curved loading strips at a constant deformation rate of **50.8 mm/minute**, at a controlled test temperature of **60 °C**, and measures two key engineering parameters: **Marshall Stability (kN)** and **Marshall Flow (mm)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Memorize Verbatim):**
> *"Marshall Stability is the maximum load resisted by the compacted bituminous specimen at 60 °C when loaded diametrically at a constant rate of 50.8 mm/min, while Marshall Flow is the total deformation of the specimen at the point of maximum load, expressed in 0.25 mm units."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are baking a **cake**:
- The **flour + sugar + eggs** represent the **aggregates** (the skeleton of strength).
- The **butter/cream** represents the **bitumen** (the binder that holds everything together).
- If you put **too much butter**, the cake becomes greasy, soft, and slumps (high flow, low stability — *rutting failure*).
- If you put **too little butter**, the cake crumbles under pressure (low density, high air voids — *raveling failure*).
- The **Marshall Method** is like the **chef's standard recipe** — you try **5 different butter quantities** (typically 3.5%, 4.0%, 4.5%, 5.0%, 5.5% binder by weight of mix), bake identical cakes (compact 3 specimens per trial), squeeze them (load test), and pick the **butter amount that gives the firmest cake with just the right squeeze (flow)** before it collapses.

The "compaction" in the lab simulates the **roller passes on the road**, and the "squeeze test" simulates the **weight of trucks rolling over the pavement at high summer temperatures (60 °C)**.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value |
|---|---|
| Specimen diameter | **101.6 mm (4 inch)** |
| Specimen height | **63.5 mm (2.5 inch)** |
| Compaction blows per face | **75 (heavy traffic)** or **50 (medium traffic)** |
| Hammer weight | **4.536 kg (10 lb)** |
| Hammer drop height | **457 mm (18 inch)** |
| Test temperature | **60 ± 1 °C** |
| Loading rate | **50.8 mm/min (2 inch/min)** |
| Loading strip radius of curvature | **50.8 mm** |
| Minimum specimens per trial | **3** |
| Minimum trial binder contents | **5** (varying in 0.5% increments) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU 2024 Scheme expects students to know **why 60 °C** is chosen — it represents the **maximum pavement temperature** experienced on Indian roads during peak summer (approx. 45–55 °C surface, with bitumen acting viscoelastically at this temperature). This is also why a **lower viscosity binder (VG-30 / VG-40)** is preferred for hot climates.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Philosophy Behind Marshall Method

The Marshall Method operates on the **Triaxial Shear Theory** of bituminous mixtures. When a cylindrical specimen is loaded diametrically:
1. A **tensile stress** develops perpendicular to the load axis.
2. The **internal friction** between aggregate particles resists shear.
3. The **cohesion** provided by bitumen holds the aggregate skeleton.
4. The test measures the **combined effect** of friction and cohesion, called **Marshall Stability**.

The method is **empirical** because the loading mode does **not** replicate actual field stresses (flexure, tension at bottom of asphalt layer). However, decades of correlation with field performance have made it the de-facto standard in India.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Procedure (The "Why" Behind Each Step)

1. **Aggregate Selection & Gradation:** Combine coarse aggregate, fine aggregate, and filler to meet the specified gradation (e.g., BC–II, DBM, SDBC as per MoRTH). *Why?* The aggregate skeleton carries 80% of the load.

2. **Binder Selection:** Choose bitumen grade (VG-30 for most Indian highways, VG-40 for heavy-traffic corridors). *Why?* Viscosity must match climatic zone.

3. **Select 5 Trial Binder Contents:** Typically **3.5%, 4.0%, 4.5%, 5.0%, 5.5%** by weight of total mix. *Why?* OBC always lies in this range; 0.5% increment gives smooth graphs.

4. **Heat Aggregates & Binder:** Aggregates to **~150–170 °C**, binder to **~135–150 °C** for VG-30. *Why?* Ensures uniform coating and workability.

5. **Mix & Compact:** Mix thoroughly, then compact **3 specimens per trial** using the Marshall Hammer (75 blows/side). *Why?* Simulates field density; averaging 3 specimens reduces experimental error.

6. **Cool, Extract, Measure:** Allow specimens to cool, then measure **bulk specific gravity (G_mb)** using the saturated surface-dry (SSD) method per **ASTM D2726**.

7. **Stability & Flow Test:** Heat specimen in water bath to **60 °C for 30–40 minutes**, place in loading jig, apply load at **50.8 mm/min**, record **maximum load (Stability in kN)** and **deformation at max load (Flow in mm)**.

8. **Theoretical Maximum Specific Gravity (G_mm):** Determined separately via **Rice Test (ASTM D2041)** — mix loose (uncompacted) sample with binder at trial content, evacuate air, measure volume.

9. **Calculate Volumetric Parameters:** VMA, VFA, Air Voids (V_a) for each trial.

10. **Plot Graphs:** Six curves vs. binder content.

11. **Determine OBC:** Use the **4 % air void criterion** (most critical) — read the binder content at V_a = 4 %.

12. **Verify All Properties at OBC** against MoRTH/IRC specifications.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these six equations. They appear in 90% of KTU numerical questions.**

| # | Property | Formula | Standard Notation | Typical KTU Range |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Bulk Specific Gravity of Mix** | $G_{mb} = \dfrac{W_{dry}}{(W_{SSD} - W_{sub})}$ | $W_{dry}$ = dry mass, $W_{SSD}$ = SSD mass, $W_{sub}$ = submerged mass | 2.30 – 2.45 |
| 2 | **Theoretical Maximum Specific Gravity** | $G_{mm} = \dfrac{100}{\dfrac{P_s}{G_s} + \dfrac{P_b}{G_b}}$ | $P_s$ = % agg, $P_b$ = % binder, $G_s$ = bulk SG of agg, $G_b$ = SG of binder | 2.40 – 2.55 |
| 3 | **Air Voids (V_a)** | $V_a = \dfrac{G_{mm} - G_{mb}}{G_{mm}} \times 100$ | Expressed in % | **3 – 5 %** |
| 4 | **Voids in Mineral Aggregate (VMA)** | $VMA = 100 - \dfrac{G_{mb} \cdot P_s}{G_s}$ | $P_s$ = % agg by total mix weight | $\geq 13\text{–}14\,\%$ |
| 5 | **Voids Filled with Asphalt (VFA)** | $VFA = \dfrac{VMA - V_a}{VMA} \times 100$ | Expressed in % | 65 – 75 % |
| 6 | **Stability Correction Factor (C.F.)** | $C.F. = f(h)$ | Read from calibrated chart as function of specimen height | 0.96 – 1.18 |

> [!NOTE]
> In the table above, the symbol `$P_s$` represents the percentage of aggregate by weight of the total mix, and `$G_s$` is the **bulk specific gravity of the combined aggregate** (not the apparent specific gravity — this is a common KTU pitfall). Use `\vert` notation in calculations to avoid markdown breaks.

### 2.3.1 Stability Correction (Height Correction) — Critical for KTU

Since the Marshall jig is calibrated for a **63.5 mm (2.5 inch)** specimen, specimens with slightly varying heights must have their stability values multiplied by a **correction factor (C.F.)** read from a standard chart:

$$
C.F. = \text{value from ASTM D6927 calibration chart} = f(h)
$$

- For $h = 63.5$ mm → C.F. = 1.00
- For $h < 63.5$ mm → C.F. > 1.00 (smaller specimen = higher apparent stability)
- For $h > 63.5$ mm → C.F. < 1.00

**Corrected Stability (kN) = Measured Stability × C.F.**

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Marshall Method is the **mandatory design protocol** in India for:
- **Bituminous Concrete (BC) Grade-1 and Grade-2** wearing courses
- **Dense Bituminous Macadam (DBM)** binder course
- **Semi-Dense Bituminous Concrete (SDBC)**
- **Asphaltic Base Course**
