# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define the Group Index of a soil subgrade. How is it useful in pavement design?**

**Model Answer:**
The **Group Index (GI)** is an empirical number assigned to a soil based on its grain size and plasticity characteristics, computed as:

$$GI = 0.2a + 0.005ac + 0.01bd$$

where $a, b, c, d$ are functions of the percent passing the 75 μ sieve and the Atterberg limits.

**Use in pavement design:**
- It **quantifies subgrade quality** (GI = 0–4 excellent, 5–9 medium, ≥ 10 poor).
- It serves as a **preliminary indicator** of the strength/stiffness of the subgrade, helping engineers decide whether **subgrade stabilization** is required.
- However, GI does not account for **traffic loading or axle spectrum**, so it is a **secondary design input**; the **CBR method is preferred** for detailed design.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** 3-mark questions demand a **definition + 1 application point + 1 limitation**. Always close with the limitation to score full marks.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**State the California Bearing Ratio test procedure. What are the standard reference loads used?**

**Model Answer:**
The **CBR test** is a penetration test performed on a compacted soil specimen (soaked in water for 4 days for subgrade evaluation). A cylindrical plunger of **50 mm diameter** is pushed into the soil at a **rate of 1.25 mm/min**, and the load required to achieve specific penetrations (2.5 mm and 5.0 mm) is recorded.

**Standard Reference Loads:**

| Penetration (mm) | Standard Load (kg) |
|:----------------:|:------------------:|
| 2.5 | 1370 |
| 5.0 | 2055 |

$$CBR(\%) = \frac{\text{Test Load}}{\text{Standard Load}} \times 100$$

The CBR corresponding to **2.5 mm penetration** is normally reported; the **5.0 mm CBR** is used only if it is higher and the test is repeated.

> [!TIP]
> Always mention **3 key procedure points** (plunger size, penetration rate, soaking period) for a complete 3-mark answer.

---

## 📝 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### ➤ Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks
**[KTU University Exam — July 2024]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **Group Index method** of flexible pavement design. Define all parameters and outline the rating criteria. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A subgrade soil has the following properties: $F_{75} = 82\%$, $F_{425} = 65\%$, $LL = 48\%$, $PI = 25\%$. Calculate the **Group Index** and rate the subgrade. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Detailed Theory (7 marks)**

The **Group Index (GI) Method** was developed by the **Highway Research Board (HRB)** as a numerical indicator of the suitability of a soil for use as a subgrade. It supplements the HRB classification (A-1 to A-7) with a **quantitative score**.

**Parameters:**
- $a$ = $(F_{75} - 35)$, capped at 40 (minimum 0)
- $b$ = $(F_{75} - 15)$, capped at 40 (minimum 0)
- $c$ = $(LL - 40)$, capped at 20 (minimum 0)
- $d$ = $(PI - 10)$, capped at 20 (minimum 0)

**Formula:**

$$GI = 0.2a + 0.005ac + 0.01bd$$

**Rating Scale (HRB Subgrade Rating):**

| GI Range | Subgrade Rating |
|:--------:|:----------------|
| 0 – 4 | Excellent to Good |
| 5 – 9 | Fair to Medium |
| ≥ 10 | Poor to Very Poor |

**Limitations:**
1. Does not consider **traffic load**.
2. Highly sensitive to **test variability** (especially $F_{75}$).
3. Applicable only to **fine-grained subgrade soils**, not coarse aggregates.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Parameter $a$:**

$$a = 82 - 35 = 47 \rightarrow \text{capped at } 40$$

**Step 2 — Parameter $b$:**

$$b = 82 - 15 = 67 \rightarrow \text{capped at } 40$$

**Step 3 — Parameter $c$:**

$$c = 48 - 40 = 8 \rightarrow \text{no cap}$$

**Step 4 — Parameter $d$:**

$$d = 25 - 10 = 15 \rightarrow \text{no cap}$$

**Step 5 — Compute GI:**

$$GI = 0.2(40) + 0.005(40)(8) + 0.01(40)(15)$$

$$GI = 8.0 + 1.6 + 6.0 = 15.6 \approx 16$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation:**

Since $GI = 16 \geq 10$, the subgrade is classified as **"Poor to Very Poor"**. Strongly recommends **subgrade stabilization** with lime/cement or a thick **granular sub-base** design.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Q A):** Most students forget to **cap** parameters $a, b, c, d$ at their respective maxima (40, 40, 20, 20). Failing to cap leads to a grossly inflated GI and incorrect rating. **Always cap and then plug in.**

---

### ➤ Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Describe the **California Bearing Ratio (CBR) method** of flexible pavement design as per IRC:37-2018. What are the design inputs? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A flexible pavement is to be designed for **10 msa** traffic on a subgrade with **CBR = 5%**. Using the regression form of IRC:37, compute the **total pavement thickness** and suggest a **layered composition**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Detailed Theory (7 marks)**

The **CBR method** is the most widely used empirical pavement design method in India, codified in **IRC:37-2018 (Guidelines for the Design of Flexible Pavements)**.

**Procedure:**

1. **Subgrade evaluation:** Conduct **soaked CBR test** (4-day immersion) on the subgrade soil at the design density (95% of MDD, modified Proctor).
2. **Traffic analysis:** Conduct **axle load surveys** and compute **Vehicle Damage Factor (VDF)** for each commercial vehicle class.
3. **Cumulative traffic:** Estimate the design traffic in **million standard axles (msa)** using:

$$N = \frac{365 \times \big[(1+r)^n - 1\big]}{r} \times A \times D \times F$$

where $A$ = initial daily ESAL count, $r$ = annual growth rate, $n$ = design life, $D$ = lane distribution factor, $F$ = directional distribution factor.

4. **Design charts:** Use **IRC:37-2018 Plate 1** (total thickness vs. CBR for various msa) and **Plate 2** (layered thickness recommendations for different traffic levels).
5. **Layer composition:** Allocate thickness to **BC, DBM, WMM, GSB** as per design tables.

**Design Inputs (Summary):**

| Input | Source |
|:------|:-------|
| CBR of subgrade | Lab test |
| Design traffic (msa) | Axle load survey |
| Design life | Project requirement (typically 15–20 yrs) |
| Lane distribution factor | Code-specified (e.g., 0.75 for 4-lane) |
| Growth rate | Traffic forecast |

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Compute $\log_{10}(N)$:**

$$N = 10, \quad \log_{10}(10) = 1.0$$

**Step 2 — Apply regression formula:**

$$T = \frac{0.75 + 0.4 \cdot \log_{10}(N)}{CBR^{0.4}} = \frac{0.75 + 0.4(1.0)}{5^{0.4}}$$

$$\text{Numerator} = 0.75 + 0.4 = 1.15$$

**Step 3 — Compute $5^{0.4}$:**

$$\ln(5) = 1.6094, \quad 0.4 \times 1.6094 = 0.6438, \quad e^{0.6438} \approx 1.904$$

**Step 4 — Compute $T$:**

$$T = \frac{1.15}{1.904} = 0.604$$

For practical IRC:37 design at 10 msa and CBR = 5%, the chart gives a **total thickness ≈ 600 mm** (the regression match confirms the order of magnitude).

$$\boxed{T_{total} \approx 600 \;\text{mm}}$$

**Step 5 — Layered Composition (N = 10 msa — Moderate Traffic):**

| Layer | Thickness (mm) |
|:------|:--------------:|
| Bituminous Concrete (BC) | 30 |
| Dense Bituminous Macadam (DBM) | 70 |
| Wet Mix Macadam (WMM) | 200 |
| Granular Sub-Base (GSB) | 300 |
| **Total** | **600** |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Q B):** Students often **forget to specify the soaking condition** for CBR (4-day soak = simulates worst-case moisture). The 4-day soaked CBR is the **only acceptable design CBR** for subgrade in IRC:37. Marking penalty: **up to 2 marks** for omitting this.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Mark-Loss Reasons in Flexible Pavement Empirical Design:**
> 1. **Forgetting to cap GI parameters** ($a, b, c, d$) at their maxima — leads to wrong GI and wrong rating. (–2 marks)
> 2. **Using 5.0 mm CBR as default** — the 2.5 mm CBR is the primary design value. (–1 mark)
> 3. **Mixing up formula notation** (e.g., confusing $F_{75}$ with $F_{425}$ in GI formula). (–2 marks)
> 4. **Skipping traffic unit conversion** — forgetting that $N$ in IRC formulas is in **msa**, not total axles. (–2 marks)
> 5. **No mention of soaked vs. unsoaked CBR** in the procedure — IRC:37 mandates **soaked** CBR. (–1 mark)

---

## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Flexible pavements** distribute load through **layered interlock and friction**, with the topmost layer carrying the highest stress.
- **Empirical design** relies on **observed field performance** (e.g., AASHO Road Test) — it is *valid only within the tested envelope*.
- The **Group Index (GI)** quantifies subgrade quality from **gradation and plasticity** but ignores traffic — a major limitation.
- The **CBR method** is the **backbone of IRC:37**; the 4-day **soaked** CBR is the mandatory design input for subgrade.
- **IRC:37-2018 design charts** relate **CBR + msa** to total pavement thickness; bituminous layer thickness is governed by **traffic**, while **granular layer thickness** is governed by **subgrade strength**.
- **McLeod method** is used for **airfield and unsurfaced roads**; $t = K \cdot \log_{10}(P/S)$.
- **Burmister method** uses a **two-layer elastic theory** with the **modulus ratio** $E_1/E_2$ as the governing parameter.
- **Always cap** GI parameters $(a, b \leq 40;\; c, d \leq 20)$ and **floor them at 0**.
- **Standard loads** for CBR: 1370 kg at 2.5 mm; 2055 kg at 5.0 mm.
- **Design CBR is always soaked CBR** — this is a non-negotiable IRC:37 requirement.
- **Traffic design life** for NH/SH is typically **15–20 years**; for rural roads, **10–15 years**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Semi-Empirical and Theoretical Approaches for Flexible Pavement Design - Design of Pavement Using CBR Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET746 - HIGHWAY MATERIALS AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Semi-Empirical and Theoretical Approaches for Flexible Pavement Design - Design of Pavement Using CBR Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The California Bearing Ratio (CBR) — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **California Bearing Ratio (CBR)** is defined as the ratio of the test load required to cause a standard plunger to penetrate the soil at a rate of **1.25 mm/min** to a specific penetration depth, compared to the **standard load** required to produce the same penetration in a standard sample of *crushed stone (limestone)*, expressed as a percentage.

The mathematical statement of the CBR is:

$$\text{CBR (\%)} = \frac{P_{\text{test}}}{P_{\text{standard}}} \times 100$$

where $P_{\text{test}}$ is the penetration load on the test sample (in kg or kN) and $P_{\text{standard}}$ is the standard load corresponding to the same penetration depth.

The **standard reference loads** (as established by the California Division of Highways) are:

| Penetration Depth | Standard Load (kg) | Standard Load (kN) |
|:-----------------:|:------------------:|:------------------:|
| 2.5 mm | 1370 kg | 13.44 kN |
| 5.0 mm | 2055 kg | 20.16 kN |

> [!NOTE]
> **Convention adopted by IRC and IS 2720:** The CBR value reported is **usually the one corresponding to 2.5 mm penetration**. However, if the CBR at 5.0 mm penetration is *greater* than that at 2.5 mm, the test is **repeated**. If the repeat test gives the same result, then the **5.0 mm value is adopted**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Footprint Test"

Imagine you are standing barefoot on a soft beach versus a hard floor. The beach deforms deeply under your foot (weak subgrade) — the floor barely deforms (strong subgrade). The CBR test is essentially a **scientific version of this footprint test**:

- A **standard piston (plunger)** is pushed into the soil at a constant rate.
- A *weak soil* (think wet clay) resists very little — **CBR is low (1–3%)**.
- A *strong soil* (think compacted gravel) resists a lot — **CBR is high (20–80%)**.
- The result is a single percentage that engineers use to decide **how thick the pavement must be** to safely carry the traffic.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Significance:** The CBR method is the *empirical backbone* of flexible pavement design in India, codified by the **Indian Roads Congress (IRC:37)**. It links **subgrade strength** to **pavement thickness** through a family of design charts that are simple, reliable, and field-validated over decades.

## 1.3 Historical Lineage

| Era | Milestone |
|:---:|:---------|
| **1928–1929** | Developed by the **California Division of Highways** during pavement failure studies on highways. |
| **1940s (WWII)** | Adapted by the **U.S. Army Corps of Engineers** for designing military airfields and roads. |
| **1942–1943** | Empirical design curves published by O.J. Porter relating wheel load, tire pressure, and CBR. |
| **1968** | First Indian code **IRC:37** published, adopting the CBR approach. |
| **1970 → 2018** | Successive revisions: IRC:37-1970, 1984, 1990, 2001, 2012, **2018 (5th Revision)**. |
| **Current** | Modern IRC:37-2018 uses **Million Standard Axles (MSA)** as the traffic parameter and **effective subgrade CBR** as the strength parameter. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The CBR method is **classified as Semi-Empirical** because it relies on a laboratory index (CBR) and field-calibrated design charts rather than pure elasticity theory. The empirical curves were drawn to satisfy observed performance of in-service pavements.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The CBR Test — Mechanism and Logic

The CBR test (standardized under **IS 2720 (Part 16)**) is performed on a *soaked* soil sample to simulate the *worst-case moisture condition* that the subgrade will experience in service. The test unfolds in the following sequence:

1. **Sample Preparation:** Compact the soil in a CBR mould (internal diameter **152 mm**, height **127 mm**) at **O.M.C.** (Optimum Moisture Content) using **heavy compaction (Modified Proctor, IS 2720 Part 8)** or light compaction depending on design intent.
2. **Soaking:** Submerge the sample in water for **96 hours (4 days)** with a surcharge weight equivalent to the field pavement overburden. This simulates the weakest in-service subgrade condition.
3. **Penetration Test:** Push a **standard plunger of 50 mm diameter** into the sample at a uniform rate of **1.25 mm/min**. Record the load at penetration depths of **0.5, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0, 2.5, 3.0, 4.0, 5.0, 7.5, 10.0, 12.5 mm**.
4. **CBR Computation:** Calculate CBR at 2.5 mm and 5.0 mm; adopt the higher following the IRC convention described earlier.

## 2.2 The Underlying Engineering Logic of the CBR Design Method

The semi-empirical CBR design rests on three pillars:

- **Strength Pillar:** The subgrade's resistance to penetration (CBR) is the *primary design input*.
- **Load Pillar:** The cumulative damage caused by standard axle repetitions (80 kN single-axle) over the design life determines the pavement thickness.
- **Structural Pillar:** The pavement must be thick enough that the **vertical compressive stress at the subgrade level** remains below the safe bearing pressure of the subgrade.

The historical U.S. Army Corps of Engineers formula (often quoted) embodies this logic:

$$t = \sqrt{\frac{P}{8.1 \times \text{CBR}}} - \frac{P}{28 \cdot \sigma_{bc}}$$

where $t$ = total pavement thickness (cm), $P$ = wheel load (kg), CBR = subgrade CBR (%), and $\sigma_{bc}$ = bearing capacity of the sub-base or subgrade material (kg/cm²).

> [!NOTE]
> In **current Indian practice (IRC:37-2018)**, this analytical equation is **NOT** used directly. Instead, the design is performed using **standard design charts** that give the total pavement thickness as a function of subgrade CBR and design traffic (in MSA). The underlying equation, however, remains the foundation of the chart family.

## 2.3 IRC:37 Design Philosophy — Components of the Flexible Pavement

A modern flexible pavement (per IRC:37-2018) consists of the following *distinct layers* stacked over the prepared subgrade:

| Layer | Function | Typical Material |
|:------|:---------|:-----------------|
| **Bituminous Wearing Course (BC)** | Direct traffic abrasion resistance, skid resistance, waterproofing | Dense Bituminous Macadam / Bituminous Concrete |
| **Bituminous Binder Course (DBM/BM)** | Distribute wheel load, add structural capacity | Dense Bituminous Macadam / Bituminous Macadam |
| **Granular Base Course (WMM/WBM)** | Principal load-spreading layer | Wet Mix Macadam / Water Bound Macadam |
| **Granular Sub-base Course (GSB)** | Drainage, frost protection, secondary load distribution | Granular Sub-base (Moorum, gravel, crushed aggregate) |
| **Subgrade (Prepared)** | Foundation that supports the entire pavement | Compacted natural soil |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Effective Subgrade CBR** is the CBR of the top **500 mm** of the subgrade *after* compaction at the highest water table condition expected. The subgrade is *replaced or improved* (e.g., with a capping layer of higher-CBR soil) if its natural CBR is too low to meet the design chart's minimum threshold.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **The following table is the "must-memorize" cheat sheet for KTU Part A and Part B questions on this topic.**

| # | Quantity | Formula / Relation | Typical Units | Notes |
|:-:|:---------|:-------------------|:-------------:|:------|
| 1 | CBR | $\text{CBR} = (P_{\text{test}}/P_{\text{std}}) \times 100$ | % | Standard loads: 1370 kg (2.5 mm), 2055 kg (5.0 mm) |
| 2 | CBR Standard Load (2.5 mm) | $P_{\text{std}} = 13.44$ kN | kN | Reference value |
| 3 | CBR Standard Load (5.0 mm) | $P_{\text{std}} = 20.16$ kN | kN | Used if 5 mm CBR > 2.5 mm CBR |
| 4 | Penetration rate | $1.25$ mm/min | mm/min | Mandatory per IS 2720 Part 16 |
| 5 | Soaking period (CBR test) | $96$ hours | hours | Simulates worst moisture |
| 6 | Design Traffic (Cumulative Standard Axles) | $N = 365 \times A \times \left[ \dfrac{(1+r)^{n}-1}{r} \right] \times D \times F$ | MSA | A = initial CVPD, r = growth, n = years, D = lane dist., F = VDF |
| 7 | Corps of Engineers (older) | $t = \sqrt{P/(8.1 \cdot \text{CBR})} - P/(28\sigma_{bc})$ | cm | Semi-empirical, not used in IRC:37 charts |
| 8 | Design Lane Distribution Factor (D) | $0.5$ (2-lane), $0.4$ (4-lane undivided), $0.75$ (single lane) | dimensionless | Per IRC:37 |
| 9 | Vehicle Damage Factor (F) | $1.0$ to $3.0$ typical | dimensionless | Depends on axle spectrum |
| 10 | Standard Axle Load | $80$ kN (single axle) | kN | Per IRC:37 |
| 11 | Surface Course minimum thickness | $30$ to $50$ mm BC + DBM/BM | mm | For low to high traffic |

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

The CBR method is the **workhorse of national highway, state highway, and rural road design** across India. Every kilometer of PMGSY rural road and most NHAI projects under older contracts were designed using CBR charts. While newer Mechanistic-Empirical methods (IRC:37-2018 incorporates some M-E elements) are emerging, the **CBR remains the single most quoted subgrade parameter** in Indian pavement engineering practice.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed CBR Computation — Worked Example 1

> **Problem:** A CBR test was conducted on a soaked subgrade soil sample. The following penetration-load readings were recorded:

| Penetration (mm) | Load (kg) |
|:----------------:|:---------:|
| 0.5 | 25 |
| 1.0 | 55 |
| 1.5 | 90 |
| 2.0 | 130 |
| 2.5 | 170 |
| 3.0 | 195 |
| 4.0 | 230 |
| 5.0 | 250 |
| 7.5 | 295 |
| 10.0 | 320 |
| 12.5 | 340 |

> Compute the CBR of the soil.

**Step 1 — Identify the load-penetration relationship:**
The load-penetration curve typically plots a near-straight line at low penetrations. Read the corrected loads at 2.5 mm and 5.0 mm.

**Step 2 — Compute CBR at 2.5 mm penetration:**

$$\text{CBR}_{2.5} = \frac{P_{\text{test at 2.5 mm}}}{P_{\text{std at 2.5 mm}}} \times 100$$

$$\text{CBR}_{2.5} = \frac{170}{1370} \times 100 = 12.41\%$$

**Step 3 — Compute CBR at 5.0 mm penetration:**

$$\text{CBR}_{5.0} = \frac{P_{\text{test at 5.0 mm}}}{P_{\text{std at 5.0 mm}}} \times 100$$

$$\text{CBR}_{5.0} = \frac{250}{2055} \times 100 = 12.17\%$$

**Step 4 — Apply the IRC convention:**

$$\text{CBR}_{2.5} = 12.41\% \quad > \quad \text{CBR}_{5.0} = 12.17\%$$

Since CBR at 2.5 mm is **greater** than CBR at 5.0 mm, **no retest is required**, and the **CBR at 2.5 mm is adopted**:

$$\boxed{\text{CBR} = 12.41\% \;\; (\approx 12\%)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The student should clearly articulate the *convention rule* in the answer. Many students lose marks by writing the 5 mm value without justification.

---

## 3.2 Full Pavement Design — Worked Example 2

> **Problem:** A new district road is to be designed. The following data are provided:
> - Initial commercial traffic (CVPD) on the road *just before opening* : $A = 450$ CVPD
> - Annual growth rate : $r = 7.5\%$
> - Design life : $n = 15$ years
> - Lane distribution factor : $D = 0.50$ (two-lane road, single-carriageway)
> - Vehicle damage factor : $F = 1.5$ (typical mixed traffic, secondary road)
> - Subgrade effective CBR : $8\%$
>
> Design the **total flexible pavement thickness** using the IRC:37 approach.

### Step 1 — Convert CVPD to Cumulative Standard Axles (CSA) and MSA

The standard formula for cumulative standard axles is:

$$N = \frac{365 \times \left[ (1+r)^{n} - 1 \right]}{r} \times A \times D \times F$$

**Substituting the values:**

$$N = \frac{365 \times \left[ (1+0.075)^{15} - 1 \right]}{0.075} \times 450 \times 0.50 \times 1.5$$

**Computing the geometric term:**

$$(1.075)^{15} = \exp(15 \times \ln 1.075) = \exp(15 \times 0.07232) = \exp(1.0848) = 2.9584$$

$$(1.075)^{15} - 1 = 2.9584 - 1 = 1.9584$$

**Computing the cumulative standard axles:**

$$N = \frac{365 \times 1.9584}{0.075} \times 450 \times 0.50 \times 1.5$$

$$N = 9529.92 \times 450 \times 0.50 \times 1.5$$

$$N = 9529.92 \times 337.5$$

$$N = 3{,}216{,}873 \; \text{standard axles}$$

**Converting to MSA (Million Standard Axles):**

$$\text{MSA} = \frac{N}{10^{6}} = \frac{3{,}216{,}873}{1{,}000{,}000} = 3.22 \; \text{MSA}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Design Traffic} = 3.22 \; \text{MSA}}$$

### Step 2 — Enter the IRC:37 Design Chart

For **CBR = 8%** and **Traffic = 3.22 MSA**, the IRC:37-2018 design chart (Plate 3, or its equivalent in earlier revisions) yields the total pavement thickness over the subgrade.

From the design chart (interpolating between 3 MSA and 5 MSA curves for 8% CBR):

$$T_{\text{total}} \approx 650 \; \text{mm} = 65 \; \text{cm}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Important practical note:** In the absence of the actual IRC:37 design chart (which is a large folded plate), the design office refers to a *tabulated or numerical approximation*. For a CBR of 8% and traffic in the range 3–5 MSA, the typical design thicknesses are in the range of **60–70 cm total pavement** over the subgrade.

### Step 3 — Distribute the Total Thickness into Constituent Layers

Per IRC:37-2018 recommendations and the MoRTH Specifications, a typical layer distribution for this category of road is:

| Layer | Material | Thickness (mm) |
|:------|:---------|:--------------:|
| **Wearing Course (BC)** | Bituminous Concrete (Grade 1 or 2) | **40 mm** |
| **Binder Course (DBM)** | Dense Bituminous Macadam (Grading 1) | **60 mm** |
| **Base Course (WMM)** | Wet Mix Macadam | **250 mm** |
| **Sub-base Course (GSB)** | Granular Sub-base | **300 mm** |
| **Total Pavement Thickness** | | **650 mm** |

$$\boxed{T_{\text{total}} = 40 + 60 + 250 + 300 = 650 \; \text{mm} = 65 \; \text{cm}}$$

### Step 4 — Verification of Bituminous Layer Thickness

For 3.22 MSA, IRC:37-2018 requires that the total bituminous layer (BC + DBM) be at least:

$$T_{\text{bit, min}} \approx 90 \; \text{mm} \;\; (\text{for } 3\text{–}5 \; \text{MSA})$$

Our design provides $40 + 60 = 100$ mm of bituminous layers, which **satisfies the minimum requirement**. ✓

> [!TIP]
> **Validation check:** Always confirm that the chosen bituminous layer thickness equals or exceeds the minimum stipulated in IRC:37-2018 for the design MSA. This is a common KTU exam question.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Sensitivity of Pavement Thickness to CBR and Traffic

> The following table summarizes the **qualitative sensitivity** of the design thickness to the two main inputs. This is high-yield exam content.

| Input | Direction of Change | Effect on Total Pavement Thickness |
|:------|:-------------------|:------------------------------------|
| Subgrade CBR **decreases** | $\downarrow$ CBR | **Thickness increases significantly** (inverse-square-root relationship in the empirical formula). |
| Subgrade CBR **increases** | $\uparrow$ CBR | **Thickness decreases** — high-CBR subgrade can support thinner pavement. |
| Design Traffic **increases** | $\uparrow$ MSA | **Thickness increases** — high traffic demands more structural capacity. |
| Design Traffic **decreases** | $\downarrow$ MSA | **Thickness decreases** — light rural roads need thinner pavements. |
| Subgrade soaked CBR = **0%** (very weak) | N/A | **Use a capping layer** (200–300 mm of higher-quality soil) or **stabilize the subgrade** to raise effective CBR to a minimum of **3–5%**. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favourite Question Pattern:** "If the subgrade CBR drops from 10% to 5%, by what percentage does the total pavement thickness change?" The student must be ready to read the IRC chart at two CBR values and compute the percentage change.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Comparison: Effect of Doubling the Traffic

> **Problem:** Repeat Example 2 but with the initial traffic doubled ($A = 900$ CVPD). Comment on the change in design.

**Step 1:** Compute the new MSA. Since MSA is **linearly proportional** to $A$:

$$\text{MSA}_{\text{new}} = 3.22 \times 2 = 6.44 \; \text{MSA}$$

**Step 2:** From the IRC:37 design chart for CBR = 8% and 6.44 MSA:

$$T_{\text{total, new}} \approx 720 \; \text{mm}$$

**Step 3:** The bituminous layer must also be increased. For 5–10 MSA, IRC:37-2018 prescribes a minimum bituminous layer thickness of approximately **100–115 mm**.

**Revised layer distribution:**

| Layer | Thickness (mm) |
|:------|:--------------:|
| BC | 40 |
| DBM | 75 |
| WMM | 275 |
| GSB | 330 |
| **Total** | **720** |

**Step 4 — Percentage increase in total thickness:**

$$\Delta T\% = \frac{720 - 650}{650} \times 100 = 10.77\%$$

$$\boxed{\text{Design thickness increased by } \approx 10.8\% \text{ for a 100\% increase in traffic.}}$$

> [!TIP]
> The non-linear sensitivity of pavement thickness to traffic is a key design insight — a doubling of traffic does **not** require a doubling of pavement thickness.

---

## 3.5 Derivation of the Corps of Engineers Formula (Conceptual Derivation)

Although the **IRC:37 chart approach** is used in India, the underlying theoretical foundation is the **U.S. Army Corps of Engineers' semi-empirical formula**. The derivation logic is as follows:

**Step 1 — Equivalent Single-Wheel Load:**
A dual-wheel assembly carrying load $P$ at tire pressure $p$ is replaced by an equivalent single circular plate of contact area $A_e = 2 \times P/p$ (a simplification that aggregates the two contact circles).

**Step 2 — Radius of the equivalent contact area:**

$$a = \sqrt{\frac{A_e}{\pi}} = \sqrt{\frac{2P}{\pi p}}$$

**Step 3 — Vertical stress at depth $z$ (Boussinesq equation for a uniformly loaded flexible circular area):**

$$\sigma_z = p \left[ 1 - \frac{z^3}{(a^2 + z^2)^{3/2}} \right]$$

**Step 4 — Setting the subgrade stress equal to the allowable stress:**
The allowable stress on the subgrade is empirically related to CBR by $\sigma_{\text{allow}} = 13.6 \times \text{CBR}$ (in kN/m²). Substituting into the Boussinesq equation and solving for the pavement thickness $t = z$ yields the familiar Corps of Engineers form:

$$t = \sqrt{\frac{P}{8.1 \cdot \text{CBR}}} - \frac{P}{28 \sigma_{bc}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This equation is the **theoretical parent** of the IRC:37 design charts. Modern IRC charts are derived from this equation, calibrated against decades of in-service pavement performance, and presented in graphical form for ease of office use.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Cross-Section of a Typical Flexible Pavement

> The following block diagram maps the **layered architecture** of a flexible pavement designed using the CBR method. Each layer has a distinct material identity, structural role, and design thickness.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A0[APPLIED TRAFFIC LOAD WHEEL ASSEMBLY 80 kN STANDARD AXLE] --> A1
    subgraph A1[FLEXIBLE PAVEMENT STRUCTURE]
        L1[LAYER 1 BITUMINOUS WEARING COURSE BC GRADE 2]
        L2[LAYER 2 BITUMINOUS BINDER COURSE DBM]
        L3[LAYER 3 GRANULAR BASE COURSE WMM]
        L4[LAYER 4 GRANULAR SUB BASE GSB]
    end
    A1 --> A2[PREPARED SUBGRADE NATURAL SOIL COMPACTED AT OMC TO TARGET CBR]
    A2 --> A3[FOUNDATION BED NATURAL SUBSOIL]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the diagram:** The traffic load enters at the top, and the pavement spreads it over an increasingly larger area as the load descends. The **bituminous layers** (BC + DBM) primarily provide *flexural strength* and *fatigue resistance*; the **granular layers** (WMM + GSB) primarily provide *load distribution* and *drainage*. The **subgrade** is the *strength-defining foundation* whose CBR dictates the design.

## 4.2 Design Process Flow — IRC:37 CBR Method

> The diagram below maps the **step-by-step logic** an engineer follows when designing a flexible pavement using the CBR method.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    startNode[START PAVEMENT DESIGN BRIEF] --> step1[STEP 1 COLLECT SUBGRADE SOIL SAMPLES]
    step1 --> step2[STEP 2 DETERMINE EFFECTIVE SUBGRADE CBR LAB SOAKED TEST IS 2720 PART 16]
    step2 --> step3[STEP 3 DETERMINE INITIAL TRAFFIC A IN CVPD TRAFFIC SURVEY]
    step3 --> step4[STEP 4 ESTIMATE GROWTH RATE r AND DESIGN LIFE n]
    step4 --> step5[STEP 5 APPLY LANE DISTRIBUTION FACTOR D AND VDF F]
    step5 --> step6[STEP 6 COMPUTE CUMULATIVE STANDARD AXLES N AND MSA]
    step6 --> step7[STEP 7 ENTER IRC 37 DESIGN CHART WITH CBR AND MSA]
    step7 --> step8[STEP 8 READ TOTAL PAVEMENT THICKNESS T TOTAL]
    step8 --> step9[STEP 9 DISTRIBUTE T TOTAL AMONG BC DBM WMM GSB]
    step9 --> step10[STEP 10 VERIFY MINIMUM BITUMINOUS THICKNESS PER MSA CATEGORY]
    step10 --> step11[STEP 11 IF CBR IS VERY LOW LESS THAN 3 PERCENT PROVIDE CAPPING LAYER]
    step11 --> step12[STEP 12 FINALIZE DESIGN REPORT]
    step12 --> endNode[END PAVEMENT DESIGN COMPLETE]
```

## 4.3 CBR Test Apparatus — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    cup[SOIL SAMPLE IN MOULD] --> plunger[STANDARD PLUNGER 50 MM DIAMETER]
    plunger --> jack[LOADING FRAME CONSTANT RATE 1.25 MM PER MIN]
    jack --> loadcell[PROVING RING OR LOAD CELL READS PENETRATION LOAD]
    loadcell --> dialgauges[TWO DIAL GAUGES MEASURE PLUNGER DISPLACEMENT]
    cup --> soak[SOAKING TANK 96 HOURS 4 DAYS]
    soak --> cup
    loadcell --> data[DATA TABLE PENETRATION VS LOAD]
    data --> cbrplot[LOAD PENETRATION CURVE]
    cbrplot --> cbrcalc[CBR COMPUTATION COMPARE WITH STANDARD LOADS]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** The KTU theory paper frequently asks students to **sketch the CBR test setup** and **label its components**. Memorize the mould dimensions (152 mm × 127 mm), plunger diameter (50 mm), penetration rate (1.25 mm/min), and soaking period (96 hours).

## 4.4 IRC:37 Design Chart — Conceptual Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    axisY[VERTICAL AXIS TOTAL PAVEMENT THICKNESS T IN MM] --> chart[IRC 37 DESIGN CHART PLATE 3]
    axisX[HORIZONTAL AXIS SUBGRADE CBR IN PERCENTAGE 2 TO 15] --> chart
    curves[FAMILY OF CURVES ONE PER MSA VALUE 2 5 10 20 50 MSA] --> chart
    chart --> read[READ T AT INTERSECTION OF CHOSEN CBR AND MSA CURVE]
    read --> compose[COMPOSE TOTAL T INTO BC DBM WMM GSB LAYERS]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The IRC:37 design chart is **not** drawn in this notes file because it is a copyrighted figure. In the exam, the student is expected to **describe** the chart's structure: x-axis = CBR, y-axis = total thickness, and the family of curves = different MSA values.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define CBR. State the standard load values used in its computation. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

> **California Bearing Ratio (CBR)** is defined as the ratio of the test load required to cause a standard plunger to penetrate the soil at a rate of **1.25 mm/min** to a specific penetration depth, compared to the **standard load** required to produce the same penetration in a standard sample of *crushed stone (limestone)*, expressed as a percentage.
>
> The **standard reference loads** are:
> - At **2.5 mm penetration**: $\mathbf{1370 \; kg}$ (or 13.44 kN)
> - At **5.0 mm penetration**: $\mathbf{2055 \; kg}$ (or 20.16 kN)
>
> The standard loads are obtained from a *load-penetration curve* for the reference crushed-stone sample, and the test load is read from the corresponding *load-penetration curve* for the soil under test.

---

### Question 2: List the various steps involved in the design of a flexible pavement using the CBR method. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

The design of a flexible pavement using the CBR method involves the following steps:

1. **Determine the subgrade CBR** by conducting soaked CBR tests on representative samples of the natural subgrade soil (as per IS 2720 Part 16).
2. **Estimate the design traffic** in terms of *cumulative standard axles* (CSA) or *million standard axles* (MSA) using initial commercial traffic, growth rate, design life, lane distribution factor, and vehicle damage factor.
3. **Enter the IRC:37 design chart** (Plate 3) with the design CBR and design traffic to obtain the **total pavement thickness**.
4. **Distribute the total thickness** into the constituent layers: Bituminous Wearing Course (BC), Bituminous Binder Course (DBM/BM), Granular Base Course (WMM/WBM), and Granular Sub-base Course (GSB).
5. **Verify** that the bituminous layer thickness meets the minimum stipulated in IRC:37-2018 for the design MSA category.
6. **Provide a capping or subgrade improvement layer** if the natural subgrade CBR is below the minimum threshold (typically 3%).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert CVPD to MSA** before reading the design chart — leads to a 5–7 mark penalty in design problems.
> 2. **Using the wrong standard load** (1370 kg vs 2055 kg) in CBR computation — students frequently interchange them.
> 3. **Reporting only the total thickness** without distributing it into layers (BC, DBM, WMM, GSB) — fetches only **partial credit** (3–4 marks out of 7).
> 4. **Not verifying the minimum bituminous layer thickness** for the design MSA — KTU examiners explicitly look for this check.
> 5. **Ignoring the lane distribution factor and VDF** in the traffic formula — a single-line omission that costs 2–3 marks.
> 6. **Confusing the 2.5 mm and 5.0 mm CBR convention** — failing to state which value is adopted and why.

### Question A: Design a Flexible Pavement Using the CBR Method `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 3 - 14 marks]`

**Data Provided:**

- Subgrade effective CBR = **6%**
- Initial commercial traffic = **600 CVPD**
- Annual traffic growth rate = **7.5%**
- Design life = **15 years**
- Lane distribution factor = **0.5**
- Vehicle damage factor = **1.5**
- Two-lane single-carriageway road.

**Sub-Question (a) [7 marks]:** Compute the design traffic in MSA and determine the total pavement thickness using the IRC:37 design chart approach.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Cumulative Standard Axles (CSA):**

$$N = 365 \times \left[ \frac{(1+r)^n - 1}{r} \right] \times A \times D \times F$$

$$N = 365 \times \left[ \frac{(1.075)^{15} - 1}{0.075} \right] \times 600 \times 0.5 \times 1.5$$

**Computing the geometric factor:**

$$(1.075)^{15} = 2.9584$$

$$(1.075)^{15} - 1 = 1.9584$$

$$N = 365 \times \frac{1.9584}{0.075} \times 600 \times 0.5 \times 1.5$$

$$N = 365 \times 26.112 \times 450$$

$$N = 4{,}290{,}405 \; \text{standard axles}$$

**Converting to MSA:**

$$\text{MSA} = \frac{4{,}290{,}405}{10^6} = 4.29 \; \text{MSA}$$

**Step 2 — Total Pavement Thickness from IRC:37 Chart:**

For CBR = 6% and MSA = 4.29 (interpolating between 4 MSA and 5 MSA curves), the chart gives:

$$T_{\text{total}} \approx 700 \; \text{mm} \; (70 \; \text{cm})$$

> **Sub-question (a) carries 7 marks. Award: 2 marks for MSA computation, 3 marks for chart reading and total thickness, 2 marks for displaying the formula and units correctly.**

---

**Sub-Question (b) [7 marks]:** Distribute the total thickness into constituent pavement layers and verify the bituminous layer minimum thickness for the design MSA.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 3 — Layer Distribution (per IRC:37-2018 + MoRTH Specifications):**

For 4.29 MSA and CBR = 6%, a typical layer distribution is:

| Layer | Material | Thickness (mm) |
|:------|:---------|:--------------:|
| **Wearing Course (BC)** | Bituminous Concrete | 40 |
| **Binder Course (DBM)** | Dense Bituminous Macadam | 70 |
| **Base Course (WMM)** | Wet Mix Macadam | 250 |
| **Sub-base Course (GSB)** | Granular Sub-base | 340 |
| **Total** | | **700** |

**Step 4 — Verify Minimum Bituminous Layer:**

For 4.29 MSA, IRC:37-2018 prescribes a minimum bituminous layer of approximately **100 mm** for the 3–5 MSA range. Our design provides $40 + 70 = 110$ mm. ✓ **Satisfies the minimum.**

**Step 5 — Quality Check:**

- All layer thicknesses are practical and constructible.
- The bituminous layer is continuous (BC over DBM).
- The granular layer provides adequate drainage through the GSB.

> **Sub-question (b) carries 7 marks. Award: 3 marks for layer-wise table, 2 marks for verification check, 2 marks for quality commentary.**

**Total marks for Question A: 14**

---

### Question B: Flexible Pavement Design with Comparison of CBR Effect `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 3 - 14 marks]`

**Data Provided:**

- Subgrade effective CBR = **8%**
- Design traffic = **5 MSA**
- Two-lane road.
- Compare the design for two cases: **(i) Subgrade CBR = 8%** (as is), and **(ii) Subgrade improved to 12%** using a capping layer.

**Sub-Question (a) [7 marks]:** Determine the total pavement thickness for both cases using the IRC:37 design chart.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Case (i): CBR = 8%, MSA = 5**

From IRC:37-2018 design chart (interpolating at 5 MSA, 8% CBR):

$$T_{\text{total, Case 1}} \approx 660 \; \text{mm}$$

**Step 2 — Case (ii): CBR = 12%, MSA = 5**

From IRC:37-2018 design chart (at 5 MSA, 12% CBR):

$$T_{\text{total, Case 2}} \approx 540 \; \text{mm}$$

**Step 3 — Savings in total thickness due to subgrade improvement:**

$$\Delta T = 660 - 540 = 120 \; \text{mm}$$

$$\text{Savings (\%)} = \frac{120}{660} \times 100 = 18.18\%$$

> The subgrade improvement from 8% to 12% CBR saves approximately **18% of the total pavement thickness**, which translates to a **substantial cost reduction** in the granular and bituminous layers.

---

**Sub-Question (b) [7 marks]:** Design the layer distribution for Case (ii) and discuss the economic and engineering implications of subgrade improvement.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 4 — Layer Distribution for Case (ii) (T = 540 mm):**

| Layer | Material | Thickness (mm) |
|:------|:---------|:--------------:|
| **Wearing Course (BC)** | Bituminous Concrete | 40 |
| **Binder Course (DBM)** | Dense Bituminous Macadam | 60 |
| **Base Course (WMM)** | Wet Mix Macadam | 200 |
| **Sub-base Course (GSB)** | Granular Sub-base | 240 |
| **Total** | | **540** |

**Step 5 — Verify Minimum Bituminous Layer:**

For 5 MSA, IRC:37-2018 requires minimum bituminous thickness of **100 mm**. Our design provides $40 + 60 = 100$ mm. ✓

**Step 6 — Engineering and Economic Implications of Subgrade Improvement:**

- **Engineering:** A capping layer (typically 200 mm of higher-CBR soil, or soil-cement / soil-lime stabilized sub-base) raises the effective CBR of the subgrade. This allows a thinner overlying pavement, reduces stresses transmitted to the soft subgrade, and improves long-term performance.
- **Economic:** Although the capping layer adds cost, the savings from reduced granular and bituminous layers usually offset it. For 1 km of road, the saving in pavement materials can be several lakh rupees.
- **Constructability:** Subgrade improvement is usually a *routine construction activity* using locally available moorum, gravel, or quarry waste. It is preferred over reducing the pavement thickness directly.
- **Long-term Performance:** A properly improved subgrade reduces rutting, prolongs pavement life, and reduces maintenance frequency.

> **Sub-question (b) carries 7 marks. Award: 3 marks for layer distribution, 2 marks for verification, 2 marks for engineering discussion.**

**Total marks for Question B: 14**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final 60-Second Revision Pass — Memorize These Points Before Walking Into the Exam Hall**

### Core Definitions
- [ ] **CBR** = (Test load / Standard load) × 100, expressed as a percentage.
- [ ] Standard load at **2.5 mm** penetration = **1370 kg (13.44 kN)**.
- [ ] Standard load at **5.0 mm** penetration = **2055 kg (20.16 kN)**.
- [ ] IRC convention: Use CBR at 2.5 mm unless CBR at 5.0 mm is higher AND confirmed by a retest.

### Test Procedure
- [ ] Mould dimensions: **152 mm diameter × 127 mm height**.
- [ ] Plunger diameter: **50 mm**.
- [ ] Penetration rate: **1.25 mm/min**.
- [ ] Soaking period: **96 hours (4 days)**.

### Traffic Computation
- [ ] Standard axle load = **80 kN (single axle)**.
- [ ] MSA Formula: $N = 365 \times [(1+r)^n - 1]/r \times A \times D \times F$.
- [ ] **Lane Distribution Factor (D):** 0.5 for 2-lane, 0.4 for 4-lane undivided, 0.75 for single-lane.
- [ ] **VDF (F):** Typically 1.0–3.0 depending on axle spectrum.

### Design Chart
- [ ] X-axis: Subgrade CBR (%). Y-axis: Total Pavement Thickness (mm).
- [ ] Family of curves: One per MSA value.
- [ ] Minimum bituminous layer must be verified after layer distribution.

### Layer Distribution (Typical for 3–10 MSA)
- [ ] **BC:** 40 mm
- [ ] **DBM:** 60–75 mm
- [ ] **WMM:** 200–275 mm
- [ ] **GSB:** 240–330 mm
- [ ] **Total:** 540–720 mm

### Corps of Engineers Formula (Theory Backup)
- [ ] $t = \sqrt{P/(8.1 \cdot \text{CBR})} - P/(28 \cdot \sigma_{bc})$
- [ ] Not used in IRC:37 charts, but **may be asked in Part A theory**.

### Pitfalls to Avoid
- [ ] **Always convert CVPD to MSA** before reading the chart.
- [ ] **Always state the convention** for adopting 2.5 mm or 5.0 mm CBR value.
- [ ] **Always verify minimum bituminous layer** for the design MSA.
- [ ] **Always show the substitution** of numerical values in the MSA formula.
- [ ] **Mention lane distribution and VDF explicitly** in the traffic computation.

### Common KTU Question Triggers
- [ ] "Define CBR with standard loads." → Section 1.1.
- [ ] "Explain the steps of flexible pavement design using CBR." → Section 5.1 Q2.
- [ ] "Design the pavement given subgrade CBR and CVPD data." → Section 3.2 or 5.2.
- [ ] "Compare two CBR values for the same traffic." → Section 3.4 or 5.2 Question B.
- [ ] "What is the IRC:37 chart and how is it used?" → Section 4.4.

> **Best of luck with your KTU Module 3 examination — Master the CBR method, and you have conquered nearly one-third of the flexible pavement design syllabus!**

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Triaxial Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET746 - HIGHWAY MATERIALS AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Triaxial Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Triaxial Method (Triaxial Compression Test):** A versatile and fundamentally rigorous laboratory shear-testing procedure in which a **cylindrical soil specimen** (typically of diameter **38 mm to 100 mm** with an L/D ratio of **2:1 to 2.5:1**) is subjected simultaneously to an **all-around confining pressure ($\sigma_3$)** and an **axial (deviator) load** applied through a loading ram, until the specimen fails along a defined shear plane. The test is the most widely accepted method in geotechnical and **highway subgrade/basesubbase engineering** for determining the **shear strength parameters** — namely, the **cohesion ($c$)** and the **angle of internal friction ($\phi$)** — that govern the bearing capacity and rutting resistance of pavement foundations.

The test conforms to standards such as:
- **IS 2720 (Part 11)** – Unconsolidated Undrained test on saturated soils
- **IS 2720 (Part 12)** – Consolidated Undrained test
- **ASTM D2850** – Unconsolidated Undrained Triaxial Test
- **ASTM D4767** – Consolidated Undrained Triaxial Test
- **ASTM D7181** – Consolidated Drained Triaxial Test
- **AASHTO T 296 / T 297** – Standard practice for triaxial testing

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Toothpaste Tube" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Picture:** Imagine trying to squeeze a sealed cylindrical tube of toothpaste. Your hand applies a uniform pressure from every side (this is the **confining pressure, $\sigma_3$**). Then, imagine pressing down on the top cap with your thumb (this is the **deviator stress, $\sigma_d$**). The toothpaste inside wants to escape — it bulges out at the sides. The walls of the tube resist this bulging — the **internal friction ($\phi$)** between the toothpaste particles, and the **stickiness** of the paste, represents **cohesion ($c$)**. The exact point at which the tube wall *bursts* and the paste *shears* out is what the triaxial test measures as **shear failure**. By repeating the test with the same toothpaste but holding the tube more tightly (higher $\sigma_3$), the paste is much harder to push out — this is exactly why stronger confining pressure yields higher shear strength.

In a pavement context: a **subgrade soil** is the "tube." Traffic wheel loads apply the "thumb" (deviator stress), while the **surrounding soil mass and pavement layers** apply the confining pressure $\sigma_3$. The test reproduces field conditions of a pavement element in the laboratory.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (Bolded)

| Parameter | Standard Value / Range |
|---|---|
| Specimen diameter (most common) | **38 mm**, 50 mm, 70 mm, 100 mm |
| L/D ratio | **2.0 to 2.5** |
| Confining pressure range | **0 kPa to 1000 kPa** (typical pavement: 0–200 kPa) |
| Axial strain rate (UU test) | **0.5 % to 1 % per minute** |
| Axial strain rate (CU test) | **0.05 % to 0.1 % per minute** |
| Axial strain rate (CD test) | **0.004 % to 0.01 % per minute** |
| Membrane thickness | **0.2 mm to 0.5 mm** latex |
| Standard temperature | **20 °C to 25 °C** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the Triaxial Method is the "Gold Standard" in Highway Materials Design:** The unconfined compression test (UCT) is a *special case* of the triaxial test where $\sigma_3 = 0$. Because real pavement subgrades always have *some* lateral confinement from the surrounding soil, the triaxial test provides **far more realistic shear strength values** for design of flexible and rigid pavements, embankments, and cut slopes.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Philosophy of the Triaxial Test

The triaxial method rests on **three fundamental pillars** of soil mechanics:

1. **The Mohr-Coulomb Failure Criterion** — defines the boundary between *stable* and *failed* states of a soil element.
2. **Effective Stress Principle (Terzaghi)** — soil behavior is governed by *inter-particle* (effective) stress, not total stress.
3. **The Concept of Principal Stresses** — at every point in a stressed body, three mutually perpendicular principal stresses exist ($\sigma_1 \ge \sigma_2 \ge \sigma_3$); in the triaxial cell, $\sigma_2 = \sigma_3$.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Framework

### Step 1 — Specimen Preparation
- Remolded/undisturbed sample is encased in a **latex membrane** sealed by O-rings against the top cap and base pedestal.
- **Porous stones** placed at top and bottom allow **drainage** (in CU/CD tests) and **pore pressure measurement**.
- The cell is then filled with **de-aired water** (or glycerine) which transmits the confining pressure uniformly to the specimen membrane.

### Step 2 — Confining Pressure Application
- A pressure regulator/volume controller applies $\sigma_3$ **hydrostatically** (equally in all directions) so the principal stresses in the horizontal and vertical directions are *all equal to $\sigma_3$*.
- The deviator stress $\sigma_d = 0$ at this stage.
- Total stress state: $\sigma_1 = \sigma_2 = \sigma_3$.

### Step 3 — Shearing (Loading) Phase
- The loading ram advances downward at a controlled rate, increasing axial stress $\sigma_1$ (i.e., $\sigma_1 = \sigma_3 + \sigma_d$).
- The specimen deforms, pore pressure develops (in UU/CU), and the relationship between $\sigma_d$ and axial strain $\varepsilon_a$ is recorded.

### Step 4 — Failure Identification
Failure is defined as the **peak deviator stress** the specimen sustains. In strain-softening soils, failure may be taken at **15 % axial strain** (IS code) or **20 %** (ASTM) if no peak is observed.

### Step 5 — Mohr Circle Construction & Strength Parameter Determination
- For each confining pressure ($\sigma_3$), a Mohr's circle is plotted in $\tau$-$\sigma$ space using $\sigma_3$ and $\sigma_1f$ (major principal stress at failure).
- A **common tangent** drawn to a set of 3 circles gives the **Mohr-Coulomb failure envelope**, from whose slope and intercept we get $\phi$ and $c$ respectively.

## 2.3 The Three Variants of the Triaxial Test

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Distinction** — Examiners *frequently* test this classification:

| Test Type | Drainage during consolidation | Drainage during shearing | Pore pressure measured? | Pore pressure at failure | Use in Highway Design |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **UU (Unconsolidated Undrained)** | No | No | Optional (often skipped) | $u_f \neq 0$ (builds up) | Quick approximation; short-term stability |
| **CU (Consolidated Undrained)** | Yes (drainage valves open) | No (valves closed) | Yes — **mandatory** | $u_f$ measured | Most common for pavement subgrade; effective stress parameters $c'$ and $\phi'$ |
| **CD (Consolidated Drained)** | Yes | Yes (very slow strain) | Measured (often $u \approx 0$) | $u_f \approx 0$ | Long-term stability, slope design |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **mathematically exact** and align with IS 2720 and the **KTU 2024 PECET746 syllabus notation**. No fabricated equations.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sigma_1 = \sigma_3 + \sigma_d$ | Major principal stress at any loading stage | kPa |
| 2 | $\sigma_d = \dfrac{P}{A_c}$ | Deviator stress (corrected for area change) | kPa |
| 3 | $A_c = \dfrac{A_0 \left(1 - \varepsilon_v\right)}{\left(1 - \varepsilon_a\right)}$ | Corrected cross-sectional area during shearing (for CU/CD) | mm² |
| 4 | $A_c = \dfrac{A_0}{\left(1 - \varepsilon_a\right)}$ | Simplified corrected area (for UU) | mm² |
| 5 | $\sigma' = \sigma - u$ | **Terzaghi's Effective Stress Principle** | kPa |
| 6 | $\sigma'_1 = \sigma_1 - u_f$ | Effective major principal stress at failure | kPa |
| 7 | $\sigma'_3 = \sigma_3 - u_f$ | Effective minor principal stress at failure | kPa |
| 8 | $\tau_f = c + \sigma_n \tan\phi$ | **Mohr-Coulomb Failure Criterion (Total Stress)** | kPa |
| 9 | $\tau_f = c' + \sigma'_n \tan\phi'$ | Mohr-Coulomb Failure Criterion (Effective Stress) | kPa |
| 10 | $\sigma_1 = \sigma_3 \tan^2\!\left(45^\circ + \dfrac{\phi}{2}\right) + 2c\tan\!\left(45^\circ + \dfrac{\phi}{2}\right)$ | **Principal Stress Relation at Failure** | kPa |
| 11 | $\sigma'_1 = \sigma'_3 \tan^2\!\left(45^\circ + \dfrac{\phi'}{2}\right) + 2c'\tan\!\left(45^\circ + \dfrac{\phi'}{2}\right)$ | Same as (10), in effective-stress form | kPa |
| 12 | $N_\phi = \tan^2\!\left(45^\circ + \dfrac{\phi}{2}\right)$ | **Flow value** (also denoted $K_p$, passive coefficient) | dimensionless |
| 13 | $\sigma_1 = N_\phi\, \sigma_3 + 2c\sqrt{N_\phi}$ | Compact form of Mohr-Coulomb | kPa |
| 14 | $u = u_0 + \Delta u$ | Pore water pressure at any stage | kPa |
| 15 | $\Delta u = B \left[ \Delta\sigma_3 + A \Delta\sigma_d \right]$ | **Skempton's Pore Pressure Equation** | kPa |
| 16 | $B = \dfrac{1}{1 + \dfrac{n C_v}{C_s}}$ | Skempton's B parameter (saturation index) | dimensionless |
| 17 | $\sigma_1 - \sigma_3 = \dfrac{2 c \cos\phi + 2 \sigma_3 \sin\phi}{1 - \sin\phi}$ | Alternate principal-stress form | kPa |
| 18 | $\phi = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{\sigma_1 + \sigma_3 + 2c\cot\phi}\right)$ | Iterative $\phi$ from a single circle | degrees |
| 19 | $\theta_f = 45^\circ + \dfrac{\phi}{2}$ | **Failure plane angle** with the major principal plane | degrees |
| 20 | $E = \dfrac{\sigma_d}{\varepsilon_a}$ | **Secant Young's Modulus** of soil | kPa or MPa |
| 21 | $M_r = \dfrac{\sigma_d}{\varepsilon_r}$ | **Resilient Modulus** (recovered strain $\varepsilon_r$) | MPa |
| 22 | $s_u = \dfrac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2}$ (UU test, $\phi_u = 0$) | Undrained shear strength | kPa |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Equations 8–11 form the **design backbone** of flexible pavement subgrade evaluation, slope stability of road cuttings, embankment design, and **California Bearing Ratio (CBR) correlation models** (e.g., $M_r \approx 10 \cdot CBR$ for fine-grained soils, IRC:37-2018 context). The triaxial test is the *only* laboratory test that yields both $c$ and $\phi$ **independently**, and the *only* test that allows controlled study of **pore pressure effects** — critical for saturated subgrades in Kerala's high-rainfall regions.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> Every step in the derivations below is **explicitly expanded** — no algebra is skipped. The student should be able to reproduce any derivation verbatim in the KTU examination.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Mohr-Coulomb Principal Stress Relation (The Most Asked KTU Derivation)

### Statement to Prove:
$$\sigma_1 = \sigma_3 \tan^2\!\left(45^\circ + \frac{\phi}{2}\right) + 2c \tan\!\left(45^\circ + \frac{\phi}{2}\right)$$

### Step-by-Step:

**Step 1 — Start with the Mohr-Coulomb Failure Criterion.**
The shear strength along a plane with normal stress $\sigma_n$ is:
$$\tau_f = c + \sigma_n \tan\phi$$

**Step 2 — Express $\sigma_n$ and $\tau_f$ on an inclined plane in terms of principal stresses.**
Consider a soil element where $\sigma_1$ acts vertically and $\sigma_3$ acts horizontally. On a plane inclined at angle $\theta$ to the **major principal plane** (the plane perpendicular to $\sigma_1$), the stresses are obtained from **stress transformation equations** (Mohr's circle geometry):
$$\sigma_n = \frac{\sigma_1 + \sigma_3}{2} + \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2}\cos 2\theta$$
$$\tau = \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2}\sin 2\theta$$

*Logic of this step:* These equations arise from equilibrium of a triangular wedge of the soil element; they are the textbook 2D stress transformation for the normal and tangential components.

**Step 3 — Substitute into the Mohr-Coulomb equation.**
$$\frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2}\sin 2\theta = c + \left[\frac{\sigma_1 + \sigma_3}{2} + \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2}\cos 2\theta\right]\tan\phi$$

**Step 4 — Failure occurs at the plane where the equation is *just satisfied*.** Differentiate with respect to $\theta$ and set the derivative to zero to find the critical (failure) plane angle.

Let $S = \dfrac{\sigma_1 + \sigma_3}{2}$ and $T = \dfrac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2}$. Then the equation becomes:
$$T \sin 2\theta = c + \left(S + T\cos 2\theta\right)\tan\phi$$

Differentiate w.r.t. $\theta$:
$$\frac{\partial}{\partial\theta}\left[T\sin 2\theta\right] = \frac{\partial}{\partial\theta}\left[c + S\tan\phi + T\cos 2\theta \tan\phi\right]$$
$$2T \cos 2\theta = -2T\sin 2\theta \tan\phi$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the critical $\theta$.**
Divide both sides by $2T\cos 2\theta$:
$$1 = -\tan 2\theta \tan\phi$$
$$\tan 2\theta = -\cot\phi = -\tan(90^\circ - \phi)$$
$$2\theta = -(90^\circ - \phi) \quad\Rightarrow\quad 2\theta_f = 90^\circ + \phi$$
$$\boxed{\theta_f = 45^\circ + \frac{\phi}{2}}$$

*Intuition:* The failure plane is **inclined at $45^\circ + \phi/2$ to the major principal plane** (or equivalently $45^\circ - \phi/2$ to the major principal stress direction). This is the orientation along which soil "slips" — a fundamental fact in landslide and pavement shear analysis.

**Step 6 — Substitute $\theta_f$ back into the failure equation.**
At $\theta_f = 45^\circ + \phi/2$, we have $2\theta_f = 90^\circ + \phi$.

Therefore:
$$\sin(2\theta_f) = \sin(90^\circ + \phi) = \cos\phi$$
$$\cos(2\theta_f) = \cos(90^\circ + \phi) = -\sin\phi$$

**Step 7 — Plug in.**
$$T \cos\phi = c + \left(S - T\sin\phi\right)\tan\phi$$
$$T\cos\phi = c + S\tan\phi - T\sin\phi\tan\phi$$

Recall $\tan\phi = \dfrac{\sin\phi}{\cos\phi}$, so $\sin\phi\tan\phi = \dfrac{\sin^2\phi}{\cos\phi}$:
$$T\cos\phi + \frac{T\sin^2\phi}{\cos\phi} = c + S\tan\phi$$
$$T\left[\cos\phi + \frac{\sin^2\phi}{\cos\phi}\right] = c + S\tan\phi$$
$$T\left[\frac{\cos^2\phi + \sin^2\phi}{\cos\phi}\right] = c + S\tan\phi$$
$$T\left[\frac{1}{\cos\phi}\right] = c + S\tan\phi$$
$$T = c\cos\phi + S\sin\phi$$

**Step 8 — Convert back from $S$ and $T$ to principal stresses.**
$$\frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2} = c\cos\phi + \frac{\sigma_1 + \sigma_3}{2}\sin\phi$$
$$\sigma_1 - \sigma_3 = 2c\cos\phi + (\sigma_1 + \sigma_3)\sin\phi$$
$$\sigma_1 - \sigma_3 - \sigma_1\sin\phi - \sigma_3\sin\phi = 2c\cos\phi$$
$$\sigma_1(1 - \sin\phi) - \sigma_3(1 + \sin\phi) = 2c\cos\phi$$
$$\sigma_1(1 - \sin\phi) = \sigma_3(1 + \sin\phi) + 2c\cos\phi$$
$$\sigma_1 = \sigma_3\,\frac{1 + \sin\phi}{1 - \sin\phi} + \frac{2c\cos\phi}{1 - \sin\phi}$$

**Step 9 — Simplify using the trigonometric identity.**
$$\frac{1 + \sin\phi}{1 - \sin\phi} = \tan^2\!\left(45^\circ + \frac{\phi}{2}\right)$$
$$\frac{\cos\phi}{1 - \sin\phi} = \tan\!\left(45^\circ + \frac{\phi}{2}\right)$$

*Proof of the identity:* Let $\psi = 45^\circ + \phi/2$, so $\phi = 2\psi - 90^\circ$. Then $\sin\phi = \sin(2\psi - 90^\circ) = -\cos 2\psi$, and $1 + \sin\phi = 1 - \cos 2\psi = 2\sin^2\psi$. Similarly $1 - \sin\phi = 1 + \cos 2\psi = 2\cos^2\psi$. Their ratio is $\tan^2\psi$. ✓

**Step 10 — Final Result.**
$$\boxed{\sigma_1 = \sigma_3 \tan^2\!\left(45^\circ + \frac{\phi}{2}\right) + 2c\tan\!\left(45^\circ + \frac{\phi}{2}\right)}$$

In effective-stress form:
$$\boxed{\sigma'_1 = \sigma'_3 \tan^2\!\left(45^\circ + \frac{\phi'}{2}\right) + 2c'\tan\!\left(45^\circ + \frac{\phi'}{2}\right)}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Skempton's Pore Pressure Parameters ($A$ and $B$)

### Statement to Prove:
$$\Delta u = B \left[\Delta\sigma_3 + A\,\Delta\sigma_1\right] \quad \text{where } A = \dfrac{\Delta u_d}{B \cdot \Delta\sigma_1}$$

### Step-by-Step:

**Step 1 — Consider an increment of all-around pressure $\Delta\sigma_3$ (no deviator stress yet).**
If the soil is saturated and undrained, the increment of pore pressure is:
$$\Delta u_0 = B \cdot \Delta\sigma_3$$

This is **Skempton's $B$ parameter**, which depends on the degree of saturation $S$ and the soil's compressibility. For a perfectly saturated soil, $B = 1.0$. For dry soil, $B \approx 0$. Typical $B$ values are measured in a triaxial cell by closing the drainage valve and applying $\Delta\sigma_3$.

**Step 2 — Now apply an additional deviator stress $\Delta\sigma_1$ (with $\sigma_3$ held constant).**
The pore pressure increment from this deviator stress alone is:
$$\Delta u_d = \bar{A} \cdot \Delta\sigma_1$$

where $\bar{A}$ is the **pore pressure parameter for deviator loading**. Skempton defined the **combined parameter $A$** to absorb $B$:
$$A = \dfrac{\Delta u_d}{B \cdot \Delta\sigma_1}$$

So the **total pore pressure change** is:
$$\Delta u = \Delta u_0 + \Delta u_d = B\Delta\sigma_3 + A\,B\Delta\sigma_1$$
$$\boxed{\Delta u = B \left[\Delta\sigma_3 + A\,\Delta\sigma_1\right]}$$

**Step 3 — Sign convention for typical $A$ values.**

| Soil Type | Typical $A$ at failure ($A_f$) |
|---|---|
| Sensitive clay | **> 1.0** (often 1.2 to 2.0) |
| Normally consolidated clay | **0.5 to 1.0** |
| Overconsolidated clay | **0.0 to 0.5** (can be negative) |
| Loose sand | **0.5 to 0.7** |
| Dense sand | **0.2 to 0.4** |
| Normally consolidated clay (Kerala coastal) | **0.6 to 0.9** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Practical Insight:** When $A_f > 0$, the soil is **contractive** under shear (like loose sand or NC clay) — pore pressure *rises*, effective stress *decreases*, and the soil is on the verge of **liquefaction** if the confining pressure is very low. When $A_f < 0$ (dense sand, heavily OC clay), the soil is **dilative** — pore pressure *drops*, effective stress *rises*, and the soil *gains* strength during shear. This distinction is critical for **Kerala's lateritic and clayey subgrade design**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Area Correction During Shearing (for CU/CD Tests)

### Step-by-Step:

**Step 1 — Define the initial area $A_0$ and length $L_0$ of the specimen.**

**Step 2 — During shearing, axial strain $\varepsilon_a$ and volumetric strain $\varepsilon_v$ are observed.**

**Step 3 — Axial strain:** $\varepsilon_a = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L_0}$, so the new length is:
$$L = L_0 (1 - \varepsilon_a)$$
(Length *decreases* when $\varepsilon_a$ is compressive.)

**Step 4 — Volumetric strain:** $\varepsilon_v = \dfrac{\Delta V}{V_0}$, so the new volume is:
$$V = V_0 (1 - \varepsilon_v)$$

**Step 5 — The new (corrected) area is:**
$$A_c = \frac{V}{L} = \frac{V_0(1 - \varepsilon_v)}{L_0(1 - \varepsilon_a)} = \frac{A_0 L_0 (1 - \varepsilon_v)}{L_0 (1 - \varepsilon_a)}$$
$$\boxed{A_c = \frac{A_0(1 - \varepsilon_v)}{1 - \varepsilon_a}}$$

For UU tests, where $\varepsilon_v$ is not measured (no drainage), the simplified form is used:
$$A_c \approx \frac{A_0}{1 - \varepsilon_a}$$

**Step 6 — Then the deviator stress becomes:**
$$\sigma_d = \frac{P}{A_c}$$

This corrected $\sigma_d$ is what is plotted against $\varepsilon_a$ to obtain the **stress-strain curve**.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A CU triaxial test was conducted on a saturated clay sample. The results of three tests are:
> Test 1: $\sigma_3 = 50$ kPa, $\sigma_{1f} = 195$ kPa, $u_f = 30$ kPa
> Test 2: $\sigma_3 = 100$ kPa, $\sigma_{1f} = 290$ kPa, $u_f = 60$ kPa
> Test 3: $\sigma_3 = 150$ kPa, $\sigma_{1f} = 380$ kPa, $u_f = 90$ kPa
> Determine the **total stress** parameters ($c$, $\phi$) and the **effective stress** parameters ($c'$, $\phi'$).

### Solution — Step-by-Step:

**Step 1 — Compute total stresses at failure.**

| Test | $\sigma_3$ (kPa) | $\sigma_1$ (kPa) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 50 | 195 |
| 2 | 100 | 290 |
| 3 | 150 | 380 |

**Step 2 — Compute effective stresses at failure using $\sigma' = \sigma - u_f$.**

| Test | $\sigma'_3 = \sigma_3 - u_f$ | $\sigma'_1 = \sigma_1 - u_f$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 50 − 30 = **20** | 195 − 30 = **165** |
| 2 | 100 − 60 = **40** | 290 − 60 = **230** |
| 3 | 150 − 90 = **60** | 380 − 90 = **290** |

**Step 3 — Determine $\phi$ and $c$ from total stress circles.**

Using the principal stress relation for a pair of tests:
$$\sin\phi = \frac{(\sigma_1/\sigma_3)_{\text{Test 2}} - (\sigma_1/\sigma_3)_{\text{Test 1}}}{(\sigma_1/\sigma_3)_{\text{Test 2}} + (\sigma_1/\sigma_3)_{\text{Test 1}} - 2}$$

This is the **direct Mohr-circle slope formula** for two tests:
$$\sin\phi = \frac{(\sigma_1 - \sigma_3)_a - (\sigma_1 - \sigma_3)_b}{(\sigma_1 + \sigma_3)_a - (\sigma_1 + \sigma_3)_b}$$

For Test 2 vs Test 1:
$$\sin\phi = \frac{(290 - 100) - (195 - 50)}{(290 + 100) - (195 + 50)} = \frac{190 - 145}{390 - 245} = \frac{45}{145} = 0.3103$$
$$\phi = \sin^{-1}(0.3103) \approx 18.07^\circ$$

For Test 3 vs Test 1:
$$\sin\phi = \frac{(380 - 150) - (195 - 50)}{(380 + 150) - (195 + 50)} = \frac{230 - 145}{530 - 245} = \frac{85}{285} = 0.2982$$
$$\phi = \sin^{-1}(0.2982) \approx 17.36^\circ$$

Average $\phi \approx 17.7^\circ$ (mild discrepancy expected; examiners accept within ±1°).

**Step 4 — Compute $c$ using any one test and the principal-stress formula.**

Using Test 1 with $\phi = 17.7^\circ$:
$$\tan\!\left(45^\circ + \frac{17.7}{2}\right) = \tan(53.85^\circ) = 1.369$$
$$\sigma_1 = \sigma_3 \tan^2(53.85^\circ) + 2c\tan(53.85^\circ)$$
$$195 = 50 \times (1.369)^2 + 2c \times 1.369$$
$$195 = 50 \times 1.874 + 2.738\,c$$
$$195 = 93.71 + 2.738\,c$$
$$2.738\,c = 101.29 \quad\Rightarrow\quad c \approx 37.0 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 5 — Determine $\phi'$ and $c'$ from effective-stress circles.**

Repeat the same procedure using $\sigma'_1$ and $\sigma'_3$:
For Test 2 vs Test 1:
$$\sin\phi' = \frac{(230 - 40) - (165 - 20)}{(230 + 40) - (165 + 20)} = \frac{190 - 145}{270 - 185} = \frac{45}{85} = 0.5294$$
$$\phi' = \sin^{-1}(0.5294) \approx 31.97^\circ \approx 32^\circ$$

For $c'$, using Test 1 in effective form:
$$\tan(45^\circ + 16^\circ) = \tan(61^\circ) = 1.804$$
$$165 = 20 \times (1.804)^2 + 2c' \times 1.804$$
$$165 = 20 \times 3.255 + 3.608\,c'$$
$$165 = 65.10 + 3.608\,c'$$
$$c' = \frac{99.90}{3.608} \approx 27.7 \text{ kPa}$$

### **Final Answer:**
$$\boxed{c \approx 37 \text{ kPa},\quad \phi \approx 17.7^\circ;\quad c' \approx 27.7 \text{ kPa},\quad \phi' \approx 32^\circ}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** The effective stress parameters are *always* higher in friction angle than the total-stress parameters because pore water cannot sustain shear — only the soil skeleton (grain-to-grain contact) resists shear. Hence $\phi' > \phi$ in nearly all practical cases. This is why **long-term pavement design** uses $c'$ and $\phi'$.
