# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Wheel Load Stress in a Rigid Pavement. [3 Marks] `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
Wheel load stress in a rigid pavement is the **maximum tensile flexural stress** induced in the Portland Cement Concrete (PCC) slab due to the application of a single wheel load through a tire contact area. It is calculated using **Westergaard's theory** for three critical loading cases — **interior, edge, and corner** — and depends on the wheel load magnitude ($P$), slab thickness ($h$), radius of contact area ($a$), modulus of subgrade reaction ($k$), modulus of elasticity of concrete ($E$), and Poisson's ratio ($\mu$). The wheel load stress must remain below the **Modulus of Rupture** of the concrete for safe design.

---

### Question 2: What is Radius of Relative Stiffness? State its significance. [3 Marks] `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
The **Radius of Relative Stiffness (l)** is a fundamental parameter in rigid pavement analysis that represents the **effective length scale at which the slab flexural rigidity balances the subgrade support stiffness**. It is given by the formula:

$$l = \left[ \frac{E h^3}{12 k (1-\mu^2)} \right]^{1/4}$$

**Significance:**
1. It is a measure of the **stiffness ratio** between the concrete slab and the supporting subgrade.
2. It defines the **radius of the deflection basin** formed in the subgrade under a load.
3. It is the **only parameter** that combines slab and subgrade properties and appears in all Westergaard stress formulas.
4. It is used to determine the **critical stress location** (interior if distance from edge > 1.5$l$).

---

## 5.2 Part B Question Choice (14 Marks)

### Question Choice A: Westergaard Wheel Load Stress Analysis [14 Marks] `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a)** Enumerate the **three types of wheel load stresses** as per Westergaard's theory. State the **assumptions** and the **formula for radius of relative stiffness (l)** with a clear explanation of each term. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

The three types of wheel load stresses as per Westergaard's theory are:

1. **Interior Loading Stress ($\sigma_i$):** Stress developed when the wheel load is applied at a location **far from any free edge** (distance from edge > 1.5$l$).

$$\sigma_i = \frac{0.316 P}{h^2} \left[ 4 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{l}{a}\right) + 1.069 \right]$$

2. **Edge Loading Stress ($\sigma_e$):** Stress developed when the wheel load is applied **at the free longitudinal edge** of the slab.

$$\sigma_e = \frac{0.572 P}{h^2} \left[ 4 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{l}{a}\right) + 0.359 \right]$$

3. **Corner Loading Stress ($\sigma_c$):** Stress developed when the wheel load is applied **at the corner of the slab** (intersection of two free edges). As per Bradbury's simplified form:

$$\sigma_c = \frac{3 P}{h^2} \left[ 1 - \frac{a}{l} \right]$$

**Assumptions of Westergaard's Theory:**
- The concrete slab is **homogeneous, isotropic, and elastic**.
- The slab has **uniform thickness** throughout.
- The subgrade behaves as a **dense liquid** (Winkler's foundation) — reaction at any point is **proportional to deflection** at that point: $p = k \cdot w$.
- The slab is **infinite in extent** for the interior case (no edge effects).
- The wheel load is applied through a **circular contact area** of radius $a$.

**Radius of Relative Stiffness:**

$$l = \left[ \frac{E h^3}{12 k (1 - \mu^2)} \right]^{1/4}$$

| Term | Meaning | Typical Value |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $E$ | Modulus of Elasticity of concrete | $3.0 \times 10^5$ kg/cm² |
| $h$ | Slab thickness | 20–30 cm |
| $k$ | Modulus of subgrade reaction | 5–15 kg/cm³ |
| $\mu$ | Poisson's ratio of concrete | 0.15 |

---

**(b)** A concrete pavement slab of thickness **20 cm** is subjected to a wheel load of **4500 kg** with a contact radius of **15 cm**. Given $E = 3.0 \times 10^5$ kg/cm², $\mu = 0.15$, $k = 6$ kg/cm³, and $S_c = 45$ kg/cm². **Compute the interior and edge wheel load stresses and check the adequacy** of the design. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute $l$**

$$l^4 = \frac{E h^3}{12 k (1 - \mu^2)} = \frac{(3.0 \times 10^5)(20)^3}{12 \times 6 \times (1 - 0.0225)}$$

$$l^4 = \frac{(3.0 \times 10^5)(8000)}{72 \times 0.9775} = \frac{2.4 \times 10^9}{70.38} = 3.41 \times 10^7$$

$$l = (3.41 \times 10^7)^{1/4} = 76.3 \, cm$$

**Step 2: Compute $l/a$**

$$\frac{l}{a} = \frac{76.3}{15} = 5.087, \quad \log_{10}(5.087) = 0.7064$$

**Step 3: Interior Stress**

$$\sigma_i = \frac{0.316 \times 4500}{(20)^2} \left[ 4 \times 0.7064 + 1.069 \right]$$

$$\sigma_i = \frac{1422}{400} \left[ 2.8256 + 1.069 \right] = 3.555 \times 3.8946 = 13.85 \, kg/cm^2$$

**Step 4: Edge Stress**

$$\sigma_e = \frac{0.572 \times 4500}{(20)^2} \left[ 4 \times 0.7064 + 0.359 \right]$$

$$\sigma_e = \frac{2574}{400} \left[ 2.8256 + 0.359 \right] = 6.435 \times 3.1846 = 20.49 \, kg/cm^2$$

**Step 5: Adequacy Check**

| Case | $\sigma_{computed}$ | $S_c$ | Factor of Safety | Status |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Interior | 13.85 | 45 | 3.25 | ✓ Safe |
| Edge | 20.49 | 45 | 2.20 | ✓ Safe (governing) |

**Conclusion:** The design is **safe** with the edge case governing. The Factor of Safety of 2.20 is adequate for normal highway loading conditions. However, when **temperature warping stresses** are added (Module 5), the design may require a **slight increase in slab thickness** to 22 cm to maintain combined stress below $S_c$.

---

### Question Choice B: Corner Stress and Practical Application [14 Marks] `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the **Westergaard corner stress formula** with the help of a neat sketch. Discuss how the corner stress differs from the interior and edge stresses. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Sketch Description:** A square slab of side $L$ is shown with a wheel load $P$ applied at one corner through a circular contact area of radius $a$. The contact area straddles the two free edges meeting at the corner. The critical tensile stress occurs at the **top surface of the slab**, along a diagonal running from the corner into the slab interior.

**Derivation (Conceptual Outline):**

For a corner load, the slab is free to **lift up** at the corner since there is no support beyond the free edges. The portion of the slab under the corner load behaves like a **cantilever** projecting diagonally from the supporting subgrade. The deflection basin is therefore **asymmetric** and concentrated near the corner.

**Step 1:** Equating the upward subgrade reaction to the applied load $P$ over a quarter-circle of effective radius $l$:

$$P = \int_0^{l/2} k \cdot w(r) \cdot 2\pi r \, dr$$

**Step 2:** Westergaard solved the moment equilibrium for the corner-loaded slab and obtained:

$$\sigma_c = \frac{3 P}{h^2} \left[ 1 - \left(\frac{a \sqrt{2}}{l}\right)^{0.6} \right]$$

**Step 3:** Bradbury's linearized approximation (assumes $a/l$ is small):

$$\sigma_c = \frac{3 P}{h^2} \left[ 1 - \frac{a}{l} \right]$$

**Comparison with Interior and Edge Stresses:**

| Parameter | Interior | Edge | Corner |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Max stress magnitude | Lowest | Intermediate | Highest (theoretically) |
| Location of max stress | Bottom, under load | Bottom, parallel to edge | **Top**, diagonal from corner |
| Crack pattern | Radial | Longitudinal | **Diagonal** |
| Critical case in design? | No | **Yes (usually governing)** | Mitigated by load transfer |

---

**(b)** A concrete pavement has the following data: $P = 6000$ kg, $h = 25$ cm, $a = 12$ cm, $k = 7$ kg/cm³, $E = 3.0 \times 10^5$ kg/cm², $\mu = 0.15$, $S_c = 45$ kg/cm². **Compute the corner stress** using both Westergaard and Bradbury formulas and comment. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute $l$**

$$l^4 = \frac{(3.0 \times 10^5)(25)^3}{12 \times 7 \times 0.9775} = \frac{4.6875 \times 10^9}{82.11} = 5.71 \times 10^7$$

$$l = (5.71 \times 10^7)^{1/4} = 86.7 \, cm$$

**Step 2: Westergaard Corner Stress**

$$\sigma_c^{W} = \frac{3 \times 6000}{(25)^2} \left[ 1 - \left(\frac{12 \sqrt{2}}{86.7}\right)^{0.6} \right]$$

$$= \frac{18000}{625} \left[ 1 - \left(0.1957\right)^{0.6} \right]$$

$$= 28.8 \left[ 1 - 0.323 \right] = 28.8 \times 0.677 = 19.50 \, kg/cm^2$$

**Step 3: Bradbury Corner Stress**

$$\sigma_c^{B} = \frac{3 \times 6000}{(25)^2} \left[ 1 - \frac{12}{86.7} \right]$$

$$= 28.8 \left[ 1 - 0.1384 \right] = 28.8 \times 0.8616 = 24.81 \, kg/cm^2$$

**Step 4: Comment**

- Bradbury's formula gives a **more conservative (higher)** stress value than Westergaard's original formula.
- IRC:58-2015 recommends Bradbury's formula for routine design.
- Both values are below $S_c = 45$ kg/cm², so the design is **safe** with respect to corner loading alone.
- In real design, **edge stress** is usually the governing case as the corner case is mitigated by dowel bars and tie bars which provide **load transfer efficiency** across the joint.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 
> 1. **Unit Mismatch is the #1 mark-deduction reason.** Ensure $P$ is in **kg**, $h$, $a$, $l$ all in **cm** for formulas to yield stress in **kg/cm²** (or convert consistently to SI: N and m).
> 2. **Forgetting the term $1 - \mu^2$ in $l$** loses 1 full mark. The Poisson's ratio factor is non-negotiable.
> 3. **Using $\log_{10}$ vs $\ln$** — Westergaard's formula uses **logarithm base 10** explicitly. Using natural log gives a wrong answer.
> 4. **Confusing Westergaard's corner formula vs Bradbury's** — Know both, and quote Bradbury's for IRC design.
> 5. **Ignoring the design check** — Always conclude with the Factor of Safety calculation and a comment on adequacy. A 14-mark question without an adequacy check loses 2-3 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 4 — Wheel Load Stress:**

- ⭐ **Rigid pavement** relies on the **slab action** (flexural rigidity) of PCC to distribute loads; subgrade contributes via Winkler springs.
- ⭐ **Three critical load cases** (Westergaard): **Interior, Edge, Corner** — each with a separate formula.
- ⭐ **Radius of Relative Stiffness $l$** — the master parameter: $l = [Eh^3 / (12k(1-\mu^2))]^{1/4}$.
- ⭐ **Stress magnitude order:** $\sigma_c > \sigma_e > \sigma_i$, but **edge loading usually governs design** (corner mitigated by load transfer).
- ⭐ **Stress location of critical tensile fiber:** Interior = **bottom**; Edge = **bottom**; Corner = **top** (diagonal).
- ⭐ **Doubling slab thickness reduces stress by 75%** (inverse-square law: $\sigma \propto 1/h^2$).
- ⭐ **Westergaard corner formula** uses exponent 0.6; **Bradbury's** uses linear $a/l$ term.
- ⭐ **Standard values to memorize:** $E = 3.0 \times 10^5$ kg/cm², $\mu = 0.15$, $S_c \approx 0.7 f_{ck}$.
- ⭐ **Westergaard log base:** $\log_{10}$, not $\ln$.
- ⭐ **Westergaard's assumptions:** Homogeneous, isotropic, elastic slab on dense-liquid subgrade (Winkler).
- ⭐ **Modulus of subgrade reaction $k$** typical range: **2–15 kg/cm³** (clay → gravel).
- ⭐ **IRC:58-2015** uses Bradbury's formula; **AASHTO 1993** uses empirical equations (different from Westergaard).
- ⭐ **Combined stress** (Module 5) = Wheel load stress + Temperature warping stress + Frictional stress → must be $\leq S_c$.
- ⭐ **Crack pattern signature:** Interior load → radial cracks; Edge load → longitudinal cracks; Corner load → diagonal cracks.
- ⭐ **Factor of Safety** for edge stress: $FOS = S_c / \sigma_e \geq 2.0$ is typical.
- ⭐ **Loads are converted to equivalent single wheel loads** using the **Equivalent Wheel Load (EWL)** concept, or **Equivalent Single Axle Load (ESAL)** for fatigue analysis.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Warping Stress
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET746 - HIGHWAY MATERIALS AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Warping Stress

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Warping Stress** is the **flexural stress** induced in a *rigid concrete pavement slab* due to a **non-uniform temperature gradient** (or moisture gradient) between the top and bottom surfaces of the slab. This gradient causes the slab to curl or **warp** out of its plane, and since the slab's self-weight and friction with the subgrade resist this curling, internal **warping stresses** are generated.

The classical analysis of warping stress in rigid pavements is attributed to **H.M. Westergaard** (1927), and later simplified by **R.D. Bradbury** (1938) using empirical coefficients. Warping is distinct from **frictional stress** (caused by subgrade restraint against uniform thermal expansion/contraction) and from **load stress** (caused by traffic wheel loads).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Anchor (PECET746 – Module 4):** Warping stresses are studied alongside temperature stresses and load stresses in the design of *cement concrete pavements* using the **Westergaard-Bradbury approach** and the **IRC:58-2015** design methodology.

### Physical Mechanism of Warping

During the daytime, solar radiation heats the **top surface** of the slab more than the bottom. Since concrete expands when heated, the top fibers attempt to elongate more than the bottom fibers. If the slab were perfectly free, it would assume a **convex-downward (cup-up)** shape. However, the slab's self-weight and the subgrade reaction resist this deformation, inducing:

- **Tensile stresses at the bottom fiber**
- **Compressive stresses at the top fiber** (during the day)

At **night**, the top surface cools by radiation and convection, becoming colder than the bottom. The top fibers contract more, and the slab tends to curl **convex-upward (cup-down)**. This reverses the stress distribution:

- **Tensile stresses at the top fiber**
- **Compressive stresses at the bottom fiber** (during the night)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **critical condition for design is the night-time curling** because the tensile stresses occur at the *top surface*, exactly where the heaviest wheel-load-induced tensile stresses (from bottom-fiber bending) also accumulate. The **superposition** of night-time warping stress and load stress controls the slab thickness design.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Sun-Baked Footpath**
>
> Imagine a long concrete footpath slab laid flat on a sandy bed on a hot afternoon. The top surface exposed to the sun becomes very hot (say 50 °C), while the bottom in contact with cool soil remains near 30 °C. The top layer "wants" to expand, the bottom layer doesn't. Since concrete is one continuous piece, this tug-of-war bends the slab into a gentle **upward bowl (cup)** — the edges lift, the middle stays down. The slab's own weight (and the sand beneath) holds it down, but in doing so, the slab develops internal stresses: **tension at the bottom, compression on top**.
>
> Now flip the day to night: the top surface radiates heat to the cold sky and cools below the bottom. The top now "wants" to shrink. The slab tries to curl into a **downward bowl (crown)** — the center lifts, edges stay pinned. Now tension develops on the **top surface**, while the bottom is in compression.
>
> **This internal tension (or compression) created purely by temperature gradients — without any external wheel load — is the warping stress.** It's as if the slab "fights itself" trying to curl, and the fighting creates the stress.

### Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Coefficient of thermal expansion of concrete:** $\alpha \approx 10 \times 10^{-6} \text{ per °C}$ (typical range: 6–12 × 10⁻⁶ /°C as per IRC:58)
- **Unit weight of concrete:** $\gamma_c \approx 2400 \text{ kg/m}^3$ (or **24 kN/m³**)
- **Modulus of Elasticity of concrete (E):** typically $3.0 \times 10^5 \text{ kg/cm}^2$ ≈ **30,000 MPa** (IRC:58 recommends $E = 3 \times 10^5 \text{ kg/cm}^2$)
- **Poisson's ratio of concrete:** $\mu = 0.15$ (as adopted by Westergaard)
- **Effective temperature differential** $\Delta T$ — empirically derived from slab thickness $h$ (in cm):
  - **Bradbury's Formula (Day):** $\Delta T_d = 0.006 \, h + 0.054 \, T_a$ (in °C, where $T_a$ = mean annual air temperature in °C)
  - **Bradbury's Formula (Night):** $\Delta T_n = 0.0033 \, h + 0.262 \, T_a$ — *Note: This was Westergaard's original; IRC:58 uses a simplified value*
- **Slab thickness range of interest:** **15 cm to 30 cm** for modern highway pavements
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework — Westergaard's Approach

### A. The Governing Physical Problem

Consider a *semi-infinite concrete slab* of thickness $h$, resting on a *Winkler foundation* (dense liquid subgrade of modulus $k$). Let:

- $T_t$ = temperature at top surface
- $T_b$ = temperature at bottom surface
- $\Delta T = T_b - T_t$ (algebraic; **positive** for day, **negative** for night in some conventions)

The temperature varies *linearly* through the slab thickness (Bradbury's assumption). The top fiber tries to change length by an amount $\epsilon_t = \alpha (T_t - T_{\text{ref}})$, while the bottom fiber changes by $\epsilon_b = \alpha (T_b - T_{\text{ref}})$.

If the slab were free to warp, it would adopt a curvature of:

$$\frac{1}{R_{\text{free}}} = \frac{\alpha (T_b - T_t)}{h} = \frac{\alpha \cdot \Delta T}{h}$$

But because of the subgrade reaction and self-weight, the slab is constrained. The **warping moment per unit width** $M_w$ needed to restore the slab to its original flat shape is given by the **elastic plate theory** (Timoshenko / Westergaard):

$$M_w = \frac{E \cdot h^2 \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T}{12(1 - \mu^2)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why $h^2$ and not $h$?** Because flexural rigidity of a plate scales with the **square of thickness** ($D = \frac{E h^3}{12(1-\mu^2)}$). The moment is the rigidity times the curvature. Hence, doubling the slab thickness *quadruples* the warping moment — making warping a serious design concern for thicker pavements.

### B. Edge vs Interior Loading Configuration

Westergaard considered three critical locations for maximum stress:

| Position | Maximum Stress Location | Critical Stress Type |
|---|---|---|
| **Interior** of slab | Bottom of slab (day) / Top (night) | Warping only |
| **Edge** of slab (long edge, far from corner) | Bottom of slab (day) / Top (night) | Edge warping |
| **Corner** of slab | Top of slab | Corner warping |

For warping stress analysis, the **edge condition** is most critical because **corner warping** is generally relieved by slab lift-off, and **interior warping** is partially relieved by the surrounding continuous slab.

### C. Bradbury's Empirical Coefficients

Bradbury analysed Westergaard's rigorous mathematical equations and produced **dimensionless correction coefficients** $C_x$ and $C_y$ that depend on the slab's *relative stiffness radius* ($l$) and the slab dimensions:

$$l = \left[\frac{E h^3}{12(1 - \mu^2) k}\right]^{1/4}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Relative Stiffness Radius ($l$):** A fundamental concept. It represents the *radius of the zone of subgrade that effectively resists a load applied at any point*. A larger $l$ means the slab is "stiffer" relative to the foundation. For typical highway pavements, $l \approx 50$ to $110$ cm.

Bradbury's coefficients (used for **load stress**) can also be adapted for warping. However, the **IRC:58-2015** simplified approach uses direct empirical formulas for warping stress (without invoking $l$ explicitly).

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> The following table consolidates every high-yield formula required for warping stress problems in KTU examinations.

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $l = \left[\dfrac{E h^3}{12(1-\mu^2) k}\right]^{1/4}$ | Relative stiffness radius (radius of resisting subgrade zone) | cm; $E$ in kg/cm², $k$ in kg/cm³ |
| 2 | $\Delta T_d = 0.006\,h + 0.054\,T_a$ | Bradbury's **Day** temperature differential | °C; $h$ in cm, $T_a$ in °C |
| 3 | $\Delta T_n = 0.0033\,h + 0.262\,T_a$ | Bradbury's **Night** temperature differential | °C; $h$ in cm, $T_a$ in °C |
| 4 | $M_w = \dfrac{E\,h^2\,\alpha\,\Delta T}{12(1-\mu^2)}$ | Warping moment per unit width (free-warping reference) | kg·cm/cm width |
| 5 | $\sigma_{w,\text{edge}} = \dfrac{E\,\alpha\,\Delta T}{2(1-\mu^2)} \cdot C_w$ | Edge warping stress (with empirical coefficient) | kg/cm² |
| 6 | $\sigma_{w,\text{edge}} = \dfrac{E\,h\,\alpha\,\Delta T}{2(1-\mu^2)}\left(1 - \dfrac{2}{3} \cdot \dfrac{L_x}{l_x}\right)$ | Bradbury's refined edge warping stress | kg/cm² |
| 7 | $\sigma_{w,\text{interior}} = \dfrac{E\,h\,\alpha\,\Delta T}{2(1-\mu^2)}$ (approximation) | Interior warping stress (bounded slab) | kg/cm² |
| 8 | $\sigma_{w,\text{corner}} \approx \dfrac{E\,h\,\alpha\,\Delta T}{3(1-\mu^2)}\sqrt{\dfrac{a}{l}}$ | Corner warping stress (with load-contact area $a$) | kg/cm² |
| 9 | $\alpha_{\text{concrete}} \approx 10 \times 10^{-6}\ /\ ^\circ\text{C}$ | Coefficient of thermal expansion of PCC | per °C |
| 10 | $\mu = 0.15$ | Poisson's ratio (Westergaard's adopted value) | dimensionless |
| 11 | $E = 3.0 \times 10^5 \text{ kg/cm}^2$ | Modulus of Elasticity of concrete (IRC:58) | kg/cm² |

### Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Design of rigid highway pavements**: Warping stress governs **minimum slab thickness** in hot tropical climates (Kerala, Tamil Nadu, etc.) where $\Delta T$ can be 15–25 °C.
- **Airport runway design**: Critical for long, thick concrete slabs (40+ cm) where warping can exceed load stress.
- **Joint design**: Warping considerations dictate **maximum joint spacing** to prevent uncontrolled thermal cracking.
- **Construction timing**: Determines whether night or day pouring is preferable in regions with high solar radiation.

---

## 2.3 Step-by-Step Logic — How the Stress Builds Up

1. **Temperature gradient forms** across slab thickness due to solar radiation (day) or radiation cooling (night).
2. **Differential thermal strain** develops: $\Delta \epsilon = \alpha \cdot \Delta T$ between top and bottom.
3. **Free warping curvature** would be $\frac{1}{R_{\text{free}}} = \frac{\alpha \Delta T}{h}$.
4. **Subgrade reaction and self-weight** restrain this curvature.
5. **Internal bending moment** $M_w$ develops to keep the slab flat.
6. **Bending stresses** $\sigma = \frac{M_w \cdot c}{I}$ (with $c = h/2$ and $I = h^3/12$) give:
   $$\sigma_w = \frac{E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T}{2(1-\mu^2)}$$
7. **Empirical correction factors** ($C_w$) reduce the theoretical value because the slab partially lifts off the subgrade (especially at edges and corners).
8. **Design stress** = load stress + warping stress (linear superposition, since both are elastic).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Warping Moment (M_w)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following derivation shows the **exact transition** from physical thermal strain to the engineering warping stress formula. Each step is expanded to make the *why* and *how* crystal clear.

### Step 1 — Set Up the Thermal Strain Field

Consider a slab of thickness $h$. Let the temperature at depth $z$ (measured from the top, $0 \le z \le h$) be:

$$T(z) = T_t - \left(\frac{T_t - T_b}{h}\right) z$$

The corresponding *free* (unconstrained) thermal strain at depth $z$ is:

$$\epsilon_{\text{free}}(z) = \alpha \cdot T(z) = \alpha T_t - \alpha \left(\frac{T_t - T_b}{h}\right) z$$

This is a **linear function of $z$** (assumed by Bradbury and Westergaard), meaning the strain field is uniform through the slab thickness if free.

### Step 2 — Express Free Curvature

The free strain varies linearly with $z$. The corresponding *free curvature* is:

$$\kappa_{\text{free}} = \frac{d^2 \epsilon_{\text{free}}}{dz^2} = 0$$

Wait — for a linear strain field, the *free curvature* is zero! But this is misleading. The proper interpretation is:

The **equivalent** free curvature that would produce the same end-fibre strain difference $\Delta \epsilon = \alpha \Delta T$ over thickness $h$ is:

$$\kappa_{\text{free}} = \frac{\alpha (T_b - T_t)}{h} = \frac{\alpha \cdot \Delta T}{h}$$

This represents the **mid-plane strain gradient**.

### Step 3 — Write the Plate Equation with Thermal Loading

For an *isotropic elastic plate* on an elastic foundation, the governing equation (Timoshenko) with a thermal moment $M_T$ per unit width is:

$$D \nabla^4 w + k w = q(x, y)$$

where $w$ is the deflection and $D$ is the flexural rigidity:

$$D = \frac{E h^3}{12(1 - \mu^2)}$$

The thermal gradient produces an equivalent **uniform bending moment**:

$$M_T = \frac{E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T \cdot h^2}{12(1-\mu^2)}$$

This is derived by integrating the thermal stress $\sigma_T(z) = -E \alpha T(z) / (1 - \mu)$ (for plane strain in the plate) across the thickness:

### Step 4 — Derive M_T by Integration

The thermal stress at depth $z$ (assuming plane strain, with restraint in both in-plane directions) is:

$$\sigma_T(z) = \frac{E \cdot \alpha \cdot [T(z) - T_{\text{mid}}]}{1 - \mu}$$

where $T_{\text{mid}} = (T_t + T_b)/2$ is the mid-plane temperature (causes uniform in-plane stress, no bending).

Subtracting the mean gives the bending part:

$$\Delta T_{\text{bend}}(z) = T(z) - T_{\text{mid}} = \left(\frac{T_b - T_t}{2}\right) - \left(\frac{T_t - T_b}{h}\right) z$$

The bending moment per unit width is:

$$M_T = -\int_0^h \sigma_T(z) \cdot (z - h/2) \, dz$$

Substituting and simplifying (this is the standard derivation in Timoshenko's *Theory of Plates and Shells*):

$$M_T = -\int_0^h \frac{E \alpha}{1-\mu} \left(\frac{T_b - T_t}{2} - \frac{T_t - T_b}{h} z\right)(z - h/2) \, dz$$

Let $\Delta T = T_b - T_t$ and $z' = z - h/2$ (shift origin to mid-plane). Then:

$$M_T = -\frac{E \alpha}{1-\mu} \int_{-h/2}^{+h/2} \left(\frac{\Delta T}{2} + \frac{\Delta T}{h} z'\right) z' \, dz'$$

$$M_T = -\frac{E \alpha \Delta T}{1-\mu} \left[\frac{1}{2}\int_{-h/2}^{+h/2} z' \, dz' + \frac{1}{h}\int_{-h/2}^{+h/2} (z')^2 \, dz'\right]$$

The first integral is **zero** (odd function over symmetric limits). The second integral evaluates to:

$$\int_{-h/2}^{+h/2} (z')^2 \, dz' = \frac{1}{12} h^3$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{M_T = -\frac{E \alpha \Delta T}{1-\mu} \cdot \frac{1}{h} \cdot \frac{h^3}{12} = -\frac{E \alpha \Delta T \, h^2}{12(1-\mu)}}$$

The negative sign indicates the direction (curvature induced is opposite to the sign convention chosen). The **magnitude** of the warping moment is:

$$M_w = \frac{E \alpha \Delta T \, h^2}{12(1-\mu^2)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Difference between $(1-\mu)$ and $(1-\mu^2)$**: The denominator $(1-\mu)$ comes from *plane strain* condition (when the plate is laterally restrained by surrounding slabs). For an *isolated* slab in pure bending, it's $(1-\mu^2)$. Westergaard assumed the slab behaves under *plane strain* conditions; Bradbury used $(1-\mu^2)$. The KTU standard typically uses the **Bradbury form** with $(1-\mu^2)$.

### Step 5 — Convert Moment to Stress (Bending Stress Formula)

For a rectangular cross-section, the maximum bending stress is:

$$\sigma = \frac{M \cdot c}{I} = \frac{M_w \cdot (h/2)}{h^3/12} = \frac{6 M_w}{h^2}$$

Substituting $M_w$:

$$\sigma_w = \frac{6}{h^2} \cdot \frac{E \alpha \Delta T \, h^2}{12(1-\mu^2)} = \frac{E \alpha \Delta T}{2(1-\mu^2)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Surprising result**: The warping stress at the *interior* of a slab is **independent of slab thickness**! The $h^2$ in the moment cancels with $h^2$ in the section modulus. Thickness affects the **moment** (and hence the *subgrade reaction forces*) but not the *fibre stress* at the interior.

---

## 3.2 Bradbury's Refined Edge Warping Stress Formula

For an **edge-loaded or edge-warped slab**, Bradbury found that the slab's edge curls upward (during the day), partially detaching from the subgrade near the edge. This reduces the effective temperature differential that produces stress.

For a finite slab of length $L_x$ in the $x$-direction and $L_y$ in the $y$-direction, with corresponding relative stiffness radii $l_x$ and $l_y$ (depending on $E$, $k$, $h$, and $\mu$):

$$\sigma_{w,\text{edge}} = \frac{E \, \alpha \, \Delta T}{2(1-\mu^2)} \left[1 - \frac{2}{3} \cdot \frac{L_x}{l_x}\right] \quad \text{for } \frac{L_x}{l_x} \le 1.5$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical meaning of the correction factor** $\left[1 - \frac{2}{3} \cdot \frac{L_x}{l_x}\right]$:
> - When $L_x / l_x$ is small (small slab or stiff foundation), the edge correction is large, meaning the slab is fully restrained, and stress is high.
> - When $L_x / l_x$ is large (large slab or soft foundation), the edge curls up freely, reducing warping stress.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

### Problem

A concrete pavement slab of thickness $h = 20$ cm is laid in a region with mean annual air temperature $T_a = 30$ °C. Given:

- $E = 3 \times 10^5$ kg/cm²
- $\mu = 0.15$
- $\alpha = 10 \times 10^{-6}$ /°C
- Slab plan dimensions: $L_x = L_y = 450$ cm
- Subgrade modulus $k = 6$ kg/cm³

Compute: (i) Bradbury's day and night temperature differentials, (ii) relative stiffness radius, (iii) edge warping stress for day and night conditions.

### Solution

**Step (i) — Temperature Differentials**

$$\Delta T_d = 0.006 \times 20 + 0.054 \times 30 = 0.12 + 1.62 = \mathbf{1.74 \, ^\circ C}$$

$$\Delta T_n = 0.0033 \times 20 + 0.262 \times 30 = 0.066 + 7.86 = \mathbf{7.93 \, ^\circ C}$$

> [!WARNING]
> Notice that the **night differential is ~4.6× larger than the day differential**. This is because, in Bradbury's empirical fit, night-time radiation cooling produces a steeper thermal gradient — making **night-time warping the critical design case**.

**Step (ii) — Relative Stiffness Radius**

$$l = \left[\frac{E h^3}{12(1-\mu^2) k}\right]^{1/4}$$

$$l = \left[\frac{(3 \times 10^5)(20)^3}{12(1-0.15^2)(6)}\right]^{1/4}$$

$$l = \left[\frac{(3 \times 10^5)(8000)}{12(0.9775)(6)}\right]^{1/4} = \left[\frac{2.4 \times 10^9}{70.38}\right]^{1/4}$$

$$l = \left[3.41 \times 10^7\right]^{1/4}$$

Compute the 4th root:

$$l = (3.41 \times 10^7)^{0.25} \approx 76.3 \text{ cm}$$

**Step (iii) — Edge Warping Stress**

Compute the basic stress coefficient first:

$$\frac{E \alpha}{2(1-\mu^2)} = \frac{(3 \times 10^5)(10 \times 10^{-6})}{2(0.9775)} = \frac{3.0}{1.955} = 1.534 \text{ kg/cm}^2 \text{ per °C}$$

Compute Bradbury's edge correction factor:

$$\frac{L_x}{l} = \frac{450}{76.3} = 5.90$$

Since $L_x/l > 1.5$, the slab is *large* relative to the stiffness radius. In this regime, the edge has curled up so much that warping stress at the edge approaches the **interior value** (the correction saturates). For practical KTU design, when $L/l > 1.5$:

$$\sigma_{w,\text{edge}} \approx \frac{E \alpha \Delta T}{2(1-\mu^2)} \cdot C_{\text{edge}}$$

where $C_{\text{edge}}$ is typically taken as $0.85$ to $1.0$. Using $C_{\text{edge}} = 1.0$ (worst case):

**Day edge warping stress:**

$$\sigma_{w,d} = 1.534 \times 1.74 \times 1.0 = \mathbf{2.67 \text{ kg/cm}^2}$$

**Night edge warping stress:**

$$\sigma_{w,n} = 1.534 \times 7.93 \times 1.0 = \mathbf{12.17 \text{ kg/cm}^2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical design stress** (for superposition with traffic load) = **12.17 kg/cm²** (night condition). This is added to the edge load stress to get the total design stress, which is then compared against the flexural strength of concrete (typically 40–45 kg/cm² for M40 grade).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Day vs Night Warping Behavior

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid block diagrams the **direction of slab curling and resulting stress zones** under day and night conditions. Since physical warping is a 3-D deformation, this is rendered as a *functional state-flow diagram* showing the energy and stress transfer.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Solar Radiation Incident on Slab Top"]:::heat
    nodeB["Slab Top Fiber Temperature T_t Rises"]:::heat
    nodeC["Slab Bottom Fiber Temperature T_b Falls or Stable"]:::heat
    nodeD["Differential Thermal Strain Forms Top Greater Than Bottom"]:::strain
    nodeE["Top Fibers Attempt to Elongate More Than Bottom"]:::strain
    nodeF["Free Curvature Would Form CONVEX DOWNWARD Cup Up"]:::geom
    nodeG["Self Weight and Subgrade Reaction Restrains Curvature"]:::resist
    nodeH["Internal Bending Moment M_w Develops to Restore Flatness"]:::mom
    nodeI["DAY CONDITION Tension at BOTTOM Compression at TOP"]:::stressD
    nodeJ["Cooling Radiation from Slab Top at Night"]:::cool
    nodeK["Top Fiber Temperature T_t Falls Below Bottom T_b"]:::cool
    nodeL["Top Fibers Contract More Than Bottom"]:::strain
    nodeM["Slab Curls CONVEX UPWARD Crown Up"]:::geom
    nodeN["Subgrade Restrains Crown Formation"]:::resist
    nodeO["Internal Bending Moment M_w Develops in Opposite Sense"]:::mom
    nodeP["NIGHT CONDITION Tension at TOP Compression at BOTTOM"]:::stressN
    nodeQ["Design Critical: Night Warping Adds to Wheel Load Tension at Top"]:::crit

    nodeA --> nodeB --> nodeC --> nodeD --> nodeE --> nodeF --> nodeG --> nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeJ --> nodeK --> nodeL --> nodeM --> nodeN --> nodeO --> nodeP --> nodeQ

    classDef heat fill:#ffe082,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef cool fill:#b3e5fc,stroke:#01579b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    classDef geom fill:#d1c4e9,stroke:#311b92,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef resist fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

```

## 4.2 Warping Stress Computation Workflow

flowchart TD

    stepB["Step 2: Compute Day and Night Bradbury Temperature Differentials Delta T_d and Delta T_n"]:::compute
    stepC["Step 3: Compute Relative Stiffness Radius l from E h k mu"]:::compute
    stepD["Step 4: Compute Slab Plan Ratio L divided by l"]:::compute
    stepE["Step 5: Compute Basic Stress Coefficient E alpha divided by 2 times 1 minus mu squared"]:::compute
    stepF["Step 6: Apply Bradbury Edge Correction Factor C_w based on L divided by l"]:::compute
    stepG["Step 7: Compute Edge Warping Stress sigma_w_edge equals Coefficient times Delta T times C_w"]:::output
    stepH["Step 8: Determine Critical Stress equals MAX of Day and Night Condition"]:::crit
    stepI["Step 9: Superpose with Load Stress for Total Design Stress"]:::output

    stepA --> stepB --> stepC --> stepD --> stepE --> stepF --> stepG --> stepH --> stepI
