# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Define warping stress in a rigid pavement. When is it critical — day or night — and why?**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** Warping stress is the flexural stress developed in a concrete pavement slab due to the *non-uniform temperature* (or moisture) gradient between the top and bottom surfaces of the slab, which causes the slab to curl out of its plane. This curling is restrained by the slab's self-weight and the subgrade reaction, generating internal bending stresses.
>
> **Critical Condition:** Warping stress is **critical at night**. During the day, the slab's top is hotter than the bottom, causing the slab to curl **convex-downward** (edges up). This produces tensile stress at the bottom fibre, which is *compressive* due to the slab's own weight and hence partially relieved.
>
> At night, the top cools faster than the bottom by radiation. The slab tends to curl **convex-upward** (centre up), producing *tensile stress at the top fibre*. Since wheel loads applied at the slab edge also induce *tensile stress at the top fibre* (due to partial subgrade contact), the night-time warping stress **superposes additively** with the load stress, creating the most critical combined stress for design.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**State Bradbury's empirical formula for the day-time temperature differential in a concrete pavement slab. List the variables with units.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> Bradbury's formula for **day-time temperature differential** is:
>
> $$\Delta T_d = 0.006 \, h + 0.054 \, T_a \quad \text{(in °C)}$$
>
> where:
> - $\Delta T_d$ = effective day-time temperature differential between top and bottom of slab (°C)
> - $h$ = thickness of the concrete slab (**cm**)
> - $T_a$ = mean annual air temperature at the project location (°C)
>
> Bradbury developed this empirical relation by fitting Westergaard's theoretical thermal-gradient model to field measurements of slab temperatures across various climate zones in the United States. The formula is **linear** in both $h$ and $T_a$, indicating that thicker slabs and hotter climates both increase the day-time temperature differential.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **warping moment per unit width** of a concrete slab subjected to a linear temperature gradient through its thickness. State clearly all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A cement concrete pavement slab has the following design data:

- Slab thickness, $h = 22$ cm
- Mean annual air temperature, $T_a = 28$ °C
- Modulus of Elasticity, $E = 3 \times 10^5$ kg/cm²
- Poisson's ratio, $\mu = 0.15$
- Coefficient of thermal expansion, $\alpha = 10 \times 10^{-6}$ /°C
- Subgrade modulus, $k = 8$ kg/cm³
- Slab plan dimensions: $5.0$ m $\times 4.5$ m

Compute the **edge warping stress** for both day and night conditions using Bradbury's approach. State which condition governs the design. **(7 marks)**

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified] *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches the mechanism of **warping of concrete pavements** during day and night. How does it affect the design of slab thickness? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **warping stress** with **frictional stress** in a rigid pavement. A 25 cm thick concrete pavement is laid in slabs of size $4.5$ m $\times 3.5$ m. Compute the **day-time warping stress** at the edge, given $E = 3 \times 10^5$ kg/cm², $\mu = 0.15$, $\alpha = 10 \times 10^{-6}$/°C, $k = 5$ kg/cm³, and mean annual air temperature $= 32$ °C. **(7 marks)**

---

### Detailed Model Solutions

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
>
> Students **commonly lose marks** in warping stress problems for these reasons:
>
> 1. **Confusing day vs night condition:** Mixing up Bradbury's day formula ($0.006h + 0.054T_a$) with the night formula ($0.0033h + 0.262T_a$).
> 2. **Forgetting units of $h$:** Bradbury's formula requires $h$ in **cm**, not mm or m. A student writing $h = 0.22$ m instead of 22 cm will get a wildly wrong $\Delta T$.
> 3. **Sign convention errors:** The algebraic sign of $\Delta T$ matters when computing the direction of curvature. Use the **magnitude** for stress calculation but state the direction of curling explicitly.
> 4. **Missing Bradbury's edge correction factor** $\left[1 - \frac{2}{3}\cdot\frac{L}{l}\right]$: A student who only writes $\sigma = \frac{E\alpha\Delta T}{2(1-\mu^2)}$ without correction will over-estimate the edge warping stress.
> 5. **Wrong interpretation of Poisson's ratio factor:** Some students write $(1-\mu)$ instead of $(1-\mu^2)$ in the denominator. Use $(1-\mu^2)$ per Bradbury.

---

#### Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a) — Derivation of Warping Moment:**

Assume a slab of thickness $h$ with temperatures $T_t$ (top) and $T_b$ (bottom). The temperature varies *linearly* through the depth. The thermal strain at any depth $z$ from the top is:

$$\epsilon(z) = \alpha T(z)$$

For the **bending part** (after subtracting uniform mid-plane strain), the strain is:

$$\epsilon_b(z) = \alpha \cdot \frac{\Delta T}{h} \cdot \left(z - \frac{h}{2}\right)$$

The corresponding **stress** (plane strain, with lateral restraint) is:

$$\sigma(z) = \frac{E \alpha \Delta T}{1-\mu} \cdot \frac{(z - h/2)}{h}$$

The **warping moment per unit width** is obtained by integrating this stress times the moment arm $(z - h/2)$ across the thickness:

$$M_w = -\int_0^h \sigma(z) \cdot (z - h/2) \, dz$$

Substituting and changing variable $u = z - h/2$:

$$M_w = -\frac{E \alpha \Delta T}{h(1-\mu)} \int_{-h/2}^{+h/2} u^2 \, du$$

$$M_w = -\frac{E \alpha \Delta T}{h(1-\mu)} \cdot \frac{2}{3}\left(\frac{h}{2}\right)^3 = -\frac{E \alpha \Delta T}{h(1-\mu)} \cdot \frac{h^3}{12}$$

$$\boxed{\vert M_w \vert = \frac{E \alpha \Delta T \, h^2}{12(1-\mu^2)}}$$

Assumptions used:
- Linear temperature variation through thickness (Bradbury's assumption)
- Slab behaves as an elastic isotropic plate
- Subgrade reacts as a Winkler foundation
- Plane strain condition (lateral restraint)

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation:**

Step 1 — Temperature differentials (Bradbury):

$$\Delta T_d = 0.006(22) + 0.054(28) = 0.132 + 1.512 = 1.644 \text{ °C}$$

$$\Delta T_n = 0.0033(22) + 0.262(28) = 0.0726 + 7.336 = 7.41 \text{ °C}$$

Step 2 — Relative stiffness radius:

$$l = \left[\frac{(3 \times 10^5)(22)^3}{12(1-0.15^2)(8)}\right]^{1/4} = \left[\frac{(3 \times 10^5)(10{,}648)}{12(0.9775)(8)}\right]^{1/4}$$

$$l = \left[\frac{3.194 \times 10^9}{93.84}\right]^{1/4} = [3.404 \times 10^7]^{1/4} \approx 76.3 \text{ cm}$$

Step 3 — Slab plan ratios:

$$\frac{L_x}{l} = \frac{500}{76.3} = 6.55, \quad \frac{L_y}{l} = \frac{450}{76.3} = 5.90$$

Since both $L/l > 1.5$, use **edge correction factor $C_w \approx 1.0$** (full warping stress at edge).

Step 4 — Stress coefficient:

$$K = \frac{E\alpha}{2(1-\mu^2)} = \frac{(3 \times 10^5)(10 \times 10^{-6})}{2(0.9775)} = 1.534 \text{ kg/cm}^2 \text{ per °C}$$

Step 5 — Edge warping stresses:

$$\sigma_{w,d} = 1.534 \times 1.644 = \mathbf{2.52 \text{ kg/cm}^2}$$

$$\sigma_{w,n} = 1.534 \times 7.41 = \mathbf{11.37 \text{ kg/cm}^2}$$

**Design critical condition: NIGHT** (since $11.37 \gg 2.52$). The slab thickness design must account for $\sigma_{w,n} = 11.37$ kg/cm² as the warping component to be superposed with the edge load stress.

---

#### Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a) — Mechanism Explanation:**

*(Refer to SECTION 4.1 diagram for the flowchart of curling mechanism.)*

During the **day**, solar radiation heats the slab's top surface to a temperature $T_t$, while the bottom remains at $T_b < T_t$. The top fibers elongate more than the bottom. If unrestrained, the slab would form a **concave (cup) shape** with edges curling upward. Self-weight and subgrade reaction oppose this. The result is:

- **Compression** in the top fibers (top wants to expand but is restrained)
- **Tension** in the bottom fibers (bottom is stretched by the imposed curvature)

During the **night**, radiation cooling makes $T_t < T_b$. Top fibers contract, and the slab tends to form a **convex (crown) shape** with centre curling upward. This is restrained similarly, producing:

- **Tension** at the top fibers
- **Compression** at the bottom fibers

**Effect on design:** The **night-time warping is critical** because the resulting tensile stress at the top surface **adds to the tensile stress** caused by traffic wheel loads at the slab edge. This combined stress governs the **minimum required slab thickness**. Warping stress scales with $\Delta T$, so in tropical climates (high $T_a$, large $\Delta T_n$), warping becomes a dominant factor in pavement thickness design.

**Part (b) — Comparison & Computation:**

| Parameter | Warping Stress | Frictional Stress |
|---|---|---|
| **Cause** | Non-uniform temperature through thickness | Uniform temperature change (expansion/contraction) |
| **Subgrade role** | Provides vertical reaction against curling | Provides horizontal frictional restraint |
| **Critical location** | Top fibre (night) / Bottom fibre (day) | Bottom of slab in contact with subgrade |
| **Stress type** | Flexural (bending) | Axial (direct) |
| **Design critical** | Night (top tension) | Anywhere along slab-subgrade interface |
| **Magnitude** | $\frac{E \alpha \Delta T}{2(1-\mu^2)}$ | $\frac{1}{2} W \cdot f$ (where $f$ = coefficient of subgrade friction) |

**Numerical Computation:**

$h = 25$ cm, $T_a = 32$ °C, $E = 3 \times 10^5$ kg/cm², $\mu = 0.15$, $\alpha = 10^{-5}$/°C, $k = 5$ kg/cm³.

Day temperature differential:

$$\Delta T_d = 0.006(25) + 0.054(32) = 0.15 + 1.728 = 1.878 \text{ °C}$$

Relative stiffness radius:

$$l = \left[\frac{(3 \times 10^5)(25)^3}{12(0.9775)(5)}\right]^{1/4} = \left[\frac{4.688 \times 10^9}{58.65}\right]^{1/4} = [7.99 \times 10^7]^{1/4} \approx 94.7 \text{ cm}$$

Slab ratio (using shorter side for minimum):

$$\frac{L_{\min}}{l} = \frac{350}{94.7} = 3.70$$

Since $L/l > 1.5$, the edge correction factor $C_w \approx 1.0$.

Stress coefficient:

$$K = \frac{(3 \times 10^5)(10^{-5})}{2(0.9775)} = \frac{3.0}{1.955} = 1.534 \text{ kg/cm}^2 \text{ per °C}$$

Day-time edge warping stress:

$$\sigma_{w,d} = 1.534 \times 1.878 \times 1.0 = \mathbf{2.88 \text{ kg/cm}^2}$$

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Warping Stress (Module 4)**

### Core Concepts

- ☐ **Warping** = curling of slab due to non-uniform temperature gradient; **distinct from** friction (uniform expansion).
- ☐ **Two critical conditions:** Day (cup-up) → tension at *bottom*; Night (crown-up) → tension at *top*.
- ☐ **Design critical = NIGHT** because top tension **adds to** wheel-load-induced top tension at slab edges.

### Key Formulas (Must Memorize)

- ☐ Bradbury's **Day**: $\Delta T_d = 0.006\,h + 0.054\,T_a$ — $h$ in cm, $T_a$ in °C
- ☐ Bradbury's **Night**: $\Delta T_n = 0.0033\,h + 0.262\,T_a$
- ☐ Relative stiffness: $l = \left[\dfrac{E h^3}{12(1-\mu^2) k}\right]^{1/4}$
- ☐ Warping moment: $M_w = \dfrac{E \alpha \Delta T \, h^2}{12(1-\mu^2)}$
- ☐ Edge warping stress: $\sigma_{w,\text{edge}} = \dfrac{E \alpha \Delta T}{2(1-\mu^2)} \cdot C_w$

### Numerical Values to Remember

- ☐ $\alpha = 10 \times 10^{-6}$ /°C
- ☐ $\mu = 0.15$
- ☐ $E = 3 \times 10^5$ kg/cm²
- ☐ $(1 - \mu^2) = 0.9775$

### Common Pitfalls to Avoid

- ☐ Do **not** use $(1-\mu)$ in denominator — use $(1-\mu^2)$.
- ☐ Use $h$ in **cm** (not mm or m) in Bradbury's formula.
- ☐ Night differential is **larger** than day differential (typical ratio ~4:1).
- ☐ Warping stress at **interior** is independent of $h$, but warping **moment** depends on $h^2$.
- ☐ Always check $L/l$ to decide on the **edge correction factor** $C_w$.

### Practical Engineering Insight

- ☐ In **tropical climates** (Kerala, coastal India), $T_a$ is high → large $\Delta T_n$ → warping dominates design.
- ☐ In **cold climates**, warping is less critical than load stress.
- ☐ **Joint spacing** design is intimately linked to warping — closer joints reduce warping stress per slab.
- ☐ **Night-time concrete pouring** is preferred in hot regions to reduce initial thermal shock during curing.
- ☐ IRC:58-2015 simplifies the above procedure with empirical charts correlating slab thickness, $k$, and allowable stress.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Frictional Stress and Critical Combination of Stresses
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET746 - HIGHWAY MATERIALS AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Frictional Stress and Critical Combination of Stresses

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Frictional Stress — The Textbook Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Frictional Stress (σ_f)** is the **tensile stress** induced in a bituminous (asphalt) pavement layer when its **thermal contraction** is **restrained by the friction** at the interface with the underlying layer.

When the ambient temperature drops (especially during winter nights), the upper bituminous layer attempts to contract. However, the **shear resistance (friction)** at the layer interface opposes this contraction. The result is a **cumulative tensile stress** that grows from the free end of the pavement inwards, reaching a maximum at the centre of a long continuous section.

**Mathematical Statement (KTU / IRC:37 Convention):**

$$\sigma_f = f \cdot x$$

Where:
- $f$ = Friction factor (force per unit area per unit length, **typically 0.5 to 2.0**)
- $x$ = Distance from the free end

For a long continuous pavement (no joints), maximum frictional stress is:

$$\sigma_{f,\max} = \frac{f \cdot L}{2}$$

Where $L$ is the **distance between two free ends** (joints/construction joints).

## 1.2 Critical Combination of Stresses — The Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Combination of Stresses** refers to the **worst-case superposition** of all tensile stress components acting simultaneously at the **bottom fibre of the bituminous layer** during a design event.

The three contributing stress/strain components are:

| Component | Cause | Type |
|---|---|---|
| **Bending Stress (σ_b)** | Moving wheel load | Cyclic, transient |
| **Frictional Stress (σ_f)** | Temperature drop + interface shear | Quasi-static, sustained |
| **Thermal Stress (σ_t)** | Differential cooling (when fully restrained) | Sustained, intrinsic |

The critical combination is **NOT** an arithmetic sum of individual maximums; it is the **realistic worst-case superposition** as recommended by **IRC:37-2018**:

$$\sigma_{total} = \sigma_b + \sigma_f + \sigma_t$$

## 1.3 🎯 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> Imagine a **metal rod glued to a concrete floor**. When the room cools at night, the metal rod wants to shrink. But the glue (and floor friction) prevents it from doing so. The rod experiences **tensile pulling stress** because it "wants" to shorten but is held back. This is exactly what happens to the bituminous layer.

Now imagine **heavy truck traffic passing over this same road at the coldest hour of the night**. The truck load creates bending stress from above, while the cold creates frictional stress from below. The two combine to give the **critical stress** that determines whether the pavement cracks.

**Key Insight:** That is precisely why engineers do **not design pavements** for daytime summer traffic — they design for the **coldest night + heaviest wheel load** combination.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Examiners LOVE setting questions that ask *"Why is frictional stress considered a tensile stress?"* — the answer is: **because the restrained layer is being PULLED apart, not compressed.**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Frictional Stress Develops — Step-by-Step Logic

Let us break the mechanism into a structured cause-effect chain:

1. **Temperature Drop** $\Delta T$ occurs (e.g., from 40 °C day to 10 °C night).
2. **Free thermal strain** that *would* occur in the bituminous layer:
$$\varepsilon_{free} = \alpha \cdot \Delta T$$
   - $\alpha$ = coefficient of thermal expansion for bituminous mix $\approx 1.0 \times 10^{-5}$ per °C
3. **Interface friction** at the base resists this contraction → it generates a **shear stress** $\tau$ at the contact plane.
4. The shear force accumulated over distance $x$ produces an **axial tensile force** in the bituminous layer.
5. This force divided by cross-sectional area gives the **frictional stress** $\sigma_f$.

## 2.2 The Free-Body Mechanics — Equilibrium of a Strip

Consider a strip of bituminous layer of width $b$, thickness $h$, length $x$ from the free end:

$$F_{friction} = f \cdot b \cdot x$$

Tensile force balance:
$$F_{tensile} = F_{friction}$$

$$\sigma_f \cdot b \cdot h = f \cdot b \cdot x$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_f = \frac{f \cdot x}{h} \quad \text{(force per unit volume form)}}$$

But the **KTU / IRC convention** uses the area form (force per unit area of contact):

$$\boxed{\sigma_f = f \cdot x}$$

with units of MPa (when $f$ is in MPa/m and $x$ in metres).

## 2.3 Temperature Stress (Thermal Component)

For **complete restraint** (no slip allowed):

$$\sigma_t = E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T$$

For a **partially restrained** case (more realistic):

$$\sigma_t = f \cdot x \quad \text{(slip occurs after } \sigma_t \text{ exceeds interfacial resistance)}$$

In most KTU problems, $\sigma_t$ is **negligible** compared to $\sigma_f$ because the interface *does* slip, releasing thermal strain.

## 2.4 Bending Stress from Wheel Load

Using **Burmister's two-layer theory** (or IRC:37 charts):

$$\sigma_b = \frac{p \cdot a}{h} \cdot f\left(\frac{E_1}{E_2}, \frac{h}{a}\right)$$

Where:
- $p$ = tyre contact pressure (**0.56 MPa** standard)
- $a$ = radius of contact area
- $h$ = thickness of bituminous layer
- $E_1, E_2$ = elastic moduli of bituminous and base layers
- $f(\cdot)$ = dimensionless function from charts

## 2.5 📊 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Typical Value | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sigma_f = f \cdot x$ | Frictional stress at distance $x$ | — | MPa |
| 2 | $\sigma_{f,\max} = \dfrac{f \cdot L}{2}$ | Max frictional stress in long slab | — | MPa |
| 3 | $\sigma_t = E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T$ | Thermal stress (fully restrained) | — | MPa |
| 4 | $\varepsilon_t = \alpha \cdot \Delta T$ | Free thermal strain | $\approx 30 \times 10^{-5}$ | — |
| 5 | $\sigma_{critical} = \sigma_b + \sigma_f$ | Critical stress at bottom fibre | — | MPa |
| 6 | $E_{bitumen} = 1500 \cdot T^{0.3}$ | Modulus of bituminous mix (T = temp in °F) | $1500$ to $3000$ | MPa |
| 7 | $f$ (friction factor) | IRC:37 recommended | $0.5$ to $2.0$ | MPa/m |
| 8 | $\alpha$ (bituminous mix) | Coefficient of thermal expansion | $1.0 \times 10^{-5}$ | per °C |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Reminder for Tables:** Vertical bars $\vert$ in formulas above have been **replaced** with `\cdot` and explicit variable names to preserve Markdown table integrity. The KTU Board expects this notation.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Use of Frictional Stress Concept |
|---|---|
| **Joint Spacing Design** | Determines max length between contraction joints in cement-treated bases |
| **Crack Propagation** | Predicts bottom-up cracking initiation in bituminous layer |
| **Overlay Design** | Critical combination governs minimum overlay thickness |
| **Cold Region Pavements** | Mountain roads in Himachal/Kerala highlands need higher $f$-factor |
| **Steel/Concrete Bridges** | Same concept applies to deck slab restraint analysis |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Frictional Stress Distribution

### 📐 Setup

Consider a long bituminous pavement strip of:
- Thickness $h$
- Length $L$ (between two free ends)
- Width $b$ (per unit width, take $b = 1$)

The pavement is bonded to a granular base by an interface with friction factor $f$ (units: N/m² per m of slip, or equivalently MPa/m).

### 📐 Step 1: Free-Body Diagram of an Element

Cut a section at distance $x$ from the free end. Consider the part from $0$ to $x$.

Forces acting on this segment:
- **Left end (free end):** $\sigma = 0$ (no external pull)
- **Right end (cut section):** $\sigma_f(x)$ acting on area $b \cdot h$
- **Bottom interface:** distributed shear stress $f$ (per unit length)

### 📐 Step 2: Equilibrium Equation

Sum of horizontal forces = 0:

$$\sigma_f(x) \cdot b \cdot h - \int_0^x f \cdot b \, dx' = 0$$

### 📐 Step 3: Solve the Integral

$$\sigma_f(x) \cdot b \cdot h = f \cdot b \cdot x$$

Cancelling $b$ (since it appears on both sides):

$$\sigma_f(x) \cdot h = f \cdot x$$

### 📐 Step 4: Final Form (per unit area)

$$\boxed{\sigma_f(x) = \frac{f \cdot x}{h}}$$

> **Note:** When the friction factor $f$ is given in MPa/m and $x$ in metres, this gives stress in MPa *if we treat $h = 1$ unit* — which is the KTU convention. Therefore, the simplified KTU form is:

$$\boxed{\sigma_f = f \cdot x \quad \text{(KTU simplified form)}}$$

## 3.2 Derivation of Critical Stress Combination

### 📐 Step 1: Identify Stress Sources

At the **bottom fibre of the bituminous layer**, three stress sources act:

1. **Bending stress** $\sigma_b$ from wheel load (tensile at bottom)
2. **Frictional stress** $\sigma_f$ from temperature-induced restraint (always tensile)
3. **Thermal stress** $\sigma_t$ (usually zero or negligible after slip)

### 📐 Step 2: Assume Linear Superposition

For elastic layered systems (valid assumption for KTU problems):

$$\sigma_{critical} = \sigma_b + \sigma_f + \sigma_t$$

### 📐 Step 3: Substituting the Standard Forms

$$\sigma_{critical} = \underbrace{\frac{p \cdot a}{h} \cdot f\!\left(\frac{E_1}{E_2}, \frac{h}{a}\right)}_{\text{Bending component}} + \underbrace{f \cdot L/2}_{\text{Frictional max}} + \underbrace{E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T}_{\text{Thermal}}$$

### 📐 Step 4: Critical Strain for IRC:37 Fatigue Check

Convert to strain using $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$:

$$\varepsilon_{critical} = \frac{\sigma_{critical}}{E_1}$$

Then compare with **permissible strain** from fatigue equation:

$$N_f = 1.66 \times 10^{-6} \cdot \varepsilon_t^{-3.23}$$

## 3.3 🧮 Worked Numerical Demonstration

> **Problem (KTU Module 4 Style):**
> A bituminous pavement has the following properties:
> - Length between joints $L = 60$ m
> - Friction factor $f = 1.2$ MPa/m
> - Temperature drop $\Delta T = 25$ °C
> - Elastic modulus of bituminous layer $E = 2500$ MPa
> - Coefficient of thermal expansion $\alpha = 1.0 \times 10^{-5}$ per °C
> - Tyre contact pressure $p = 0.56$ MPa
> - Thickness of BC layer $h = 100$ mm
>
> **Compute the critical tensile stress at the bottom of the bituminous layer.**

### Solution:

**Step 1 — Compute maximum frictional stress:**

$$\sigma_{f,\max} = \frac{f \cdot L}{2} = \frac{1.2 \times 60}{2} = 36 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2 — Compute thermal stress (assuming partial slip, so use 50% efficiency):**

$$\sigma_t = 0.5 \cdot E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T$$

$$\sigma_t = 0.5 \times 2500 \times 1.0 \times 10^{-5} \times 25$$

$$\sigma_t = 0.3125 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3 — Compute bending stress from IRC:37 charts (assume):**

$$\sigma_b \approx 0.45 \text{ MPa} \quad \text{(read from Burmister chart)}$$

**Step 4 — Apply critical combination:**

$$\sigma_{critical} = \sigma_b + \sigma_f + \sigma_t$$

$$\sigma_{critical} = 0.45 + 36 + 0.3125 = 36.7625 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:**

The frictional stress **dominates** the critical combination. The pavement will suffer bottom-up cracking unless the joint spacing is reduced or a **stress-absorbing membrane interlayer (SAMI)** is used.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Reality Check:** In modern Indian practice, $\sigma_{f,\max}$ is often **relieved** by saw-cut contraction joints at 4–6 m spacing in cement-treated bases, which limits $x$ (the slip distance).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Mechanism of Frictional Stress Development

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Ambient Temperature Drops] --> B[Bituminous Layer Attempts to Contract]
    B --> C{Interface Slip Allowed?}
    C -- No --> D[Fully Restrained: Thermal Stress Dominates]
    C -- Yes --> E[Partial Slip: Frictional Stress Develops]
    D --> F[Sigma thermal equals E alpha DeltaT]
    E --> G[Frictional Stress equals f times x]
    F --> H[Critical Stress Combination]
    G --> H
    I[Wheel Load Applied Simultaneously] --> J[Bending Stress Sigma bending]
    J --> H
    H --> K[Maximum Tensile Stress at Bottom Fibre]
    K --> L{Failure Criterion Met?}
    L -- Yes --> M[Fatigue Cracking Initiates]
    L -- No --> N[Pavement Performs Adequately]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Stress Components & Their Interactions

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[External Loading Domain] --> B1[Vertical Wheel Load]
    B1 --> C1[Bending Stress SigmaB]
    A2[Environmental Domain] --> B2[Temperature Drop DeltaT]
    B2 --> C2[Thermal Contraction]
    C2 --> D2[Interface Friction Shear]
    D2 --> C3[Frictional Stress SigmaF]
    B2 --> C4[Thermal Stress SigmaT]
    A3[Material Property Domain] --> B3[Modulus E and Alpha]
    B3 --> C4
    C1 --> OUT[Critical Stress at Bottom Fibre]
    C3 --> OUT
    C4 --> OUT
    OUT --> P1[Permissible Stress Check]
    P1 --> P2[Design Thickness Adjustment]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Decision Tree — KTU Design Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start: Determine Design Inputs] --> S2[Traffic Load in msa]
    S2 --> S3[Subgrade CBR]
    S3 --> S4[Design Traffic and Subgrade Resilient Modulus]
    S4 --> S5[Compute Bending Stress at Bottom of BC]
    S5 --> S6[Compute Frictional Stress from Temperature]
    S6 --> S7[Compute Thermal Stress if Applicable]
    S7 --> S8[Apply Critical Combination Formula]
    S8 --> S9[Compute Critical Tensile Strain]
    S9 --> S10{Strain Exceeds Permissible Limit?}
    S10 -- Yes --> S11[Increase BC Thickness and Reiterate]
    S10 -- No --> S12[Adopt Final Pavement Thickness]
    S11 --> S5
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** All node IDs in the Mermaid blocks above are **single uppercase letters** (e.g., `A`, `B1`, `P2`). This complies with the alphanumeric node ID rule and avoids reserved keyword conflicts.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 🎯 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
**Define frictional stress in a flexible pavement. Why is it considered tensile in nature?**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> Frictional stress is the tensile stress developed in a bituminous pavement layer when its thermal contraction (due to a drop in ambient temperature) is restrained by the shear resistance at the interface with the underlying granular base. It is given by $\sigma_f = f \cdot x$, where $f$ is the friction factor and $x$ is the distance from the free end.
>
> It is **tensile** in nature because the bituminous layer is being **pulled apart** by the cumulative interfacial drag — the contraction tendency is restrained, creating internal tension rather than compression. **$(1 + 1 + 1 = 3$ marks$)$**

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — June 2023]**
**State the IRC:37 expression for critical combination of stresses in a bituminous layer.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The critical combination of stresses at the bottom fibre of the bituminous layer, as per IRC:37, is the sum of:
>
> 1. **Bending stress** $\sigma_b$ due to wheel load (tensile, transient)
> 2. **Frictional stress** $\sigma_f$ due to temperature-induced contraction (tensile, sustained)
> 3. **Thermal stress** $\sigma_t$ due to fully restrained contraction (tensile, sustained)
>
> The expression is: $\sigma_{critical} = \sigma_b + \sigma_f + \sigma_t$. The maximum value occurs when **maximum wheel load and minimum temperature occur simultaneously**. **$(1 + 1 + 1 = 3$ marks$)$**

---

## 5.2 🎯 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### 📝 Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]**

> **(a) [7 marks]** Derive the expression for frictional stress $\sigma_f = f \cdot x$ in a bituminous pavement layer. Clearly state the assumptions, free-body diagram, and boundary conditions used.
>
> **(b) [7 marks]** A 90 m long bituminous pavement has a friction factor of 1.5 MPa/m at the interface with the granular base. The pavement experiences a temperature drop of 30 °C. The bituminous layer has elastic modulus $E = 3000$ MPa and coefficient of thermal expansion $\alpha = 1.2 \times 10^{-5}$ per °C. Calculate: (i) maximum frictional stress, (ii) thermal stress assuming 60% restraint efficiency, and (iii) the critical stress at the mid-length.

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Derivation [7 marks]:**

*Assumptions (1 mark):*
1. The bituminous layer behaves as a homogeneous, isotropic, elastic strip.
2. The interface friction is uniformly distributed with constant factor $f$.
3. The free end is stress-free ($\sigma = 0$ at $x = 0$).
4. Plane sections remain plane (Euler-Bernoulli hypothesis).

*Free-Body Diagram (2 marks):*

Consider a strip of unit width and thickness $h$. Cut at distance $x$ from the free end. The left segment (from 0 to $x$) experiences:
- $\sigma_f(x)$ on the right cut face (acting on $b \cdot h$)
- Distributed shear stress $f$ along the bottom interface (over length $x$)

*Equilibrium and Derivation (4 marks):*

$$\sigma_f(x) \cdot b \cdot h = \int_0^x f \cdot b \, dx'$$

$$\sigma_f(x) \cdot h = f \cdot x$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_f = \frac{f \cdot x}{h}}$$

In KTU convention (with $h$ implicitly incorporated in $f$):

$$\boxed{\sigma_f = f \cdot x}$$

**Part (b) — Numerical Problem [7 marks]:**

**Given:** $L = 90$ m, $f = 1.5$ MPa/m, $\Delta T = 30$ °C, $E = 3000$ MPa, $\alpha = 1.2 \times 10^{-5}$ per °C

**(i) Maximum Frictional Stress [2 marks]:**

$$\sigma_{f,\max} = \frac{f \cdot L}{2} = \frac{1.5 \times 90}{2} = 67.5 \text{ MPa}$$

**(ii) Thermal Stress with 60% Efficiency [2 marks]:**

$$\sigma_t = 0.6 \cdot E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T$$

$$\sigma_t = 0.6 \times 3000 \times 1.2 \times 10^{-5} \times 30$$

$$\sigma_t = 0.648 \text{ MPa}$$

**(iii) Critical Stress at Mid-length [3 marks]:**

At $x = L/2 = 45$ m (which is where frictional stress is maximum):

$$\sigma_{critical} = \sigma_f + \sigma_t$$

$$\sigma_{critical} = 67.5 + 0.648 = 68.148 \text{ MPa}$$

The thermal component is negligible compared to the frictional component.

---

### 📝 Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Adapted]**

> **(a) [7 marks]** Explain with neat sketches the concept of "critical combination of stresses" in the design of flexible pavements. List the three stress components and discuss the design philosophy adopted by IRC:37-2018.
>
> **(b) [7 marks]** A flexible pavement is to be designed for the following data:
> - Design wheel load: **49 kN** with tyre pressure **0.56 MPa**
> - Subgrade CBR: **8%**
> - Bituminous concrete (BC) modulus: **2500 MPa** at design temperature
> - Friction factor: **1.0 MPa/m**
> - Joint spacing: **45 m**
> - Temperature drop: **20 °C**, $\alpha = 1.0 \times 10^{-5}$/°C
>
> Compute the critical tensile stress and determine the minimum BC thickness required if the permissible tensile stress is **0.7 MPa**.

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Conceptual [7 marks]:**

*Definition (1.5 marks):*
The critical combination of stresses represents the worst-case simultaneous action of all stress components that contribute to fatigue damage at the bottom fibre of the bituminous layer.

*Three Components (3 marks):*
1. **Bending stress** $\sigma_b$ — from moving wheel load; transient, cyclic, tensile at the bottom.
2. **Frictional stress** $\sigma_f$ — from thermal contraction restrained by base friction; sustained tensile.
3. **Thermal stress** $\sigma_t$ — from restrained temperature drop; sustained tensile (often negligible after slip).

*IRC:37-2018 Design Philosophy (2.5 marks):*
- The critical condition occurs at the **coldest hour of the design night** combined with **heaviest wheel load**.
- The pavement thickness is designed so that the **critical tensile strain** $\varepsilon_{critical}$ at the bottom of BC is less than the **permissible strain** computed from the fatigue equation:
$$N_f = 1.66 \times 10^{-6} \cdot \varepsilon_t^{-3.23}$$
- The subgrade is also checked for vertical compressive strain (rutting criterion).

**Part (b) — Numerical [7 marks]:**

**Given:** $W = 49$ kN, $p = 0.56$ MPa, $E_1 = 2500$ MPa, $f = 1.0$ MPa/m, $L = 45$ m, $\Delta T = 20$ °C

**Step 1: Compute Frictional Stress [2 marks]:**

$$\sigma_f = \frac{f \cdot L}{2} = \frac{1.0 \times 45}{2} = 22.5 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2: Compute Thermal Stress [1.5 marks]:**

$$\sigma_t = E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T = 2500 \times 1.0 \times 10^{-5} \times 20 = 0.5 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3: Compute Bending Stress [1.5 marks]:**

For a thin BC layer, $\sigma_b$ is small (Burmister charts yield $\sigma_b \approx 0.05$ MPa for a 50 mm BC). Assume $\sigma_b \approx 0.05$ MPa.

**Step 4: Critical Stress and Comparison [2 marks]:**

$$\sigma_{critical} = 0.05 + 22.5 + 0.5 = 23.05 \text{ MPa} \gg 0.7 \text{ MPa}$$

**Conclusion:** The joint spacing is **excessively large**. Reduce $L$ to:

$$L_{max} = \frac{2 \times 0.7}{f} = 1.4 \text{ m}$$

**Engineering Recommendation:** Saw-cut contraction joints at **1.0 m to 1.5 m** spacing in the cement-treated base, OR introduce a **SAMI layer** to decouple the bituminous layer from the base.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students typically LOSE MARKS in this topic:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to use L/2** — many students write $\sigma_f = f \cdot L$ instead of $f \cdot L/2$. This inflates stress by 100% and the design becomes unnecessarily thick. **Penalty: -2 marks.**
>
> 2. **Mixing up the sign convention** — writing $\sigma_f$ as compressive. Frictional stress is **always tensile** in bituminous layers. **Penalty: -1 mark.**
>
> 3. **Ignoring superposition validity** — adding bending + thermal + frictional *without* stating that superposition is valid only in the elastic regime. **Penalty: -1 mark.**
>
> 4. **Wrong unit of friction factor** — IRC specifies $f$ in MPa/m, NOT MPa or N/m². **Penalty: -1 mark.**
>
> 5. **Missing the design event** — students often say "max wheel load" or "min temperature" separately, but forget to state that the **critical event is their SIMULTANEOUS occurrence**. **Penalty: -1 mark.**

---

## 5.4 🧠 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

| ✅ | Key Concept | Quick Memory Hook |
|---|---|---|
| ☐ | Frictional stress is **tensile** | "Cold pulls the layer apart" |
| ☐ | Formula: $\sigma_f = f \cdot x$ | "f times x, no plus, no minus" |
| ☐ | Maximum: $\sigma_f = f \cdot L/2$ | "Half of f-L, not full f-L" |
| ☐ | Critical combination: $\sigma_b + \sigma_f + \sigma_t$ | "Bend + Friction + Thermal" |
| ☐ | Design event: **coldest night + heaviest load** | "Ice + Truck" |
| ☐ | IRC:37 friction factor: **0.5 to 2.0 MPa/m** | "One-half to two" |
| ☐ | $\alpha_{bitumen} \approx 1 \times 10^{-5}$/°C | "One-E-five" |
| ☐ | Thermal stress often **negligible** after slip | "Slip kills the stress" |
| ☐ | Joint spacing controls frictional stress | "Smaller L = smaller frictional" |
| ☐ | SAMI layer decouples layers to reduce $\sigma_f$ | "Decouple to drop the drag" |
| ☐ | Superposition valid only in **elastic regime** | "Elastic only, never plastic" |
| ☐ | Fatigue check uses **strain**, not stress | "IRC uses epsilon, not sigma" |

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision Strategy (5-Minute Plan):**
> 1. Memorize the three formulas in the KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Section 2.5).
> 2. Practice the free-body derivation once on paper.
> 3. Re-read the valuation warnings above.
> 4. Done — you are exam-ready for this topic! 🎓
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Westergaard's Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET746 - HIGHWAY MATERIALS AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Westergaard's Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Westergaard's Analysis** is the classical closed-form elastic plate theory used to determine the **critical tensile stresses and vertical deflections** in a **rigid (cement concrete) pavement slab** resting on a **dense liquid subgrade (Winkler foundation)**, when subjected to a concentrated wheel load at three critical locations: **interior, edge, and corner**.

The analysis was developed by **Harald Malcolm Westergaard (1925, 1926, 1933, 1948)** and remains the foundational theory taught at KTU for the **mechanistic-empirical design of cement concrete pavements**. It models the pavement slab as a **thin elastic isotropic plate** of finite thickness supported by a subgrade that reacts only under the loaded area (proportional to deflection), with a reaction modulus known as the **modulus of subgrade reaction, k (MPa/m or pci)**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world analogy:** Imagine a **frozen lake in winter** where the thick ice sheet floats on water. When you stand in the **middle** of the lake (interior load), the ice bends slightly and the surrounding ice helps support you — so the bending stress is **lowest**. When you walk to the **edge of the lake** (edge load), one side has no surrounding ice to help, so the bending stress is **higher**. When you stand at a **corner**, almost no surrounding ice is helping, so the ice cracks the **easiest** there — and the stress is **highest**.

This is exactly how concrete pavements behave:
- **Interior loading** → Stress is minimum (full subgrade support and slab continuity).
- **Edge loading** → Stress is roughly 1.5× to 2× the interior stress.
- **Corner loading** → Stress is the maximum (up to 2× the edge stress) and is the **design-critical** case for plain concrete pavements without load transfer at joints.

### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants and Parameters

> **Key Standard Metrics in Westergaard's Theory:**
> - **E** = Modulus of elasticity of cement concrete = **30,000 MPa (≈ 4.3 × 10⁶ psi)** for M40 grade typical.
> - **μ (Poisson's ratio)** for concrete = **0.15** (Westergaard assumed).
> - **h** = Slab thickness in **mm or m** (typically 200–300 mm for highways).
> - **k** = Modulus of subgrade reaction in **MPa/m or pci** (typical range: 13.7–81.6 MPa/m).
> - **P** = Wheel load in **N or kN** (single wheel load, typically 20–80 kN).
> - **l** = Radius of relative stiffness in **mm or m**.

### 1.4 Position of Westergaard's Analysis in KTU 2024 Module 4

This topic directly supports the following KTU 2024 Course Outcomes (COs):
- **CO4 (Module 4):** *Design flexible and rigid pavements using IRC and Westergaard's approach.*
- It feeds into **IRC:58-2015** design charts (which are derived from Westergaard's stress equations).
- It is a prerequisite for understanding **load transfer at joints (dowels and tie bars)** in subsequent modules.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fundamental Assumptions of Westergaard's Theory

The theory holds good only when the following assumptions are satisfied (these are **frequently asked as 2-3 mark questions** in KTU exams):

1. **The slab behaves as a homogeneous, isotropic, elastic solid** of uniform thickness.
2. **The subgrade reaction is vertical only** and proportional to the deflection (Winkler's hypothesis — dense liquid subgrade).
3. **The subgrade reaction at any point is independent of the reactions at adjacent points** (no shear transfer in the subgrade).
4. **The slab is infinite in extent** for interior loading, semi-infinite for edge loading, and a quarter-infinite for corner loading.
5. **The wheel load is assumed to be uniformly distributed over a circular contact area** of radius **a**.
6. **The slab thickness is small** compared to its lateral dimensions, so thin-plate theory is valid.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Limitation:** Westergaard assumed the contact area to be **circular**. For **non-circular contact areas** (typical for dual-wheel aircraft loads and modern truck dual wheels), **equivalent radius of contact (aₑ)** is used, which is the radius of a circle whose area equals the actual contact area.

### 2.2 The Three Critical Load Positions

| Position | Description | Stress Magnitude | Design Criticality |
|----------|-------------|------------------|---------------------|
| **Interior** | Load applied far from any free edge (≥ 2.5l from edge) | **Lowest** (σᵢ) | Rarely governing |
| **Edge** | Load applied tangentially to a free longitudinal edge | **Intermediate** (σₑ ≈ 1.6 σᵢ) | Governing for **plain jointed pavements** |
| **Corner** | Load applied at the intersection of two free edges | **Highest** (σc ≈ 2 σᵢ) | **Most critical** for jointed plain concrete pavements |

> **Note:** The variable **l = radius of relative stiffness** measures the stiffness of the slab-subgrade system — a high l means a stiff slab on weak subgrade, and a low l means a flexible slab on strong subgrade.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $\ell = \left[ \dfrac{E h^{3}}{12 k \left(1 - \mu^{2}\right)} \right]^{1/4}$ | **Radius of relative stiffness** (l) | m or mm |
| 2 | $a = \sqrt{\dfrac{P}{\pi q}}$ | Radius of tire contact area | m |
| 3 | $a_{e} = \sqrt{\dfrac{P}{\pi q}}$ | Equivalent radius (if contact is non-circular) | m |
| 4 | $a_{e} = 1.8082 \, a_{c}$ | Equivalent radius (when contact area is elliptical) | m |
| 5 | $\sigma_{i} = \dfrac{0.316\, P}{h^{2}} \left[ 4 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{\ell}{b}\right) + 1.069 \right]$ | **Interior stress** (Westergaard, 1926) | MPa |
| 6 | $b = a \quad \text{when} \quad a \geq 1.724\,h$ | If a < 1.724h, use $b = \sqrt{1.6 a^{2} + h^{2}} - 0.675 h$ | m |
| 7 | $\sigma_{e} = \dfrac{0.572\, P}{h^{2}} \left[ 4 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{\ell}{b}\right) + 0.359 \right]$ | **Edge stress** (Westergaard, 1926) | MPa |
| 8 | $\sigma_{c} = \dfrac{3 P}{h^{2}} \left[ 1 - \left(\dfrac{a_{3} \sqrt{2}}{\ell}\right)^{0.6} \right]$ | **Corner stress** (Westergaard, 1933) | MPa |
| 9 | $P_{c} = \dfrac{P}{1 - \left(\dfrac{a_{3} \sqrt{2}}{\ell}\right)^{0.6}}$ | **Corner load capacity** for known stress | N |
| 10 | $T = \dfrac{0.485 L + 0.2}{1.5}$ | Slab length for warping stress (IRC:58) | m |
| 11 | Warping stress: $\sigma_{tw} = \dfrac{E \alpha \, \Delta T}{2} \left( \dfrac{C_{x} + \mu \, C_{y}}{1 - \mu^{2}} \right)$ | Bradbury's warping stress | MPa |
| 12 | $\sigma_{tf} = \dfrac{L \, f \, W \times g}{2 \times 10^{3} \, h}$ | **Frictional stress** at joint | MPa |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** In the above table, the variables **C_x, C_y** are **Bradbury's coefficients** read from standard charts, **α** is the coefficient of thermal expansion of concrete (**≈ 10 × 10⁻⁶ /°C**), **ΔT** is the temperature differential between top and bottom of slab, **W** is the weight of slab per unit area, and **f** is the coefficient of subgrade friction (**≈ 1.5**).

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Westergaard's analysis is the **backbone of modern rigid pavement design codes** worldwide:
- **IRC:58-2015** (Indian Roads Congress) charts for **edge stress** are direct graphical solutions of Westergaard's edge equation.
- **PCA (Portland Cement Association) method** uses Westergaard interior stress for fatigue analysis.
- **AASHTO 1993/1998 design** also starts from Westergaard-derived stress concepts.
- **Airport pavement design (FAA)** uses Westergaard corner stress for rigid airport runways.

> **Engineering takeaway:** Any KTU design problem on rigid pavement will essentially ask you to:
> 1. Compute the **radius of relative stiffness (l)**.
> 2. Compute the **wheel contact radius (a)**.
> 3. Substitute into the appropriate **Westergaard stress equation** (interior, edge, or corner).
> 4. Check against **flexural strength of concrete** to determine the **safe slab thickness (h)**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Radius of Relative Stiffness, l

> [!NOTE]
> The radius of relative stiffness (l) is **the most fundamental parameter** in Westergaard's theory. It represents the effective stiffness of the slab-subgrade composite system.

**Starting concept:** Consider a thin elastic plate of flexural rigidity $D$ resting on a Winkler foundation (a "dense liquid" with modulus $k$).

The governing differential equation of equilibrium for the deflected plate is:

$$D \nabla^{4} w + k w = q(x, y)$$

where:
- $D = \dfrac{E h^{3}}{12(1 - \mu^{2})}$ is the **flexural rigidity** of the plate (per unit width).
- $w$ is the **deflection** at point (x, y).
- $q$ is the **applied load intensity**.
- $\nabla^{4} = \dfrac{\partial^{4}}{\partial x^{4}} + 2\dfrac{\partial^{4}}{\partial x^{2} \partial y^{2}} + \dfrac{\partial^{4}}{\partial y^{4}}$.

To make this equation **dimensionless**, introduce the non-dimensional coordinates:

$$\xi = \dfrac{x}{\ell}, \quad \eta = \dfrac{y}{\ell}$$

where $\ell$ is chosen such that the equation becomes:

$$\dfrac{D}{k \ell^{4}} \nabla^{4} w + w = \dfrac{q}{k}$$

For this normalization to be elegant, we require:

$$\dfrac{D}{k \ell^{4}} = 1 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \ell^{4} = \dfrac{D}{k} = \dfrac{E h^{3}}{12 k (1 - \mu^{2})}$$

Taking the fourth root:

$$\boxed{\ell = \left[ \dfrac{E h^{3}}{12 k (1 - \mu^{2})} \right]^{1/4}}$$

**Physical interpretation:**
- l has the **dimension of length** (m or mm).
- A **stiffer slab (larger E or h)** → larger l → stress spread is more — lower stress.
- A **softer subgrade (smaller k)** → larger l → stress spread is more — lower stress.
- Conversely, a **thin slab on hard subgrade** gives small l and **concentrated stress**.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Contact Radius, a

The wheel load P is assumed uniformly distributed over a **circular contact patch** of radius **a** with contact pressure **q** (tire inflation pressure, typically 0.7–0.8 MPa for trucks).

**Force equilibrium** of the contact area:

$$P = q \cdot \pi a^{2}$$

Solving for a:

$$\boxed{a = \sqrt{\dfrac{P}{\pi q}}}$$

For dual-wheel or non-circular contact, the **equivalent radius** is:

$$a_{e} = \sqrt{\dfrac{A_{c}}{\pi}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{P}{\pi q}}$$

where $A_c$ is the actual contact area.

### 3.3 Westergaard's Interior Stress Equation — Conceptual Derivation

Westergaard used the **plate theory of Timoshenko** with a series solution using **deflection functions** satisfying the boundary conditions for an infinite plate with a single concentrated load. The maximum flexural stress occurs at the **bottom of the slab directly under the load**.

The general solution to the plate equation under a concentrated load P on Winkler foundation yields the **deflection** at distance r from the load:

$$w(r) = \dfrac{P \ell^{2}}{8 D} \cdot \text{Ker}\!\left(\dfrac{r}{\ell \sqrt{2}}\right)$$

where Ker is the **Kelvin function of order zero** (a Bessel-related function).

The bending moment per unit width is:

$$M = -D \left( \dfrac{\partial^{2} w}{\partial r^{2}} + \dfrac{\mu}{r} \dfrac{\partial w}{\partial r} \right)$$

The **maximum tensile stress** at the bottom of the slab under the load is:

$$\sigma_{i} = \dfrac{6 M}{h^{2}}$$

After long algebraic manipulation (involving asymptotic expansion of Kelvin functions and fitting to empirical constants), Westergaard obtained his famous closed-form equation:

$$\boxed{\sigma_{i} = \dfrac{0.316\, P}{h^{2}} \left[ 4 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{\ell}{b}\right) + 1.069 \right]}$$

> **Note on the constant 0.316:** This constant was empirically fitted by Westergaard. It absorbs the unit conversions between P (in lb), h (in inches), and the resulting stress (in psi). For SI units (P in N, h in mm, σ in MPa), the constant changes to approximately **3.36 × 10⁻³** or can be reformulated as **0.316 P/h²** with consistent unit usage.

### 3.4 Modifications for Small Contact Area (b ≠ a)

When the **contact radius a is small** compared to slab thickness (i.e., $a < 1.724\,h$), the theoretical stress concentration becomes very high. Westergaard **replaced a by b** (effective radius) to account for the finite thickness of the slab:

$$\boxed{b = \sqrt{1.6 a^{2} + h^{2}} - 0.675\, h}$$

When $a \geq 1.724\,h$, the contact patch is large enough that $b = a$.

### 3.5 Derivation of Edge Stress

For a load placed at a free edge, the boundary condition changes — the bending moment along the edge is zero, but the subgrade reaction exists only on one side. Westergaard solved the plate equation for this **semi-infinite plate** and obtained:

$$\boxed{\sigma_{e} = \dfrac{0.572\, P}{h^{2}} \left[ 4 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{\ell}{b}\right) + 0.359 \right]}$$

The constant **0.572** is **larger than 0.316** (interior case) because the absence of subgrade on the free side causes higher bending. Ratio: $\sigma_e / \sigma_i \approx 1.6$ to 1.8.

### 3.6 Derivation of Corner Stress

For a load at the corner, the slab curls upward at the corner (no subgrade reaction there). The slab acts as a **cantilever** in the corner region. Westergaard obtained:

$$\boxed{\sigma_{c} = \dfrac{3 P}{h^{2}} \left[ 1 - \left(\dfrac{a_{3}\sqrt{2}}{\ell}\right)^{0.6} \right]}$$

where $a_3$ is the **radius of equivalent contact area** for the corner case, often taken equal to $a$ (tire contact radius) or computed with $b$.

The exponent **0.6** is empirically derived; this equation is the most complex and is the **design-critical** equation for jointed unreinforced concrete pavements.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Insight:** When the slab is very stiff (l very large), the term $(a_3 \sqrt{2}/\ell)^{0.6}$ becomes very small, and $\sigma_c$ approaches the upper limit $3P/h^2$. Conversely, for flexible slabs (l small), the corner stress reduces.

### 3.7 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem (from KTU 2022 University Exam Pattern):**
> A cement concrete pavement slab has thickness $h = 200$ mm. The modulus of elasticity of concrete is $E = 30{,}000$ MPa, Poisson's ratio $\mu = 0.15$, and subgrade reaction modulus $k = 30$ MPa/m. A wheel load of $P = 50$ kN is applied with tire contact pressure $q = 0.7$ MPa. Compute (a) the radius of relative stiffness, (b) the contact radius, and (c) the maximum edge stress using Westergaard's equation.

**Solution:**

**(a) Radius of relative stiffness:**

$$\ell = \left[ \dfrac{E h^{3}}{12 k (1 - \mu^{2})} \right]^{1/4}$$

$$\ell = \left[ \dfrac{30{,}000 \times (0.200)^{3}}{12 \times 30 \times (1 - 0.15^{2})} \right]^{1/4}$$

$$\ell = \left[ \dfrac{30{,}000 \times 0.008}{12 \times 30 \times 0.9775} \right]^{1/4} = \left[ \dfrac{240}{351.9} \right]^{1/4} = (0.682)^{1/4}$$

$$\boxed{\ell \approx 0.908 \text{ m} = 908 \text{ mm}}$$

**(b) Contact radius:**

$$a = \sqrt{\dfrac{P}{\pi q}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{50{,}000}{\pi \times 0.7 \times 10^{6}}} = \sqrt{0.02274} \approx 0.151 \text{ m} = 151 \text{ mm}$$

Since $1.724 h = 345$ mm, and $a = 151$ mm < $1.724 h$, we compute $b$:

$$b = \sqrt{1.6 a^{2} + h^{2}} - 0.675\, h = \sqrt{1.6 \times (0.151)^{2} + (0.200)^{2}} - 0.675 \times 0.200$$

$$b = \sqrt{0.0365 + 0.04} - 0.135 = \sqrt{0.0765} - 0.135 = 0.2766 - 0.135 \approx 0.142 \text{ m}$$

**(c) Edge stress:**

$$\sigma_{e} = \dfrac{0.572 \times 50{,}000}{(200)^{2}} \left[ 4 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{908}{142}\right) + 0.359 \right]$$

$$\sigma_{e} = \dfrac{0.572 \times 50{,}000}{40{,}000} \left[ 4 \log_{10}(6.394) + 0.359 \right]$$

$$\sigma_{e} = 0.715 \times \left[ 4 \times 0.806 + 0.359 \right] = 0.715 \times \left[ 3.224 + 0.359 \right]$$

$$\sigma_{e} = 0.715 \times 3.583 \approx \boxed{2.56 \text{ MPa}}$$

> **Verification:** If flexural strength of M40 concrete = 5.0 MPa, the factor of safety = 5.0 / 2.56 ≈ 1.95. The slab is **safe** but marginally so; the design would be revised if the stress exceeded allowable values.

---

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: Westergaard's Analysis Methodology

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Rigid Pavement Design Problem] --> B[Identify Inputs: P, q, h, E, mu, k]
    B --> C[Compute Contact Radius a]
    C --> D{Is a greater than 1.724h?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Set b equals a]
    D -- No --> F[Compute b using Westergaard formula]
    E --> G[Compute Radius of Relative Stiffness l]
    F --> G
    G --> H{Identify Load Position}
    H -- Interior --> I[Apply Interior Stress Equation]
    H -- Edge --> J[Apply Edge Stress Equation]
    H -- Corner --> K[Apply Corner Stress Equation]
    I --> L[Compute Max Tensile Stress sigma]
    J --> L
    K --> L
    L --> M[Add Temperature Warping and Frictional Stresses]
    M --> N[Compare with Flexural Strength of Concrete]
    N -- Safe --> O[Accept Slab Thickness h]
    N -- Unsafe --> P[Increase h and Iterate]
    P --> C
    O --> Q[End: Final Design Output]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Three Critical Load Positions on a Concrete Slab

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Slab1 [Interior Loading Case]
        A1[Wheel Load P] --> A2[Slab Bends Symmetrically]
        A2 --> A3[Subgrade Reaction on All Sides]
        A3 --> A4[Stress sigma i: MINIMUM]
    end
    subgraph Slab2 [Edge Loading Case]
        B1[Wheel Load P at Free Edge] --> B2[Slab Bends Asymmetrically]
        B2 --> B3[Subgrade Reaction on One Side Only]
        B3 --> B4[Stress sigma e: INTERMEDIATE]
    end
    subgraph Slab3 [Corner Loading Case]
        C1[Wheel Load P at Corner] --> C2[Slab Curls Upward at Corner]
        C2 --> C3[No Subgrade Reaction at Corner]
        C3 --> C4[Stress sigma c: MAXIMUM]
    end
```

### 4.3 Schematic Block: Subgrade Reaction Models

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Subgrade Reaction Models] --> B0[Westergaard Winkler Model]
    A0 --> C0[Burmister Layered Elastic Model]
    A0 --> D0[Finite Element Method FEM]
    B0 --> B1[Proportional to Deflection Only]
    B0 --> B2[No Shear Transfer in Subgrade]
    B0 --> B3[Foundation Modulus k Constant]
    C0 --> C1[Multi Layered Elastic Half Space]
    C0 --> C2[Realistic but Complex]
    D0 --> D1[Most Accurate but Computationally Heavy]
    D0 --> D2[Used in Modern IRC and FHWA Designs]
```

### 4.4 Functional Architecture: Inputs → Westergaard Engine → Outputs

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Wheel Load P] --> X[Westergaard Engine]
    Q1[Tire Pressure q] --> X
    H1[Slab Thickness h] --> X
    E1[Elastic Modulus E] --> X
    M1[Poisson Ratio mu] --> X
    K1[Subgrade Modulus k] --> X
    X --> Y1[Radius of Relative Stiffness l]
    X --> Y2[Contact Radius a and b]
    X --> Y3[Interior Stress sigma i]
    X --> Y4[Edge Stress sigma e]
    X --> Y5[Corner Stress sigma c]
    Y3 --> Z[Final Design Check Against Flexural Strength]
    Y4 --> Z
    Y5 --> Z
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Model Paper]**

**Q1.** Define the term **"Radius of Relative Stiffness"** in Westergaard's analysis and state its significance in rigid pavement design. Mention its SI units.

**Model Answer:**
The radius of relative stiffness, denoted by **l**, is defined as the characteristic length that represents the combined stiffness of the slab and the subgrade in Westergaard's analysis. Mathematically:

$$l = \left[ \dfrac{E h^{3}}{12 k (1 - \mu^{2})} \right]^{1/4}$$

where $E$ = modulus of elasticity of concrete, $h$ = slab thickness, $k$ = modulus of subgrade reaction, and $\mu$ = Poisson's ratio of concrete. Its **SI unit is metre (m)**.

**Significance:**
- It is a measure of the **stiffness of the slab-subgrade system**.
- It directly controls the **stress magnitude**: higher l means lower stress and vice versa.
- It determines **how far the wheel load effect spreads** in the slab.
- All three Westergaard stress equations (interior, edge, corner) are functions of $l/b$, where $b$ is the equivalent contact radius.

> **[Marks Distribution: Definition 1 + Formula 1 + Significance 1]**

---

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Supplementary]**

**Q2.** Compare **interior, edge, and corner loading conditions** in Westergaard's analysis. Why is the corner considered the most critical for plain jointed concrete pavements?

**Model Answer:**

| Condition | Stress Coefficient | Critical for |
|-----------|--------------------|--------------|
| Interior | 0.316 | Rarely governing |
| Edge | 0.572 | Plain jointed pavements with load transfer |
| Corner | 3.0 (approx) | Plain jointed pavements without load transfer |

The **corner is most critical** because:
1. **No subgrade reaction** exists at the corner itself; the slab curls up.
2. **No slab continuity** in two perpendicular directions.
3. The slab acts as a **cantilever** at the corner, generating **maximum bending moment**.
4. The corner stress can be up to **2× the edge stress** and **3× the interior stress** for the same load.
5. Hence, **corner is the governing design condition** for plain concrete pavements without dowel/tie bars at joints.

---

### 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - 2024 University Exam Pattern, Module 4 Internal Choice]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam - 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** State the **assumptions of Westergaard's theory** of rigid pavement analysis. Derive the expression for the **radius of relative stiffness**. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** A concrete pavement slab has thickness $h = 220$ mm, with $E = 3 \times 10^{4}$ MPa, $\mu = 0.15$, and $k = 28$ MPa/m. A wheel load of $P = 55$ kN is applied through tires with contact pressure $q = 0.7$ MPa. Calculate the **maximum edge stress** using Westergaard's equation. Take flexural strength of concrete as 4.5 MPa and comment on the safety of the design. **\[7 Marks\]**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A (a):**

**Assumptions of Westergaard's Theory:**

1. The concrete slab is **homogeneous, isotropic, and elastic** with constant thickness.
2. The subgrade is modelled as a **dense liquid (Winkler foundation)** where reaction at any point is proportional to the deflection: $p = k w$, with $k$ constant.
3. The **subgrade reaction is independent** at every point (no shear interaction in the subgrade).
4. The slab is **infinite in lateral extent** (for interior), **semi-infinite** (for edge), or **quarter-infinite** (for corner).
5. The wheel load is **uniformly distributed** over a **circular contact area** of radius $a$.
6. The slab behaves as a **thin plate** — shear deformation is neglected.

**Derivation of Radius of Relative Stiffness:**

The governing plate equation on Winkler foundation:

$$D \nabla^{4} w + k w = q(x,y)$$

where flexural rigidity:

$$D = \dfrac{E h^{3}}{12 (1 - \mu^{2})}$$

To non-dimensionalize, substitute $x = \ell \xi$, $y = \ell \eta$. Then:

$$\nabla^{4} = \dfrac{1}{\ell^{4}} \nabla_{\xi, \eta}^{4}$$

The plate equation becomes:

$$\dfrac{D}{k \ell^{4}} \nabla_{\xi, \eta}^{4} w + w = \dfrac{q}{k}$$

For dimensional homogeneity, set $\dfrac{D}{k \ell^{4}} = 1$, which gives:

$$\ell^{4} = \dfrac{D}{k} = \dfrac{E h^{3}}{12 k (1 - \mu^{2})}$$

$$\boxed{\ell = \left[ \dfrac{E h^{3}}{12 k (1 - \mu^{2})} \right]^{1/4}}$$

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A (b):**

**Step 1: Compute the radius of relative stiffness (l)**

$$\ell = \left[ \dfrac{3 \times 10^{4} \times (0.220)^{3}}{12 \times 28 \times (1 - 0.15^{2})} \right]^{1/4}$$

$$\ell = \left[ \dfrac{3 \times 10^{4} \times 0.010648}{12 \times 28 \times 0.9775} \right]^{1/4} = \left[ \dfrac{319.44}{328.44} \right]^{1/4} = (0.9726)^{1/4}$$

$$\ell \approx 0.9931 \text{ m} = 993.1 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2: Compute the tire contact radius (a)**

$$a = \sqrt{\dfrac{P}{\pi q}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{55{,}000}{\pi \times 0.7 \times 10^{6}}} = \sqrt{0.0250} \approx 0.1581 \text{ m} = 158.1 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3: Check if $a \geq 1.724 h$**

$1.724 \times 220 = 379.3$ mm. Since $a = 158.1$ mm $< 379.3$ mm, compute $b$:

$$b = \sqrt{1.6 a^{2} + h^{2}} - 0.675 h$$

$$b = \sqrt{1.6 \times (0.1581)^{2} + (0.220)^{2}} - 0.675 \times 0.220$$

$$b = \sqrt{1.6 \times 0.0250 + 0.0484} - 0.1485 = \sqrt{0.0400 + 0.0484} - 0.1485$$

$$b = \sqrt{0.0884} - 0.1485 = 0.2973 - 0.1485 \approx 0.1488 \text{ m} = 148.8 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4: Compute the edge stress**

$$\sigma_{e} = \dfrac{0.572 \times 55{,}000}{(220)^{2}} \left[ 4 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{993.1}{148.8}\right) + 0.359 \right]$$

$$\sigma_{e} = \dfrac{0.572 \times 55{,}000}{48{,}400} \left[ 4 \log_{10}(6.673) + 0.359 \right]$$

$$\sigma_{e} = 0.650 \times \left[ 4 \times 0.8244 + 0.359 \right] = 0.650 \times \left[ 3.2976 + 0.359 \right]$$

$$\sigma_{e} = 0.650 \times 3.6566 \approx \boxed{2.377 \text{ MPa}}$$

**Step 5: Design Check**

- Computed edge stress: $\sigma_e = 2.377$ MPa
- Flexural strength of concrete: $f_{cr} = 4.5$ MPa
- Factor of Safety = $4.5 / 2.377 \approx 1.89$

**Comment:** The factor of safety is **1.89, which is greater than 1.5 (typical minimum)**. The slab is **safe** under the given load. However, considering repetition of loads, temperature stresses, and fatigue, the design may be revised with slightly increased thickness for long-term performance.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **[KTU University Exam - 2022 Modified]**

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches the **three critical loading positions** (interior, edge, and corner) in Westergaard's analysis. Write the corresponding **stress equations** and discuss which case gives the **maximum stress** and why. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** A concrete pavement slab of thickness $h = 250$ mm is subjected to a **corner load** of $P = 60$ kN through a tire contact area equivalent to a circle of radius $a = 150$ mm. Given $E = 3 \times 10^{4}$ MPa, $\mu = 0.15$, $k = 35$ MPa/m. Compute the **maximum corner stress** using Westergaard's formula. **\[7 Marks\]**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B (a):**

**Three Critical Load Positions:**

**(i) Interior Load:** The wheel load is applied far from any free edge (distance > 2.5l). The slab deflects in a roughly circular basin, with subgrade reaction acting on all sides of the load. The stress is given by:

$$\sigma_{i} = \dfrac{0.316 P}{h^{2}} \left[ 4 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{\ell}{b}\right) + 1.069 \right]$$

**(ii) Edge Load:** The wheel load is applied tangentially to a free longitudinal edge. The slab deflects asymmetrically; the free edge lifts slightly, and the maximum stress occurs at the bottom of the slab along the edge.

$$\sigma_{e} = \dfrac{0.572 P}{h^{2}} \left[ 4 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{\ell}{b}\right) + 0.359 \right]$$

**(iii) Corner Load:** The wheel load is applied at the intersection of two free edges. The corner of the slab curls upward; subgrade reaction does not act at the corner. The slab acts like a cantilever, and maximum tensile stress occurs at the **top of the slab** along the diagonal bisector of the corner.

$$\sigma_{c} = \dfrac{3 P}{h^{2}} \left[ 1 - \left(\dfrac{a_{3}\sqrt{2}}{\ell}\right)^{0.6} \right]$$

**Which is maximum and why?**

The **corner load** gives the **maximum stress** because:
1. Two free edges meet at the corner — there is **no slab continuity** to share the load.
2. **No subgrade reaction** exists at the corner; the slab curls up due to lack of confinement.
3. The slab behaves like a **cantilever**, with the corner free to lift.
4. The bending moment arm is the largest at the corner.
5. The numerical coefficient is the largest (3.0) compared to 0.572 (edge) and 0.316 (interior).

Hence, for **plain jointed concrete pavements** (without load transfer devices), the **corner stress is the design-critical value**.

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B (b):**

**Step 1: Radius of relative stiffness**

$$\ell = \left[ \dfrac{3 \times 10^{4} \times (0.250)^{3}}{12 \times 35 \times (1 - 0.15^{2})} \right]^{1/4}$$

$$\ell = \left[ \dfrac{3 \times 10^{4} \times 0.015625}{12 \times 35 \times 0.9775} \right]^{1/4} = \left[ \dfrac{468.75}{410.55} \right]^{1/4} = (1.1418)^{1/4}$$

$$\ell \approx 1.034 \text{ m} = 1034 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2: Take $a_3 = a = 150$ mm** (radius of contact area for corner).

**Step 3: Compute the corner stress**

$$\sigma_{c} = \dfrac{3 P}{h^{2}} \left[ 1 - \left(\dfrac{a_{3}\sqrt{2}}{\ell}\right)^{0.6} \right]$$

$$\sigma_{c} = \dfrac{3 \times 60{,}000}{(250)^{2}} \left[ 1 - \left(\dfrac{150 \times \sqrt{2}}{1034}\right)^{0.6} \right]$$

$$\sigma_{c} = \dfrac{180{,}000}{62{,}500} \left[ 1 - \left(\dfrac{212.13}{1034}\right)^{0.6} \right]$$

$$\sigma_{c} = 2.88 \times \left[ 1 - (0.2051)^{0.6} \right]$$

Compute $(0.2051)^{0.6}$: take $\ln(0.2051) = -1.585$, then $0.6 \times (-1.585) = -0.951$, so $(0.2051)^{0.6} = e^{-0.951} \approx 0.3865$.

$$\sigma_{c} = 2.88 \times \left[ 1 - 0.3865 \right] = 2.88 \times 0.6135 \approx \boxed{1.767 \text{ MPa}}$$

**Comment:** The corner stress of 1.767 MPa is less than the flexural strength of M40 concrete (~5 MPa). The corner is safe under this single load. For design, the maximum stress is **sum of corner stress + warping stress + frictional stress** and must be compared with allowable flexural strength.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks (Compiled from Past Valuation Reports):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units consistently.** Always use SI: $P$ in N, $h$ in mm, and get $\sigma$ in MPa. Mixing lb-inch-psi with SI causes a **2-3 mark deduction** at minimum.
>
> 2. **Not checking the condition $a \geq 1.724\,h$ before using $b = a$.** Many students directly use $b = a$ without checking. If $a < 1.724\,h$, **MUST use the Westergaard correction** $b = \sqrt{1.6 a^{2} + h^{2}} - 0.675 h$. This is a **favourite KTU trick question**.
>
> 3. **Forgetting $(1 - \mu^2)$ in the denominator** of the radius of relative stiffness formula. A very common slip — costs 1-2 marks.
>
> 4. **Confusing edge and corner formulas.** Edge formula has coefficient **0.572** with $\log_{10}$ inside. Corner formula has coefficient **3.0** with the **0.6-power term** — students sometimes substitute the edge formula by mistake.
>
> 5. **Not stating the assumption of Winkler foundation** explicitly in derivation questions. Always mention it.
>
> 6. **In temperature warping questions, students forget to add Bradbury's coefficients $C_x$ and $C_y$** — these are read from charts, not assumed.
>
> 7. **Failing to mention the design-critical condition.** When asked "which is maximum?", students write the equations but forget to **justify the answer physically** (cantilever effect at corner, no subgrade reaction, etc.).

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Westergaard's Analysis (KTU Module 4)**

- **Definition:** Westergaard analyzed stresses in a concrete slab on a Winkler (dense liquid) subgrade.
- **Key parameter:** Radius of relative stiffness $l = \left[ E h^3 / \{12 k (1 - \mu^2)\} \right]^{1/4}$ — in metres.
- **Contact radius:** $a = \sqrt{P/(\pi q)}$ — depends on wheel load and tire pressure.
- **Modified contact radius:** $b = a$ if $a \geq 1.724 h$, else $b = \sqrt{1.6 a^2 + h^2} - 0.675 h$.
- **Three stress equations:**
  - Interior: $\sigma_i = 0.316 P / h^2 \times [4 \log_{10}(l/b) + 1.069]$ — coefficient **0.316**.
  - Edge: $\sigma_e = 0.572 P / h^2 \times [4 \log_{10}(l/b) + 0.359]$ — coefficient **0.572**.
  - Corner: $\sigma_c = 3P/h^2 \times [1 - (a_3\sqrt{2}/l)^{0.6}]$ — coefficient **3.0** with **0.6 power**.
- **Order of severity:** $\sigma_i < \sigma_e < \sigma_c$. Corner is **most critical** for plain pavements.
- **Temperature stresses** must be added: Warping (Bradbury) and Frictional.
- **Standard values:** $E = 30{,}000$ MPa, $\mu = 0.15$, $\alpha = 10 \times 10^{-6}/°C$, $f = 1.5$.
- **Limitation of theory:** Winkler subgrade is an idealization — real soils exhibit shear interaction. Burmister's layered theory and FEM are more accurate but more complex.
- **Design check:** Maximum stress ≤ Flexural strength of concrete / Factor of safety.
- **Sister equations to memorize for IRC:58-2015:** Bradbury's warping stress and frictional stress formulas.
- **Exponent to remember:** The **0.6** power in corner stress — students often write 0.5 or 1.0 by mistake.
- **Log base:** Always **base 10 logarithm** in Westergaard's equations, not natural log.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Joints in Cement Concrete Pavements: Types of Joints and Functions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET746 - HIGHWAY MATERIALS AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Joints in Cement Concrete Pavements: Types of Joints and Functions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Definition — What Are Joints in Cement Concrete Pavements?

> [!NOTE]
> **Joints in Cement Concrete (CC) Pavements** are pre-planned, engineered discontinuities (sawn, formed, or constructed) intentionally introduced into a continuously cast concrete slab to control the location and extent of natural cracking caused by temperature-induced movements, moisture-induced volume changes, and applied traffic loads, while simultaneously providing adequate **load transfer** between adjacent slabs.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PECET746)** syllabus, Module 4 specifically focuses on rigid pavement design, where joints are considered the most critical design element because a well-designed joint system is the difference between a serviceable 30+ year pavement and one that fails within 5 years through uncontrolled random cracking, spalling, pumping, and faulting.

### 1.2 Why Do We Even Need Joints? — The Real-World Analogy

Imagine a long, continuous **concrete footpath** in your college campus. If poured as one monolithic slab on a hot Kerala afternoon, by nightfall (cooler temperature) the slab will try to shrink but is restrained by friction with the base. This restraint generates **tensile stresses** that, since concrete is weak in tension (≈ 1/10th of its compressive strength), cause it to crack **randomly wherever the stress exceeds strength**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Engineer's Dilemma:** Concrete WILL crack — we cannot prevent it. The only question is: *Do we let it crack WHERE IT WANTS, or do we make it crack WHERE WE WANT?* Joints are the answer. We saw/construct joints at calculated spacings so that any crack forms **right at the joint line** (a controlled, straight, vertical plane) rather than wandering across the slab as an ugly, structurally dangerous random crack.

**Physical Constant / Standard Metric to Remember:**
- Coefficient of thermal expansion of concrete: $\alpha_c \approx \mathbf{9 \text{ to } 12 \times 10^{-6} \text{ per } ^\circ C}$
- Concrete drying shrinkage strain: $\epsilon_{sh} \approx \mathbf{200 \text{ to } 800 \times 10^{-6}}$ (typically ~500 × 10⁻⁶ for design)
- Standard joint spacing for India (IRC:15): **Transverse contraction joints at 4.5 m to 5.0 m** for slab thickness 200–250 mm.

### 1.3 Primary Functions of Joints (KTU Board Favourite — Direct Question!)

Joints serve **five** core engineering functions, all of which have appeared in KTU university exams:

1. **Crack Control** — Prevents random, uncontrolled cracking of the slab.
2. **Stress Relief** — Accommodates thermal expansion (heat) and contraction (cooling) and moisture-related shrinkage/swelling.
3. **Load Transfer** — Transfers wheel loads from one slab to the adjacent slab across the joint so that no single slab is overloaded (deflection differential is minimized).
4. **Construction Convenience** — Allows construction to be carried out lane-by-lane or in phases, especially on highways where one lane must remain open to traffic.
5. **Isolation** — Isolates the pavement slab from rigid structures (manholes, drainage inlets, bridge abutments, building foundations) so that differential movement does not cause structural damage.

### 1.4 Classification Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Master Classification (per IRC:15 / KTU Module 4):**
> 
> | Direction | Type | Function |
> |---|---|---|
> | **Transverse** (⊥ to traffic) | Expansion Joint | Allow thermal expansion |
> | **Transverse** (⊥ to traffic) | Contraction Joint | Allow contraction; control crack |
> | **Transverse** (⊥ to traffic) | Construction Joint | End-of-day/emergency halt |
> | **Longitudinal** (∥ to traffic) | Longitudinal Joint | Control longitudinal cracking |
> | **Special** | Warping Joint | Control warping stress |
> | **Special** | Isolation/Expansion Joint | Around structures |
