## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Detailed Anatomy of Each Joint Type

#### A. Expansion Joints (अधिकतम चौड़ाई वाले जॉइंट)

- **Purpose:** Provide space for the slab to **expand** (lengthen) during high temperatures without developing compressive buckling or "blow-ups."
- **Location:** Installed **transversely** at fixed intervals along the pavement length.
- **Construction Detail:**
  - Full-depth joint across the entire slab thickness (no partial saw cut).
  - A **compressible filler board** (impregnated fibreboard, cork, rubber, or bitumen-impregnated cane board, typically **20 mm to 25 mm** thick) is placed vertically between the two slabs.
  - **No bond** is permitted between the slab and filler — concrete must be debonded.
  - **Dowel bars** ARE provided for load transfer (since this is a transverse joint, traffic must be transferred across it).
  - **No tie bars** across an expansion joint (expansion must remain free).
  - A **joint sealant** (bitumen, polysulphide, polyurethane, or silicone) seals the top 6–25 mm of the joint against water and incompressible intrusion.
- **Spacing (IRC:15):** Typically **60 m to 140 m** depending on slab thickness, local temperature range, and aggregate type. Closer spacing (≈ 60 m) is recommended for regions with high diurnal temperature variation (e.g., inland Indian cities). For Kerala's moderate climate, **~90 m to 120 m** is typical.
- **Movement Equation:** Total expansion of a slab of length L over a temperature rise $\Delta T$:
  $$\Delta L = \alpha_c \cdot L \cdot \Delta T$$
  where $\alpha_c$ = coefficient of thermal expansion of concrete.

#### B. Contraction Joints (सिकुड़न जॉइंट / Dummy Joints)

- **Purpose:** Provide a **weakened plane** in the slab so that when concrete contracts (cools, dries, or both), the crack forms neatly at this predetermined location rather than randomly.
- **Location:** Installed transversely at regular intervals.
- **Construction Detail:**
  - Created by **sawing** a partial-depth groove (typically **1/4 to 1/3 of slab depth**, e.g., 50–70 mm in a 200 mm slab) into the hardened concrete, usually **8 to 24 hours** after placement (after the concrete has gained enough strength to be sawn but before internal stresses exceed tensile strength — the "**green-cut window**").
  - **Dowel bars** ARE provided for load transfer.
  - **No tie bars** across contraction joints.
  - Joint sealant applied in the upper portion of the saw cut.
- **Spacing Rule (KTU High-Yield):**
  $$L_{max} \approx 24 \cdot h \text{ (in inches) or } 2.0 \cdot h \text{ (in SI, with L in m, h in mm)}$$
  where $L$ = joint spacing (m), $h$ = slab thickness (mm). For a 200 mm slab, $L \approx 4.0$ m; for a 250 mm slab, $L \approx 5.0$ m. **IRC:15 recommends 4.5–5.0 m** for normal Indian conditions.

#### C. Construction Joints (निर्माण जॉइंट)

- **Purpose:** Joint formed **only when construction stops** — at end of day's work, equipment breakdown, or weather emergency. They are **unplanned structurally** but unavoidable operationally.
- **Location:** Transverse (at end of day's pour) or longitudinal (at lane edge when paving is done lane-by-lane).
- **Construction Detail:**
  - A **header board / bulkhead** is fixed at the joint location to terminate the pour with a vertical face.
  - **Dowel bars** are installed (transverse type) or **tie bars** (longitudinal type).
  - The next-day concrete is placed directly against the hardened face (roughened, cleaned, and a bonding agent / cement slurry applied).
  - The joint acts as a **combined expansion-contraction joint** — it can be sealed as a regular joint.
- **Rule:** Construction joints should preferably coincide with the planned location of a contraction or expansion joint to avoid introducing an extra, irregular joint.

#### D. Longitudinal Joints (अनुदैर्ध्य जॉइंट)

- **Purpose:** Control **longitudinal cracking** caused by repeated wheel load applications inducing fatigue in the slab mid-slab, and by differential temperature/warping between the lane edges and the central part of a wide slab.
- **Location:** Parallel to the centreline of the pavement, typically at **every 3.0 m to 4.5 m** of lane width (one lane is 3.5 m wide, so a longitudinal joint is at the lane edge of a 2-lane undivided road; for wider pavements, additional longitudinal joints are introduced).
- **Construction Detail:**
  - Created by **sawing** a groove (or sometimes formed by a plastic/timber insert during paving) of depth ≈ 1/3 of slab thickness.
  - **Tie bars** (deformed steel bars, typically **12 mm dia., 600 mm long, spaced at 300 mm c/c**) are provided across the joint. They hold the adjacent slabs together vertically and horizontally but permit horizontal sliding for thermal movement.
  - **No dowel bars** in pure longitudinal joints (since traffic is parallel to the joint — no need for vertical load transfer).
  - Joint sealant applied.
- **Tie Bar Design Equation:**
  $$A_s = \frac{b \cdot h \cdot f_t \cdot L_{joint}}{2 \cdot f_s}$$
  where $A_s$ = total steel area at the joint (mm²), $b$ = distance to nearest free edge (mm), $h$ = slab thickness (mm), $f_t$ = working stress in steel of the adjoining slab (≈ 0.6 to 0.7 MPa), $L_{joint}$ = length of joint from free end to point under consideration (mm), $f_s$ = permissible tensile stress in tie bar steel (typically 140–200 MPa for mild steel).

#### E. Warping Joints

- **Purpose:** Control cracking due to **warping stresses** — these arise from the **temperature and moisture differential** between the top and bottom of the slab (top is hot and dry; bottom is cool and wet, or vice versa). The slab tends to curl, and the self-weight restrains this curling, generating tensile stresses.
- **Location & Construction:** Provided in **long, wide slabs** where warping stresses would exceed the bending strength of plain concrete. Their construction is identical to longitudinal joints — a partial-depth groove with tie bars.
- **KTU Note:** In modern practice, warping joints are usually **not provided separately**; longitudinal joints are designed to serve the dual purpose of controlling warping and longitudinal cracking. However, the term is still in the syllabus for conceptual questions.

#### F. Isolation / Expansion Joints Around Structures

- **Purpose:** Completely isolate the pavement slab from any **rigid embedded object** (manhole, drainage inlet, catch basin, bridge approach slab, building foundation, light pole foundation, sign post, etc.) so that thermal expansion of the slab does not push against the structure and cause damage.
- **Construction Detail:** Full-depth joint with compressible filler material (≥ 25 mm thick) all around the structure. **No dowels or tie bars** pass through an isolation joint — the joint must allow completely free movement.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Formulas for KTU 2024 ESE — Joints Topic**

| # | Formula / Concept | Notation | Units (SI) | Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\Delta L = \alpha_c \cdot L \cdot \Delta T$ | $\alpha_c$ = thermal expansion coefficient; $L$ = slab length; $\Delta T$ = temperature rise | $\alpha_c$: per $^\circ C$; $L$: m; $\Delta T$: $^\circ C$ | Thermal expansion of slab between expansion joints |
| 2 | Joint Spacing $L_{max} \approx 2 \cdot h$ | $h$ = slab thickness | $L$ in m, $h$ in mm | Spacing of contraction joints (rule of thumb) |
| 3 | Dowel bar diameter $d = \frac{h}{8}$ (approx.) | $h$ = slab thickness | mm | Diameter of load-transfer dowel in transverse joints |
| 4 | Dowel length $L_d \approx 40 \cdot d$ to $50 \cdot d$ | $d$ = dowel diameter | mm | Length of embedded dowel bar (one side of joint) |
| 5 | Dowel spacing $s_d \approx 1.5 \cdot L_d$ to $2.0 \cdot L_d$ | $L_d$ = dowel length | mm | Spacing of dowels along the joint |
| 6 | Tie bar $A_s = \dfrac{b \cdot h \cdot f_t \cdot L_{joint}}{2 \cdot f_s}$ | See Section 2.1.D | mm², MPa | Tie bar design for longitudinal joints |
| 7 | Filler board thickness $t_f = \alpha_c \cdot L \cdot \Delta T_{max}$ (or use 20–25 mm) | — | mm | Expansion joint filler design |
| 8 | Joint sealant recess = $\dfrac{1}{4} \text{ to } \dfrac{1}{2} \times$ joint width, depth = $\dfrac{1}{2} \times$ width | — | mm | Sealant reservoir geometry |

**Load Transfer Devices — Quick Comparison:**

| Property | Dowel Bar | Tie Bar |
|---|---|---|
| **Used in** | Transverse joints | Longitudinal joints |
| **Function** | Load transfer (vertical shear + bending) | Holds slabs together horizontally |
| **Type** | Smooth, round bar | Deformed bar |
| **Bonded to concrete?** | Yes (one side only — slip on other) | Yes (both sides) |
| **Permits movement?** | Permits slab expansion (slip end) | Yes (horizontal sliding) |
| **Typical dia.** | 25–38 mm | 10–16 mm |
| **Typical length** | 450–600 mm | 500–700 mm |
| **Typical spacing** | 250–300 mm c/c | 300–750 mm c/c |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> In India, **NH-44 (Srinagar to Kanyakumari)** and **NH-48** have several hundred kilometres of CC pavement. The Mumbai–Pune Expressway has both plain jointed CC pavement (with all six joint types above) and continuously reinforced concrete pavement (CRCP, which has only longitudinal and construction joints — no transverse contraction joints, but closely spaced transverse cracks that are "controlled" by the continuous reinforcement). The joint design philosophy for the expressway was guided by **IRC:15-2011** and **IRC:58-2015** (the two key KTU-referenced codes for rigid pavement design). Every single joint that you drive over on a CC highway is a tribute to this Module 4 design philosophy.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Spacing of Contraction Joints Using the Westergaard-Type Subgrade Drag Theory

We will derive the **maximum allowable spacing** of contraction joints using the classical subgrade-drag (or "friction-restraint") theory, which is what IRC:15 implicitly uses.

> **Scenario:** A long CC pavement slab of thickness $h$, length $L$ (between two adjacent contraction joints), rests on a subgrade. The concrete contracts by a uniform strain $\epsilon$ (due to combined thermal + moisture effects). The subgrade exerts a **distributed frictional resistance** $F$ per unit area on the bottom of the slab, opposing this contraction.

**Step 1 — Setup of Forces**

Let the slab be of unit width, length $L$, thickness $h$. Take the centre of the slab as the origin; the slab wants to contract uniformly. The friction force at the bottom resists this.

The unit weight of concrete: $\gamma_c \approx 24 \text{ kN/m}^3$.

Frictional resistance per unit area at the slab–subgrade interface:
$$f = \mu \cdot \gamma_c \cdot h$$
where $\mu$ = coefficient of friction between slab and subbase (typically **1.5 to 2.0**, dimensionless for IRC design — sometimes called "subgrade drag coefficient"). Note: the actual physical friction coefficient is ~0.6–1.0, but IRC uses an "effective" drag coefficient that accounts for slab flexure and warping as well.

**Step 2 — Tensile Stress in Concrete due to Friction Restraint**

The total restraining friction force over the half-length of the slab (i.e., from centre to free end) acts on the cross-section at the centre. This force is in equilibrium with the tensile stress in the concrete at the centre:

$$\sigma_t \cdot h \cdot 1 = f \cdot \frac{L}{2}$$

(Force per unit width on cross-section = frictional force per unit width on half-slab length)

**Step 3 — Solve for Tensile Stress**

$$\sigma_t = \frac{f \cdot L}{2 \cdot h} = \frac{\mu \cdot \gamma_c \cdot h \cdot L}{2 \cdot h}$$

$$\sigma_t = \frac{\mu \cdot \gamma_c \cdot L}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Cracking Criterion**

The concrete will crack when this restrained tensile stress equals the **flexural (modulus of rupture) strength** of concrete, $f_r$:

$$\sigma_t = f_r \implies \frac{\mu \cdot \gamma_c \cdot L}{2} = f_r$$

**Step 5 — Solve for Maximum Joint Spacing**

$$L_{max} = \frac{2 \cdot f_r}{\mu \cdot \gamma_c}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute Typical Indian Design Values**

For M40 concrete, $f_r \approx 4.5 \text{ MPa} = 4500 \text{ kN/m}^2$
$\gamma_c = 24 \text{ kN/m}^3$
$\mu = 1.5$ (typical for India)

$$L_{max} = \frac{2 \times 4500}{1.5 \times 24} = \frac{9000}{36} = 250 \text{ m}$$

This is the **absolute maximum** for unreinforced (no steel) contraction joint spacing. However, in practice we never go beyond 5–6 m, because:
- Thermal gradients between day and night create additional warping stresses not accounted for in this simple formula.
- Random material variability reduces effective $f_r$.
- IRC:15 caps contraction joint spacing at **4.5 m to 5.0 m** as a safety measure.

**Step 7 — Numerical Worked Example (KTU Typical Problem)**

> **Problem (KTU Pattern):** A 200 mm thick CC pavement slab is to be designed for a state highway. Concrete has unit weight 24 kN/m³, modulus of rupture 4.2 MPa, and subgrade drag coefficient 1.5. Determine the maximum permissible spacing of contraction joints using the subgrade-drag theory.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{max} &= \frac{2 \cdot f_r}{\mu \cdot \gamma_c} \\[6pt]
L_{max} &= \frac{2 \times 4.2 \times 10^3 \text{ kN/m}^2}{1.5 \times 24 \text{ kN/m}^3} \\[6pt]
L_{max} &= \frac{8400}{36} \text{ m} \\[6pt]
L_{max} &= 233.33 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** Theoretical maximum spacing = 233 m. But in practice, IRC:15 limits this to 4.5–5.0 m. So the **governing spacing is 4.5 m**, and the theoretical value acts only as a sanity check.

> **Conversion Logic Summary:**
> - Convert $f_r$ from MPa to kN/m² (1 MPa = 1000 kN/m², so 4.2 MPa = 4200 kN/m²).
> - Subgrade drag $\mu$ is dimensionless; $\gamma_c$ in kN/m³ gives a denominator in kN/m².
> - The 2 in the numerator comes from symmetry (slab restrained at centre, free at ends — force accumulates over L/2).
> - The governing IRC value is much smaller because the formula ignores thermal warping and stress concentrations.

### 3.2 Derivation: Thermal Expansion Joint Opening (Why Expansion Joints are Sized the Way They Are)

**Step 1 — Total Free Thermal Expansion**
For a slab of length $L_e$ between two adjacent expansion joints, exposed to a maximum temperature rise $\Delta T_{max}$ from construction temperature:

$$\Delta L = \alpha_c \cdot L_e \cdot \Delta T_{max}$$

**Step 2 — Compressible Filler Compression**
The compressible filler board of initial thickness $t_f$ must compress by an amount equal to $\Delta L$. If the filler's compressive strain capacity is $\epsilon_f$ (typically 30–50% for fibreboard; 25% for cork):

$$\Delta L = \epsilon_f \cdot t_f$$

**Step 3 — Equate to Solve for Filler Thickness**

$$t_f = \frac{\alpha_c \cdot L_e \cdot \Delta T_{max}}{\epsilon_f}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical Example**

> **Problem:** Expansion joints are spaced at 90 m. Maximum temperature rise = 30 °C. $\alpha_c = 10 \times 10^{-6} \text{ per } ^\circ C$. Filler strain capacity $\epsilon_f = 0.4$ (40%). Determine filler thickness.

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_f &= \frac{10 \times 10^{-6} \times 90 \times 30}{0.4} \\[6pt]
t_f &= \frac{27 \times 10^{-3}}{0.4} \text{ m} \\[6pt]
t_f &= 67.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 67.5 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Note:** In practice, IRC recommends a **standard filler thickness of 20 to 25 mm**, with the understanding that for longer spacing or higher $\Delta T$, the joint opening at maximum contraction (winter) must also be checked to ensure sealant remains intact (the "joint opening" must not exceed the sealant's elongation capacity, typically 25–50%).

### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Joint When?

| Engineering Situation | Required Joint Type | Why |
|---|---|---|
| End of day's paving work | **Transverse Construction Joint** | Paving must stop; restart next day with proper load transfer |
| New lane being added beside existing lane | **Longitudinal Construction Joint** | Two lanes poured separately; needs tie bars for integrity |
| Slab length > 60 m without other transverse joints | **Transverse Expansion Joint** | To absorb thermal expansion over long length |
| Every 4.5–5.0 m of slab length | **Transverse Contraction Joint** | Control shrinkage/thermal crack spacing |
| Slab width > 4.5 m (e.g., 2-lane undivided) | **Longitudinal Joint** | Control longitudinal cracking & warping |
| CC pavement meets a bridge abutment | **Isolation/Expansion Joint** | Differential vertical & horizontal movement |
| Around a manhole in the middle of the carriageway | **Isolation Joint** (all four sides) | Free slab movement around rigid object |

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper PECET746]**
*List and briefly explain the different types of joints provided in cement concrete pavements.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — write 3 key points, ~80 words each):**

1. **Transverse Joints** (perpendicular to traffic): These include **expansion joints** (allow slab to expand thermally — full depth, with compressible filler and dowel bars), **contraction joints** (control shrinkage/temperature cracking — partial depth saw cut, with dowel bars, spaced 4.5–5.0 m), and **construction joints** (formed at end-of-day paving — vertical face, with dowel/tie bars).
2. **Longitudinal Joints** (parallel to centreline, between lanes): Control longitudinal cracking and warping. Partial-depth groove with **tie bars** (deformed, 12 mm dia) provided across the joint.
3. **Isolation (or Expansion) Joints**: Provided around rigid structures like manholes, drainage inlets, and bridge abutments to allow free movement of the slab, using a full-depth compressible filler with no dowels or tie bars.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PECET746]**
*What is the function of dowel bars and tie bars in cement concrete pavements? Why are they provided at joints?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Dowel bars** are smooth, round, mild-steel bars (typically 25–38 mm diameter, 450–600 mm long) installed across **transverse joints** (expansion, contraction, and transverse construction). Their function is to **transfer wheel loads** from one slab to the adjacent slab, reducing **deflection differential**, **faulting**, and **pumping** at the joint. One end is bonded to the concrete; the other end has a slip-coat (grease or plastic) so that thermal expansion of the slab is not restrained.
- **Tie bars** are deformed steel bars (typically 12 mm dia, 600 mm long, spaced 300 mm c/c) installed across **longitudinal joints**. Their function is to **tie adjacent lanes together** so that the joint does not open up under traffic. They permit horizontal sliding for thermal movement but resist vertical and horizontal separation.
- Together, they ensure **structural integrity and ride quality** of the jointed CC pavement.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – July 2024, PECET746]

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches the different types of joints provided in cement concrete pavements. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design the spacing of expansion joints and contraction joints for a 200 mm thick plain cement concrete pavement with the following data: coefficient of thermal expansion of concrete = $10 \times 10^{-6}$ per °C, maximum temperature rise = 30 °C, subgrade drag coefficient = 1.5, unit weight of concrete = 24 kN/m³, modulus of rupture of concrete = 4.5 MPa, allowable filler strain = 50%. Also mention the IRC-recommended maximum spacing for both joint types. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

Refer to Section 2.1 of these notes. Briefly outline the following 6 joint types with their functions and key features:

| Joint Type | Orientation | Purpose | Key Features |
|---|---|---|---|
| Expansion | Transverse | Allow thermal expansion | Full depth; 20–25 mm compressible filler; **dowels**; sealant; spacing 60–140 m |
| Contraction | Transverse | Control crack location | Partial saw cut (1/3 depth); **dowels**; sealant; spacing 4.5–5.0 m |
| Construction (Transverse) | Transverse | End of day's work | Bulkhead; **dowels**; rough face; sealing |
| Longitudinal | Parallel to traffic | Control longitudinal cracking & warping | Partial cut; **tie bars** 12 mm dia @ 300 mm c/c; sealant; spacing 3.0–4.5 m |
| Warping | (Generally coincides with longitudinal) | Control warping stress | Identical to longitudinal joint |
| Isolation | All around structures | Free movement | Full depth; 25 mm filler; **no dowels or tie bars** |

*(Examiner note: 1.5 marks for each major joint type well described, 0.5 mark for a labelled sketch.)*

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Expansion joint spacing (using thermal expansion formula):**

For a typical 90 m spacing, calculate the required filler thickness:

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_f &= \frac{\alpha_c \cdot L_e \cdot \Delta T_{max}}{\epsilon_f} \\[6pt]
t_f &= \frac{10 \times 10^{-6} \times 90 \times 30}{0.50} \\[6pt]
t_f &= 54 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 54 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

This exceeds the standard 20–25 mm filler thickness, so the spacing must be reduced. For 20 mm filler (a practical maximum):

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_e &= \frac{t_f \cdot \epsilon_f}{\alpha_c \cdot \Delta T} = \frac{20 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.5}{10 \times 10^{-6} \times 30} \\[6pt]
L_e &= \frac{10 \times 10^{-3}}{3 \times 10^{-4}} = 33.3 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

So for 20 mm filler with 50% allowable strain, max expansion joint spacing ≈ **33 m**. For 25 mm filler, spacing ≈ 41.6 m. **As per IRC:15, expansion joint spacing of 60–140 m is permitted when thicker fillers or higher strain capacity fillers are used (e.g., closed-cell foam, 50 mm thick).**

**Step 2 — Contraction joint spacing (using subgrade drag formula):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{max} &= \frac{2 \cdot f_r}{\mu \cdot \gamma_c} \\[6pt]
L_{max} &= \frac{2 \times 4.5 \times 10^3}{1.5 \times 24} \\[6pt]
L_{max} &= \frac{9000}{36} = 250 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Theoretical maximum = 250 m.** However, IRC:15 recommends a **practical maximum of 4.5 m to 5.0 m** for 200 mm thick slabs, to account for warping stresses, temperature gradient effects, and material variability. **Therefore, governing spacing = 4.5 m.**

**Final Answer:**
- **Expansion joint spacing:** ≤ 33 m (for 20 mm filler, 50% strain) → In practice use 60–140 m as per IRC:15 with appropriately designed filler.
- **Contraction joint spacing:** Theoretical 250 m, **governing IRC value 4.5 m**.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks — ALTERNATIVE)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PECET746]

**(a)** What is the function of joints in cement concrete pavements? Describe in detail the construction and function of **expansion joints** and **contraction joints**, with a comparison of the load-transfer devices used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 250 mm thick plain cement concrete pavement is to be designed. The following data are given: $f_r = 4.7$ MPa, $\mu = 1.5$, $\gamma_c = 24$ kN/m³, $\alpha_c = 10 \times 10^{-6}$ per °C, $\Delta T = 25$ °C, filler strain capacity = 40%. Determine (i) the theoretical maximum spacing of contraction joints using the subgrade-drag formula, and (ii) the theoretical filler thickness required for an expansion joint spacing of 100 m. Compare your results with IRC:15 recommendations. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Functions of joints (1 mark):**
1. Crack control
2. Stress relief from thermal & moisture movements
3. Load transfer across joints
4. Construction convenience
5. Isolation from rigid structures

**Expansion joints (3 marks):**
- Transverse, full-depth, compressible filler (20–25 mm impregnated fibre/cork).
- Dowel bars for load transfer; **no tie bars** — full freedom for thermal expansion.
- Spacing 60–140 m as per IRC:15.
- Joint sealant in upper portion to prevent water & debris entry.

**Contraction joints (3 marks):**
- Transverse, partial-depth saw cut (1/4 to 1/3 of slab thickness, e.g., 60–80 mm in a 250 mm slab).
- Saw window: 8–24 hours after placement (when concrete is strong enough to saw but stresses have not yet exceeded tensile strength).
- Dowel bars for load transfer; **no tie bars**.
- Spacing 4.5–5.0 m as per IRC:15.

**Comparison table (within the 3 marks — implicit in the descriptions above):**

| Property | Expansion Joint | Contraction Joint |
|---|---|---|
| Purpose | Expansion | Shrinkage / cooling |
| Depth | Full | 1/3 to 1/4 of slab |
| Filler | Yes (20–25 mm) | No (just sealant) |
| Dowel | Yes | Yes |
| Spacing (IRC) | 60–140 m | 4.5–5.0 m |

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**(i) Contraction joint spacing using subgrade-drag formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{max} &= \frac{2 \cdot f_r}{\mu \cdot \gamma_c} \\[6pt]
L_{max} &= \frac{2 \times 4.7 \times 10^3 \text{ kN/m}^2}{1.5 \times 24 \text{ kN/m}^3} \\[6pt]
L_{max} &= \frac{9400}{36} \\[6pt]
L_{max} &= 261.1 \text{ m (theoretical)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Compare with IRC:15:** Theoretical 261 m is impractical because the formula ignores warping, diurnal thermal gradients, and material variability. **IRC:15 recommends 4.5–5.0 m for a 250 mm slab.**

**(ii) Filler thickness for 100 m expansion joint spacing:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_f &= \frac{\alpha_c \cdot L_e \cdot \Delta T}{\epsilon_f} \\[6pt]
t_f &= \frac{10 \times 10^{-6} \times 100 \times 25}{0.40} \\[6pt]
t_f &= \frac{25 \times 10^{-3}}{0.40} = 62.5 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Compare with IRC:15:** Standard filler thickness is 20–25 mm. For 100 m spacing with this filler thickness, allowable strain would need to be:
$$\epsilon_f = \frac{10 \times 10^{-6} \times 100 \times 25}{25 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.0 \; (100\%)$$
This is unrealizable with conventional fillers. Therefore, **either reduce expansion joint spacing to ~30–40 m, or use a thicker/softer filler material** (e.g., 50 mm closed-cell foam with 50% strain capacity would allow L ≈ 100 m).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 
> 1. **Confusing "warping joints" with "contraction joints":** Warping joints are NOT separate physical joints in modern practice — they are conceptually served by longitudinal joints. If the question asks "list the types of joints," listing 6 (including warping) is correct; if it asks "sketch the construction," only 5 distinct constructions exist.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting the slip-coat on dowel bars:** A common 1-mark loss. Dowel bars at expansion joints must have a debonding agent (grease, bitumen, or plastic sleeve) on **one end** to allow the slab to expand freely. Students often write "dowel bars" without mentioning the slip-end.
> 
> 3. **Mixing up tie bars and dowel bars:** Tie bars go in **longitudinal** joints (hold lanes together); dowel bars go in **transverse** joints (transfer wheel load). Reversing these is a guaranteed 2-mark loss.
> 
> 4. **Not mentioning sealant:** Every joint, regardless of type, has a **joint sealant** in the upper portion to prevent water ingress, debris, and incompressible intrusion. Forgetting this in a sketch loses 1 mark.
> 
> 5. **Subgrade-drag formula units mismatch:** Always convert $f_r$ to **kN/m²** when $\gamma_c$ is in kN/m³. Mixing MPa with kN/m³ is a 1-mark deduction.
> 
> 6. **Skipping the IRC recommendation in numerical problems:** The theoretical answer from the formula is always larger than the practical IRC value. Both must be stated, and the IRC value should be marked as **"governing."**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Joints = "planned cracks."** Concrete *will* crack; joints decide *where*.
- ✅ **Three transverse joints** (perpendicular to traffic): Expansion (allow expansion), Contraction (control shrinkage), Construction (end of pour).
- ✅ **One longitudinal joint** (parallel to traffic): Controls longitudinal cracking + warping.
- ✅ **Two special joints**: Warping (≡ longitudinal) and Isolation (around rigid structures).
- ✅ **Dowel bars** → Transverse joints → Load transfer → Smooth round bars 25–38 mm dia, 450–600 mm long, slip-coated on one end.
- ✅ **Tie bars** → Longitudinal joints → Hold slabs together → Deformed bars 12 mm dia, 600 mm long, @ 300 mm c/c.
- ✅ **Expansion joint spacing** (IRC:15): **60–140 m**, full depth, with 20–25 mm compressible filler.
- ✅ **Contraction joint spacing** (IRC:15): **4.5–5.0 m**, partial saw cut (1/3 depth) within 8–24 hours of placement.
- ✅ **Longitudinal joint spacing** (IRC:15): **3.0–4.5 m** (one per lane).
- ✅ **Subgrade-drag formula**: $L_{max} = \dfrac{2 f_r}{\mu \gamma_c}$ — gives theoretical maximum; IRC value is the governing practical value.
- ✅ **Filler thickness formula**: $t_f = \dfrac{\alpha_c L_e \Delta T}{\epsilon_f}$ — must be compared with practical 20–25 mm; if exceeded, reduce spacing or use a thicker filler.
- ✅ **Joint sealant** is mandatory on all joint types (upper 6–25 mm, with proper width-to-depth ratio of 1:1 to 1:2).
- ✅ **No bond** between slab and expansion joint filler; **no dowels or tie bars** in isolation joints.
- ✅ IRC codes to remember: **IRC:15-2011** (jointed CC pavement design) and **IRC:58-2015** (rigid pavement design guidelines).
- ✅ **Saw-cut window is critical:** Saw too early → raveling; saw too late → random crack has already formed. Standard: 8–24 hours.
- ✅ **Kerala-specific note:** With moderate climate (max temperature ~35 °C, min ~20 °C), $\Delta T \approx 15$–20 °C; expansion joint spacing of 90 m is typical.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Joint Spacings
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET746 - HIGHWAY MATERIALS AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Joint Spacings

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Academic Definition

**Joint Spacing** in the context of **rigid (cement concrete) pavements** refers to the deliberate, engineered longitudinal and transverse separation distances provided between successive concrete slabs to accommodate the **inevitable volumetric changes** caused by thermal expansion, thermal contraction, moisture-related warping, and subgrade restraint forces. These joints are physical discontinuities introduced to **pre-empt uncontrolled random cracking** of the pavement slab, ensuring that any movement-induced stresses remain within the permissible tensile capacity of the pavement concrete.

As per **IRC:58-2015** (Guidelines for the Design of Plain Jointed Rigid Pavements for Highways) and **IRC:15-2017** (Standard Specifications and Code of Practice for Construction of Concrete Roads), the joint spacing is a function of:
- **Coefficient of thermal expansion** of concrete
- **Tensile strength** of concrete at the relevant age
- **Coefficient of subgrade friction** (slab–subgrade interaction)
- **Unit weight of concrete**
- **Design temperature differential** (seasonal + daily)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 PECET746):** Module 4 of *Highway Materials and Design* rigorously covers the *design philosophy*, *spacing calculations*, and *joint detailing* for plain jointed cement concrete (PJC) pavements, with derivations anchored to **Westergaard's temperature stress theory** and **IRC 58 spacing charts**.

---

## Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have baked a very large, thin, brittle **chocolate brownie** (the concrete slab) sitting on a **non-stick tray** (the subgrade). When the brownie comes out of the oven, it begins to **cool and shrink**. The tray resists the shrinkage due to friction, pulling the brownie back. If the brownie is too long, internal tensile stresses build up and it **cracks randomly** wherever the stress overcomes the brownie's tensile strength.

Now, what if you **pre-cut the brownie** into smaller, regular rectangular pieces *before* it shrinks? The shrinkage is accommodated at the **predefined cut lines (joints)** rather than as random cracks. The pieces move slightly toward the centre, and the *maximum tensile stress in any one piece* stays safely below the breaking point.

> This is *exactly* the engineering rationale behind **joint spacings in concrete pavements** — *we trade controlled, serviceable joints for uncontrolled, catastrophic cracks*.

The **larger** the piece (greater spacing), the higher the induced stress → higher **cracking risk**. The **smaller** the piece, the lower the stress but **more joints** = higher construction cost, more sealant maintenance, and reduced riding comfort.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** *Joint spacing is fundamentally an optimisation problem* — maximise slab length to reduce joints, but never so long that temperature + friction stresses exceed the concrete's flexural/tensile capacity.

### Standard Joint Spacing Magnitudes (IRC:58-2015)

| Joint Type | Direction | Typical Spacing |
|---|---|---|
| **Expansion Joint** | Transverse | **20 m to 60 m** (max 60 m as per IRC) |
| **Contraction Joint** | Transverse | **4 m to 5 m** (plain concrete) |
| **Contraction Joint** | Transverse | **5 m to 6 m** (lightly reinforced) |
| **Longitudinal Joint** | Longitudinal | **3.0 m to 3.5 m** (one lane width) |
| **Construction Joint** | Both | As per construction sequence |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Are Joints Needed? — The Physics of Stresses

When a cement concrete slab cools (contraction) or its subgrade restrains its free movement, **three principal stress systems** develop that the joint designer must counter:

1. **Axial Contraction Stress (Direct Tensile Stress)**
   - Caused by uniform drop in slab temperature.
   - Slab wants to shorten but subgrade friction holds it back.
   - Maximum at the **centre** of the slab length.
   - This is the **primary driver** of joint spacing in contraction joints.

2. **Warping Stress (Bending Stress)**
   - Caused by the **temperature gradient** through the slab thickness (top warmer than bottom during the day, reversed at night).
   - Generates curling moments; *self-equilibrating* (does not directly govern spacing but controls slab thickness).

3. **Frictional Resistance Force**
   - The subgrade exerts a **longitudinal shear drag** on the slab underside.
   - Force per unit width = $f \cdot \gamma \cdot h$ (where $h$ = slab thickness).

---

## 2.2 Derivation Logic of the Spacing Formula (Stepwise)

Consider a slab of length $L$, width $b$, thickness $h$, free to contract by $\Delta L$ due to a temperature drop $\Delta T$.

**Step 1: Free thermal contraction (no restraint):**
$$\delta_{free} = L \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T$$
where $\alpha$ = coefficient of thermal expansion of concrete ≈ **10 × 10⁻⁶ per °C**.

**Step 2: Subgrade friction restraint force (per unit width):**
The frictional force per unit area of slab bottom = $f \cdot \gamma \cdot h$, where $f$ is the coefficient of subgrade friction.

**Step 3: Equating strain energy to elastic strain:**
The slab centre is the point of maximum restraint. The *resultant restraint stress* must not exceed the permissible tensile stress $\sigma_{st}$ of concrete.

**Step 4: The Bradbury/Coefficient-of-Friction Spacing Formula (Most commonly tested in KTU):**
$$L = \frac{2 \cdot \sigma_{st}}{f \cdot \gamma \cdot T}$$
(where $T$ in this notation is the **friction coefficient × unit weight × temperature** product, or simply the design restraint parameter).

> [!NOTE]
> In different textbook notations, the same formula is sometimes expressed as:
> $$L = \frac{2\,\sigma_{st}}{f \cdot w \cdot T}$$
> where $w$ = unit weight of concrete (kN/m³) and $T$ = temperature drop (°C).

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sigma_t = \dfrac{E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T}{2}$ | Westergaard axial stress for fully restrained slab centre | N/mm² or MPa |
| 2 | $L = \dfrac{2\,\sigma_{st}}{f \cdot \gamma \cdot \Delta T}$ | Joint spacing (Bradbury / IRC) | m |
| 3 | $\delta_{free} = L \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T$ | Free thermal expansion/contraction of slab | m |
| 4 | $F_{fric} = f \cdot \gamma \cdot h \cdot L$ | Total frictional restraint force per unit width | kN/m |
| 5 | $\sigma_{st} = 0.8 \, f_{ct}$ (approx.) | Safe tensile stress ≈ 80% of modulus of rupture | MPa |
| 6 | $\alpha_{concrete} \approx 10 \times 10^{-6}$ | Coefficient of thermal expansion of PCC | per °C |
| 7 | $f = 1.0$ to $1.5$ (typical) | Coefficient of subgrade friction | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\gamma = 24$ kN/m³ | Unit weight of plain cement concrete | kN/m³ |
| 9 | Max spacing, expansion joint | $\le 60$ m (IRC:58) for plain CC | m |
| 10 | Max spacing, contraction joint | $4$–$5$ m (plain), $5$–$6$ m (RCC) | m |
| 11 | Longitudinal joint spacing | $3.0$–$3.5$ m (one lane) | m |
| 12 | Dowel bar diameter | $d = \dfrac{h}{8}$ (≈) | mm |
| 13 | Dowel bar length | $L_d = 500$ mm to $600$ mm | mm |
| 14 | Tie bar diameter | $10$–$12$ mm dia, mild steel | mm |
| 15 | Tie bar spacing | $300$–$600$ mm c/c along the joint | mm |
| 16 | Joint seal width | $6$–$25$ mm (depends on movement) | mm |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Convention:** All forces in **kN**, lengths in **m**, stresses in **MPa (N/mm²)**, and temperature in **°C** unless otherwise stated. Be *consistent* with units in derivations — marks are deducted for unit mismatch.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The joint spacing design procedure is the **backbone of rigid pavement design** in India. It directly impacts:
- **Cracking control** — primary performance criterion.
- **Initial cost** — fewer joints = less dowel/tie bars, less sealant.
- **Maintenance cost** — joints are the **first to fail** (spalling, faulting, sealant loss).
- **Riding comfort** — fewer joints = smoother ride.
- **Load transfer** — at transverse joints, dowels must transfer wheel load across the joint for > 40% efficiency.

> A poorly designed joint spacing leads to **uncontrolled mid-slab cracking** (the dreaded "natural crack"), which converts a *serviceable pavement* into a *maintenance liability* within 5–7 years.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Full Derivation: Axial Contraction Stress at Slab Centre

### Given
- Slab length = $L$ (m), width = unit width (b = 1 m)
- Slab thickness = $h$ (m)
- Coefficient of thermal expansion of concrete = $\alpha$ per °C
- Temperature drop = $T$ (°C)
- Coefficient of subgrade friction = $f$
- Unit weight of concrete = $\gamma$ (kN/m³)
- Modulus of elasticity of concrete = $E$ (MPa)

### Assumed Behaviour
The slab **end** (at the joint) is free to move. The slab **centre** is fully restrained. The restraint varies **linearly** from zero at the ends to maximum at the centre.

### Derivation

**Step 1 — Define the frictional restraint force per unit area of slab bottom:**

The frictional force per unit length of slab (per unit width) is:
$$p = f \cdot \gamma \cdot h \quad \text{(kN/m²)}$$

**Step 2 — Cumulative restraint force at distance $x$ from the free end:**

The total frictional force accumulated from the end up to a section at distance $x$ is:
$$F(x) = p \cdot x = f \cdot \gamma \cdot h \cdot x$$

**Step 3 — Equate cumulative restraint to internal tensile force at section $x$:**

For equilibrium of the slab segment from 0 to $x$:
$$\sigma(x) \cdot h \cdot 1 = f \cdot \gamma \cdot h \cdot x$$

$$\therefore \sigma(x) = f \cdot \gamma \cdot x$$

**Step 4 — Identify the maximum at the centre ($x = L/2$):**

$$\sigma_{max} = f \cdot \gamma \cdot \frac{L}{2}$$

**Step 5 — Apply the safety criterion: $\sigma_{max} \le \sigma_{st}$ (allowable tensile stress):**

$$f \cdot \gamma \cdot \frac{L}{2} \le \sigma_{st}$$

**Step 6 — Solve for maximum slab length (joint spacing):**

$$\boxed{\,L = \frac{2\,\sigma_{st}}{f \cdot \gamma}\,}$$

When the design temperature drop $T$ is *explicitly* included as a multiplier (as in the Bradbury refinement, since the frictional force is mobilised only in proportion to the actual contraction):

$$\boxed{\,L = \frac{2\,\sigma_{st}}{f \cdot \gamma \cdot T}\,}$$

> This second form is **the one KTU examiners expect** when a *numerical temperature* is given in the problem.

---

## 3.2 Numerical Solved Example (KTU-Style)

### Problem
Design the spacing of **contraction joints** for a plain cement concrete pavement with the following data:
- Allowable tensile stress in concrete, $\sigma_{st} = 1.40$ MPa = **1400 kN/m²**
- Coefficient of subgrade friction, $f = 1.2$
- Unit weight of concrete, $\gamma = 24$ kN/m³
- Design temperature drop, $T = 20$ °C
- Slab thickness, $h = 0.25$ m (not required for spacing, but for context)

### Solution

**Step 1:** Write down the formula:
$$L = \frac{2 \cdot \sigma_{st}}{f \cdot \gamma \cdot T}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the values. Note the unit conversion: $1$ MPa $= 1000$ kN/m².
$$L = \frac{2 \times 1400}{1.2 \times 24 \times 20}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the denominator:
$$1.2 \times 24 = 28.8$$
$$28.8 \times 20 = 576$$

**Step 4:** Compute the spacing:
$$L = \frac{2800}{576} = 4.861 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5:** Round down to a **practical** construction value:
$$L_{adopted} = 4.5 \text{ m}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Always round down, never up.** If a calculation yields 4.86 m, the engineer must adopt the next lower practical value (e.g., 4.5 m) — exceeding the calculated limit defeats the purpose of the design.

**Step 6:** Verify this against IRC:58-2015 → $4.5$ m is within the prescribed **$4$–$5$ m** range for plain CC. ✔

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Spacing Decision Tree

| Scenario | Governing Criterion | Recommended Spacing |
|---|---|---|
| Hot tropical climate, $\Delta T > 25$ °C | Tighten spacing to control contraction | **3.5 – 4.5 m** |
| Moderate climate, $\Delta T = 15$–$25$ °C | Standard IRC spacing | **4.5 – 5.0 m** |
| Cold climate, $\Delta T < 15$ °C | Relax spacing | **5.0 – 5.5 m** |
| Plain jointed CC (no reinforcement) | IRC:58 (2015) | **4.0 – 5.0 m** |
| Continuously Reinforced CC (CRCP) | IRC:SP:46 | **No transverse joints**; only at structures |
| Expansion joint every $N$ contraction joints | IRC:58 | Max **60 m** between two expansion joints |

---

## 3.4 Joint Spacing for Expansion Joints

Expansion joints must accommodate the slab's **expansion** under the **maximum temperature rise** $\Delta T_{max}$. The design width of the joint gap is:
$$w = L \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T_{max} + \text{construction tolerance (≈ 5 mm)}$$

For typical values ($L = 60$ m, $\alpha = 10 \times 10^{-6}$, $\Delta T_{max} = 30$ °C):
$$w = 60 \times 10 \times 10^{-6} \times 30 = 0.018 \text{ m} = 18 \text{ mm} + 5 \text{ mm} = 23 \text{ mm}$$

Joint filler (compressible, e.g., *Impregnated fibreboard*) of width **20–25 mm** is typically used.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Plan View of a Concrete Pavement with Joint Layout

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph LAY["PLAN VIEW - JOINT LAYOUT (Two Lane Highway)"]
        direction LR
        J1["EXPANSION JOINT E1\n(Transverse, 25mm gap,\nfiller + sealant)"]
        C1["CONTRACTION JOINT C1\n(Saw cut, dummy groove)"]
        C2["CONTRACTION JOINT C2\n(Saw cut, dummy groove)"]
        C3["CONTRACTION JOINT C3\n(Saw cut, dummy groove)"]
        C4["CONTRACTION JOINT C4\n(Saw cut, dummy groove)"]
        J2["EXPANSION JOINT E2\n(Transverse, 25mm gap,\nfiller + sealant)"]
        L1["LONGITUDINAL JOINT\n(Tie bars 10mm dia\n@ 500mm c/c)"]
        L2["LANE 1 (3.5m wide)"]
        L3["LANE 2 (3.5m wide)"]
        L4["SHOULDER (1.0m to 2.0m)"]
    end
    J1 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> C3
    C3 --> C4
    C4 --> J2
    L1 -.- L2
    L1 -.- L3
    L3 -.- L4
    style J1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style J2 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style C1 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333
    style C2 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333
    style C3 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333
    style C4 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333
    style L1 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

---

## 4.2 Cross-Sectional View of Joint Types and Load-Transfer Hardware

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CROSS["CROSS SECTION - JOINT TYPES & DOWEL/TIE BARS"]
        direction TB
        A["SLAB TOP SURFACE\n(Concrete, M40 grade)"]
        B["DOWEL BAR\n(Plain round, 25-32mm dia,\n500mm long,\n@ 250-300mm c/c\nin TRANSVERSE joints)"]
        C["SLAB THICKNESS h\n(typically 200-300mm)"]
        D["SUBBASE / DLC\n(Dry Lean Concrete)"]
        E["SUBGRADE"]
        F["TIE BAR\n(Deformed, 10-12mm dia,\n@ 500mm c/c\nin LONGITUDINAL joints)"]
        G["JOINT FILLER\n(Impregnated fibreboard)\n+ SEALANT (polysulphide)"]
        H["EXPANSION JOINT\n(Full slab depth)"]
        I["CONTRACTION JOINT\n(1/3 to 1/4 slab depth saw cut)"]
        J["CONSTRUCTION JOINT\n(Longitudinal, butt type)"]
    end
    A --> C
    C --> D
    D --> E
    B -.- H
    F -.- J
    G -.- H
    I -.- C
    style A fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#000
    style B fill:#FF8C00,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#000
    style D fill:#A9A9A9,stroke:#000
    style E fill:#8B7355,stroke:#000
    style F fill:#4682B4,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style G fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000
    style H fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000
    style I fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000
    style J fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000
```

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Joint Spacing Design Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["START: Define Inputs\n- sigma_st (tensile stress)\n- f (friction coeff)\n- gamma (unit weight)\n- Delta T (temperature drop)"]
    S2["Apply Bradbury formula\nL = 2 sigma_st / f gamma T"]
    S3["Compute L_max (theoretical)"]
    S4["Check against IRC:58 limits\n4-5m plain / 5-6m RCC"]
    S5{"Within IRC range?"}
    S6["Adopt L = 4.5 m\n(or calculated value)"]
    S7["Adopt IRC upper limit\n(5.0 m) and revise concrete\nstrength assumption"]
    S8["Design expansion joint\nspacing (max 60m)"]
    S9["Design longitudinal joint\n(3.0-3.5m = lane width)"]
    S10["Detail dowel bars\n(d = h/8, L_d = 500mm)"]
    S11["Detail tie bars\n(10-12mm @ 500mm c/c)"]
    S12["END: Final joint drawing"]
    S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5
    S5 -- YES --> S6 --> S8 --> S9 --> S10 --> S11 --> S12
    S5 -- NO --> S7 --> S8
    style S1 fill:#90EE90
    style S12 fill:#FF6347
    style S5 fill:#FFD700
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**What are the different types of joints provided in a cement concrete pavement? List any four with their primary function.**

**Model Answer:**
The four major joint types in a rigid pavement are:

1. **Expansion Joint** — Accommodates thermal *expansion* of the slab; prevents compressive buckling and blow-ups. Provided transversely at intervals of up to **60 m**. Gap filled with compressible filler (e.g., impregnated fibreboard).

2. **Contraction Joint** — Accommodates thermal *contraction*; controls the location of shrinkage cracks. Saw-cut to **1/3 to 1/4** of slab depth. Spacing **4–5 m** (plain CC) per **IRC:58-2015**.

3. **Longitudinal Joint** — Separates lanes paved separately; prevents irregular longitudinal cracking. Spacing equals **lane width (3.0–3.5 m)**. Reinforced with tie bars (10–12 mm dia deformed bars @ 500 mm c/c).

4. **Construction Joint** — Provided at the end of a day's pour or where paving is interrupted. May be of *butt* or *keyed* type. Functions as a tied joint.

> Marks distribution: Listing (1) + Brief function each (0.5 × 4 = 2). Total **3 marks**.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**State the Bradbury formula for joint spacing in concrete pavements. Explain the significance of each term.**

**Model Answer:**
$$L = \frac{2 \, \sigma_{st}}{f \cdot \gamma \cdot \Delta T}$$

Where:
- $L$ = **Joint spacing** in metres.
- $\sigma_{st}$ = **Allowable tensile stress** of concrete (MPa or kN/m²).
- $f$ = **Coefficient of subgrade friction** (typically 1.0 to 1.5).
- $\gamma$ = **Unit weight of concrete** (kN/m³).
- $\Delta T$ = **Design temperature drop** (°C).

**Significance:** The formula gives the *maximum* spacing beyond which the cumulative frictional restraint at the slab centre will exceed the permissible tensile stress, causing **uncontrolled transverse cracking**.

> Marks distribution: Formula (1.5) + Term explanation (0.3 × 5 = 1.5). Total **3 marks**.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### ✦ Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — May 2023]**

**(a)** Derive the Bradbury formula for the spacing of contraction joints in a plain cement concrete pavement, clearly stating the underlying assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A plain cement concrete pavement has the following design data:
- Allowable tensile stress in concrete, $\sigma_{st} = 1.30$ MPa
- Coefficient of subgrade friction, $f = 1.5$
- Unit weight of concrete, $\gamma = 24$ kN/m³
- Design temperature drop, $\Delta T = 22$ °C

Determine the spacing of the contraction joints. Comment on whether IRC:58-2015 specifications are satisfied. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a)

**Assumptions (1 mark):**
1. Slab is free to move at the joint ends and fully restrained at the centre.
2. Frictional restraint varies **linearly** from zero at the ends to maximum at the centre.
3. Slab is of **uniform thickness** and **isotropic** material.
4. Subgrade is **uniform** and provides a constant coefficient of friction.
5. Slab is sufficiently long so that **plane sections remain plane** (1-D analysis).

**Derivation (6 marks):**

Let $L$ = length of slab, $b$ = unit width, $h$ = thickness.

The frictional shear force per unit area on the slab bottom = $f \cdot \gamma \cdot h$.

At a distance $x$ from the free end, the cumulative frictional force (per unit width) is:
$$F(x) = f \cdot \gamma \cdot h \cdot x$$

By equilibrium of the slab segment of length $x$:
$$\sigma(x) \cdot h = f \cdot \gamma \cdot h \cdot x$$
$$\sigma(x) = f \cdot \gamma \cdot x$$

Maximum tensile stress occurs at the **centre** ($x = L/2$):
$$\sigma_{max} = f \cdot \gamma \cdot \frac{L}{2}$$

For safety, $\sigma_{max} \le \sigma_{st}$:
$$f \cdot \gamma \cdot \frac{L}{2} \le \sigma_{st}$$

For a temperature drop of $\Delta T$, the restraint is mobilised proportionally, and the refined formula becomes:
$$\boxed{\,L = \frac{2 \, \sigma_{st}}{f \cdot \gamma \cdot \Delta T}\,}$$

---

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Given:**
$\sigma_{st} = 1.30 \text{ MPa} = 1300 \text{ kN/m}^2$, $f = 1.5$, $\gamma = 24 \text{ kN/m}^3$, $\Delta T = 22$ °C.

**Apply Bradbury formula (4 marks):**
$$L = \frac{2 \times 1300}{1.5 \times 24 \times 22}$$

Denominator:
$$1.5 \times 24 = 36$$
$$36 \times 22 = 792$$

Therefore:
$$L = \frac{2600}{792} = 3.28 \text{ m}$$

**Adopt a practical spacing (1 mark):**
Since IRC:58-2015 specifies a **minimum practical spacing of 4.0 m** for plain CC, and the theoretical value (3.28 m) is *below* this minimum, the limiting spacing is governed by **constructability**, not the formula. Hence:

$$L_{adopted} = 4.0 \text{ m}$$

**Comment (2 marks):**
- The calculated value 3.28 m < 4.0 m → **IRC:58 lower limit** governs.
- The spacing of 4.0 m lies at the lower edge of the IRC range (4–5 m), which is *safe* and practical.
- The design is **acceptable**; the concrete grade may be considered slightly over-designed for the temperature regime, but the safety margin is welcome.

---

### ✦ Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Differentiate clearly between **expansion joints** and **contraction joints** in a cement concrete pavement. Discuss their **spacing criteria** as per IRC:58-2015. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design the **expansion joint gap width** for a concrete pavement with the following data:
- Slab length between two expansion joints = **54 m**
- Coefficient of thermal expansion of concrete = $10 \times 10^{-6}$ per °C
- Maximum temperature rise at site = **35 °C**
- Construction tolerance = **5 mm**
- Joint filler compressibility = **50%**

Also state the recommended dowel bar and tie bar dimensions. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a)

**Comparison Table (5 marks):**

| Feature | Expansion Joint | Contraction Joint |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Accommodate *expansion* | Accommodate *contraction* |
| **Movement** | Closure of gap | Opening of joint |
| **Gap width** | **20–25 mm** with compressible filler | **6–10 mm** saw-cut, no filler |
| **Depth of cut** | Full slab depth | **1/3 to 1/4** of slab depth |
| **Filler** | Compressible (fibreboard) | None (only sealant in groove) |
| **Dowel bars** | **One end smooth + other end greased** (slip-end) to permit free movement | **Both ends bonded** to transfer load |
| **Spacing (IRC:58-2015)** | **Max 60 m** between two expansion joints | **4–5 m** for plain CC; **5–6 m** for RCC |
| **Frequency** | Less frequent (every 3rd to 12th contraction joint) | More frequent (every 4–5 m) |
| **Cost** | Higher (filler + sealer) | Lower |

**Spacing criteria (2 marks):**
- *Expansion joint spacing* is governed by the maximum *expansion* the joint gap can absorb without buckling; IRC limits to **60 m** to prevent blow-ups.
- *Contraction joint spacing* is governed by the **Bradbury formula** and limited to a **minimum of 4 m** for practical construction (formwork, dowel installation) and **maximum of 5 m** for plain CC to keep shrinkage stress within safe limits.

---

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Step 1: Free thermal expansion (2 marks):**
$$\delta = L \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T = 54 \times 10 \times 10^{-6} \times 35$$
$$\delta = 0.0189 \text{ m} = 18.9 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2: Account for filler compressibility (2 marks):**
Since the filler compresses by 50%, the slab can close by only 50% of the initial gap. To absorb $\delta$ fully:
$$w_{required} = \frac{\delta}{0.50} = \frac{18.9}{0.50} = 37.8 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3: Add construction tolerance (1 mark):**
$$w_{total} = 37.8 + 5.0 = 42.8 \text{ mm} \approx 45 \text{ mm}$$

**Adopt: 45 mm** joint gap with pre-moulded impregnated fibreboard filler.

**Step 4: Dowel and tie bar details (2 marks):**
- **Dowel bars (transverse expansion joint):**
  - Diameter: $d = h/8$. Assuming $h = 250$ mm, $d = 31.25$ mm → adopt **32 mm** dia.
  - Length: **500 mm** embedment + 50 mm gap on slab side → **550 mm** total. Half-length (250 mm) embedded in each slab; one end **greased + capped** to allow slip.
  - Spacing: **250–300 mm c/c** along the joint.
- **Tie bars (longitudinal joint):**
  - Diameter: **10–12 mm** deformed bars.
  - Length: **600–1000 mm** (e.g., 600 mm @ 500 mm c/c).
  - Spacing: **500 mm c/c** along the joint length.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Unit conversion error** — Mixing MPa with kN/m² without converting ($1 \text{ MPa} = 1000 \text{ kN/m}^2$). *Loss: 1–2 marks per sub-question.*
> 2. **Forgetting the 50% compressibility of the filler** when computing expansion joint gap width. *Loss: 1 mark.*
> 3. **Adopting L > calculated L** — Students sometimes *round up* the spacing to a "nicer" number, defeating the design intent. *Loss: 0.5–1 mark.*
> 4. **Not writing units** in the final numerical answer. *Loss: 0.5 mark (poor presentation).*
> 5. **Confusing the role of dowel vs. tie bars** — Dowels transfer *load* across transverse joints; tie bars hold *adjacent lanes together* longitudinally. *Loss: 1 mark.*
> 6. **Omitting the assumption of partial restraint at ends** in the derivation. *Loss: 1 mark.*

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Joint Spacings (Module 4)**

- ☐ **Joint spacing = optimisation** between too-few joints (cracking risk) and too-many joints (cost + ride quality).
- ☐ **Master formula:** $L = \dfrac{2 \sigma_{st}}{f \cdot \gamma \cdot \Delta T}$ (Bradbury's formula).
- ☐ **Expansion joint spacing:** Max **60 m** (IRC:58-2015).
- ☐ **Contraction joint spacing:** **4–5 m** (plain CC), **5–6 m** (RCC), **CRCP** has no transverse joints.
- ☐ **Longitudinal joint spacing:** **3.0–3.5 m** = lane width.
- ☐ **Expansion joint gap:** $w = L \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T_{max} \div \text{compressibility} + \text{tolerance}$.
- ☐ **Dowel bar** = load transfer device at transverse joints → diameter ≈ $h/8$, length ≈ 500 mm, spacing ≈ 250–300 mm c/c.
- ☐ **Tie bar** = lane-tie device at longitudinal joints → 10–12 mm dia deformed bar, @ 500 mm c/c.
- ☐ **Coefficient of subgrade friction** $f$ is dimensionless; typical = **1.0 to 1.5**.
- ☐ **Unit weight of PCC** $\gamma = 24$ kN/m³ (always quote this value unless stated otherwise).
- ☐ **Coefficient of thermal expansion** of concrete $\alpha = 10 \times 10^{-6}$ per °C.
- ☐ **Always round DOWN** the calculated spacing to the next practical construction value (typically a multiple of 0.5 m).
- ☐ **Minimum spacing of 4 m** is a *constructability* floor — even if formula gives a smaller value, do not go below 4 m for plain CC.
- ☐ **IRC:58-2015** is the governing code; cite it in all numerical answers.
- ☐ **Joint sealant** (polysulphide / bitumen) is filled in the upper 25–30 mm of all joint grooves to prevent water ingress and incompressible entry.

> **One-line memory aid:** *"The slab wants to shrink — the subgrade won't let it — so we cut the slab at every 4–5 m and let it shrink harmlessly at the cuts."*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design of Tie Bar and Dowel Bar Using IRC Method
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Design of Tie Bar and Dowel Bar Using IRC Method

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET746 - HIGHWAY MATERIALS AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Design of Tie Bar and Dowel Bar Using IRC Method

---

### 1.1 What Are Tie Bars and Dowel Bars?

In a **Cement Concrete Pavement (CCP)**, the large continuous slab is deliberately divided into smaller panels using **contraction joints** (transverse) and **longitudinal joints**. These joints are weak planes that control cracking, but they also create a structural challenge: **the slabs must still behave as a unified pavement under traffic loads.** This is precisely the role of **dowel bars** and **tie bars**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Dowel Bars** are *smooth, round, mild-steel bars* placed **across transverse contraction/expansion joints** to **transfer load** from one slab panel to the adjacent one. They permit horizontal movement (slab expansion/contraction) but resist vertical differential deflection under wheel loads.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tie Bars** are *deformed (ribbed) steel bars* placed **across longitudinal joints** to **hold the slabs together horizontally** and prevent separation of adjacent lanes. They do **NOT** transfer vertical load — they are structural "anchors" against horizontal pull-apart forces.

### 1.2 Real-World Analogy — The "Bookshelf and Book" Concept

Imagine a **row of identical books standing on a shelf**, side by side:

- **Dowel bars** are like the **wooden pegs that join two adjacent books at mid-height**. When you push down on one book (a wheel load), the peg helps the *neighboring book share that load* — this is **load transfer** across a transverse joint. But the peg is smooth, so the books can still *slide* slightly along the shelf as humidity changes — this is the **thermal expansion/contraction allowance**.

- **Tie bars** are like a **piece of string tying two adjacent books together at the spine**. They prevent the books from drifting apart from each other (no horizontal separation), but they don't help one book *carry* the weight of another.

> [!NOTE]
> **Simple memory hook:**
> - **Dowel = "Down-load" transfer** → vertical load sharing across transverse joints.
> - **Tie = "Together-apart" prevention** → horizontal integrity across longitudinal joints.

### 1.3 Why Are They Designed Using the IRC Method?

The **Indian Roads Congress (IRC)** publishes standardized design codes to ensure uniformity, safety, and economy in Indian highway practice. The primary codes governing this design are:

- **IRC:58-2015** — *Guidelines for the Design of Plain Jointed Rigid Pavements for Highways* (Fourth Revision). This is the **primary reference** for tie bar and dowel bar design.
- **IRC:15-2017** — *Standard Specifications and Code of Practice for Construction of Concrete Roads*.

The IRC method provides **empirical and semi-empirical formulas** calibrated to Indian climatic, material, and traffic conditions — making it more appropriate for Indian B.Tech students and practitioners than the AASHTO or PCA methods for typical KTU problems.

### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|
| Unit weight of concrete (PCC) | **24 kN/m³** |
| Coefficient of friction (f) at slab-subbase interface | **1.5** (dimensionless) |
| Allowable flexural stress of dowel bar steel (F<sub>s</sub>) | **140 MPa** (or 1000 kg/cm²) |
| Allowable bearing stress of concrete on dowel (F<sub>b</sub>) | **10 MPa** (or 100 kg/cm²) |
| Allowable tensile stress in tie-bar steel (S) | **140 MPa** (mild steel) / 230 MPa (deformed HYSD) |
| Dowel bar diameter range | **25 mm to 38 mm** |
| Dowel bar length range | **500 mm to 700 mm** |
| Tie bar diameter range | **10 mm to 16 mm** |
| Tie bar length range | **600 mm to 1000 mm** |
| Spacing of dowel bars | **250 mm to 300 mm** center-to-center |

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Functions and Behavioural Requirements

### 2.1.1 Functions of Dowel Bars
1. **Transfer wheel load** from the loaded slab to the adjacent unloaded slab.
2. **Reduce differential deflection (faulting)** across transverse joints.
3. **Maintain vertical alignment** of slab edges.
4. **Allow horizontal movement** of the slab due to thermal expansion/contraction (hence *smooth* and *one end coated with bond-breaker or capped with a sleeve*).

### 2.1.2 Functions of Tie Bars
1. **Prevent lane separation** at longitudinal joints.
2. **Resist curling/warping stresses** that tend to open up longitudinal joints.
3. **Maintain aggregate interlock continuity** at the joint.
4. Tie bars are **deformed** so they bond with concrete on both sides — they are anchored in the concrete, not free to slide.

## 2.2 Forces Acting on Tie Bars — IRC Philosophy

The IRC:58 method models the slab as resting on a frictional sub-base. As the slab tries to **shrink** (due to temperature drop and drying shrinkage) or **expand** and contract cyclically, the **frictional resistance** of the subbase tries to restrain this movement. This restraint force must be resisted by the **tensile capacity** of the tie bars.

The **maximum friction force** that develops in a slab of length *L* (between free ends) is:

$$F_{friction} = f \cdot W \cdot L$$

where:
- *f* = coefficient of friction between slab and subbase (typically **1.5**)
- *W* = weight of slab per unit area (kN/m² or kg/m²)
- *L* = length of slab between free expansion joints (m)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **critical principle**: The total tensile force in *all* tie bars along a longitudinal joint must be **≥ the frictional resistance** of the slab panel. This is the equilibrium equation the IRC design is based on.

## 2.3 IRC Method — Tie Bar Design Equations

Let:
- *d* = diameter of tie bar (mm)
- *A<sub>s</sub>* = cross-sectional area of one tie bar (mm²) = $\frac{\pi}{4} d^2$
- *L<sub>t</sub>* = spacing of tie bars along the longitudinal joint (mm or m)
- *S* = allowable tensile stress in tie bar steel (MPa or N/mm²)
- *h* = thickness of concrete slab (mm)
- *ρ* = unit weight of concrete (kN/m³)
- *W* = $\rho \times h$ = weight of slab per unit area
- *L* = distance between successive transverse free joints (length of slab panel) (m)

**The fundamental IRC equation** (equilibrium of forces per unit length of joint):

$$\frac{A_s \cdot S}{L_t} = f \cdot W \cdot L$$

**Solving for spacing of tie bars:**

$$L_t = \frac{A_s \cdot S}{f \cdot W \cdot L}$$

**Solving for required cross-sectional area of one tie bar:**

$$A_s = \frac{f \cdot W \cdot L \cdot L_t}{S}$$

**Tie bar length** is governed by **bond development length** in concrete:

$$L_{tie} = \frac{\sigma_s \cdot d}{4 \cdot \tau_{bd}}$$

where $\tau_{bd}$ is the **bond stress** between steel and concrete (typically **1.0 to 1.4 MPa** for plain mild steel, **2.0 to 2.5 MPa** for deformed HYSD bars as per IRC:58).

## 2.4 IRC Method — Dowel Bar Design Equations

The dowel bar acts as a **short cantilever beam** embedded in concrete, loaded by the wheel load transferred from the adjacent slab. The IRC method uses **two governing criteria**:

### 2.4.1 Criterion 1: Shear Capacity of the Dowel Bar

The dowel is a short beam, so its **flexural rigidity** governs. The IRC:58-2015 formula for **allowable load per dowel bar (P<sub>all</sub>)** is:

$$P_{all} = \frac{F_s \cdot d^3}{L_d + 8.8 \cdot d}$$

where:
- *F<sub>s</sub>* = allowable flexural stress of dowel steel (**140 MPa** for mild steel)
- *d* = diameter of dowel bar
- *L<sub>d</sub>* = effective length of embedment of dowel in concrete (≈ half the dowel length minus joint width)

For **long dowels** (where *L<sub>d</sub>* + 8.8*d* ≈ *L<sub>d</sub>*, the denominator is dominated by embedment length), the simplified IRC formula is:

$$P_{all} = \frac{F_s \cdot d^3}{L_d}$$

### 2.4.2 Criterion 2: Bearing Stress on Concrete Below the Dowel

The dowel bears against the concrete below it. The **bearing stress** must not exceed the allowable bearing stress of concrete:

$$\sigma_b = \frac{P_{all}}{d \cdot L_d} \leq F_b$$

where *F<sub>b</sub>* = **10 MPa** (allowable bearing stress on concrete).

From this, the **minimum embedment length** required:

$$L_d = \frac{F_s \cdot d}{4 \cdot F_b}$$

### 2.4.3 Total Length of Dowel Bar

$$L_{total} = 2 \cdot L_d + joint\ width$$

The **joint width** is usually **20 mm to 25 mm**. With bond-breaker on one half, the effective embedment *L<sub>d</sub>* in IRC calculations is the **bonded side** (the side that resists load).

### 2.4.4 Number of Dowel Bars Across the Joint

$$N = \frac{\text{Width of slab (m)}}{Spacing\ of\ dowel\ bars\ (m)} + 1$$

The **central dowel** in a slab panel must also resist the **maximum wheel load**, but IRC distributes the wheel load across multiple dowels using the concept of **effective load distribution width**.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is a high-yield summary **specifically curated for KTU 2024 Scheme Board Examinations** on this topic. Memorize the highlighted entries.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Governing Criterion |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Slab weight per unit area | $W = \rho \cdot h$ | kN/m² | — |
| 2 | Friction force on slab | $F_f = f \cdot W \cdot L$ | kN/m | — |
| 3 | **Tie bar spacing** | $L_t = \dfrac{A_s \cdot S}{f \cdot W \cdot L}$ | mm | IRC:58 Cl. 5.5 |
| 4 | Required area of one tie bar | $A_s = \dfrac{f \cdot W \cdot L \cdot L_t}{S}$ | mm² | — |
| 5 | Tie bar length (development) | $L_{tie} = \dfrac{S \cdot d}{4 \cdot \tau_{bd}}$ | mm | IS:456 / IRC:58 |
| 6 | **Allowable load per dowel** | $P_{all} = \dfrac{F_s \cdot d^3}{L_d + 8.8d}$ | N | IRC:58 Cl. 5.4 |
| 7 | Bearing stress on concrete | $\sigma_b = \dfrac{P_{all}}{d \cdot L_d} \leq F_b$ | MPa | IRC:58 |
| 8 | **Required embedment length** | $L_d = \dfrac{F_s \cdot d}{4 \cdot F_b}$ | mm | IRC:58 |
| 9 | Total dowel length | $L_{total} = 2L_d + 20\ mm$ | mm | — |
| 10 | Allowable load per dowel (Alt.) | $P_{all} = \dfrac{F_s \cdot d^3}{L_d}$ | N | Simplified IRC |
| 11 | Cross-section of dowel | $A_d = \dfrac{\pi}{4} d^2$ | mm² | — |
| 12 | Load transferred per dowel | $P = \dfrac{P_{wheel}}{N_{eff}}$ | N | Wheel load distribution |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** This design methodology is used in the design of **National Highways, Expressways, Urban Rigid Pavements, and Airport Rigid Aprons** in India. Mastering these formulas is essential for careers in **NHAI, PWD, IRC, RITES, and private highway consultants** like L&T, Afcons, and Reliance Infrastructure.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Tie Bar Spacing Equation (IRC:58)

### 3.1.1 Setup and Free-Body Concept

Consider a longitudinal joint of length *L* (the slab length between two consecutive free transverse joints). A strip of slab of unit width (1 m) along this joint is in equilibrium under:

- **Tensile force** in the tie bars: $T = \dfrac{A_s \cdot S}{L_t} \times L$ (resisting pull-apart)
- **Frictional resistance** from subbase: $F_f = f \cdot W \cdot L$ (resisting slab movement)

### 3.1.2 Equilibrium Equation

For the slab panel not to separate at the longitudinal joint, the **tensile capacity of all tie bars along the joint length** must equal (or exceed) the **maximum frictional force** developed over that length:

$$\sum T_{tie} = F_{friction}$$

$$\frac{A_s \cdot S}{L_t} \cdot L = f \cdot W \cdot L$$

> **Note:** *L* appears on both sides and cancels out (a beautiful feature of this derivation — the result is independent of slab length).

### 3.1.3 Solving for Spacing $L_t$

$$\frac{A_s \cdot S}{L_t} = f \cdot W$$

$$\boxed{L_t = \frac{A_s \cdot S}{f \cdot W}}$$

This is the **fundamental IRC tie bar spacing equation**.

### 3.1.4 Solving for Required Cross-Section

Rearranging for *A<sub>s</sub>*:

$$\boxed{A_s = \frac{f \cdot W \cdot L_t}{S}}$$

### 3.1.5 Solving for Tie Bar Diameter

$$A_s = \frac{\pi}{4} d^2$$

$$\boxed{d = \sqrt{\frac{4 \cdot f \cdot W \cdot L_t}{\pi \cdot S}}}$$

## 3.2 Derivation of the Required Dowel Embedment Length

### 3.2.1 Setup

The dowel is a **cantilever of span $L_d$** fixed in concrete, loaded at the joint face by the transferred wheel load $P_{all}$. The bending moment at the concrete face is:

$$M = P_{all} \cdot L_d$$

For a circular cross-section, the **section modulus** is:

$$Z = \frac{\pi \cdot d^3}{32}$$

### 3.2.2 Flexural Stress Limitation

The maximum bending stress must not exceed the allowable flexural stress of the steel:

$$\sigma = \frac{M}{Z} = \frac{P_{all} \cdot L_d}{\pi d^3 / 32} \leq F_s$$

$$P_{all} \leq \frac{F_s \cdot \pi \cdot d^3}{32 \cdot L_d}$$

Since $\pi/32 \approx 1/10.2$, the IRC conservatively writes:

$$P_{all} = \frac{F_s \cdot d^3}{L_d}$$

### 3.2.3 Bearing Stress Limitation

Beneath the dowel, the **contact bearing area** is approximately $d \cdot L_d$. So the bearing stress on concrete is:

$$\sigma_b = \frac{P_{all}}{d \cdot L_d} \leq F_b$$

Substituting $P_{all} = \dfrac{F_s \cdot d^3}{L_d}$:

$$\sigma_b = \frac{F_s \cdot d^3 / L_d}{d \cdot L_d} = \frac{F_s \cdot d^2}{L_d^2}$$

Setting $\sigma_b = F_b$ and solving for *L<sub>d</sub>*:

$$L_d^2 = \frac{F_s \cdot d^2}{F_b}$$

$$\boxed{L_d = d \cdot \sqrt{\frac{F_s}{F_b}}}$$

For the IRC standard values *F<sub>s</sub>* = 140 MPa and *F<sub>b</sub>* = 10 MPa:

$$L_d = d \cdot \sqrt{\frac{140}{10}} = d \cdot \sqrt{14} \approx 3.74 \cdot d$$

Equivalently, the IRC formula:

$$\boxed{L_d = \frac{F_s \cdot d}{4 \cdot F_b} = \frac{140 \cdot d}{4 \cdot 10} = 3.5 \cdot d}$$

> **Note:** The two derivations yield *L<sub>d</sub>* ≈ 3.5*d* to 3.74*d*. IRC:58 uses **3.5*d* to 4*d*** in practice.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Examination Standard)

### Problem Statement
Design the **tie bars and dowel bars** for a concrete pavement with the following data (typical KTU 2-mark and 14-mark style):

| Given Data | Value |
|---|---|
| Slab thickness (*h*) | 250 mm |
| Slab panel length between free joints (*L*) | 4.5 m |
| Slab panel width (*B*) | 3.75 m (one lane) |
| Unit weight of concrete (*ρ*) | 24 kN/m³ |
| Coefficient of friction (*f*) | 1.5 |
| Allowable tensile stress in tie bar (*S*) | 140 MPa |
| Allowable flexural stress in dowel (*F<sub>s</sub>*) | 140 MPa |
| Allowable bearing stress on concrete (*F<sub>b</sub>*) | 10 MPa |
| Wheel load (*P<sub>w</sub>*) | 50 kN (single wheel) |
| Joint width | 20 mm |
| Spacing of dowel bars assumed | 300 mm c/c |

### 3.3.1 Solution — Tie Bar Design

**Step 1: Slab weight per unit area**
$$W = \rho \cdot h = 24 \times 0.250 = 6.0\ kN/m^2$$

**Step 2: Assume tie bar diameter and find area**
Assume *d* = 12 mm (standard HYSD bar):
$$A_s = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (12)^2 = 113.1\ mm^2$$

**Step 3: Required tie bar spacing (IRC:58)**
$$L_t = \frac{A_s \cdot S}{f \cdot W \cdot L}$$

$$L_t = \frac{113.1 \times 140}{1.5 \times 6.0 \times 4.5 \times 10^3}\ (converting\ units\ to\ N\ \&\ mm)$$

Convert: 1 kN/m² = 10⁻³ N/mm². So *f · W · L* = 1.5 × 6.0 × 10⁻³ × 4500 = 40.5 N/mm.

$$L_t = \frac{113.1 \times 140}{40.5} = 390.9\ mm \approx 390\ mm$$

> **Provide a spacing of 300 mm c/c** (rounded down for safety) → 4 bars per 3.75 m lane width.

**Step 4: Tie bar length (development length)**
For deformed HYSD bar, $\tau_{bd} = 2.0$ MPa:
$$L_{tie} = \frac{S \cdot d}{4 \cdot \tau_{bd}} = \frac{140 \times 12}{4 \times 2.0} = 210\ mm$$

> **Standard practice:** Provide 600 mm length to be on the safer side (this also accommodates the embedment across the longitudinal joint).

### 3.3.2 Solution — Dowel Bar Design

**Step 1: Assume dowel diameter**
For medium-to-heavy traffic, use *d* = **32 mm** standard dowel.

**Step 2: Required embedment length**
$$L_d = \frac{F_s \cdot d}{4 \cdot F_b} = \frac{140 \times 32}{4 \times 10} = 112\ mm$$

> **Standard practice (IRC:58):** Provide *L<sub>d</sub>* = 4*d* to 4.5*d* = **140 to 144 mm**.

**Step 3: Total length of dowel bar**
$$L_{total} = 2 \cdot L_d + joint\ width = 2 \times 140 + 20 = 300\ mm$$

> **Standard practice (IRC:58):** Provide 500 mm to 600 mm total length.

**Step 4: Allowable load per dowel**
$$P_{all} = \frac{F_s \cdot d^3}{L_d} = \frac{140 \times (32)^3}{140} = 140 \times 32^2 = 140 \times 1024 = 143{,}360\ N \approx 143.4\ kN$$

> **Check against wheel load:** If a 50 kN wheel is distributed over *N* effective dowels (typically 4 to 5 dowels carry the load for a 3.75 m wide slab, depending on slab thickness), the load per dowel = 50/4 ≈ 12.5 kN, which is **far less than 143.4 kN** — design is safe.

**Step 5: Number of dowels across the lane**
$$N = \frac{B}{spacing} + 1 = \frac{3750}{300} + 1 = 12.5 + 1 \approx 13\ dowels\ per\ lane$$

> Provide **13 dowel bars of 32 mm diameter, 500 mm long, spaced at 300 mm c/c**.

**Step 6: Bearing stress check**
$$\sigma_b = \frac{P_{applied}}{d \cdot L_d} = \frac{50{,}000 / 4}{32 \times 140} = \frac{12{,}500}{4480} = 2.79\ MPa < F_b = 10\ MPa \quad \checkmark$$

### 3.3.3 Final Design Summary Table

| Component | Design Result |
|---|---|
| Tie bar diameter | **12 mm** deformed HYSD |
| Tie bar length | **600 mm** |
| Tie bar spacing | **300 mm c/c** |
| Number of tie bars per lane | **13** |
| Dowel bar diameter | **32 mm** smooth mild steel |
| Dowel bar length | **500 mm** |
| Dowel embedment length | **140 mm** |
| Dowel bar spacing | **300 mm c/c** |
| Number of dowels per joint | **13** |

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Architecture — Tie Bar Design Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Input Slab Data] --> B[Slab thickness h mm]
    A --> C[Slab panel length L m]
    A --> D[Unit weight of concrete 24 kN per m3]
    A --> E[Coefficient of friction 1.5]
    A --> F[Allowable steel stress 140 MPa]
    B --> G[Compute W = rho times h]
    C --> G
    D --> G
    G --> H[Assume tie bar diameter d]
    H --> I[Compute area As = pi d squared over 4]
    I --> J[Compute spacing Lt = As times S over f times W times L]
    J --> K{Spacing within IRC limits 250 to 750 mm}
    K -->|Yes| L[Compute development length Ltie = S times d over 4 tau bd]
    K -->|No| M[Revise d and iterate]
    L --> N[Final Design Output]
    M --> H
    N --> O[Tie Bar Specification]
    O --> P[Diameter 12 to 16 mm]
    O --> Q[Length 600 to 1000 mm]
    O --> R[Spacing 250 to 400 mm c to c]
```

## 4.2 Functional Block Architecture — Dowel Bar Design Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Input Pavement Data] --> B[Wheel Load Pw kN]
    A --> C[Slab width B m]
    A --> D[Allowable flexural stress Fs 140 MPa]
    A --> E[Allowable bearing stress Fb 10 MPa]
    A --> F[Joint width 20 mm]
    B --> G[Estimate load per dowel P = Pw divided by N eff]
    C --> H[Assume dowel diameter d]
    D --> I[Compute allowable load Pall = Fs d cubed divided by Ld]
    E --> J[Compute embedment Ld = Fs d divided by 4 Fb]
    H --> I
    H --> J
    I --> K{P applied less than Pall}
    K -->|Yes| L[Check bearing stress sigma b = Pall divided by d Ld]
    K -->|No| M[Increase d and iterate]
    L --> N{sigma b less than Fb}
    N -->|Yes| O[Compute total length Ltotal = 2 Ld plus joint width]
    N -->|No| M
    O --> P[Compute number of dowels N = B by spacing plus 1]
    P --> Q[Final Design Output]
    Q --> R[Diameter 25 to 38 mm]
    Q --> S[Length 500 to 700 mm]
    Q --> T[Spacing 250 to 300 mm c to c]
    M --> H
```

## 4.3 Cross-Sectional Schematic of Joints (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph Transverse_Joint["TRANSVERSE JOINT - Equipped with DOWEL BARS"]
        TJ1[Slab Panel 1] -->|Wheel Load Transfer| TJ2[Slab Panel 2]
        TJ3[Smooth Dowel 32 mm dia 500 mm long] --- TJ1
        TJ3 --- TJ2
        TJ4[Bond Breaker on one half of dowel] --- TJ3
    end
    subgraph Longitudinal_Joint["LONGITUDINAL JOINT - Equipped with TIE BARS"]
        LJ1[Lane 1 Slab] -->|Hold Together| LJ2[Lane 2 Slab]
        LJ3[Deformed Tie Bar 12 mm dia 600 mm long] --- LJ1
        LJ3 --- LJ2
        LJ4[Full Bond - both halves anchored] --- LJ3
    end
    Transverse_Joint --- Longitudinal_Joint
```

## 4.4 Force Equilibrium Diagram (Tie Bar Mechanism)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Slab Panel] -->|Tries to Shrink| B[Friction Force Ff]
    B -->|f times W times L| C[Subbase Resistance]
    A -->|Resisted by| D[Tie Bar Tension T]
    D -->|As times S over Lt times L| E[Longitudinal Joint]
    F[Equilibrium Condition] --> G[T equals Ff]
    G --> H[Spacing Equation Lt = As S over f W]
```
